1.1: biblos geneseos (biblos geneseos) — book of the origin/genealogy — the same phrase as LXX Gen 5:1 (haute he biblos geneseos anthropon). Matthew deliberately opens with the creation-formula, presenting Jesus as inaugurating a new genesis. Iesou Christou — Jesus Christ — the name first, immediately followed by both titles: huiou Dauid (son of David, the royal Messianic line) and huiou Abraam (son of Abraham, the covenant promise-bearer). The reverse order (David before Abraham) emphasizes the Messianic dimension; then the founding covenant is named.
1.18: mnesteutheises (mnesteuo) — betrothed, engaged — the Jewish betrothal (erusin) was a legally binding covenant, not merely a modern engagement; breaking it required a divorce (v.19 apolyein = to release/divorce). ek pneumatos hagiou — of/from the Holy Spirit — the preposition ek denotes origin: the conception is sourced from the Holy Spirit, not from human generation. ephanerothe — was revealed, appeared — Matthew uses a passive suggesting divine disclosure; the pregnancy was not hidden but theologically explained by the angel as divine action.
1.21: Iesous — Jesus, the Greek form of Hebrew Yeshua (Joshua), which means YHWH saves or YHWH is salvation. The angel's explanation (autos gar sosei = for he himself will save) plays on the name's etymology: the one named YHWH-saves will in fact save (sosei). ton laon autou — his people — the possessive is striking; even before the birth, the angel calls Israel his people. apo ton hamartion auton — from their sins — not merely from enemies or oppressors (the expected Davidic deliverance) but from sins; the scope of salvation is transformed.
1.23: parthenos (parthenos) — virgin — Matthew uses the LXX reading of Isa 7:14, which translates Hebrew almah (young woman of marriageable age) with the Greek term specifically denoting virginity. Matthew stakes the fulfillment on this reading. Emmanuel — Hebrew Immanu El = God with us — not merely a descriptive title but a theological claim: the presence of God (the Shekinah) is now embodied in the person of Jesus. Matthew frames the entire Gospel between Immanuel (1:23) and the presence-promise of 28:20.
1.2: The genealogical formula egennesen (aorist of gennao, to father, beget) runs throughout vv.2-16, establishing the chain of legal covenant descent. The first entry names Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, then specifies Iouda kai tous adelphous autou — Judah and his brothers — the first appearance of "brothers" signals the wider family unit even as the Messianic line passes through the specific son. The Greek egennesen is the technical genealogical verb, equivalent to the Hebrew holid in the OT toledot formulas.
1.3: The first of four women in the genealogy: ex autes — from/out of her — the preposition ek with the genitive feminine pronoun (autes) singles out the maternal contribution, departing from the uniform egennesen formula to flag the irregular or notable circumstance. Tamar (Gen 38) bore Perez and Zerah to her father-in-law Judah through a deception she resorted to when he withheld her rightful marriage. Matthew includes her without explanation, leaving the reader to supply the narrative context.
1.4: The genealogical formula continues without interruption from v.2 through v.16. These verses establish the covenant lineage through which the Messianic promise (2 Sam 7:12-16; Ps 89) passes. The names in vv.4-6 (Ram, Amminadab, Nahshon, Salmon) bridge the wilderness generation to the period of the judges and the establishment of the monarchy — each link in the chain is necessary to the Davidic credential that the genealogy establishes for Jesus.
1.5: Two more women appear: Rahab (a Gentile prostitute of Jericho, Josh 2; 6:25) and Ruth (a Moabite widow, Ruth 1-4). Both are Gentiles incorporated into the Israelite community through covenant faithfulness. Rahab: ek tes Raab; Ruth: ek tes Routh. The same ek construction marks each woman. Matthew's inclusion of Gentile women in the Messianic genealogy anticipates the Gospel's movement toward all nations (28:19).
1.6: The fourth notable woman: ek tes tou Ouriou — from the wife of Uriah — Bathsheba is not named; she is identified by her dead husband's name, emphasizing the adultery that was the context of Solomon's conception (2 Sam 11-12). The periphrasis keeps the moral reality visible: Jesus's lineage passes through covenant violation as well as covenant faithfulness. The inclusion of all four women signals that the genealogy does not idealize the Messianic line's history.
1.7: The post-Solomonic period: Rehoboam (Roboam), Abijah (Abia), Asa. These names carry the weight of the divided kingdom period and Israel's repeated covenant failures. Matthew does not editorialize; the genealogy moves forward through the historical chain, including kings who were largely unfaithful, because the covenant promise to David (2 Sam 7:16) was unconditional in its ultimate scope. The lineage passes through the kings despite their failures.
1.8: Verse 8 omits three kings between Joram and Uzziah: Ahaziah, Joash, and Amaziah (cf. 1 Chr 3:11-12). Matthew's selective genealogy follows the practice of OT genealogies that sometimes omit names for literary or theological purposes. The omissions may reflect a deliberate 14-14-14 structuring (v.17) or judgments about dynasties contaminated by Ahab's house (through Ahaziah's mother Athaliah). The LXX and MT genealogies themselves vary, and Matthew's authority is his apostolic presentation, not genealogical completeness.
1.9: The genealogical chain continues through the late Judahite monarchy: Uzziah (= Azariah in Kings), Jotham, Ahaz, Hezekiah — a period marked by the growing Assyrian threat that culminated in the northern kingdom's fall (722 BCE). The names carry narrative freight for readers familiar with the history, but Matthew's genealogy abstracts them into the covenantal chain: each king is a necessary link in the Davidic promise moving toward its culmination in Jesus.
1.10: Hezekiah, Manasseh, Amos/Amon, Josiah — the final pre-exilic generation. Manasseh was the most apostate of Judah's kings (2 Kgs 21:1-18); Josiah was the great reformer (2 Kgs 22-23). The contrast within a single genealogical sequence demonstrates that the Messianic line did not depend on consistent covenant faithfulness in its bearers. The promise endures through the worst of Israel's kings as well as the best.
1.11: epi tes metoikesias Babylonos — at the time of the Babylonian exile (metoikesia = deportation, change of residence by force) — Matthew uses metoikesia (also v.12, 17) rather than the harsher aichmalōsia (captivity, imprisonment) perhaps to emphasize the displacement dimension rather than the penal one. The exile is the structural pivot of the genealogy's three sections (v.17). Jechoniah (= Jehoiachin) is the king taken to Babylon (2 Kgs 24:15).
1.12: The post-exilic section of the genealogy (meta de ten metoikesian Babylonos = after the Babylonian exile) continues from Jechoniah through Shealtiel and Zerubbabel — the restoration figures (Ezra, Nehemiah, Haggai, Zechariah). Zerubbabel was the governor of the returned community and a Davidic figure around whom royal hopes clustered (Hag 2:23; Zech 4:6-10). The genealogy passes through the restoration generation on its way to the birth of Jesus.
1.13: The post-Zerubbabel section enters the period of Second Temple Judaism where no biblical genealogies exist: Abiud, Eliakim, Azor are otherwise unknown from canonical sources. Matthew either had access to Davidic family genealogical records or worked from traditional sources. The names are historically plausible (all are common Second Temple Jewish names) but cannot be independently verified. The genealogy is making a legal and theological claim about Jesus's Davidic descent, not a purely archival historical record.
1.14: The genealogy continues through the otherwise unattested post-exilic figures: Zadok, Achim, Eliud. The covenant chain is maintained through the dark centuries of Persian, Ptolemaic, and Seleucid rule when the Davidic dynasty held no political power. The genealogy preserves the claim that the legal heir to the Davidic throne continued to exist even when no Davidic king ruled — a historically significant claim for a 1st-century Jewish audience familiar with the messianic expectation.
1.15: The final generation before Joseph: Eleazar, Matthan, Jacob. The name Jacob echoes the patriarch, creating an inclusio with Abraham (v.2): the genealogy begins with the founding patriarch and ends with the patriarch's name repeated one generation before the climax. Iakōb de egennesen ton Iōseph — Jacob fathered Joseph — the repetition of the patriarch name immediately before Joseph (the patriarch Joseph's namesake) strengthens the typological resonance: as the patriarch Joseph brought salvation to Israel through suffering, so this Joseph becomes the legal father of the one who saves.
1.16: Iōseph ton andra Marias — Joseph the husband of Mary — a grammatical pivot: all previous egennesen entries say "A was the father of B"; here the formula shifts to ex hes egennethe Iesous — from whom Jesus was born. The masculine genealogical formula is broken precisely at the point of the virgin birth: Joseph is identified as Mary's husband, not as Jesus's biological father. Ho legomenos Christos — the one called Christ — the appended title marks Jesus as the culmination of the entire 42-generation sequence.
1.17: Pasai oun hai geneai apo Abraam heos Dauid geneai dekatessares — thus all the generations from Abraham to David are fourteen generations — the triple fourteen pattern (14-14-14: Abraham→David, David→exile, exile→Christ) may reflect gematria: the numerical value of David's name in Hebrew (dalet=4, vav=6, dalet=4 = 14), making the genealogy itself a literary-numerical declaration that Jesus is the Son of David. Matthew's arrangement required selective omission of some kings (v.8), a recognized genealogical convention.
1.19: Iōseph de ho aner autes dikaios on — Joseph her husband, being a righteous man — dikaios (righteous) describes one who observes Torah faithfully. His plan to divorce Mary lathrai (secretly, quietly) means using the private bill of divorce rather than public accusation (Deut 22:23-24 would have required stoning if adultery were publicly alleged). Apolyein auten — to release/divorce her — the legal vocabulary confirms the betrothal's binding status: apolyein is the same verb for divorce of a married woman (5:31-32). Joseph's mercy goes beyond Torah's minimum requirement.
1.20: kat' onar — in a dream — Matthew uses this phrase five times (1:20; 2:12, 13, 19, 22) for divine communication, corresponding to OT dream-revelation (Gen 37-40; Dan 2, 7). Iōseph huios Dauid — Joseph son of David — the angel's address identifies Joseph's Davidic genealogy as the legal basis for Jesus's Davidic sonship: the legal father (even if not biological) transmits the lineage. Mē phobēthēs paralabein Marian ten gynaika sou — do not fear to take Mary as your wife — the aorist infinitive paralabein (to take alongside, to receive) confirms that the consummation of the marriage had not yet occurred.
1.22: touto de holon gegonen hina plerothe — all this happened in order that it might be fulfilled — the hina (in order that) with subjunctive indicates divine purpose: the events were arranged for fulfillment, not merely correlated. plerothe (aorist passive subjunctive of pleroo) — filled up, completed. Matthew's fulfillment formula (to rhēthen hypo kyriou dia tou prophētou = what was spoken by the Lord through the prophet) traces the divine word through two agents: the Lord (kyrios) as the primary speaker, the prophet as the instrument. This form of the formula appears 11 times in Matthew.
1.24: egertheis de ho Iōseph apo tou hypnou epoiesen hos prosetaxen auto ho angelos kyriou — Joseph, having been aroused from sleep, did as the angel of the Lord commanded him. The participle egertheis (having been aroused/raised up) is the same root as the resurrection verb (egeiro), though here merely "woke." The obedience formula (epoiesen hos = he did as...) mirrors OT compliance patterns (Gen 6:22; Exod 12:50 — Noah and Israel "did as God commanded") — Joseph as the obedient servant of the divine will, regardless of personal cost or public scrutiny.
1.25: heos hou eteken huion — until she bore a son — the conjunction heos hou (until) establishes a temporal boundary: the statement describes what obtained during the pregnancy. Greek heos hou does not grammatically imply the resumption of the condition after the boundary event; the text makes no statement about the state of the marriage after the birth. Matthew's concern is entirely with the virgin birth and the legal naming of the child. Kai ekalesen to onoma autou Iesoun — and he called his name Jesus — Joseph's naming of the child is the legal act of paternity that establishes Jesus's Davidic lineage.

2.1: magoi (magi, plural of magos) — originally a priestly caste of Persian Zoroastrians skilled in astronomy, interpretation of signs, and dream interpretation. By the 1st century the term had broadened to include wise men, court astrologers, and interpreters of omens throughout the eastern Mediterranean. Matthew does not specify three or kings — the church tradition of three kings derives from the three gifts (v.11). apo anatolon — from the east — the same direction from which the Babylonian exile came; now wise men from the nations seek the Jewish King.
2.11: prosekynesan auto — they worshiped/did obeisance to him — proskyneo (fall before in worship or homage) is used throughout Matthew for worship of Jesus (4:10, 8:2, 9:18, 14:33, 15:25, 28:9,17) and specifically for worship of God (4:10 quoting Deut 6:13). The Magi perform before the infant what the disciples perform before the risen Lord. edoran auto dora — they offered him gifts — the noun dora (gifts, offerings) carries cultic overtones; these are tribute-offerings appropriate to a king and priest.
2.2: ton astera autou en te anatole — his star in the rising/east — anatole can mean "east" (the direction) or "rising" (of a star: heliacal rising, the first appearance of a star above the eastern horizon at dawn, an astronomically significant event). The magi may have seen the star "in its rising" — at its first appearance. The definitive article (ton astera autou = his star) assumes prior knowledge of a messianic astral sign; Numbers 24:17 ("a star shall come out of Jacob") provides the OT background.
2.3: etarachthe — he was troubled, disturbed — aorist passive of tarasso (to agitate, throw into confusion; used in 14:26 of the disciples seeing Jesus on the water and in 27:65 for the disturbed watchers at the tomb). Herod's disturbance is political: a rival king of the Jews threatens his Idumean dynasty. Kai pasa Ierosolyma met' autou — and all Jerusalem with him — the city's collective anxiety parallels its rulers'; whether from genuine messianic concern or fear of Herod's violent reaction to perceived challenges is left ambiguous by Matthew.
2.4: archiereis kai grammateis tou laou — chief priests and scribes of the people — archiereis (chief priests, plural) encompasses the ruling priestly families, not merely the current high priest. Grammateis (Torah-scribes, learned interpreters) are distinguished from the Pharisees (not mentioned here) and function as the official biblical scholars. Their role is deeply ironic: they correctly identify Bethlehem (v.5) as the messianic birthplace from scripture but do not go to seek the child — biblical knowledge without response.
2.5: The chief priests and scribes cite Micah 5:2 without hesitation: en Bethleem tes Ioudaias — in Bethlehem of Judea — the specification "of Judea" distinguishes this Bethlehem from the Galilean Bethlehem (Josh 19:15). Their citation is accurate; their indifference is the irony. The learned authorities of Jerusalem know the scripture that the Magi (Gentiles from the east) had followed by astral observation. Matthew contrasts pagan seekers with Jewish experts: the Gentiles travel; the experts inform without moving.
2.6: The Micah citation (5:2) is modified significantly: oudamos elachiste ei en tois hegesin Iouda — by no means are you least among the rulers of Judah — changes the MT's "too small" to a positive declaration, and adds hostis poimanei ton laon mou ton Israel (who will shepherd my people Israel) from 2 Sam 5:2 (David's commission). The composite citation (Micah + 2 Samuel) is characteristic of Matthew's interpretive freedom in demonstrating scriptural coherence: the Messianic ruler is both Davidic birthplace (Micah) and Davidic shepherd-king (2 Sam).
2.7: ton chronon tou phainomenou asteros — the time of the appearing star — phainomai (present participle: the appearing, manifesting star) describes the star as currently visible/manifest. Herod determines the time akribos (exactly, precisely — the same adverb repeated in v.8 for his commission to the Magi). This precise timing becomes the basis for the massacre calculation in v.16: "two years old and under, according to the time he had determined." The detail is not incidental; it sets up the subsequent atrocity.
2.8: akribos exetasate — search carefully/precisely — the adverb akribos (exactly, precisely) repeats Herod's precision motif from v.7. The command to search carefully serves as the deceptive frame for his stated pious purpose (hopOs kagO elthon proskyneso auto — so that I too may come and worship him). Matthew presents the irony without editorial comment: the language of religious devotion (proskyneso = worship) is borrowed to cloak murder. The Magi are unwitting instruments of Herod's information-gathering.
2.9: kai idou ho aster — and behold, the star — the idou (behold!) signals the narrative's highlighting of a key event: the star's reappearance after their Jerusalem visit. Proegen autous — it went before them — the imperfect proegen (was going before) indicates ongoing movement of the star ahead of the Magi. Heos elthon estathē epano hou en to paidion — until having come it stood over where the child was — the aorist passive estathē (was stationed, halted) suggests a definitive stopping. The star-guidance echoes the wilderness pillar (Exod 13:21) in leading God's people to his purposes.
2.10: Echaresan charan megalen sphodra — they rejoiced a very great joy — the cognate construction (verb chairo + related noun chara) is a Hebraism (equivalent to the Hebrew infinitive absolute) used to intensify the expression: they rejoiced greatly. The adverb sphodra (very greatly, exceedingly) further intensifies. The construction appears in the LXX for superlative joy (e.g., 1 Kgs 1:40). The Magi's response to finding the star stationary is unbounded relief and celebration, marking the climactic resolution of their long journey.
2.12: Chresmatisthentes kat' onar — having been warned by a divine oracle in a dream — chrematizo in the passive (to receive a divine oracle, divine communication) is stronger than a general dream: the Magi receive a specific divine directive. The verb is used in LXX for divine responses (Jer 29:23; 37:2) and in NT for divine warnings (Heb 8:5; 11:7). Di' alles hodou anechoresan — through another road they withdrew — anachoreo (to withdraw, retire: used throughout Matt 2 for strategic repositioning) describes the Magi's obedient departure by an alternate route, thwarting Herod's intelligence-gathering.
2.13: Phyge eis Aigypton — flee into Egypt — pheugo (aorist imperative: flee!) conveys urgency. The command is delivered at night and obeyed at night (nyktos). Egypt as refuge is historically plausible (large Jewish population in Alexandria) and typologically resonant: the land of Israel's slavery becomes the Messiah's sanctuary. Heos an eipo soi — until I tell you — the conditional an with subjunctive (eipo = tell) makes the return contingent on a future divine communication, maintaining the pattern of Joseph's complete dependence on divine direction.
2.14: Anastas paralaben to paidion kai ten metera autou nyktos — having risen he took the child and his mother by night — the aorist participle anastas (having risen, stood up) is the same root as anastasis (resurrection), here simply "got up from sleep." The night departure mirrors the urgency of the Exodus (Exod 12:31-42: Israel departed from Egypt at night). Anechoreosen eis Aigypton — he withdrew into Egypt — the verb anachoreo appears again (vv.12, 13, 14, 22) as Matthew's term for divinely-directed strategic movement.
2.15: Ex Aigyptou ekalesa ton huion mou — out of Egypt I called my son — the citation of Hosea 11:1 in the MT (the LXX reads differently: ta tekna autou = his children). Matthew follows the MT reading, which has the singular "my son." Hosea 11:1 originally refers to Israel's exodus as God's son called out of Egypt. Matthew applies this typologically to Jesus: Israel as "son" collectively is the type; Jesus as "Son" individually is the antitype who recapitulates and surpasses Israel's history. The hina plerothe formula frames this as fulfillment of a deeper pattern, not merely verbal prediction.
2.16: Ethumothē lian — he was furious/enraged greatly — thymoo (to be enraged, infuriated) is a strong word for violent anger; the adverb lian (greatly, exceedingly) intensifies it. Anaireo — to do away with, kill — a strong term also used for executions (Acts 2:23; 7:52 of Jesus's death). Apo dietons kai katateron kata ton chronon hon ekribosen para ton magon — two years old and under, according to the time he had precisely determined from the Magi — the calculation explains the two-year range: Herod builds in a margin of error from the star's appearance time.
2.17: Tote eplerothe to rhēthen dia Ieremiou — then was fulfilled what was spoken through Jeremiah — note the absence of hina (in order that) that appears in other fulfillment formulas (1:22; 2:15, 23; 4:14). The adverb tote (then, at that time) replaces the purpose conjunction: Matthew does not say the massacre happened in order to fulfill, but that it was then fulfilled — a pastorally sensitive distinction that avoids making the children's deaths merely instrumental to prophetic fulfillment while still insisting on the scriptural correspondence.
2.18: The Jeremiah 31:15 citation: phone en Rama ekousthe, klauthmos kai odyrmos polys — a voice was heard in Ramah, weeping and great mourning — the aorist passive ekousthe (was heard) treats the prophetic text as a past event now seen in correspondence with a present event. Rachel weeping for her children (ta tekna autes) is a matriarchal mourning for descendants. The location of Ramah in Benjamin's territory (near Bethlehem in Judah's tribal area) was a deportation staging point for the Babylonian exiles (Jer 40:1). Matthew sees a structural correspondence between the Babylonian exile's weeping and Herod's massacre.
2.19: Teleutesantos de tou Herodou — after Herod died — the genitive absolute construction (teleuteo = to die, reach one's end) marks the temporal boundary of Jesus's Egyptian exile. Herod the Great died in 4 BCE, confirming that the birth of Jesus occurred before 4 BCE. Idou angelos kyriou phainetai kat' onar — behold, an angel of the Lord appears in a dream — the fourth occurrence of the divine-dream-announcement formula in Matthew 1-2 (1:20; 2:12, 13, 19), marking the sustained pattern of divine communication to Joseph throughout the infancy narrative.
2.20: Tethnekasin gar hoi zetountes ten psychen tou paidiou — for those who were seeking the child's soul/life are dead — the plural tethnekasin (perfect: have died and remain dead) and hoi zetountes (those who were seeking) are a precise verbal allusion to Exod 4:19 LXX where God tells Moses "for all those who were seeking your life are dead." Matthew's verbal echo is exact: Jesus's return from Egypt recapitulates Moses's return to Egypt after the death of those who sought his life, positioning Jesus as the new Moses from the very beginning of his life.
2.21: Anastas paralaben to paidion kai ten metera autou — having risen he took the child and his mother — the obedience formula repeats exactly (cf. v.14 departure formula), creating a structural parallel: departure (v.14) and return (v.21) framed by identical language. The anastas (having risen) + paralaben (took) sequence signals Joseph's immediate and complete compliance with the divine instruction, without deliberation or delay — the same pattern as v.24 (ch.1) where he took Mary as his wife after the angel's instruction.
2.22: Archelaus basileuei tes Ioudaias anti tou patros autou Herodou — Archelaus is reigning over Judea instead of his father Herod — Herod Archelaus (ruled 4 BCE–6 CE) was notorious for cruelty; he was deposed by Rome after Jewish complaints of his harsh rule and sent into exile. Phobētheis ekei apelthein — he was afraid to go there — the aorist passive phobētheis (having been made afraid, terrified) is a divine or providential fear, confirmed by a dream-warning. Joseph's fear is both rational (Archelaus's reputation) and divinely validated.
2.23: Nazōraios klethesetai — he will be called a Nazarene — the formula cites "the prophets" (plural, dia ton propheton) but no single OT text contains this phrase. Most likely explanations: (1) wordplay on Hebrew nezer (branch, shoot: Isa 11:1 — "a nezer from Jesse's roots") — the Nazarene/branch connection; (2) possible connection to Nazir (Nazirite, consecrated one: Num 6; Judg 13); (3) typological use of "prophets" as composite reference rather than single verbal citation. Matthew's formula signals prophetic fulfillment at the pattern level, not the specific-quotation level.

3.2: metanoeite (metanoeo) — repent, change one's mind and direction — the verb combines meta (after, change) + noeo (to think, perceive). True repentance is not mere remorse (metamelomai) but a reorientation of the whole person's direction. hengiken gar he basileia ton ouranon — for the kingdom of the heavens has drawn near — engiken is perfect tense (has come near and stands near), indicating the kingdom's arrival is not merely future but has approached to proximity. Basileia ton ouranon is Matthew's distinctive phrase (vs. Mark/Luke's basileia tou theou); the ouranon (heavens plural) is the reverential Jewish circumlocution for God.
3.11: baptizo (baptizo) — to immerse, dip, plunge — used for dyeing cloth, sinking ships, and ritual washings; in Jewish contexts (mikveh) it denoted purification immersion. John's baptism is eis metanoian (unto/for repentance) — the preposition eis indicates purpose or result. He contrasts it with Jesus's baptism en pneumati hagio kai pyri — in/with the Holy Spirit and fire — the preposition en is instrumental: the Spirit and fire are the medium, not the destination.
3.17: houtos estin ho huios mou ho agapetos — this is my beloved Son — the demonstrative houtos identifies the specific individual; agapetos (beloved, uniquely loved) appears in the LXX at Gen 22:2 for Isaac (the only son, yahid); the term carries the Aqedah resonance of the uniquely beloved son offered. en ho eudokesa — in whom I am well pleased — the aorist eudokesa (to be well pleased, to take delight in) combines the royal Ps 2:7 investiture with the Servant Isa 42:1 anointing; king and servant in one declaration.
3.1: En de tais hemerais ekeinais — in those days — a temporal bridge after the summary of Jesus's childhood in Nazareth (2:23). The phrase is vague about the intervening period (Jesus's childhood and youth are unnarrated). Iōannes ho baptistēs — John the baptizer — the active participle used as a title (baptistēs), denoting one who regularly baptizes. Keryssōn en te eremo tes Ioudaias — proclaiming in the wilderness of Judea — keryssōn (herald-proclaiming, present participle) indicates ongoing public proclamation; the wilderness of Judea is the Jordan River valley, which immediately fulfills the Isaiah 40:3 oracle cited in v.3.
3.3: Phone boontos en te eremo — a voice crying in the wilderness — the Isaiah 40:3 citation follows the LXX (which differs from the MT in punctuation: the MT places the wilderness with "prepare the way," but the LXX and Matthew place it with the crying: a voice crying in the wilderness). This punctuation locates John's wilderness ministry as the fulfillment-marker. Hetoimasate ten hodon kyriou — prepare the way of the Lord — kyrios in the LXX citation translates the Tetragrammaton (YHWH) in Isaiah; Matthew applies it to John's preparation for Jesus, implying Jesus is the YHWH whose way is being prepared.
3.4: trichōmatos kamēlou — camel's hair — the same description as Elijah in 2 Kgs 1:8 LXX (anēr dasys = a hairy man, zōnēn dermatinēn = leather belt). Matthew's readers would recognize the Elijah-correspondence (confirmed in 11:14; 17:12-13). Akridas kai meli agrion — locusts and wild honey — locusts were a permitted food under Levitical law (Lev 11:22); wild honey from rock or tree was a wilderness food (Deut 32:13; 1 Sam 14:25-26). Both are subsistence foods that locate John outside civilized society as a prophetic figure living on creation's provision.
3.5: Exeporeueto pros auton Ierosolyma kai pasa he Ioudaia kai pasa he perichoros tou Iordanou — Jerusalem was going out to him, and all Judea, and all the region around the Jordan — the imperfect exeporeueto (was going out, kept going out) indicates an ongoing, sustained movement of people over an extended period. The triple pasa (all) — all Jerusalem, all Judea, all the Jordan region — emphasizes the national scope and intensity of John's revival. The geographic compression (center of power + entire province + the border region) signals a movement of whole-population dimensions.
3.6: Ebaptizonto en to Iordane potamo hyp' autou — they were being baptized by him in the Jordan River — the imperfect passive ebaptizonto indicates ongoing, continuous action: crowds were being baptized one after another over an extended period. Exomologoumenoi tas hamartias auton — confessing their sins — the present participle exomologoumenoi (openly confessing, acknowledging publicly) indicates that the verbal confession accompanied the immersion, making baptism a public act of acknowledged sinfulness. Exomologeo in the middle voice (making a confession on behalf of oneself) carries the sense of voluntary, frank acknowledgment before God and witnesses.
3.7: Gennēmata echidnōn — offspring of vipers — the vocative combines genealogical metaphor (gennēmata = offspring, products of generation) with viper-imagery. Echidna (viper, adder) was considered particularly dangerous; the offspring inherit the parent's character and venom. Tis hypedexen hymin phygein apo tēs mellousēs orgēs — who warned you to flee from the coming wrath? — the aorist hypedexen (showed, pointed out beforehand) implies someone tipped them off. The question is rhetorically sarcastic: the Pharisees and Sadducees have come to secure the baptismal credential without genuine repentance.
3.8: Karpous axious tēs metanoias — fruit worthy of repentance — the plural karpous (fruits) suggests multiple visible evidences; a singular fruit would suggest one definitive act. Axios (worthy, proportional, of appropriate weight) measures the fruit against the repentance: genuine repentance produces correspondingly genuine behavioral evidence. The metaphor of fruit-bearing is used throughout Matthew for the test of authentic profession (7:16-20; 12:33; 21:43) — trees are known by their fruit, not by their claims.
3.9: Patrikas echomen ton Abraam — we have Abraham as our father — the claim of Abrahamic descent as covenantal security was a firmly held conviction in Second Temple Judaism: descent from Abraham placed one within the covenant and its protections. Ek tōn lithōn toutōn dunatos estin ho theos egeirai tekna tō Abraam — from these stones God is able to raise up children for Abraham — the wordplay between lithōn (stones) and tekna (children) may reflect an Aramaic pun: bnaya (children) vs. abnaya (stones). Egeirai (to raise up) is also the resurrection verb — God who raises from the dead can raise children from stones.
3.10: Ēdē de hē axinē pros tēn rizan tōn dendrōn keitai — already the axe is lying at the root of the trees — ēdē (already, now) makes this present urgency rather than future threat: the axe is not being raised; it is already positioned. The present participle keitai (lies, is placed) confirms its current location. Pan dendron mē poioun karpon kalon ekkoptetai — every tree not producing good fruit is cut down — the present ekkoptetai functions either as a gnomic present (describing the invariable rule) or as an anticipatory present of imminent action: the cutting down is as certain as if it is already happening.
3.12: Ptyon en tē cheiri autou — the winnowing fork is in his hand — ptyon is the wooden fork used to toss threshed grain into the wind so the lighter chaff separates from the heavier wheat. The present tense (estin implied) makes this present reality, not future promise. Diakathariein tēn alōna autou — to thoroughly clean his threshing floor — diakathariō (compound verb with dia = through + kathariō = cleanse) indicates complete, thorough cleaning. Achyron katakausei pyri asbesto — the chaff he will burn with unquenchable fire — asbestos (inextinguishable, quenchless) appears in the LXX for inescapable divine judgment (Isa 34:10; 66:24; Jer 7:20).
3.13: Tote paraginetai ho Iēsous apo tēs Galilaias epi ton Iordanēn pros ton Iōannēn — then Jesus arrives from Galilee to the Jordan to John — the historical present paraginetai (arrives, comes alongside) brings the narrative into vivid immediacy. This is Jesus's first appearance in Matthew since his childhood in Nazareth (2:23); Matthew gives no account of the intervening years. The tote (then) connects John's ministry as the preparation and Jesus's arrival as the fulfillment of that preparation — what the Baptist announced, the one he announced now arrives.
3.14: Diekolyen auton — he was trying to prevent/deter him — the imperfect diekolyen suggests persistent, repeated attempts to hinder rather than a single objection. Egō chreian echō hypo sou baptisthēnai — I have need to be baptized by you — the emphatic pronoun egō (I) contrasts with the implied "you"; the construction hypo sou (by you) identifies Jesus as the one who should be baptizing John, not the reverse. The need (chreian) John acknowledges is his own spiritual need for the Spirit-baptism that only Jesus can give (v.11).
3.15: Prepon hēmin estin plērōsai pasan dikaiosynēn — it is fitting for us to fulfill all righteousness — prepon (fitting, proper, becoming) is the language of theological and moral appropriateness. Pasan dikaiosynēn — all righteousness — the scope is comprehensive: this one act fulfills the entirety of what God's salvific plan requires. Jesus's baptism is not a confession of sin (he has none) but a covenant-identification with God's people, a commissioning act, and the inauguration of the mission. The plural hēmin (for us) includes both Jesus and John in the propriety of the act.
3.16: Anebē apo tou hydatos — he came up from the water — apo (from away from) with the water indicates prior immersion: he came up out of it. Ēneōchthēsan autō hoi ouranoi — the heavens were opened to him — anoigō in the aorist passive (ēneōchthēsan) echoes Ezek 1:1 LXX (ēnoichthēsan hoi ouranoi = the heavens were opened) at the inauguration of Ezekiel's prophetic ministry. Peristeron — a dove — in Jewish tradition (b. Chagigah 15a) the Spirit at creation hovered like a dove over the waters (Gen 1:2); the Spirit's dove-form at Jesus's baptism signals a new-creation inauguration.

4.1: anechthe (anago) — led up — the passive (was led up) makes the Spirit the agent of the wilderness-leading, just as YHWH led Israel in the wilderness (Deut 8:2, the immediate context of Jesus's first temptation response). peirasthēnai — to be tested/tempted — peirazo carries both meanings: external testing (to prove quality) and internal temptation (to induce sin). The wilderness testing recapitulates Israel's 40 years (Jesus's 40 days) with the key difference that Jesus passes where Israel failed.
4.17: apo tote erxato — from that time he began — apo tote is a Matthean structural marker used at key transitions (also 16:21 for the passion-announcements). kerusso — to herald, proclaim — the verb of public announcement rather than private teaching; Jesus speaks as one with royal authority proclaiming the King's news. The message is identical to John's (3:2): metanoeite, engiken gar he basileia ton ouranon — but Jesus proclaims it as the one in whom the kingdom is present, not merely approaching.
4.2: Nēsteusas hēmeras tessarakonta kai nyktas tessarakonta — having fasted forty days and forty nights — the dual formula (days and nights) precisely parallels Moses's Sinai fast (Exod 34:28; Deut 9:9, 18: both forty days and forty nights) and Elijah's wilderness fast (1 Kgs 19:8: forty days and forty nights to Horeb). Jesus recapitulates both figures. Husteron epeinasen — afterward he was hungry — the aorist epeinasen (he became hungry) is the physiological trigger: genuine human hunger is the entry-point for the tempter. The forty-day fast is completed; the vulnerability is real.
4.3: Ei huios ei tou theou — if you are the Son of God — ei with present indicative is a first-class condition assuming the reality: "since you are the Son of God..." The tempter does not deny the sonship but attempts to redirect it toward self-service. Eipe hina hoi lithoi houtoi artoi genōntai — tell these stones to become bread — the aorist subjunctive genōntai (become) requests a creative word, a use of divine-son power for immediate physical self-provision. The temptation is to deploy the power of sonship for private benefit rather than for the mission to which the Father has called.
4.4: Gegraptai — it is written, it stands written — the perfect passive of graphō (to write): the scripture has been written and its authority remains in effect. Jesus's triple use of gegraptai (vv.4, 7, 10) positions his responses as authoritative appeals to standing divine word, not ad hoc arguments. The Deuteronomy 8:3 citation: ouk ep' artō monō zēsetai ho anthrōpos — man shall not live by bread alone — directly addresses Israel's wilderness murmuring for food; Jesus, unlike Israel, trusts the Father's provision and does not demand bread from stone.
4.5: Tēn hagian polin — the holy city — Matthew's distinctive designation for Jerusalem (also 27:53), reflecting the city's sanctified cultic status. Pterygion tou hierou — the pinnacle/parapet of the temple — pterygion (diminutive of pteryx, wing) = wing, parapet, or projecting edge; used for the highest point of the temple complex. According to Josephus (Antiquities 15.11.5), the southeast corner of the temple platform overlooks a sheer drop of several hundred feet into the Kidron Valley — a height sufficient to make the test of angelic protection dramatically visible.
4.6: The devil cites Psalm 91:11-12 (LXX 90): tois angelois autou enteleitai peri sou — he will command his angels concerning you — but omits the clause "to guard you in all your ways" that follows in the Psalm. The omission strips the promise from its covenantal context (the path of covenant obedience) to make it a basis for presumption (demanding miraculous rescue to prove God's care). Jesus's counter (Deut 6:16) identifies this as "putting the Lord to the test" — exactly the misuse of divine promise that the Psalm's context guards against.
4.7: Gegraptai palin — it is written again — Jesus meets the devil's scripture-citation with another scripture-citation, demonstrating that the devil's use of Ps 91 requires correction by Deut 6:16, not by denying the Psalm. Ouk ekpeiraseis kyrion ton theon sou — you shall not put the Lord your God to the test — Deuteronomy 6:16 explicitly cites the Massah incident (Exod 17:1-7) where Israel demanded water as a test of God's presence. The verb ekpeiraseis (future indicative, from ekpeirazō) means to put to an extreme test, to demand proof by creating a crisis. Jesus refuses to engineer a rescue to prove the Father's faithfulness.
4.8: Oros hypsēlon lian — a very high mountain — lian (very, exceedingly) intensifies the height beyond any natural geography. The mountain may be visionary or apocalyptic rather than a specific physical peak — Matthew does not identify it. Pasas tas basileias tou kosmou kai tēn doxan autōn — all the kingdoms of the world and their glory — pasas (all, every) and doxan (glory, splendor, magnificence) make the offer genuinely comprehensive. Whether the vision was literal or representative, Matthew presents Jesus's response as treating the offer as real — which gives his refusal its full moral weight.
4.9: Tauta soi panta dōsō ean pesōn proskunēsēs moi — all these I will give to you if having fallen you worship me — the conditional ean with aorist subjunctive presents a single, decisive act: one moment of submission. Pesōn (having fallen) describes the prostration of worship; combined with proskunēsēs (do obeisance, worship) the action is unmistakable. The verb proskyneō is used throughout Matthew for worship of Jesus (2:11; 8:2; 14:33; 28:9, 17) and for the proper worship of God alone (4:10) — the devil demands the specific act reserved for God.
4.10: Hypage, Satana — go away, Satan — the imperative hypage (depart, go away) is authoritative dismissal; the naming Satana (transliteration of Hebrew Satan, the Adversary) identifies the tempter definitively for the first time in the exchange. The name will recur at Peter's rebuke (16:23: "Hypage opiso mou, Satana" — the verbatim repetition there is deliberate). The Deuteronomy 6:13 citation: Kyrion ton theon sou proskunēseis kai autō monō latreueis — you shall worship the Lord your God and him alone you shall serve — the emphatic monō (alone, only) is not in the MT but appears in Matthew's text.
4.11: Kai idou angeloi prosēlthon kai diēkonoun autō — and behold, angels came to him and were serving/ministering to him — idou (behold!) signals a significant moment. Prosēlthon (aorist: came to, approached) is followed by the imperfect diēkonoun (were serving, ministering — continuous action): the angels provide sustained care, not a single act. Diakonein (to serve, minister) is the same word for table-service (Matt 8:15; 25:44; 27:55) — the angels provide physical care (food and rest?) after the extended fast. The episode recalls Elijah's angelic refreshment after his own wilderness fast (1 Kgs 19:5-8).
4.12: Akousas de hoti Iōannēs paredothē — but having heard that John was handed over — paradidōmi (to hand over, deliver up) is Matthew's term for betrayal and arrest (17:22; 20:18; 26:2, 15, 21, 23, 24, 25, 45, 46, 48); its use for John's arrest prefigures the same fate for Jesus. Anechōrēsen eis tēn Galilaian — he withdrew into Galilee — anachoreo (to withdraw, retire strategically) is used throughout Matthew 2 for divinely-guided repositioning; its reappearance here frames Jesus's Galilean move as theologically intentional rather than a retreat from danger.
4.13: Katōkēsen eis Kapharnaoum tēn paraliian — he took up residence in Capernaum by the sea — katōkēsen (aorist of katoikeo, to settle, take up permanent residence — stronger than temporary lodging) indicates Capernaum became Jesus's home base for the Galilean ministry. The descriptor paraliia (coastal, by the sea) locates Capernaum on the shore of the Sea of Galilee, important for the following Isaianic citation's mention of "the Way of the Sea." En horiois Zaboulōn kai Nephaleim — in the regions of Zebulun and Naphtali — the tribal territories frame the prophetic fulfillment.
4.14: Hina plērōthē to rhēthen dia Ēsaiou tou prophētou — so that what was spoken through Isaiah the prophet might be fulfilled — the fifth hina plērōthē fulfillment formula in Matthew 1-4 (1:22; 2:15, 23; 4:14), each marking a new stage in the Christological fulfillment sequence. Legontos (present participle = who says) treats Isaiah's oracle as a living word still speaking — the prophet's voice is not past tense but present, awaiting its moment of actualization. The formula signals to Matthew's readers that this geographic relocation is not accidental but prophetically determined.
4.15: The Isaiah 9:1 citation: Gē Zaboulōn kai gē Nephaleim — land of Zebulun and land of Naphtali — the territories of the northern tribes, first to fall to Assyria in 732 BCE (2 Kgs 15:29). Hodos thalassēs, peran tou Iordanou — the Way of the Sea, beyond the Jordan — hodos thalassēs is the ancient trade and military road running along the Mediterranean coast and through the Jordan valley (the Via Maris of Latin tradition). Galilaia tōn ethnōn — Galilee of the Gentiles — the mixed-population region where Gentiles lived alongside Jews, appropriate for a ministry that will eventually extend to all nations (28:19).
4.16: Ho laos ho kathēmenos en skotia phōs eiden mega — the people sitting in darkness saw a great light — the present participle kathēmenos (sitting, dwelling) describes a state of settled habitation in darkness, not merely passing through it. Phōs eiden mega — they saw a great light — the aorist eiden is punctiliar: a decisive, singular moment of seeing. Tois en chōra kai skia thanatou — to those in the region and shadow of death — skia thanatou (shadow of death) is a compound expression found in Ps 22:4; 23:4 (LXX); Job 3:5 for the deepest darkness. Isaiah's oracle of reversal — darkness to light — is fulfilled in Jesus's Galilean ministry.
4.18: Amfiblēstron — a casting net — the specific net-type (amphiblestron, a net thrown so it spreads in a circle and closes like a bag around the fish) distinguishes from the dragnet (sagēnē, 13:47) used from a boat. The present participle ballontas (throwing, casting) indicates the brothers were actively at work fishing when Jesus encountered them — their occupation was fully engaged, making the call's interruption and their response more striking. Hēsan gar halieis — for they were fishermen — the explanatory imperfect identifies their occupation and qualifications for the metaphor Jesus will use.
4.19: Deute opisō mou — come after me — the imperative deute (come!, plural) combined with opisō mou (behind me, after me) is the standard teacher-disciple relationship formula: to come after a rabbi was to follow in his path, learn his way, and eventually replicate his teaching. Poiēsō hymas halieis anthrōpōn — I will make you fishers of people — the word-play on their occupation: the same halieuo (to fish) now describes their mission. The metaphor is active: fishing requires skill, effort, and going to where the fish are.
4.20: Hoi de eutheos aphentes ta diktya ēkolouthēsan autō — they immediately left the nets and followed him — eutheos (immediately, at once) is a Matthean marker of decisive, unhesitating response. Aphentes (having left/abandoned) carries the weight of real cost: the nets (their livelihood and family business equipment) are left behind. Ēkolouthēsan autō — they followed him — the aorist of akoloutheo (to follow, accompany in discipleship) is used throughout Matthew for committed following (8:22-23; 9:9; 10:38; 16:24; 19:21, 27, 28).
4.21: Katartizontas ta diktya auton — mending/repairing their nets — the present participle katartizontas (from katartizō, to restore, mend, put in order) indicates the ongoing work of net repair between fishing expeditions — a normal part of the fisher's routine. The verb also means "to equip" or "make complete" (used in Eph 4:12 for equipping the saints; 1 Cor 1:10 for being united in the same mind). James and John are at work in the boat with their father Zebedee (meta Zebedaiou tou patros auton) — the family business context frames the cost of the call.
4.22: Aphentes to ploion kai ton patera auton — leaving the boat and their father — the objects left by James and John include both property (the boat, the family business) and family relationships (their father). Matthew's parallel structure (Peter and Andrew / James and John) is deliberate: the two pairs are called in identical circumstances and respond identically (eutheos/aphentes/ēkolouthēsan = immediately/having left/followed). The escalating cost (nets → nets + boat + father) is highlighted by the parallel construction.
4.23: Periēgen en holē tē Galilaia — he was going about in all of Galilee — the imperfect periēgen (was going about, was traveling through) indicates an extended, ongoing campaign throughout the region. Three simultaneous activities described by present participles: didaskon (teaching, in the synagogues), kēryssōn (proclaiming the gospel of the kingdom), and therapeuōn (healing every disease and every infirmity). Pasan noson kai pasan malakian — every disease and every weakness/infirmity — the double pasan (every) emphasizes comprehensive scope; malakia (weakness, infirmity) covers conditions that are not strictly diseases.
4.24: Apēlthen hē akoē autou eis holēn tēn Syrian — the report/news of him went out into all of Syria — akoē (report, news, hearing) is the word for what is heard about someone; the phrase marks the first explicit geographic extension of Jesus's fame beyond Jewish territory to the Roman province of Syria (north and east of Galilee). Daimonizoumenous kai selēniazomenous kai paralytikous — those possessed by demons and those who were moonstruck (epileptics?) and those who were paralyzed — the three categories cover demonic affliction, neurological/psychiatric conditions, and physical paralysis.
4.25: Ēkolouthēsan autō ochloi polloi — great crowds followed him — the verb akoloutheo (to follow) appears again, now applied to crowds rather than disciples; in Matthew the word carries both levels of meaning (physical accompanying and committed discipleship). The five geographic sources — apo tēs Galilaias kai Dekapoleōs kai Ierosolymōn kai Ioudaias kai peran tou Iordanou — cover virtually all the Jewish population regions: the Galilean heartland, the Gentile Decapolis, the capital and its province, and Transjordan (Perea). The gathering from all directions sets the stage for the Sermon on the Mount (5:1-2).

5.3: makarioi (makarios) — blessed, happy, fortunate — in Hellenistic usage the word described the blissful state of the gods or those who lived like gods; in Jewish usage (Hebrew ashre) it described the one who is in a right relationship with YHWH and experiences his favor. The plural (blesseds) is a Hebraism. hoi ptochoi to pneumati — the poor in spirit — ptochos is the beggar-poor (from ptosso, to crouch) rather than the merely hard-pressed penes; in spirit (dative) specifies the domain: those who know their total spiritual bankruptcy before God.
5.17: katalyo — to abolish, dissolve, tear down — used for dismantling buildings and nullifying covenants; Jesus uses the strongest possible abolition-verb to deny that his intention is abolition. pleroō — to fill full, complete, fulfill — the same verb as the formula citations (hina plerothe). Jesus comes not to add to the law but to bring it to its intended fullness and goal; his life, death, and resurrection are what the Torah was pointing toward. iota hen e mia keraia — one iota or one tittle — the smallest letter of the Greek alphabet (iota = yod in Hebrew) and a serif-mark on a letter; not the smallest detail will pass unfulfilled.
5.21: ercousin is the formula ekouste hoti errethe — you have heard that it was said — the passive errethe (it was said) leaves the speaker unnamed, placing the authority on the received tradition. ego de lego hymin — but I say to you — the ego is emphatic by position; the antithesis is not Moses vs. Jesus but tradition-understood vs. authoritatively-interpreted. Jesus speaks with exousia (authority from the one who has the right to speak), not like the scribes who derived authority by citation.
5.48: esesthe oun hymeis teleioi — you therefore shall be perfect/complete/mature — teleios (from telos, end/goal) means having reached one's full end or purpose; it is not the impossible Platonic ideal of moral perfection but the wholeness/undividedness of character that mirrors God's wholeness. The parallel in Luke 6:36 reads oiktirmon (merciful), suggesting teleios here means wholeness-in-love, the same indiscriminate love that God shows to all. hos ho pater hymon ho ouranios teleios estin — as your heavenly Father is perfect — the standard is not an abstract ideal but the character of the Father.
5.1: anabas eis to oros — having gone up into the mountain — anabas is an aorist participle of anabainō, establishing ascent as the frame for what follows. Mountains in Matthew carry covenantal freight (cf. 4:8, 17:1, 28:16); the location is not incidental. Ekathisen (he sat down) is the posture of the authoritative Jewish teacher: a rabbi sat to teach formally. The crowds draw near (v.1), but the address is directed to the disciples (proselthon autō hoi mathetai autou), marking this as discipleship instruction rather than a general ethical manifesto.
5.2: anoixas to stoma autou — having opened his mouth — a Semitic idiom (cf. Job 33:2; Ps 78:2) signaling formal, deliberate, weighty speech, not casual conversation. Edidasken is the imperfect of didaskō — he was teaching them — a continuous action extending through the discourse. The imperfect is inceptive: "he began to teach and continued doing so."
5.4: penthountes — those who mourn (pentheō, present active participle) — an ongoing state of grief, not a single act of weeping. The verb is used for mourning the dead (cf. Gen 23:2 LXX) and for penitential grief over sin; in context both senses may be live. Paraklēthēsontai — they will be comforted — future passive divine; the agent is God (the passive avoids the divine name). The same root as the Paraclete (paraklētos) in John 14–16.
5.5: praeis — the meek (praus) — not passive weakness but the quality of controlled, disciplined strength that does not assert itself for personal advantage. The word is used of the trained warhorse and of Moses (Num 12:3 LXX: praus sphodra). Klēronomēsousin tēn gēn — will inherit the earth — a direct echo of Ps 37:11 LXX (hoi de praeis klēronomēsousin gēn); the Beatitude functions as a fulfillment citation without attribution, assuming the audience knows the Psalm.
5.6: peinontes kai dipsontes tēn dikaiosynēn — hungering and thirsting for righteousness — both present active participles, denoting ongoing, urgent appetite. Dikaiosynē (G1343) spans legal standing before God and moral conformity to his will; both senses are alive in Matthew's usage. Chortasthēsontai — they will be filled/satisfied — chortazō is agricultural: the filling of animals in a stall, then by extension anyone satisfied to the full. The hunger is intense; the filling will be complete.
5.7: eleēmones — the merciful (eleos-group, corresponding to Hebrew hesed) — those who show active covenant loyalty, not merely feelings of sympathy. Eleēthēsontai — will be shown mercy — future passive divine: God will show them mercy. The Beatitude is not a commercial transaction but a correlation: those who inhabit the world as mercy-givers live in the register where mercy is received.
5.8: katharoi tē kardia — pure in heart — katharos in cultic usage meant ritually clean; here it is applied to the kardia (heart), the biblical center of will, intellect, and moral intention. The antithesis is not ritual impurity but divided loyalty (cf. Jas 4:8, dipsychoi, double-minded). Ton theon opsontai — they will see God — horaō future; the eschatological vision of God (cf. Ps 11:7; Rev 22:4). Purity of the inner faculty is the condition for the ultimate perception.
5.9: eirēnopoioi — peacemakers — a compound unique to Matthew in the NT: eirēnē (peace, shalom) + poieō (make, do); those who actively construct peace rather than merely possess it. Huioi theou klēthēsontai — they will be called sons of God — future passive: God will name them his children. In biblical usage, to be "called" by God is to be constituted as what the name declares, not merely titled.
5.10: dedioxmenoi — those who have been persecuted (diōkō, perfect passive participle) — the perfect marks completed action with continuing effect: they already bear the mark of persecution received. The reason: heneken dikaiosynēs — because of righteousness — the same dikaiosynē as v.6, the pursuit of God's right order that attracts opposition. The reward is identical to v.3: autōn estin hē basileia tōn ouranōn — present tense brackets the eight Beatitudes (vv.3, 10), framing them as a single structure.
5.11: oneidisōsin hymas kai diōxōsin — insult you and persecute you — oneidizō is active shaming, verbal attack designed to strip social honor. The shift from third person (vv.3-10) to second person plural (hymas) personalizes the eighth Beatitude. Pseudomenoi — falsely — the adjectival participle specifies slander, not merely erroneous accusation. Heneken emou — because of me — the ground of persecution shifts: not righteousness in the abstract but allegiance to Jesus specifically.
5.12: chairete kai agalliasthe — rejoice and be exceedingly glad — chairō is standard joy; agalliaō intensifies it: exultation, jubilant leaping (used for Messianic celebration in Ps 14:7 LXX; Luke 1:44). Misthos — reward (misthos) — the commercial word for wages owed, not a gift; the framework is one of rightful payment. Houtōs gar edioxan tous prophētas — for so they persecuted the prophets — edioxan aorist: historical completed act; the suffering disciples are identified with the prophetic tradition.
5.13: hymas este to halas tēs gēs — you are the salt of the earth — hymas is emphatic by position: you (and no one else) are. Halas (salt) in the ancient world preserved, flavored, and was associated with covenant fidelity (Lev 2:13, "covenant of salt"). Moranthe — should lose its saltiness — aorist passive subjunctive of morainō: to become flat, tasteless, foolish. The same verb means "to make foolish" (cognate with mōros, fool — cf. v.22); the salt that loses its saltiness becomes mōros in both senses. En tini alisthēsetai — by what will it be salted again? — a rhetorical impossibility.
5.14: to phōs tou kosmou — the light of the world — contrasting scale with v.13: salt is for the earth (gē); light is for the world (kosmos). Polis epanō orous keimenē — a city set on a hill — present passive participle, already in place; the city does not choose visibility, it cannot avoid it. The metaphor reinforces the communal dimension: the disciples together constitute the visible settlement.
5.15: kaiousi lychnon — they light a lamp — present active, a generic statement of normal practice. Modion — a bushel/measuring vessel, typically a wooden container of about 8.75 liters; placing a lamp under it extinguishes it. Lychnian — the lampstand — the proper location for a lamp; lampei pasin tois en tē oikia — it gives light to all who are in the house. The domestic scale prepares for v.16's address to a wider audience.
5.16: lampsatō to phōs hymōn — let your light shine (lampō, aorist imperative) — the only imperative in the salt-and-light passage; the preceding metaphors describe what the disciples are, this commands what they must not suppress. Emprosthen tōn anthrōpōn — before people — visibility is the point. The purpose clause (hopōs): that they may see your kala erga (good works) and glorify — not you, but ton patera hymōn ton en tois ouranois — your Father in heaven. Visibility is not self-promotion; it is the medium through which the Father is glorified.
5.18: iōta hen ē mia keraia — one iota or one stroke — iōta is the Greek transliteration of Hebrew yod (י), the smallest letter; keraia (horn, serif) is the smallest distinguishing mark between Hebrew letters (e.g., ד and ר). Together they represent the minimum possible unit of textual precision. Ou mē parelthē — will by no means pass away — double negative for emphatic negation, the strongest denial available in Greek. Heōs an panta genētai — until all things come to pass — the same verb (ginomai) as plērōsai in v.17; fulfillment of the Law coincides with fulfillment of all things.
5.19: hos ean lysē mian tōn entolōn toutōn tōn elachistōn — whoever breaks one of the least of these commandments — lyō is the same root as katalyō in v.17; to break a commandment is the micro-scale equivalent of abolishing the Law. The ranking system (elachistos/least, megas/great) is recognized within the kingdom: some commandments are lesser, but none are dismissible. Klēthēsetai elachistos — will be called least — passive future divine: their standing in the kingdom reflects their practice, not merely their teaching.
5.20: ean mē perisseusē hē dikaiosynē hymōn pleon tōn grammateōn kai Pharisaiōn — unless your righteousness exceeds that of the scribes and Pharisees — perisseusē (aorist subjunctive of perisseuō, to abound, exceed) with pleon (more than) sets an explicit comparative bar. The scribes and Pharisees were the recognized specialists in Torah observance; "exceeding" them is not a call to more meticulous rule-keeping but to a different kind of dikaiosynē — one that addresses the heart (as the antitheses of vv.21–48 will demonstrate). Ou mē eiselthēte — you will certainly not enter — the same double-negative construction as v.18.
5.22: orgizomenos tō adelphō autou — being angry at his brother or sister — present participle, ongoing anger as opposed to a momentary flash. The verse escalates through three levels of offense and corresponding courts: (1) ongoing anger — liable to local judgment (krisei); (2) rhaka — probably from Aramaic rēqā (empty one, blockhead), a term of contemptuous dismissal — liable to the Sanhedrin (synedriō); (3) mōre (fool, from mōros) — liable to the Gehenna of fire (geennan tou pyros). Geenna refers to the Valley of Hinnom outside Jerusalem, used as a metaphor for final judgment.
5.23: ean oun prospherēs to dōron sou — if then you are offering your gift — prospherō present active subjunctive: in the act of offering, the memory arrives. The altar context places this in the Temple cult. Kakei mnesthēs — and there you remember — mnaomai aorist passive subjunctive: a sudden recollection. The scenario presupposes a grievance held against the offerer by the brother or sister (echei ti kata sou — has something against you), not merely that the offerer feels guilty.
5.24: aphes ekei to dōron sou — leave your gift there — aphiēmi aorist imperative: a decisive action. The gift is left at the altar, not abandoned. Hypage prōton — go first — the adverb prōton establishes priority: reconciliation precedes cultic offering. Diallagēthi tō adelphō sou — be reconciled to your brother or sister — diallassō aorist passive imperative: reconciliation is received as much as performed; it requires both parties. The sequence is: leave → go → be reconciled → return → offer.
5.25: euthopon — quickly, while on the way (euthys + en tē hodō met autou) — the urgency is temporal: settle before reaching the court. Antidikos — adversary/plaintiff (anti + dikē, lawsuit) — the technical term for the opposing party in litigation. The sequence of officials (kritēs/judge → hyperetēs/officer → phylakē/prison) traces the legal process to its worst outcome, extending the anger-reconciliation theme of vv.22–24.
5.26: ou mē exelthēs — you will certainly not come out — double negative for emphatic futurity. Heōs an apodōs ton eschaton kodrantēn — until you have paid the last kodrantes — the kodrantes was the smallest Roman coin, a quadrans worth a quarter of an as; eschaton (last) reinforces completeness: not one smallest payment will be waived. The legal metaphor underscores the completeness of the moral debt if reconciliation is not pursued.
5.27: ou moicheuseis — you shall not commit adultery — the direct citation of the seventh commandment (Exod 20:14 LXX). The formula ēkousate hoti errethē (you have heard that it was said) follows the pattern of v.21; the passive errethē leaves the speaker unnamed, but the contrast introduced by egō de legō hymin (but I tell you) in v.28 sets Jesus's authority alongside — not against — the Torah.
5.28: ho blepōn gynaika pros to epithymēsai autēn — the one who looks at a woman in order to desire her — pros to epithymēsai is an articular infinitive of purpose: the look is characterized by its intent. Epithymeō (G1937) is the LXX word for "covet" in the tenth commandment (Exod 20:17); Jesus draws the seventh commandment into the orbit of the tenth. Ēdē emoicheusen autēn en tē kardia autou — has already committed adultery with her in his heart — ēdē (already) marks the act as completed at the moment of intentional looking, not pending physical action.
5.29: ei de ho ophthalmos sou ho dexios skandalizei se — if your right eye causes you to stumble (skandalizō, to put a snare, cause to fall) — the right eye (dexios, valued, dominant) is chosen precisely because it is the more precious one; the hyperbole shocks. Exele auton kai bale apo sou — tear it out and throw it from you — aorist imperatives: decisive, immediate action. Sympherei gar soi — for it is better for you (literally, it profits you) — sympherō is used in commercial calculation; losing a body part is preferable to the whole body (holon to soma) being thrown into Gehenna.
5.30: hē dexia sou cheir — your right hand — the dominant, more valued hand, parallel to the right eye of v.29. The repeated hyperbole of self-amputation is not a literal prescription but a graphic measure of moral seriousness. Apoles hen tōn melōn sou — that one of your members be lost — apollymi aorist subjunctive: the destruction of a part to preserve the whole. Geenna recurs as in v.29; the eschatological framework governs both illustrations.
5.31: hos an apolyē tēn gynaika autou — whoever divorces his wife — the citation draws on Deut 24:1 (the practice of apostasion = certificate of divorce/release). The Pharisaic debate over the grounds (ervat dabar, "some indecency": interpreted broadly by Hillel and narrowly by Shammai) is the unstated background. Jesus addresses the mechanism, not merely the grounds.
5.32: parektos logou porneias — except for the matter of sexual immorality — porneia (G4202) covers a range of sexual violations; the exception clause is unique to Matthew (cf. Mark 10:11-12, Luke 16:18, which have no exception). Poiei autēn moicheuthenai — makes her commit adultery — moicheuō passive: she is the victim of the situation created by the divorce. Apolelymenen is a perfect passive participle marking her ongoing status as the divorced woman.
5.33: ouk epiorkēseis, apodōseis de tō kyriō tous horkous sou — you shall not swear falsely, and you shall fulfil to the Lord your oaths — a conflation of Lev 19:12 (ou pseudorkēseis) and Num 30:2 (fulfilling vows to the LORD). In Second Temple Judaism elaborate systems of oath-taking had developed to classify binding versus non-binding oaths by formula (cf. Matt 23:16-22). Jesus addresses the whole practice rather than the classification system.
5.34: mē omosai holōs — do not swear at all (holōs, altogether, at all) — the adverb makes the prohibition comprehensive. Mēte en tō ouranō — neither by heaven — the escalating list of oath-objects (heaven, earth, Jerusalem, one's own head) all fail for the same reason: none are fully in human control. Thronos gar estin tou theou — for it is the throne of God — heaven is already defined by its relationship to God and cannot be used as a neutral object of oath.
5.35: mēte en tē gē, hoti hypopodion estin tōn podōn autou — nor by the earth, for it is his footstool — drawing on Isa 66:1 (hē gē hypopodion tōn podōn mou). Mēte eis Hierosolyma — nor by Jerusalem — hoti polis estin tou megalou basileōs — for it is the city of the Great King — from Ps 48:2 (LXX 47:3). Both OT references are cited without attribution, assuming audience recognition. The logic: any oath-object that belongs to God cannot be invoked independently of God.
5.36: mēte en tē kephalē sou omosēs — nor swear by your head — the most personal oath-object, but also not within human control. Hoti ou dynastai mian tricha leukēn poiēsai ē melainan — because you cannot make one hair white or black — dynastai present: a permanent incapacity. The inability to control one's own hair color is the argument: if you cannot govern even that, your head is not an adequate basis for an oath.
5.37: estō de ho logos hymōn nai nai, ou ou — let your word be "yes yes, no no" — estō third-person imperative of eimi: let it be. The reduplication is emphatic affirmation/negation rather than a prescribed formula. To de perisson toutōn ek tou ponērou estin — anything beyond these comes from the evil one (or: from evil) — ek tou ponērou: the article allows both readings; Matthew's usage elsewhere (6:13, apo tou ponērou) tends toward the personal referent (the devil).
5.38: ophthalmon anti ophthalmou kai odonta anti odontos — eye for eye and tooth for tooth — the lex talionis, cited from Exod 21:24 / Lev 24:20 / Deut 19:21. In its original legislative context this was a principle of proportionate justice limiting disproportionate revenge; Jesus addresses not the legal system but the personal disposition of retaliation that the formula had come to sanction in popular ethics.
5.39: mē antistēnai tō ponērō — do not resist the evil one/person (anthistēmi aorist infinitive) — tō ponērō is ambiguous: it can be the evil person (the one who wrongs you) or the evil one (the devil). The examples that follow suggest a human adversary. Rhapizō — slap/strike — the right cheek specified (tēn dexian siagona) suggests a backhanded blow, which in Roman-era culture was a gesture of particular contempt rather than a serious assault.
5.40: tō thelonti soi krithēnai kai ton chitōna sou labein — to the one who wants to sue you and take your tunic — krinō in the legal sense. Chitōn is the inner tunic; himation is the outer cloak. The cloak was more valuable (it doubled as a blanket) and legally protected from permanent seizure as a pledge (Exod 22:26-27). Jesus inverts the legal protection: surrender the more-protected garment voluntarily, beyond what the lawsuit demands.
5.41: angareusei se milion hen — compels you to go one mile — angareuo is a loanword from Persian through Aramaic: the requisition of civilian labor or transport by military or imperial authority (cf. 27:32, Simon of Cyrene compelled to carry the cross). Milion — a Roman mile (approximately 1.48 km). The right of military requisition was a source of everyday resentment under Roman occupation; Jesus reframes compelled service as a site of voluntary extension.
5.42: tō aitounti se dos — give to the one who asks you — dos aorist imperative: decisive action. Ton thelonta apo sou daneisasthai mē apostraphēs — do not turn away from the one who wants to borrow from you — apostraphēs aorist passive subjunctive of apostrephō: to turn away, refuse. The contrast with vv.38-41 is noteworthy: those verses address forced or hostile demands; this verse addresses voluntary requests, extending the principle to generosity.
5.43: agapēseis ton plēsion sou — you shall love your neighbor (agapaō) — from Lev 19:18. Ton plēsion — the neighbor — the LXX term for the fellow-Israelite (or resident alien) covered by covenant obligation. Kai misēseis ton echthron sou — and hate your enemy — this second clause is not found in the Torah; it represents a popular inference that restricted the scope of "neighbor" to exclude enemies. Jesus addresses the interpretation, not a scriptural citation.
5.44: agapate tous echthrous hymōn — love your enemies (agapaō present imperative, ongoing action) — the extension of agapē to enemies is the pivot of the antithesis. Proseuchesthe hyper tōn diōkontōn hymas — pray for those who persecute you — proseuchomai present imperative: ongoing intercession, not a one-time prayer. The combination of love and prayer as active dispositions toward enemies marks the qualitative difference from the reciprocal-love of v.46.
5.45: hopōs genesthe huioi tou patros hymōn — so that you may become sons of your Father — genesthe aorist subjunctive: the purpose clause points to a transformation, a becoming. Ton hēlion autou anatellei epi ponērous kai agathous — he causes his sun to rise on evil and good — anatellō causative: God is the agent of the sun's rising. Brechei epi dikaious kai adikous — sends rain on righteous and unrighteous. The non-discriminating character of God's common grace becomes the model for the disciples' non-discriminating love.
5.46: ean agapate tous agapōntas hymas — if you love those who love you — conditional with agapaō present participle: those who are actively loving you back. Tina misthon echete — what reward (misthos, wages owed) do you have? Ouchi kai hoi telōnai to auto poiousin — do not even the tax collectors do the same? — telōnai were toll-collectors in the service of Herod or Rome, despised as collaborators and extortionists; they represent the moral low bar of the surrounding culture.
5.47: ean aspassēsthe tous adelphous hymōn monon — if you greet only your own people — aspasēsthe aorist subjunctive of aspazomai; greeting in ancient Mediterranean culture was a formal acknowledgment of social relationship and mutual obligation, not a casual pleasantry. Restricting greeting to one's own group was a form of social boundary maintenance. Ti perisson poieite — what are you doing more than others (perisson = extra, exceptional)? Ouchi kai hoi ethnikoi to auto poiousin — do not even the Gentiles do the same? — Gentiles, like tax collectors in v.46, represent the lowest comparable moral baseline.

6.9: Pater hemon ho en tois ouranois — Our Father who is in the heavens — the address combines intimacy (Pater = Father, probably translating Aramaic Abba) with transcendence (en tois ouranois = in the heavens, indicating divine majesty). hagiastheto to onoma sou — hallowed be your name — the passive imperative (hagiastheto) is a divine passive: may God himself act to hallow his name (sanctify it before the world). This is not a prayer that we will reverence the name but that God will act eschatologically to vindicate his own name (Ezek 36:23).
6.12: aphes hemin ta opheilemata hemon — forgive us our debts — opheilema (debt) is the commercial-legal term for what is owed; sin as moral debt to God is a Semitic conceptual framework (Aramaic hoba = debt = sin). The aorist aphes (forgive, once) vs. the perfect in aphekamen (we have forgiven, already done) — the community's forgiveness of others is presented as the completed precondition, not a parallel request.
6.24: mammonas (mammonas) — Mammon — an Aramaic loanword (mammon), wealth or possessions as a personified power; Jesus personifies wealth as a rival lord (kyrios). ou dynasthe theo douleuein kai mammona — you cannot serve God and Mammon — the impossibility is structural: a slave belongs entirely to one master; divided service is definitionally impossible in the slave-master relationship. The two masters are presented as mutually exclusive rivals for ultimate loyalty.
6.1: tēn dikaiosynēn hymōn mē poiein emprosthen tōn anthrōpōn — do not perform your righteousness before people — dikaiosynē here governs the three practices that follow (almsgiving, prayer, fasting); it is the broader category of covenant faithfulness, not righteousness as legal standing. Theathēnai autois — to be seen by them — aorist infinitive of purpose: the audience-seeking is the problem, not the practice itself. Misthon ouk echete para tō patri hymōn — you have no reward from your Father — the reward from God and the reward from human observers are mutually exclusive.
6.2: mē salpises emprosthen sou hōsper hoi hypokritai poiousin — do not sound a trumpet before you as the hypocrites do — hypokritai is from hypokrinomai, to play a theatrical role; in Hellenistic Greek, a theater actor. Jesus applies it to religious performance where the inner motive contradicts the outer act. Apechousin ton misthon autōn — they have received their reward in full — apechō is the technical term from papyrus receipts: "paid in full." The heavenly ledger shows zero balance; they have been paid entirely by human applause.
6.3: mē gnōtō hē aristera sou ti poiei hē dexia sou — do not let your left hand know what your right hand is doing — gnōtō third-person aorist imperative: let it not know. The image targets the reflex of self-congratulation: even internal awareness of one's own generosity becomes a form of self-audience. The left hand (aristera) and right hand (dexia) are the same person's own faculties; the command points to unawareness of self as the ideal of pure giving.
6.4: hopōs ē sou hē eleēmosynē en tō kryptō — so that your giving may be in secret (kryptos, hidden) — the purpose clause governs the action: secrecy is the goal, not a byproduct. Ho patēr sou ho blepōn en tō kryptō apodōsei soi — your Father who sees in secret will reward you — blepōn present participle: ongoing active observation; apodōsei future: certain future payment. The Father's observation substitutes completely for human observation.
6.5: hoi hypokritai philousin — the hypocrites love — phileō (love by affection/inclination) rather than agapaō; they are drawn to public prayer by desire, not duty. Stantontes en tais synagōgais kai en tais gōniais tōn plateiōn proseuchesthai — standing in the synagogues and on the street corners to pray — stantontes is a perfect active participle (histamai): having taken their stand, remaining standing. The position is deliberate and held. Apechousin — paid in full — same receipt-formula as v.2.
6.6: tameion — inner room, storeroom — a private interior room, often windowless, used for storage; the most private space in a household. Kleisas tēn thyran sou — having shut your door — kleisas aorist participle: the closed door is a precondition, not an afterthought. Proseuxai tō patri sou tō en tō kryptō — pray to your Father who is in secret — the Father inhabits the hidden space; he is present in the hiddenness itself. Ho blepōn en tō kryptō apodōsei soi — identical reward-formula to 6:4.
6.7: battalogēsēte — do not babble (battologeō) — a verb found almost nowhere in classical Greek, likely onomatopoeic: stuttering, repeating nonsense syllables. Hōsper hoi ethnikoi — like the Gentiles — pagan prayer often involved extensive repetition of divine names and epithets to compel divine attention. Dokousin gar hoti en tē polylogia autōn eisakousthēsontai — for they think that by their many words (polylogia, a word coined for the occasion) they will be heard — the theology of prayer-by-volume contrasted with address to a Father who already knows.
6.8: oiden ho patēr hymōn hōn chreian echete — your Father knows what you need — oiden is a perfect form used as a present: the Father's knowledge is complete and current. Pro tou hymas aitēsai auton — before you ask him — prayer is not informational; it is relational. Prayer does not supply God with data he lacks but is the mode of relationship in which the Father's foreknowledge becomes the context for the disciple's dependence.
6.10: elthatō hē basileia sou — let your kingdom come (elthátō aorist imperative of erchomai) — the petition is eschatological: the full arrival of the reign of God. Genēthētō to thelēma sou — let your will be done (genēthētō aorist passive imperative of ginomai): let it come to be. Hōs en ouranō kai epi gēs — as in heaven, so also on earth — the adverbial phrase qualifies how the will is to be done: the manner of heavenly obedience is the standard for earthly obedience.
6.11: ton arton hēmōn ton epiousion dos hēmin sēmeron — give us today our daily bread — epiousion (G1967) is one of the rarest words in the Greek New Testament, found almost nowhere outside this prayer and its Lukan parallel (11:3).
