It may derive from epi + ousia (for existence: the bread necessary for existence) or epi + iōn (for the coming day: tomorrow's bread). Origen noted that the word did not exist in classical Greek, suggesting it was coined for the prayer. The MKT "daily" follows the most common rendering; "necessary for existence" or "for the coming day" are defensible alternatives.
6.13: mē eisenenkēs hēmas eis peirasmon — do not lead us into temptation/testing (peirasmos) — eisenenkēs aorist active subjunctive: a point-action. Peirasmos covers both testing (a trial through which faith is proved) and temptation (an inducement to sin); the petition covers both senses. Alla rhysai hēmas apo tou ponērou — but deliver us from the evil one — rhysai aorist imperative of rhyomai (to rescue from danger); apo tou ponērou likely personal (cf. 5:37). The doxology (hoti sou estin hē basileia) is well-attested in later manuscripts but absent from the earliest witnesses (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus).
6.14: ean gar aphēte tois anthrōpois ta paraptōmata autōn — for if you forgive people their trespasses — paraptōmata (G3900, from parapiptō, to fall beside/astray): trespasses, moral deviations — a different word from opheilēmata (debts) in v.12. Aphēte aorist active subjunctive: a decisive act of forgiveness. Aphēsei kai hymin ho patēr hymōn ho ouranios — your heavenly Father will also forgive you — the correlation is conditional, not commercial; human forgiveness is evidence of a heart transformed by divine forgiveness, not its cause.
6.15: ean de mē aphēte tois anthrōpois — but if you do not forgive people — the negative counterpart to v.14 using the same subjunctive construction. Oude ho patēr hymōn aphēsei ta paraptōmata hymōn — neither will your Father forgive your trespasses — the conditional symmetry means that withholding forgiveness reveals a heart that has not received forgiveness at depth (cf. 18:23-35, the unforgiving servant), not that human forgiveness earns divine forgiveness.
6.16: nēsteuēte mē ginesthe hōsper hoi hypokritai skythropoi — when you fast, do not become like the hypocrites, gloomy-faced — skythropoi (gloomy, sullen: skythros + ōps/face) appears in the LXX of Gen 40:7 for the cup-bearer's distress. Aphanizousin gar ta prosōpa autōn — for they disfigure their faces (aphanizō, to make unseen/disappear) — they obscure their faces to make their piety visible; the irony: they make their faces invisible in order to be seen. Apechousin — paid in full — same formula as 6:2, 5.
6.17: su de nēsteuōn aleipsai sou tēn kephalēn kai to prosōpon sou nipsai — but when you fast, anoint your head and wash your face — aleipsai aorist middle imperative of aleiphō (to anoint with oil): anointing the head was a sign of festivity, not mourning (Ps 23:5; Ruth 3:3). Nipsai aorist middle imperative of niptō (to wash). The commands describe normal daily grooming; Jesus instructs disciples to look normal, i.e., to conceal the fast from human observers entirely.
6.18: hopōs mē phanēs tois anthrōpois nēsteuōn — so that you may not appear to be fasting to people — phanēs aorist passive subjunctive of phainō: appear, be visible. The purpose of normal grooming is concealment of the practice. Ho patēr sou ho blepōn en tō kryptō apodōsei soi — identical reward-formula to 6:4 and 6:6; the three practices of piety (alms, prayer, fasting) receive the identical structural treatment.
6.19: mē thēsaurizete hymin thēsaurous epi tēs gēs — do not store up treasures for yourselves on earth (thēsaurizete present imperative: stop the ongoing practice) — hopou sēs kai brōsis aphanizei — where moth and corrosion destroys — sēs (moth, the insect that consumes fabric); brōsis (eating, corrosion — used for rust or vermin gnawing grain). Kai hopou kleptai dioryssousin — and where thieves dig through (dioryssō) — thieves in ancient Palestine literally dug through the mud-brick walls of homes rather than forcing doors.
6.20: thēsaurizete de hymin thēsaurous en ouranō — but store up for yourselves treasures in heaven — the positive counterpart to v.19 with the same verb thēsaurizete (present imperative); the contrast is location, not activity. Hopou oute sēs oute brōsis aphanizei, kai hopou kleptai ou dioryssousin — the negative catalogue reverses v.19 point by point. The treasure-in-heaven language draws on Jewish tradition (Tob 4:9; Pss. Sol. 9:5) where righteous deeds credited before God constitute heavenly wealth.
6.21: hopou gar estin ho thēsauros sou, ekei estai kai hē kardia sou — for where your treasure is, there your heart will be also — thēsauros and kardia are linked by the directional hopou/ekei (where/there). This reverses the expected psychology: one might expect "where your heart is, there you will put your treasure." Jesus says that what you invest in determines what you care about. The kardia is the center of loyalty and attention, not merely emotion.
6.22: ho lychnos tou sōmatos estin ho ophthalmos — the lamp of the body is the eye — the body is illuminated by a single opening. Ean oun ē ho ophthalmos sou haplous — if then your eye is haplous — haplous (G573) means single, undivided, and in Jewish idiom generous (in contrast to the "evil eye" of v.23 which denotes stinginess); both senses are active here. Holon to sōma sou phōteinon estai — your whole body will be full of light — the single eye illuminates the entire body; the eye metaphor carries the treasure/heart teaching of vv.19-21 into the language of perception.
6.23: ean de ho ophthalmos sou ponēros ē — but if your eye is evil/diseased — ponēros (evil, and: diseased, not functioning properly). In Jewish idiom, the "evil eye" (ayin ra) denoted stinginess or greed; the "good eye" (ayin tob) denoted generosity. Both the functional (seeing clearly/poorly) and the moral (generous/stingy) dimensions are present. Ei oun to phōs to en soi skotos estin, to skotos poson — if then the light that is in you is darkness, how great is that darkness — the rhetorical question uses the impossible image (a darkness that functions as light) to measure the magnitude of the self-deception.
6.25: mē merimnate tē psychē hymōn ti phagēte ē ti piēte — do not be anxious (merimnaō, present imperative: stop the ongoing anxiety) about your life/soul, what you will eat or drink — psychē (G5590) here = life, the physical life sustained by food and drink. Mēde tō sōmati hymōn ti endysēsthe — nor for your body what you will put on. Ouchi hē psychē pleon estin tēs trophēs — is not life more than food? — a qal va-chomer (light-to-heavy) argument: if God provided the greater thing (life itself), will he not provide the lesser things that sustain it?
6.26: emblepate eis ta peteina tou ouranou — look carefully at the birds of the air (emblepō, to look into/at intently — stronger than blepō) — the birds are not examples of industriousness but of provision despite having no storage system: ou speirousin oude therizousin oude synagousin eis apothēkas — they neither sow nor reap nor gather into barns. Ho patēr hymōn ho ouranios trephei auta — your heavenly Father feeds them. Ouch hymeis mallon diapherete autōn — are you not worth much more than they — diapherō, to differ in value.
6.27: tis de ex hymōn merimnōn dynastai prostheinai epi tēn hēlikian autou pēchyn hena — which of you by worrying can add a single cubit to his span of life — hēlikia can mean height (stature) or lifespan; in context "span of life" makes better sense (adding a cubit to one's stature is trivial; adding time to one's lifespan is what worry seeks). Pēchys (a cubit, about 45 cm) applied to time is a small unit. The rhetorical question expects "no one."
6.28: ta krina tou agrou — the lilies/flowers of the field — krinon (G2918) likely refers to wildflowers broadly; archaeological work suggests the scarlet anemone or similar field flowers of Galilee, not necessarily the iris or water lily. Katamathete — observe carefully (katamanthanō, a stronger compound of manthanō, to learn) — learn by observation. Pōs auxanousin — how they grow — present indicative. Ou kopiōsi oude nēthousin — they do not labor nor do they spin — the activities of human textile production are absent from the flowers.
6.29: Solomōn en pasē tē doxē autou — Solomon in all his glory — doxa here = splendor, the visual manifestation of honor and wealth. Solomon's legendary wealth (1 Kgs 10) is the superlative of human magnificence. Ou periebleto hōs hen toutōn — was not clothed like one of these — periballō (to clothe, wrap around) aorist passive; Solomon as a passive recipient of clothing, compared unfavorably to what God clothes wild flowers with. The understatement makes the point more striking.
6.30: ton chorton tou agrou sēmeron onta kai aurion eis klibanon ballomenon — the grass of the field, which is here today and tomorrow thrown into the oven — chortos (grass, hay) encompasses lilies and common grass; klibanon is a clay pot or dome used as an oven, into which dried grass was thrown as fuel. Oligopistoi — O you of little faith — a compound word: oligos (little) + pistis (faith/trust); Matthew uses it as Jesus's characteristic address to disciples in distress (8:26; 14:31; 16:8; 17:20). The diminutive is corrective, not contemptuous.
6.31: mē oun merimnēsēte legontes — do not therefore be anxious, saying — merimnēsēte aorist subjunctive: a prohibition against taking up anxiety as a posture. The three quoted worries (ti phagōmen, ti piōmen, ti peribalōmetha — what shall we eat? what shall we drink? what shall we wear?) are subjunctive deliberatives: the internal questions of anxious deliberation. Jesus quotes the exact questions the anxious person asks herself, making the prohibition specific and recognizable.
6.32: panta gar tauta ta ethnē epizētousin — for the Gentiles seek all these things (epizēteō, to seek after earnestly) — the ethnē here are not moral exemplars but the non-covenant people who have no framework for trusting a providential Father. Oiden gar ho patēr hymōn ho ouranios hoti chrēzete toutōn hapantōn — for your heavenly Father knows that you need all these things — the same foreknowledge as 6:8 (oiden perfect-as-present); the Father's prior knowledge removes the cognitive urgency for anxiety.
6.33: zēteite de prōton tēn basileian tou theou kai tēn dikaiosynēn autou — seek first his kingdom and his righteousness — zēteite present imperative, contrasting with the anxious seeking of v.32; prōton sets the priority. Tēn dikaiosynēn autou — his righteousness — autou can be objective (the righteousness God requires) or subjective (the righteousness God himself displays); the MKT "his righteousness" preserves the ambiguity. Tauta panta prostethēsetai hymin — all these things will be added to you — prostithēmi future passive: provision comes as an addition to, not a substitute for, seeking the kingdom.
6.34: mē oun merimnēsēte eis tēn aurion — do not therefore be anxious about tomorrow — eis tēn aurion projects anxiety forward in time. Hē gar aurion merimnēsei heautēs — for tomorrow will be anxious for itself (heautēs reflexive genitive) — a personification of tomorrow as an agent carrying its own burden. Arketon tē hēmera hē kakia autēs — sufficient for the day is its own trouble — arketos (sufficient, from arkeō) + kakia (evil, trouble, hardship): the day's troubles are already enough; accumulating tomorrow's troubles before tomorrow arrives is unnecessary addition.

7.24: oikodomesen — built — the aorist indicates completed action, the wise builder actually builds (vs. merely hearing). akouon mou tous logous toutous kai poion autous — hearing my words and doing them — the two-stage requirement mirrors the Shema (shema = hear and obey). petra — bedrock, solid rock — contrasted with psammos (sand); the Semitic image of the rock as foundation for covenant stability (Isa 28:16, the tested cornerstone in Zion). The house built on rock withstands floods and winds — the twin disasters of Palestinian flash floods and seasonal windstorms.
7.1: mē krinete — do not judge (krinō, present imperative: stop the ongoing practice) — the prohibition is not against all moral evaluation (v.6 requires discriminating judgment about dogs and pigs) but against the presumptuous, condemnatory judgment that sets oneself up as a final tribunal over another. Hina mē krithēte — so that you will not be judged — purpose clause: the prohibition has a self-interested logic as well as a moral one. The passive krithēte (be judged) is a divine passive: God is the agent of the judgment returned upon the judge.
7.2: hō gar krimati krinete krithēsesthe — for with what judgment you judge, you will be judged — the sentence is an antanaclasis: the same word-root (krin-) in three forms (noun, verb, passive future) applied to the one doing the judging. Kai en hō metrō metreite metrēthēsetai hymin — and with the measure you measure, it will be measured to you — metreō/metron (to measure/measure): the commercial imagery of weights and scales applied to moral accountability. The passive metrēthēsetai is again divine passive.
7.3: to karphos to en tō ophthalmō tou adelphou sou — the speck (karphos, a tiny splinter, chip of wood or straw) in your brother's eye — karphos is the minimum visible fragment. Tēn de en tō sō ophthalmō dokon — the plank (dokos, a load-bearing beam) in your own eye — the absurdist hyperbole inverts perception: one who cannot see because of a beam in their own eye judges the vision of someone with a splinter. The contrast is not merely one of scale but of self-knowledge.
7.4: pōs ereis tō adelphō sou — how can you say to your brother — pōs introduces a rhetorical impossibility: the question is not "will you say this" but "how can you say this." Aphes ekbalō to karphos ek tou ophthalmou sou — let me take the speck out of your eye — aphes aorist imperative of aphiēmi: let me remove it; the volunteer helper asks permission, all the while blind to their own larger obstruction. Kai idou hē dokos en tō ophthalmō sou — and look, the plank is in your own eye.
7.5: hypokrita — hypocrite — the same word used for religious performers in 6:2,5,16; here applied to the self-deceived judge who sees clearly abroad but not at home. Ekbale prōton ek tou ophthalmou sou tēn dokon — first take the plank out of your own eye — prōton (first) is the structural principle: self-examination precedes correction of others. Kai tote diablepseis ekbalein to karphos ek tou ophthalmou tou adelphou sou — and then you will see clearly (diablepō, to see through/clearly) to remove the speck from your brother's eye.
7.6: mē dōte to hagion tois kysin — do not give what is holy to dogs — to hagion (the holy thing, the sacred) and tous margaritas (the pearls) are paired as objects of wisdom-teaching. Kysin (dogs) and tois choirois (pigs) were both ritually unclean animals in Jewish law and metaphors for those who treat sacred things with contempt. Mēpote katapatesousin autous en tois posin autōn kai straphentes rhēxōsin hymas — lest they trample them underfoot and turning tear you to pieces — the reversal: instead of receiving the gift, they destroy both it and the giver. The verse requires discrimination in sharing sacred wisdom, balancing 7:1 which prohibits judicial arrogance.
7.7: aiteite kai dothēsetai hymin — ask and it will be given to you — three present imperatives in sequence: aiteite (ask), zēteite (seek), krouete (knock). The present tense marks all three as ongoing, persistent activities, not one-time requests. Dothēsetai (it will be given) — future passive divine. The progression from asking to seeking to knocking may indicate increasing urgency or effort, or it may be a rhetorical triplet expressing the same reality three ways.
7.8: pas gar ho aitōn lambanei — for everyone who asks receives — the three present active participles (ho aitōn/ho zētōn/ho krouōn) are universal: every one who asks... every one who seeks... every one who knocks. Lambanei (receives), heuriskei (finds), and the passive anoigēsetai (it will be opened) match the three actions of v.7. The movement from imperative to indicative (from command to promise) grounds the exhortation in certainty of outcome.
7.9: ē tis estin ex hymōn anthrōpos hon aitēsei ho huios autou arton — or who among you is a person whose son asks for bread — a rhetorical question expecting "no one." Arton... lithon — bread... stone: similar in shape (round flat bread resembling a flat stone of the region). The question is about the father's response: would a father deliberately give what harms when asked for what nourishes? The argument moves from human paternal instinct to divine generosity.
7.10: ē kai ichthyn aitēsei — or if he asks for a fish — ichthyn (fish) and ophīn (snake) are paired as the second analogy: both elongated, similar in shape. Mē ophin epidōsei autō — will he not give him a snake? — mē introduces a question expecting "no" (negative answer). The two analogies (bread/stone, fish/snake) establish by everyday human parental behavior the principle that guides the divine argument in v.11.
7.11: ei oun hymeis ponēroi ontes — if then you, being evil — ponēroi ontes: present active participle acknowledging the moral condition of the disciples without softening it; the argument explicitly includes human sinfulness as the baseline from which the a fortiori (poso mallon, how much more) operates. Oidate domata agatha didonai tois teknois hymōn — know how to give good gifts to your children — oidate (perfect-as-present: you already know). Poso mallon ho patēr hymōn ho en tois ouranois dōsei agatha tois aitousin auton — how much more will your heavenly Father give good things to those who ask him.
7.12: panta oun hosa ean thelēte hina poiōsin hymin hoi anthrōpoi — everything therefore that you want people to do to you — oun (therefore) connects the Golden Rule to what preceded: the character of the heavenly Father (v.11) who gives good things is the ground for treating others as you wish to be treated. Houtōs kai hymeis poieite autois — so also do to them. Houtos gar estin ho nomos kai hoi prophētai — for this is the Law and the Prophets — the same phrase as 5:17; the Golden Rule is not a replacement of the Torah but its concentrated summary (cf. Rom 13:9-10, Gal 5:14).
7.13: eiselthate dia tēs stenēs pylēs — enter through the narrow gate (eiselthate, aorist imperative: a decisive act of entering) — stenē (narrow, constricted) and plateia (wide, broad) are contrasted. Hoti plateia hē pylē kai eurychōros hē hodos hē apagousa eis tēn apōleian — for wide is the gate and spacious the road leading to destruction (apōleia, loss/ruin/perdition). Kai polloi eisin hoi eiserchomenoi di autēs — and many are those entering through it — polloi (many) is not comforting here; popularity is not a mark of the correct path.
7.14: hoti stenē hē pylē kai tethlimmenē hē hodos — for narrow is the gate and constricted (thlibō, to press, squeeze) the road — tethlimmenē perfect passive participle: the road has been narrowed, pressed in from both sides; it is not merely small but actively constrained. Hē apagousa eis tēn zōēn — leading to life — zōē (life) is the counterpart to apōleia (destruction) in v.13. Kai oligoi eisin hoi heuriskontes autēn — and few are those who find it — oligoi (few): the road is so narrow it must be actively found (heuriskontes, present participle: those who are in the process of finding).
7.15: prosechete apo tōn pseudoprophētōn — beware of the false prophets (prosechō apo + genitive: to guard oneself from, hold back from) — pseudoprophētai are not simply inaccurate predictors but those who claim prophetic authority while serving false ends. Hoitines erchontai pros hymas en endymasin probatōn — who come to you in sheep's clothing (endymasin, outer garments, what one is dressed in) — the garb of the sheep: the appearance of belonging to the flock. Esōthen de eisin lykoi harpages — but inwardly they are rapacious wolves (harpax, seizing, grasping, predatory).
7.16: apo tōn karpōn autōn epignōsesthe autous — by their fruits you will recognize them (epiginōskō, to know thoroughly, recognize fully — the epi- prefix intensifying the knowing) — the criterion is practical and observable: not their words, charisma, or claimed credentials, but their karpoi (fruits, products, outcomes). Mēti syllegousin apo akanthōn staphylas ē apo tribōlōn syka — do people gather grapes from thornbushes or figs from thistles? — a rhetorical question establishing that plant-type determines fruit-type absolutely.
7.17: pan dendron agathon karpous kalous poiei — every good tree makes good fruit — agathos (intrinsically good, good in nature) vs. sapros (rotten, decayed, spoiled: used for rotting wood and bad fish, Matt 13:48). The logic is ontological: what a thing is determines what it produces. To de sapron dendron karpous ponērous poiei — but the rotten tree makes bad/evil fruit — ponērous (bad, harmful, evil): the same word as "the evil one" in 5:37 and "evil people" in 7:11.
7.18: ou dynatai dendron agathon karpous ponērous poiein — a good tree cannot bear bad fruit — ou dynatai (is not able): the logical impossibility is stated as an ontological incapacity, not merely an improbability. Oude dendron sapron karpous kalous poiein — nor can a rotten tree bear good fruit — the symmetrical impossibility stated in both directions. The botanical logic grounds the identification of false prophets in character rather than in claimed criteria.
7.19: pan dendron mē poioun karpon kalon ekkoptetai kai eis pyr balletai — every tree not bearing good fruit is cut down and thrown into fire — ekkoptetai (present passive: is being cut down) and balletai (present passive: is being thrown into fire) — the present tenses may be gnomic (general truth) or proleptic (anticipating the certain future). The language echoes John the Baptist's preaching (3:10: "even now the axe is laid at the root of the trees"). The eschatological fire is the endpoint of fruitlessness.
7.20: ara ge apo tōn karpōn autōn epignōsesthe autous — therefore by their fruits you will recognize them — a near-verbatim repetition of v.16b, forming an inclusio that frames the tree/fruit argument of vv.16-19. Ara ge is an emphatic inferential particle: "therefore indeed," drawing the conclusion firmly. The repetition drives the diagnostic criterion home: fruits are the test, not claims of authority, spiritual experience, or formal credentials.
7.21: ou pas ho legōn moi kyrie kyrie eiseleusetai eis tēn basileian tōn ouranōn — not everyone who says to me "Lord, Lord" will enter the kingdom of heaven — ou pas... all: the distributive negation singles out a subclass of "Lord-sayers" who will not enter. Kyrie kyrie — the doubled address is a Semitic intensifier (cf. "Absalom, Absalom," "Martha, Martha") indicating earnestness or familiarity; but familiarity with the formula does not constitute relationship. Ho de poiōn to thelēma tou patros mou tou en ouranois — but the one doing the will of my Father in heaven — the present active participle marks ongoing, habitual doing.
7.22: polloi erousin moi en ekeinē tē hēmera — many will say to me on that day — ekeinē tē hēmera (that day) is the day of final judgment; the plural polloi warns that this is not an edge case. Kyrie kyrie ou tō sō onomati eprophēteusamen kai tō sō onomati daimonia exebalomen kai tō sō onomati dynamous pollas epoiēsamen — Lord, Lord, did we not prophesy in your name, and in your name drive out demons, and in your name perform many miracles? — three extraordinary religious acts all performed en tō sō onomati (in your name): real acts, apparently, but performed without genuine relationship.
7.23: kai tote homologēsō autois hoti oudepote egnōn hymas — and then I will declare to them: I never knew you — homologeō (to confess, declare openly): the declaration is public and definitive. Oudepote egnōn hymas — I never knew you — oudepote (never at any time) + egnōn (aorist of ginōskō): I knew you not, not even once. Ginōskō in Hebrew thought denotes relational, covenantal knowing (cf. Amos 3:2, "you only have I known" = chosen in covenant relationship). The issue is not information but relationship. Apochōreite ap’ emou hoi ergazomenoi tēn anomian — depart from me you workers of lawlessness (anomia, lawlessness, the opposite of the law-fulfilling righteousness Jesus taught in ch5–6).
7.25: kai katebē hē brochē kai ēlthon hoi potamoi kai epneusan hoi anemoi — the rain came down and the rivers came and the winds blew — three agents of destruction in sequence: rain (brochē), flood-streams (potamoi), winds (anemoi). Kai prosepesan tē oikia ekeinē — and beat against that house — prosepiptō (to fall against, rush against). Kai ouk epesen — and it did not fall — aorist negated: a completed test with a completed non-collapse. Tetheliouto gar epi tēn petran — for it had been founded on the rock — pluperfect passive: the foundation pre-existed the storm and its condition determined the outcome.
7.26: pas ho akouōn mou tous logous toutous kai mē poiōn autous — everyone who hears these words of mine and does not do them — the two-part condition: hearing is present (ongoing), not-doing is also present (habitual omission). The contrast with v.24 is grammatically precise: v.24 has akouōn... kai poiōn (hearing and doing); v.26 has akouōn... kai mē poiōn (hearing and not-doing). Homoiōthēsetai andri mōrō — will be like a foolish man (mōros, the same root as morainō, to become foolish, in 5:13). The foolishness is not intellectual deficiency but the moral failure to act on what one has received.
7.27: kai katebē hē brochē kai ēlthon hoi potamoi kai epneusan hoi anemoi — identical storm-formula to v.25 — the same three agents strike the same way. Kai prosekospan tē oikia ekeinē — and struck against that house — proskoptō (to strike against, stumble into) vs. v.25's prosepiptō; both convey the force of the assault. Kai epesen kai ēn hē ptōsis autēs megalē — and it fell, and great was its fall — ptōsis (fall, collapse): the noun from piptō. The greatness of the fall measures the greatness of the investment in false foundation; the more one builds on sand, the more catastrophic the collapse.
7.28: kai egeneto hote etelesen ho Iēsous tous logous toutous — and it came to pass when Jesus finished these words — egeneto hote is a Matthean formula (also 11:1; 13:53; 19:1; 26:1) marking the end of the five great discourses of Matthew's Gospel. Etelesen aorist of teleō: completed, finished — the same root as teleios (mature, complete) of 5:48. Exeplessonto hoi ochloi epi tē didachē autou — the crowds were astonished (ekplēssō, to strike out of one's wits, astonish thoroughly) at his teaching.
7.29: ēn gar didaskōn autous hōs exousian echōn — for he was teaching them as one having authority (exousia, the right and power to act, legitimate authority from one's own standing) — hōs exousian echōn: "as" implies manner but also content; the comparison identifies his mode of speaking. Kai ouch hōs hoi grammateis autōn — and not as their scribes — the scribes taught by citing tradition and precedent (the rabbis say..., it has been transmitted that..., the school of Hillel holds..., the school of Shammai holds...); Jesus teaches on his own authority: "but I tell you" (5:22, 28, 32, 34, 39, 44).

8.9: kai gar ego anthropos eimi hypo exousian — for I also am a man under authority — the centurion's argument is a fortiori: if he can command soldiers at a distance because he himself is under Roman military authority (a chain of command that gives his words power), how much more can Jesus command disease at a distance, being himself under the ultimate authority. exousia — authority, the right and power to act — appears 10 times in Matthew, always associated with Jesus's teaching, healing, and forgiving (7:29, 9:6, 11:27, 28:18).
8.17: autos tas astheneias hemon elaben kai tas nosous ebastasen — he himself took our infirmities and bore our diseases — Matthew's citation of Isa 53:4 applies the Servant's vicarious bearing not to the cross but to the healing ministry. Labo (took) and bastazo (bore, carried) are the verbs of the Servant's substitutionary work. The healing miracles are not merely humanitarian acts but are themselves the Servant's carrying of human sickness — an extension of the same substitutionary principle that reaches its fullness in the passion.
8.1: katabanti de autō apo tou orous — when he came down from the mountain — katabanti dative aorist participle of katabainō: the descent from the mount of teaching (ch5–7) is the frame for the sequence of miracles in ch8–9 that demonstrate the same authority in deeds. Ēkolouthēsan autō ochloi polloi — large crowds followed him — akoloutheo (to follow) is Matthew's discipleship verb; the crowds follow physically; the question is whether they follow in commitment.
8.2: lepros — a man with leprosy — lepros covers a range of skin conditions, not necessarily what modern medicine identifies as Hansen's disease; the significance is the Levitical uncleanness and social exclusion it imposed (Lev 13-14). Prosekynei autō — was bowing before him/worshipping him — proskyneō (to prostrate oneself, do obeisance): the gesture of approach to royalty or deity. Kyrie ean thelēs dynastai me katharisai — Lord, if you are willing, you are able to cleanse me — a careful formulation: the leper affirms Jesus's power without presuming his will.
8.3: kai ekteinas tēn cheira ēpsato autou — and stretching out his hand he touched him — ekteinas aorist participle: deliberately extending the hand toward the unclean; hēpsato aorist of haptō, to touch, to grasp. Under Levitical law, contact with a leper conveyed uncleanness to the one who touched; Jesus reverses the direction: his touch cleanses rather than contracting impurity. Thelō katharisthēti — I am willing; be cleansed — thelō (I will/want) answers the leper's conditional; katharisthi is an aorist passive imperative — Jesus does not ask the man to become clean but pronounces and effects his cleansing with a single word.
8.4: hora mēdeni eipēs — see that you tell no one — hora (see to it, take heed) + mēdeni eipēs (negative aorist subjunctive: tell no one). This is the first instance of the "messianic secrecy" motif in Matthew: Jesus limits publicity about his identity and works. Alla hypage seauton deixon tō hierei — but go, show yourself to the priest — deixon aorist imperative: the showing is to be the first act, before speaking to anyone. Kai prosenegke to dōron ho prosetaxen Mōysēs — and offer the gift Moses commanded — referring to the cleansing ritual of Lev 14:2-32. Eis martyrion autois — as a testimony to them: the restored leper becomes living proof before the religious authorities.
8.5: hekatontarchos — centurion (commander of 100 soldiers in the Roman army) — a Gentile military officer; the encounter follows immediately after the Jew with leprosy, setting up a Jew/Gentile contrast in responses to Jesus. Parakalōn auton — appealing to him, imploring him (parakaleō, to call alongside, to exhort or beseech): the centurion approaches not as a superior but as a supplicant.
8.6: ho pais mou — my servant/boy (pais) — a word covering both servant and child; it has more personal warmth than the standard slave-word doulos. The centurion's concern for his pais establishes the personal stakes. Beblētai en tē oikia paralytikos deinōs basanizomenos — has been thrown (= is lying paralyzed) at home, suffering terribly — beblētai perfect passive: currently in the state of having been cast down; deinōs basanizomenos present passive: continuously being tormented.
8.7: kagō elthōn therapeusō auton — I will come and heal him — the Greek can be read as a direct statement (I will come and heal him) or as a question with implied reluctance (Shall I come and heal him?, raising the social irregularity of a Jewish teacher entering a Gentile household). The ambiguity is unresolved in the text; what matters is that the centurion's response in v.8 presupposes the greater point: Jesus's authority does not require presence. Elthōn is an aorist participle of erchomai: having come.
8.8: kyrie ouk eimi hikanos — Lord, I am not worthy (hikanos, sufficient, fit, adequate) — the centurion uses the language of social hierarchy: he is not of the rank that would make his household appropriate for Jesus to enter. Alla monon eipe logō — but only say the word — monon (only, merely) + logō (dative: with/by a word alone): a single word from Jesus is sufficient because authority does not require proximity. Kai iathēsetai ho pais mou — and my servant will be healed — iathēsetai future passive: the healing is certain, conditioned only on Jesus's word.
8.10: ethaumasen — he marveled (thaumazō, to be astonished) — one of only two occasions in the Gospels where Jesus is said to marvel (the other is Mark 6:6, at unbelief); here he marvels at unusual faith. Para oudeni tosouten pistin en tō Israēl heuron — not in anyone in Israel have I found such faith — tosouten (so great, so large): the faith is not just faith but faith of exceptional magnitude. The observation establishes the inversion: Gentile faith exceeds the faith of the covenant people.
8.11: polloi apo anatolōn kai dysmōn hēxousin kai anaklitheēsontai — many from east and west will come and will recline at table — apo anatolōn kai dysmōn (from risings and settings of the sun, i.e., east and west): a comprehensive geographical merism. Anaklitheēsontai future passive of anaklinō (to recline at table): the eschatological banquet posture. Meta Abraam kai Isaak kai Iakōb en tē basileia tōn ouranōn — with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob in the kingdom of heaven — the patriarchs serve as the expected guests; the surprise is who else joins them.
8.12: hoi de huioi tēs basileias — the sons of the kingdom — those who by birth and covenant membership expected to inherit the kingdom; the phrase simultaneously acknowledges their special status and subverts the presumption that status guarantees entry. Ekblēthēsontai eis to skotos to exōteron — will be thrown into the outer darkness — ekballō future passive: the same verb used for exorcism (throwing out demons). Skotos to exōteron (the outermost darkness): outside the light of the feast. Ekei estai ho klauthmos kai ho brygmos tōn odontōn — there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth — a Matthean formula (also 13:42,50; 22:13; 24:51; 25:30): the reaction of those excluded from the eschatological celebration.
8.13: hōs episteusan — as you believed — hōs (as) + aorist subjunctive: the healing corresponds exactly and immediately to the faith expressed. Iathē ho pais — the servant was healed — aorist passive: completed action. En tē hōra ekeinē — at that very hour (hōra, hour; ekeinē, that specific one) — the temporal precision underlines that Jesus's word effects instantaneous healing at a distance, confirming the centurion's thesis in v.8.
8.14: tēn pentheran autou beblēmenēn kai pyresousan — his mother-in-law lying (in bed) and burning with fever — beblēmenēn perfect passive participle: she has been cast down, prostrated by illness; pyresousan present active participle: actively burning with fever (from pyressō, to have a fever, from pyr, fire). The two participles together describe both her posture (lying immobilized) and her condition (fever raging). The brief scene introduces Peter, whose household is now in Capernaum.
8.15: ēpsato tēs cheiros autēs kai aphēken autēn ho pyretos — he touched her hand and the fever left her — hēpsato aorist of haptō (touched): the same touch-verb as in 8:3 with the leper. Aphēken autēn ho pyretos — the fever left her (aphiēmi aorist: to leave, release) — a near-personification: the fever is spoken of as an agent that departs. Kai ēgerthē kai diēkonei autō — and she rose and was serving him — ēgerthē aorist passive (she was raised); diēkonei imperfect active: the ongoing service. The imperfect marks the restoration as complete and immediately productive.
8.16: daimonizomonous pollous prosēnenkan autō kai exebalen ta pneumata logō — many demon-possessed were brought to him and he drove out the spirits with a word — logō (dative: by/with a word alone): the same principle as the centurion's faith (v.8); Jesus's authority operates through speech. Kai pantas tous kakōs echontas etherapeusen — and he healed all who were ill (kakōs echō, to have badly, to be in a bad condition: a Greek idiom for illness or suffering). The summary verse covers both exorcism and healing, preparing for the formula-citation of v.17.
8.18: idōn de ho Iēsous ochlon peri auton ekeleusen apelthein eis to peran — seeing the crowd around him he commanded to depart to the other side — ekeleusen aorist of keleuō (to command, order), a strong directive. Eis to peran — to the other side (of the Sea of Galilee): a geographical transition that also carries narrative weight in Matthew as entry into Gentile territory.
8.19: kai proselthōn heis grammateus eipen autō — and one scribe approaching said to him — a scribe (grammateus), a professional Torah scholar and copyist, approaches as a potential disciple. Didaskale akolouthēsō soi hopou ean aperchē — Teacher (didaskalos, note: not "Lord" as Peter and the centurion use), I will follow you wherever you go — the promise is unqualified but the address is professional, not personal. Jesus's response addresses the unstated assumption behind the offer.
8.20: ho huios tou anthrōpou ouk echei pou tēn kephalēn klinē — the Son of Man has nowhere to lay (klinē aorist active subjunctive of klinō, to recline, lean) his head — ho huios tou anthrōpou is Jesus's self-designation drawn from Dan 7:13-14, where the figure receives eternal kingdom; Matthew's irony is that this cosmic figure has no fixed residence. Hai alōpekes phōleous echousin kai ta peteina tou ouranou kataskēnōseis — foxes have dens and birds of the sky have nests: even animals have fixed abode; the Son of Man does not. The saying tests whether the scribe's unqualified offer holds when the conditions are disclosed.
8.21: heteros de tōn mathētōn autou — another of his disciples — this person is already a disciple (mathētōn genitive), unlike the scribe of v.19. Kyrie epitrepsōn moi prōton apelthein kai thapsai ton patera mou — Lord, permit me first to go and bury my father — epitrepsōn aorist participle of permission: grant me permission. Prōton (first) is the key word; the request implies that family burial duty takes precedence over immediate discipleship response. In Jewish culture, burial of a parent was among the highest filial obligations.
8.22: aphes tous nekrous thapsai tous heautōn nekrous — let the dead bury their own dead — the most radical of Jesus's discipleship sayings. Two senses of nekrous are in play: the physically dead (those who can attend to the burial) and the spiritually dead (those who have no relation to the kingdom of life). Sy de akolouthei moi — but you, follow me — sy emphatic: you, in particular; akolouthei present imperative: begin following and continue. The saying does not forbid burial but places the urgency of kingdom-following above even the most pressing legitimate family obligation.
8.23: kai embanti autō eis to ploion ēkolouthēsan autō hoi mathētai autou — and when he got into the boat his disciples followed him — embanti dative aorist participle: the disciples' following is triggered by and coordinates with Jesus's boarding. The boat becomes the context for the test of faith in v.24-27. Akoloutheo here is both narrative and theological: physical following into the boat is the literal form of the discipleship Jesus demanded in v.22.
8.24: kai idou seismos megas egeneto en tē thalassē — and behold, a great shaking arose on the sea — seismos is the standard word for earthquake (seio, to shake); its application to a sea-storm is unusual and intensifying: the shaking is of the order of an earthquake-like upheaval. Hōste to ploion kalyptesthai hypo tōn kymatōn — so that the boat was being covered by the waves — present passive: the covering is ongoing and the situation worsening. Autos de ekatheuden — but he was sleeping — ekatheuden imperfect: he continued sleeping; the contrast between the disciples' terror and Jesus's sleep underlines the nature of the test.
8.25: kai proselthontes ēgeiran auton legontes kyrie sōson apollymetha — and approaching they woke him saying: Lord, save us, we are perishing — sōson aorist imperative of sōzō (to save, rescue): the same verb used for eternal salvation; here it is physical rescue but the semantic range is never absent in Matthew. Apollymetha present: we are in the process of perishing right now. The address kyrie (Lord) marks the disciples as distinct from the scribe of v.19 who used didaskalos (Teacher).
8.26: ti deiloi este oligopistoi — why are you cowardly (deilos, fearful, cowardly — a word of character failure, not merely an emotion), you of little faith (oligopistoi, the compound from 6:30) — Jesus addresses the character of the fear before addressing the storm. Tote egertheis epetimēsen tois anemois kai tē thalassē — then rising he rebuked the winds and the sea — epetimēsen is the word used for rebuking demons (8:16; 17:18): the storm is addressed as a hostile power subject to command. Kai egeneto galēnē megalē — and there was a great calm (galēnē, the specific word for windless, flat-calm sea).
8.27: hoi de anthrōpoi ethaumasan legontes potapos estin houtos — the men marveled saying: what kind of person is this — potapos (what sort of, of what kind) asks a qualitative question about identity: the category of being Jesus occupies. Hoti kai hoi anemoi kai hē thalassa autō hypakouosin — that even the winds and the sea obey him — hypakouō (to obey, to hear under: to listen submissively): the same obedience-verb used for response to commands. The question the disciples ask ("what kind of person is this?") is the Christological question of the whole Gospel.
8.28: eis tēn chōran tōn Gadarēnōn — into the region of the Gadarenes — a Gentile region east of the Sea of Galilee; the textual tradition varies (Gadarenes, Gergesenes, Gerasenes) reflecting uncertainty about precise location, but all versions agree it is on the eastern (Gentile) shore. Dyo daimonizomenoi ek tōn mnēmeiōn — two demon-possessed men from the tombs — corpse-contamination made the tombs doubly unclean: the men live in the place of ritual impurity. Chalepoi lian — extremely violent (chalepos, harsh, dangerous, difficult): no one could pass.
8.29: ti hēmin kai soi — what to us and to you — a Semitic idiom of non-involvement or hostility (cf. John 2:4): "what do we have to do with you?" or "what business is there between us?", not a question seeking information but a declaration of contested relationship. Hyie tou theou — Son of God — the demons' identification of Jesus is accurate even where the disciples' understanding is incomplete (cf. 4:3,6, the devil's temptations use the same title). Pro kairou basanisai hēmas — to torment us before the time (kairos, the appointed time of eschatological judgment): the demons know of a future judgment and fear its premature arrival.
8.30: hē agelē tōn choirōn pollōn boskomenē — agelē — a large herd of pigs feeding (boskō, to feed by grazing, to tend; present passive participle) — pigs were unclean animals (Lev 11:7-8) and their presence confirms the Gentile context; Jews would not have kept swine. Makran ap autōn — some distance from them: the herd is within sight but separated.
8.31: hoi de daimones parekaloun auton legontes — the demons implored him saying (parakaleō, to beseech, the same word used of the centurion in v.5) — the demons ask rather than resist; their supplication acknowledges Jesus's authority to dispose of them as he chooses. Ei ekballeis hēmas aposteile hēmas eis tēn agelēn tōn choirōn — if you drive us out, send us into the herd of pigs — ei ekballeis (if you drive us out): the demons accept the inevitability of expulsion, requesting only the destination.
8.32: hypagete — go (hypago, aorist imperative: go away, depart) — a single word of command. Hoi de exelthontes apēlthon eis tous choirous — and departing they went into the pigs — the transition is immediate (aorist participles); no resistance is recorded. Kai idou hōrmēsen pasa hē agelē kata tou krēmnou eis tēn thalassan — and behold, the whole herd rushed down the cliff into the sea — hōrmēsen aorist of hormaō (to rush, surge forward, like a breaking wave): a single violent surge. Kai apethanon en tois hydasi — and died in the waters.
8.33: hoi de boskontes ephygon kai apelthontes eis tēn polin — the herdsmen fled and going into the city — ephygon aorist of pheugō (to flee): a flight of fear at what they witnessed. Apēngeilan panta kai ta tōn daimonizomenōn — they reported everything, including the things concerning the demon-possessed men — the two narrative threads (the pigs and the men) are both reported; the city learns of both.
8.34: kai idou pasa hē polis exēlthen eis hypantēsin tō Iēsou — and behold the whole city came out to meet Jesus — exēlthen aorist: a single collective movement out of the city toward him. Kai idontes auton parekalesan hopōs metabē apo tōn horiōn autōn — and seeing him they implored (parakaleō again) that he would depart from their region — the same parakaleō used by the centurion (v.5) and the demons (v.31) now used by the townspeople. The community chooses to lose Jesus rather than the economic disruption and social upheaval his presence brings. The irony: the demons beg to stay in the region; the people beg Jesus to leave it.

9.6: exousian echei ho huios tou anthropou epi tes ges aphienai hamartias — the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins — the two claims in sequence: (1) the Son of Man (Danielic title) exercises divine prerogatives, (2) epi tes ges (on earth) — this authority is exercised in history, not deferred to final judgment. The scribes' objection is theologically correct (only God can forgive sins); Jesus's claim is equally precise: the Son of Man is acting with God's authority on earth.
9.13: eleos thelo kai ou thysian — mercy I desire and not sacrifice — the Hosea citation (Hos 6:6); eleos (steadfast covenant-love, translating Hebrew hesed) is the Covenant-quality Jesus consistently prioritizes over external cultic performance. ouk elthon kalesai dikaious alla hamartoulous — I did not come to call righteous people but sinners — kaleo (to call) carries the election-language of divine summoning; the call of sinners is the unexpected reversal that defines Jesus's mission-profile.
9.1: embas eis ploion diepersen — stepping into a boat he crossed over — embas aorist participle of embainō; diepersen aorist of diaperaō (to cross over completely): a purposeful journey back. Eis tēn idian polin — to his own town: Capernaum (cf. 4:13), which Matthew treats as Jesus's base of operations. The return frames the healing of the paralytic that follows as a homecoming demonstration.
9.2: idōn tēn pistin autōn — seeing their faith — idōn aorist participle of horaō: Jesus perceives the faith not just of the paralytic but of the plural autōn (them), the group who brought him. Tharsei teknon — take heart, child (teknon, child: an affectionate address expressing personal relationship). Aphientai sou hai hamartiai — your sins are being forgiven (aphientai present passive: the forgiving is happening now, not merely declared complete). The present tense is significant: not "were forgiven" but "are forgiven in this moment."
9.3: houtoi blasphēmei — this man is blaspheming — the scribes' accusation is theologically precise: forgiveness of sins was recognized as a divine prerogative (cf. Isa 43:25; Ps 103:3); claiming to exercise it is, on their framework, either divine authority or blasphemy. The scribes do not say Jesus is wrong about the man's forgiveness; they say he has no right to announce it. En heautois — within themselves: the accusation is unspoken, a thought Jesus perceives (v.4).
9.4: gnous de ho Iēsous tas enthymēseis autōn — knowing their deliberations/thoughts (enthymēseis, from enthymeomai: deep inner deliberation, the reasoning process of the heart, not surface thought) — gnous aorist participle of ginōskō: Jesus's knowledge of their internal reasoning is direct, not inferred from facial expressions. Eis tas kardias hymōn — in your hearts: the heart (kardia) as the seat of deliberation reinforces that the scribes' error is not intellectual but volitional.
9.5: ti gar estin eukopōteron — for which is easier (eukopoteros, comparative of eukopos: easy, requiring little labor) — the question is rhetorical and exposes the asymmetry: saying "your sins are forgiven" is unverifiable (no physical change occurs); saying "get up and walk" is immediately verifiable. If Jesus can do the harder (visible) thing, it demonstrates the authority behind the easier-to-say (invisible) thing. The miracles are epistemological evidence.
9.7: kai ēgerthē — and he arose (egeirō aorist passive: to be raised up, the same verb used for resurrection; the healing terminology anticipates resurrection-language throughout Matthew) — apēlthen eis ton oikon autou (went to his house): the brevity of the report is striking; Matthew does not narrate the mechanics of the healing but the completed result. The aorist marks the action as complete and decisive.
9.8: idontes de hoi ochloi ephobēthēsan — seeing this, the crowds were afraid (phobeō aorist passive: a sudden awe-filled fear) — edoxasan ton theon (glorified God): the fear produces doxology, not paralysis. Ton donta exousian toiautēn tois anthrōpois — the one who gave such authority to human beings: the crowd's conclusion is partial; they see "authority given to human beings" but do not yet perceive the divine identity of the one exercising it. The theology of the crowds remains incomplete.
9.9: Maththaion legomenon kathēmenon epi to telōnion — a man named Matthew sitting at the tax collector's booth — telōnion (the toll-collection booth, the official station for collecting customs dues); kathēmenon present participle: he was seated, engaged in his work, when the call came. Akolouthei moi — follow me (present imperative: begin following and continue). Anastas ēkolouthēsen autō — rising he followed him — anastas aorist participle (having risen): the immediacy of response echoes 4:20,22 (the fishermen disciples).
9.10: anakeiomnou autou en tē oikia — while he was reclining at table in the house (anakeimai, to recline at a meal, the formal dining posture) — polloi telōnai kai hamartōloi (many tax collectors and sinners): telōnai (toll-collectors, collaborators with Herod/Rome; despised both as extortioners and as ritually unclean from constant Gentile contact) and hamartōloi (sinners, those either openly transgressing Torah or in professions that made observance impossible). Synanekeninto — were reclining together: shared table-fellowship implied social and covenantal solidarity.
9.11: dia ti meta tōn telōnōn kai hamartōlōn esthiei ho didaskalos hymōn — why does your teacher eat with tax collectors and sinners — the question is addressed to the disciples, not Jesus; the Pharisees use the social pressure of questioning subordinates rather than confronting the teacher directly. Didaskalos (teacher): the Pharisees acknowledge Jesus's role while challenging his practice. Table fellowship in Second Temple Judaism marked social and religious boundaries; eating with the unclean signaled solidarity with them.
9.12: ou chreian echousin hoi ischyontes iatrou — the strong do not have need of a physician — ischyontes present participle of ischyō (to be strong, to be well): those who are currently strong. The analogy reframes the question: a physician eating with the sick is not contaminated but is practicing medicine. Hoi kakōs echontes — those who are ill (having it badly): the idiom for illness sets up the mission-statement of v.13. The implication: the Pharisees misidentify who needs the physician and therefore misread the physician's table practice.
9.14: dia ti hēmeis kai hoi Pharisaioi nēsteuomen polla — why do we and the Pharisees fast often (nēsteuō, present: ongoing fasting practice; polla = much, often) — John's disciples and the Pharisees share a fasting practice despite their theological differences, presenting a united front of puzzlement. Hoi de mathētai sou ou nēsteuousin — but your disciples do not fast: the contrast is between communities defined by their devotional disciplines. Jesus's response offers a theology of the eschatological moment that explains the discrepancy.
9.15: mē dynasthai penthein hoi huioi tou nymphōnos — can the sons of the bridal chamber mourn (pentheō as in the second Beatitude; now applied to the disciples' non-fasting) — hoi huioi tou nymphōnos (the sons of the bridal chamber, the wedding guests who attend the groom): a Semitic idiom for those intimately associated with the bridegroom. Heōs hotou met autōn estin ho numphios — as long as the bridegroom is with them. Eleusontai de hēmerai hotan aparthē ap autōn ho numphios, kai tote nēsteusousin — but days will come when the bridegroom is taken from them (aparthē aorist passive subjunctive of apairō: taken away, the language of violent removal), and then they will fast. The saying anticipates the passion without naming it.
9.16: epiblemma rhakous agnaptou — a patch of unprocessed cloth (agnaptos, not yet fulled or shrunk: raw, undressed fabric) — the patch is the new element that is problematic, not the garment. Airei gar to plērōma autou apo tou himatiou — for the patch takes away (airō) its fullness/filling from the garment: the new patch, shrinking in the wash, pulls against the old garment, making the tear worse. The parable argues that Jesus's ministry is not a patch to be applied to existing religious structures but something categorically new requiring new containers.
9.17: oinos neos eis askous palaiois — new wine into old wineskins (askoi, wineskins made of animal hide) — old wineskins are already stretched from previous fermentation; new wine still fermenting generates pressure that will burst them. Rēgnyntai hoi askoi kai ho oinos ekchynetai kai hoi askoi apollyntai — the skins burst and the wine spills out and the skins are destroyed (apollymi, the same word for perishing/losing): double loss. Oinos neos eis askous kainous — new wine into new wineskins: kainos (new in quality, fresh) vs. neos (new in time). The new wine of the kingdom requires the new wineskins of the new covenant community.
9.18: archōn eis proselthōn prosekynei autō — one ruler came and was bowing before him (prosekynei imperfect: was continuing to bow/worship in ongoing prostration) — archōn (ruler, official): Luke 8:41 identifies him as a synagogue ruler (archisyagōgos). Hē thygatēr mou arti eteleutēsen — my daughter has just died (arti, just now; teleutaō aorist: has come to her end). Alla elthōn epithes tēn cheira sou ep autēn kai zēsetai — but come and place your hand on her and she will live: the petition presupposes a power over death that goes beyond normal healing expectation.
9.19: egertheis ho Iēsous ēkolouthei autō — Jesus, having risen, was following him — egertheis aorist participle (having risen, i.e., from table or from his position): the same verb as the paralytic's rising in v.7. Ēkolouthei imperfect: was following, in process; notably Matthew here describes Jesus using the discipleship-verb (akoloutheo) in Jesus's own action of following the ruler — a remarkable reversal of the usual direction.
9.20: gynaik haimorrousa dōdeka etē — a woman hemorrhaging for twelve years — haimorrousa present active participle of haimorroō (to flow with blood): the present marks the continuous, unceasing nature of her condition. Twelve years of hemorrhage would have rendered her perpetually unclean under Lev 15:25-27, barring her from the Temple, from marriage, and from normal social contact. Proselthōn opisthen ēpsato tou kraspedou — approaching from behind she touched the hem (kraspedon, fringe/tassel of a garment, possibly the tassels of Num 15:38-39): approaching from behind conceals her unclean status.
9.21: elegen gar en heautē — for she was saying within herself — en heautē (within herself, in her own interior): the speech is internal, not spoken aloud. Ean monon hapsōmai tou himatiou autou sōthēsomai — if I only touch his garment I will be saved/healed (sōthēsomai future passive of sōzō): the MKT renders this "healed" but sōzō is Matthew's word for eschatological salvation as well as physical restoration. The woman's faith operates in the register of the word's full semantic range: she expects rescue.
9.22: epistrapheis kai idōn autēn eipen — turning and seeing her he said — epistrapheis aorist passive participle of strephō (to turn, be turned): a deliberate turning toward her. Tharsei thygate — take heart, daughter (thygate: the same affectionate address as teknon in v.2): a personal word before the pronouncement. Hē pistis sou sesōken se — your faith has saved/healed you — sesōken perfect active: it has saved and the saving stands completed. Kai esōthē hē gynē apo tēs hōras ekeinēs — and the woman was saved/healed from that hour: aorist passive marking the moment of completion.
9.23: tous aulētas kai ton ochlon thorybоumenon — the flute players and the noisy crowd (aulētēs, flute player; thorybeomai, to be in an uproar, to create a commotion) — in Jewish custom, even the poorest families hired professional mourners and musicians at a death; the aulētai would be playing dirge-music. The presence of formal mourning-professionals establishes that the girl is understood to be genuinely dead by those present.
9.24: anacōreite ou gar apethanen to korasion alla katheudei — withdraw, for the girl has not died but is sleeping (anacōreō, withdraw; katheudō, to sleep — also used as a euphemism for death in Jewish and Christian usage) — kategelōn autou (they laughed at him — katagelō, to laugh down at, deride): the crowd's laughter is not disrespect but certainty; they know she is dead. Jesus's statement is either metaphor (the death is temporary, like sleep) or a claim that from his perspective death has this quality. Both may be simultaneously true.
9.25: ekblēthentos de tou ochlou eiselthōn ekratēsen tēs cheiros autēs — when the crowd was put outside, entering he took her hand (ekballō aorist passive: the crowd was thrown out — the same verb as exorcism; krateō aorist: grasped firmly) — kai ēgerthē to korasion (and the girl arose — ēgerthē aorist passive of egeirō, the resurrection verb): the brevity of the narrative matches the immediacy of the event. No word of command is recorded in Matthew; the touch alone suffices.
9.26: kai exēlthen hē phēmē autē eis holēn tēn gēn ekeinēn — this report went out into all that land — phēmē (report, reputation, from phēmi, to say): the fame that Jesus repeatedly tries to limit (8:4; 9:30) spreads despite his restrictions. Holēn tēn gēn ekeinēn (all that land, the surrounding region): the news cannot be contained to the house or town.
9.27: huie Dauid eleēson hēmas — Son of David, have mercy on us — the blind men use the Davidic Messiah title (huios Dauid), which carries the expectation of the Messianic heir who would heal and restore Israel (Isa 29:18; 35:5). This is Matthew's first significant use of the title since the genealogy (1:1); it will reappear at 12:23; 15:22; 20:30-31; 21:9,15. Eleēson aorist imperative of eleō: grant mercy, show covenant loyalty.
9.28: pisteute hoti dynasthai touto poiēsai — do you believe that I am able to do this (pisteuo present: are you maintaining this belief?) — Jesus asks the question privately, indoors, after the public pursuit. Dynasthai (to be able): the question targets the object of their faith — not whether they will be healed, but whether Jesus specifically has the power. Nai kyrie — yes, Lord: kyrie (Lord) is their address, matching the centurion and the storm-disciples; the blind recognize what the crowds did not.
9.29: kata tēn pistin hymōn genēthētō hymin — according to your faith let it be done to you — kata (according to, in proportion to): the healing corresponds to the faith, not as a reward for it but as its completion. Genēthētō aorist passive imperative of ginomai: let it happen, let it come to be. The statement is remarkable: faith is the measure by which the miracle is sized. This principle recurs in 8:13 (the centurion: "as you believed") and 15:28 (the Canaanite woman: "as you desire").
9.30: enebrimēthē autois ho Iēsous — Jesus sternly warned them (embrimaomai: to snort with emotion, to charge sternly, to rebuke with intensity — the word is used for strong emotional reaction, including indignation; Mark uses it for Jesus's reaction before raising Lazarus, John 11:33) — horate mēdeis ginōsketō (see that no one knows: present imperative + subjunctive: an ongoing prohibition). The severity of the warning underlines the messianic secrecy motif: Jesus is managing the pace and nature of disclosure about his identity.
9.31: hoi de exelthontes diephēmisan auton en holē tē gē ekeinē — but going out they spread his fame throughout all that land — diaphēmizō (to spread a report widely, to publicize): the disobedience to the command of v.30 mirrors the spreading fame after the ruler's daughter (v.26). The inability to suppress news of miraculous healing is a structural theme in Matthew's miracle chain (ch8-9): the disciples leak the news despite the charge.
9.32: kōphon daimonizomenon — a mute/deaf demon-possessed man (kōphos: the word covers both deafness and muteness; context — the man not speaking — suggests muteness here) — the case is brought to Jesus by others, as in 8:16. This is the fourth major miracle in the sequence of ch9 (paralytic, hemorrhaging woman/Jairus's daughter, blind men, mute demoniac), each addressing a different incapacity that Second Temple Judaism associated with the messianic age.
9.33: ekblēthentos de tou daimoniou elalēsen ho kōphos — when the demon was driven out the mute man spoke — the exorcism and the cure are simultaneous: the demonic cause removed, the human capacity restored. Ethaumasan hoi ochloi legontes oudepote ephanthē houtōs en tō Israēl — the crowds marveled saying: never has it appeared thus in Israel (phainō aorist passive: never has it been seen; oudepote as in 7:23: never at any time). The cumulative effect of the miracle-chain provokes a climactic statement of unprecedented occurrence.
9.34: hoi de Pharisaioi elegon en tō archonti tōn daimoniōn ekballei ta daimonia — but the Pharisees were saying: by the ruler of demons he drives out demons — elegon imperfect: they kept saying, making this a sustained accusation. Ho archōn tōn daimoniōn (the ruler of the demons): the claim that exorcistic power comes from demonic source rather than divine; Jesus addresses this explicitly in 12:25-29. The crowd's amazement (v.33) and the Pharisees' accusation (v.34) divide the audience into two responses that will persist throughout the Gospel.
9.35: periēgen ho Iēsous tas poleis pasas kai tas kōmas — Jesus went around all the towns and villages (periagō imperfect: was continuously going about) — the three present participles (didaskōn, kērysson, therapeuōn: teaching, proclaiming, healing) describe the ongoing character of his itinerant ministry. Kēryssōn to euangelion tēs basileias — proclaiming the gospel of the kingdom: euangelion (good news, the announcement of victorious news by a herald) + tēs basileias (of the kingdom). The summary verse (cf. 4:23) uses the same triadic description of Jesus's ministry.
9.36: esplanchnisthē peri autōn — he had compassion on them (splagchnizomai, from splanchnon/intestines, the visceral seat of emotion; the strongest Greek word for compassion, meaning gut-level, visceral feeling for another's suffering) — eskylmenoi kai errimmenoi (harassed and helpless/cast down: skyllomai, to be harrassed, worn out; rhiptō perfect passive: thrown about, cast down like discarded things). Hōsei probata mē echonta poimena — like sheep not having a shepherd: echoing Num 27:17; 1 Kgs 22:17; Ezek 34:5 — the image of Israel without faithful shepherding.
9.37: ho men therismos polys hoi de ergatai oligoi — the harvest is plentiful but the workers are few — therismos (harvest) shifts the metaphor from sheep/shepherd (v.36) to harvest: the compassion-move produces a harvest-assessment. Polys/oligoi (plentiful/few): the asymmetry between need and available workers is the premise for the prayer of v.38. Harvest in Jewish eschatological imagery (Joel 3:13; Isa 27:12) carried end-time associations of gathering the nations.
9.38: deēthēte oun tou kyriou tou therismou — therefore pray/beseech the Lord of the harvest (deomai aorist passive imperative: to beseech, petition urgently) — tou kyriou tou therismou (the Lord of the harvest): God is the owner of the harvest field and the ultimate agent of mission; workers are sent by him, not self-commissioned. Hopōs ekbalē ergas eis ton therismon autou — that he might send out workers into his harvest (ekballō aorist subjunctive: to throw out, drive out, send out forcefully — the same verb for exorcism; mission is as urgent as exorcism). The prayer becomes the transition into the sending of the twelve in ch10.

10.28: me phobeisthe apo ton apokteinonton to soma — do not fear those who kill the body — the first me phobos is addressed to the fear of persecutors; the second phobeisthe de mallon (rather fear) addresses the appropriate fear of God. The contrast is not between two fears of the same kind but between the fear that produces obedience to God and the paralysis produced by fear of humans. ton de psychen me dynamenon apokteinai — those who cannot kill the soul — soul (psyche) as the seat of personal existence that physical death does not terminate.
10.1: kai proskalesamenos tous dōdeka mathētas — calling to himself the twelve disciples (proskaleomai aorist middle: summoning to oneself; the middle voice emphasizes the personal initiative of Jesus) — edōken autois exousian (gave them authority — exousia, the same word as 7:29; 9:6,8): the twelve receive delegated authority, not independent power. Pneumatōn akathartōn (over unclean spirits) and pasan noson kai pasan malakian (every disease and every weakness): the authority given mirrors Jesus's own practice in 9:35.
10.2: tōn de dōdeka apostolōn ta onomata — the names of the twelve apostles (apostolos, sent one, from apostellō, to send off with a commission) — this is Matthew's first and only explicit use of the title apostolos for the twelve (cf. Mark 6:30; Luke uses it frequently). The title defines them by their function: they are sent ones, commissioned agents. The list begins with prōtos Simōn ho legomenos Petros (first, Simon who is called Peter) — prōtos marks Peter's precedence in the list, not necessarily in rank.
10.3: Philippos kai Bartholomaios — the names continue; Maththaios ho telōnēs — Matthew the tax collector: uniquely in Matthew's list, Matthew is identified by his former occupation (the other Gospels simply list his name), a mark of the self-identification of a Gospel written by the same person who was called in 9:9. The profession is both a confession and a commendation of grace: a tax collector among the sent ones.
10.4: Simōn ho Kananaios kai Ioudas ho Iskariōtēs — Simon the Zealot (Kananaios from Aramaic qan'an, zealous, probably referring to the Zealot movement or simply to intense piety) and Judas Iscariot — ho kai paradous auton (the one who also betrayed him): Judas is introduced with the shadow of betrayal already attached. The list ends where the passion narrative will end. Iskariōtēs: possibly "man of Kerioth" (a Judean town) or from Aramaic seqarya (assassin), the etymology uncertain.
10.5: eis hodon ethnōn mē apelthēte kai polin Samaritōn mē eiselthēte — do not go on a road of Gentiles and do not enter a Samaritan city — the restriction is geographical but carries covenantal significance: the first mission is concentrated on Israel, not as an ethnic preference but as the covenant-priority of fulfilling the promises to the lost sheep of the house of Israel (v.6) before the universal mission (28:19). The restriction is temporary; it is lifted at the resurrection.
10.6: ta probata ta apolōlota oikou Israēl — the lost sheep of the house of Israel (apolōlota perfect passive participle of apollymi: the sheep that have been lost, currently in the state of lostness) — the same compassion-image as 9:36 (sheep without a shepherd) but with the perfect tense adding the weight of completed lostness: they are not merely wandering but definitively lost. The phrase echoes Ezek 34's indictment of Israel's shepherds who let the flock be scattered.
10.7: ēggiken hē basileia tōn ouranōn — the kingdom of the heavens has drawn near (ēggiken perfect of engizō: has come near and remains near, the perfect combining completed approach with present proximity) — the identical proclamation as John the Baptist (3:2) and Jesus himself (4:17): the twelve proclaim the same message as their sender. Poreuomenoi de kēryssete legontes — going, proclaim saying: the proclamation accompanies movement, not a fixed location.
10.8: astheountas therapeute, nekrous egeirete, leprous katharizete, daimonia ekballete — heal the sick, raise the dead, cleanse lepers, drive out demons — four aorist imperatives of equal weight; the raising of the dead is listed without emphasis as one item in the series. Dōrean elabete dōrean dote — freely (dōrean, as a gift, gratis, without price: the adverbial accusative of dōron) you received; freely give: the disciples received their authority and gifts without payment to Jesus; they are to extend the same terms. The sentence forbids the commercialization of the miraculous.
10.9: mē ktēsēsthe chryson mēde argyron mēde chalkon eis tas zōnas hymōn — do not acquire (ktaomai aorist middle subjunctive: to acquire for oneself, to make one's own possession) gold or silver or copper in your belts — the descending order (gold → silver → copper) covers all monetary value. The belt (zōnē) was used as a money-belt in ancient travel. The prohibition is not against having money but against making the mission financially self-providing; dependence on the generosity of those receiving the gospel was itself part of the proclamation.
10.10: mē pēran eis hodon mēde dyo chitōnas mēde hypodēmata mēde rhabdon — no travel bag for the road nor two tunics nor sandals nor a staff — the list enumerates the standard equipment of an ancient traveler; the disciples are sent without the normal self-sufficiency of travel. Axios gar ho ergatēs tēs trophēs autou — for the worker is worthy of his food (axios, worthy, deserving; trophē, nourishment): the theological principle underlying the prohibition. The community receiving the proclamation owes provision to those who proclaim.
10.11: exetasate tis en autē axios estin — inquire who in it is worthy (exetazō: to examine thoroughly, investigate; not a casual inquiry but a deliberate assessment of receptivity and hospitality) — axios (worthy): the same word as 10:10; the worthiness of the worker and the worthiness of the host are parallel. Kakeikei meinate heōs an exelthēte — and there remain until you leave: the instruction against house-hopping preserves the integrity of the mission by not treating hospitality as a ladder of social advancement.
10.12: aspasasthe autēn — greet it (the household, using aspasasthe aorist imperative of aspazomai: a formal, extended greeting — not a casual wave but the covenant-greeting of shalom) — in Jewish practice, a spoken blessing upon entering a house was a form of pronouncing peace. The greeting carries theological weight: peace (eirēnē) is not just courtesy but a blessing that may or may not "rest" on the household depending on its receptivity (v.13).
10.13: ean men ē hē oikia axia elthatō hē eirēnē hymōn ep autēn — if the house is worthy, let your peace come upon it (elthatō aorist imperative: let it come) — ean de mē ē axia hē eirēnē hymōn pros hymas epistraphētō (if it is not worthy, let your peace return to you — epistraphētō aorist passive imperative of strephō): peace is not lost when rejected; it returns to the one who offered it. The agents of peace are not diminished by its rejection, but the household forfeits what was offered.
10.14: ektinaxtate ton koniorton tōn podōn hymōn — shake the dust off your feet (ektinassō aorist imperative: to shake out thoroughly) — a gesture of dissociation: by shaking off the town's dust, the disciples symbolically return the town's guilt to itself. In Jewish practice, Jews shook the dust of Gentile lands from their feet when re-entering Israel; here the gesture is applied to Israelite towns that reject the kingdom-proclamation, treating their refusal as equivalent to Gentile rejection.
10.15: anektoteron estai Sodomois kai Gomorrois en hēmera kriseōs — it will be more bearable for Sodom and Gomorrah on the day of judgment (anektoteros, comparative of anektos: bearable, tolerable) — the comparative establishes gradations of judgment: Sodom and Gomorrah, destroyed for notorious wickedness, face a lighter judgment than the towns of Israel that reject the kingdom-messenger. The logic: responsibility scales with proximity to the gospel; greater revelation produces greater accountability.
10.16: idou egō apostellō hymas hōs probata en mesō lykōn — behold I send you as sheep in the midst of wolves — apostellō (to send on a commission, the verb from which apostolos comes): the sending is Jesus's act. Phronimoi hōs hoi opheis kai akeraioi hōs hai peristerai — shrewd as snakes and innocent as doves: phronimos (practically wise, discerning — the same word as the wise builder in 7:24) and akeraios (unmixed, pure, without adulteration: used of unalloyed metal and pure wine). The combination is not cunning-plus-innocence but wisdom-that-does-not-compromise.
10.17: paradōsousin hymas eis synedria — they will hand you over to councils (synedria, plural: local councils with judicial authority, smaller than the Jerusalem Sanhedrin) — kai en tais synagōgais autōn mastigōsousin hymas (and in their synagogues they will flog you: mastigoō, to flog with a whip, the formal punishment of the synagogue court — up to 39 lashes according to Deut 25:3 as interpreted in the Mishnah). The persecution comes from within the covenant community, not only from outsiders.
10.18: kai epi hēgemonas de kai basileis achthēsesthe heneken emou — you will be brought before governors and kings because of me (hēgemōn, governor/prefect like Pilate; basileus, king like Herod) — heneken emou (because of me, on account of me): the persecution is specifically due to association with Jesus, not incidental political trouble. Eis martyrion autois kai tois ethnesin — as a testimony to them and to the Gentiles: the hostile tribunal becomes a platform for witness; suffering produces proclamation.
10.19: mē merimnēsēte pōs ē ti lalēsēte — do not be anxious how or what you will speak (merimnēsēte aorist subjunctive: do not take up this anxiety) — the double question (pōs, how; ti, what) covers both manner and content of speech under pressure. Dothēsetai gar hymin en ekeinē tē hōra ti lalēsēte — for it will be given to you in that hour what to say (dothēsetai future passive divine): the provision is eschatological-moment specific, not a general license to avoid preparation.
10.20: ou gar hymeis este hoi lalountes alla to pneuma tou patros hymōn — for it is not you who speak but the Spirit of your Father (pneuma tou patros hymōn, the Spirit of your Father — Matthew's distinctive way of identifying the Spirit, here as the Spirit who belongs to and comes from the Father) — the speaking is genuinely human speech through a genuinely divine agency; both the human subject (hymeis) and the divine subject (to pneuma) are named, not one replacing the other. To laloun en hymin — speaking in/through you.
10.21: paradōsei de adelphos adelphon eis thanaton kai patēr teknon — brother will betray brother to death and a father his child — paradōsei future of paradidōmi (to hand over, deliver up — the same verb used of Judas betraying Jesus; 10:4; 26:16,21): the vocabulary of betrayal applied to family relationships. Epanastēsontai tekna epi goneis — children will rise up against parents (epanistēmi, to rise up against, revolt): the language of insurrection applied to the family structure. This Micah 7:6 echo (cf. v.35-36) anticipates the family-division saying.
10.22: kai esesthe misoumenoi hypo pantōn dia to onoma mou — you will be hated by all on account of my name (misoumenoi present passive participle: ongoing, continuous hatred) — dia to onoma mou (on account of my name): the hatred is not incidental but specifically motivated by association with Jesus. Ho de hypomoinas eis telos sōthēsetai — the one enduring to the end will be saved (hypomeno, to remain under, endure under pressure — not passive resignation but active continuance under weight). Eis telos (to the end): the full duration, wherever the end comes.
10.23: hotan de diōkōsin hymas en tē polei tautē, pheugete eis tēn heteran — when they persecute you in this city, flee to another (pheugete present imperative: continue fleeing as a practice) — ou mē telesēte tas poleis tou Israēl heōs an elthē ho huios tou anthrōpou — you will not finish the cities of Israel before the Son of Man comes (telesēte aorist subjunctive of teleō, to complete; the double negative ou mē for emphatic futurity). The saying's precise reference to "the coming of the Son of Man" is debated (the resurrection, Pentecost, the destruction of Jerusalem in 70 CE, or the parousia); the urgency is unambiguous.
10.24: ouk estin mathētēs hyper ton didaskalon — a student is not above the teacher — the proverb establishes the basic order: disciple/teacher, servant/master. Hyper (above, beyond): the comparative locates the disciple permanently below the teacher in standing. The saying grounds the expectation of persecution: if the Teacher was opposed, the disciple should expect no better treatment. The logic is not fatalistic but orienting.
10.25: arketon tō mathētē hina genētai hōs ho didaskalos autou — it is enough for the student to become like his teacher (arketon, sufficient, from arkeō: the same word as 6:34 for the day's sufficient trouble) — ei ton oikodespotēn Beelzeboul ekalesian — if they called the master of the house Beelzebul (Beelzeboul: probably from Hebrew Ba'al Zebul, "lord of the exalted house," though the LXX's Baalzebub = "lord of the flies" is also a possible background): they have already called Jesus a demonic ally (9:34; 12:24); the household-members face the same charge.
10.26: mē oun phobetheite autous — therefore do not fear them (phobeō aorist passive subjunctive: do not be made to fear) — the prohibition rests on the certainty of disclosure: ouden gar estin kekalymmenon ho ouk apokalypsthesetai (nothing is covered that will not be uncovered — apokalyptō future passive divine: God will uncover). Kai krupton ho ou gnōsthēsetai — and nothing hidden that will not be known: what is done and said in secret, including the disciples' private instruction, will be made public in God's time.
10.27: ho legō hymin en tē skotia eipate en tō phōti — what I tell you in the darkness, speak in the light — en tē skotia (in the darkness): private, intimate instruction. En tō phōti (in the light): public proclamation. The disciples receive teaching in the private darkness of discipleship-with-Jesus and are to broadcast it publicly. Kai ho eis to ous akouete kērysate epi tōn dōmatōn — and what you hear whispered in the ear, proclaim from the rooftops: from intimate whisper to rooftop announcement — the instruction moves outward and upward in scale.
