10.29: ouchi dyo strouthia assariou pōleitai — are not two sparrows sold for an assarion (assarion, the smallest Roman copper coin, worth about 1/16 of a denarius) — the sparrow (strouthion) is the cheapest, most disposable bird in the marketplace. Kai hen ex autōn ou peseitai epi tēn gēn aneu tou patros hymōn — and not one of them will fall to the ground apart from (aneu) your Father: aneu (without, apart from) — not "without the Father's permission" but without the Father's presence and knowledge. Divine providence extends to the individual fall of the most commercially negligible creature.
10.30: hymon de kai hai triches tēs kephalēs pasai ērithmēmenai eisin — even the hairs of your head are all numbered (ērithmēmenai eisin perfect passive periphrastic: stand numbered, remain in a numbered state) — the perfect participle marks the numbering as a completed and ongoing state, not a one-time count; God's knowledge of the disciples is current and complete. The contrast with the sparrow (which is commercially counted at two per assarion) is that the disciples are not priced at all by weight of hair but individually known to the Father.
10.31: mē oun phobeisthe pollōn strouthiōn diapherete hymeis — do not fear therefore; you differ in value from many sparrows (diapherō, to carry through, to differ in worth; used for surpassing value as in 6:26) — the argument is explicit: if the Father's providential knowledge extends to sparrows, which are worth less than the disciples, it certainly extends to the disciples. The prohibition on fear (mē phobeisthe) is grounded in the asymmetric valuation: fear is irrational given the Father's specific knowledge of each disciple.
10.32: pas oun hostis homologēsei en emoi emprosthen tōn anthrōpōn — everyone who acknowledges me before people (homologeō, to speak the same thing, to confess, to acknowledge openly: the public matching of one's speech to the reality of Jesus) — en emoi (in me, using the instrumental en): to confess in Christ is not merely to speak about him but to speak from within the relationship. Homologēsō kagō en autō emprosthen tou patros mou — I will also acknowledge him before my Father: the correspondence is exact — human public acknowledgment answered by Jesus's acknowledgment before the Father.
10.33: hostis d'an arnēsētai me emprosthen tōn anthrōpōn — whoever denies me before people (arneomai: to deny, disown, say no to — the same verb Peter will use in 26:70,72,74) — arnēsomai kagō auton emprosthen tou patros mou (I will also deny him before my Father): the symmetry of vv.32-33 is stark: public acknowledgment → acknowledged by Jesus; public denial → denied by Jesus. The judgment is not punitive addition but the logical extension of the relationship one has chosen to deny.
10.34: mē nomisēte hoti ēlthon balein eirēnēn epi tēn gēn — do not think that I came to throw peace on the earth (balein, to throw, cast — an active, energetic verb: not "to bring peace" gently but to throw it; nomisēte, aorist subjunctive of nomizō, to suppose, think as a matter of convention) — ouk ēlthon balein eirēnēn alla machairan (I came not to throw peace but a sword — machaira, the short single-bladed cutting sword used in daily life and execution). The sword is not Jesus's goal but the effect of his presence: the truth he brings forces decisions that divide.
10.35: dichazō — to divide, set at variance (ēlthon dichazai anthrōpon kata tou patros autou, I came to set a man against his father) — the citation of Mic 7:6 frames the family-division as something declared in Scripture, not a new social disruption invented by Jesus. Kai thygatera kata tēs mētros autēs (daughter against her mother) kai nymphēn kata tēs pentheras autēs (daughter-in-law against mother-in-law): the three pairings are intergenerational, covering the household structure.
10.36: kai echthroi tou anthrōpou hoi oikiakoi autou — a person's enemies are the members of his household (oikiakoi, household members, those of the same house) — the direct quotation of Mic 7:6 LXX. The verse identifies the source of the opposition named in vv.21-22 (brother against brother, children against parents): those who oppose the disciple because of Jesus are closest in relationship. This is not a sociological observation but an eschatological pressure: the kingdom's arrival produces judgment-level divisions within families.
10.37: ho philōn patera ē mētera hyper eme ouk estin mou axios — whoever loves (phileō, to love by natural affection) father or mother more than me is not worthy of me (axios) — phileō is the word for natural, instinctive family love; the verse does not prohibit love of family but addresses priority when the two loyalties conflict. Hyper eme (more than me, beyond me): the comparison is explicit. The same structure governs the son/daughter clause: ho philōn huion ē thygatera hyper eme ouk estin mou axios.
10.38: kai hos ou lambanei ton stauron autou kai akolouthei opisō mou — whoever does not take up his cross and follow after me (lambanei ton stauron, take the cross — lambanō present: ongoing taking up) — the cross as the instrument of execution by crucifixion: a shocking image spoken before the passion narrative, when a person carrying their cross was a condemned person on their way to execution. Opisō mou (after me, behind me): the disciple follows in the direction Jesus has walked, including toward the cross. Ouk estin mou axios — is not worthy of me.
10.39: ho heurōn tēn psychēn autou apolesei autēn — the one who finds his life will lose it (heurōn aorist participle; apolesei future: will destroy/lose it — apollymi, the word for final ruin as well as ordinary losing) — kai ho apolesas tēn psychēn autou heneken emou heurēsei autēn (and whoever loses his life for my sake will find it): the paradox of values operates on psychē (life, soul), a word spanning physical life, personal identity, and the whole self. The one who protects self-life loses it; the one who surrenders it for Jesus finds the real thing.
10.40: ho dechomenos hymas eme dechetai — the one who receives you receives me (dechomai: to receive, welcome as a guest) — the chain of identification: receiving the disciple = receiving Jesus = receiving the one who sent Jesus (ton aposteilanta me, the one who sent me: God). The representative logic makes the disciples's reception or rejection a reception or rejection of Jesus himself, and through him of the Father. The same chain appears in reverse in v.33 (denying the disciple ↔ denying Jesus).
10.41: ho dechomenos prophētēn eis onoma prophētou misthon prophētou lēmpsetai — whoever receives a prophet in the name of a prophet will receive a prophet's reward (eis onoma prophētou, in the name of a prophet: receiving someone specifically because they are a prophet, in recognition of their prophetic identity) — misthon prophētou (a prophet's reward): the same misthos (wage, reward owed) as in 5:12 and 6:1-18. Receiving the prophet because he is a prophet associates the receiver with the prophet's mission and its reward.
10.42: potērion psychrou hydatos monon — a cup of cold water only (psychros, cold — cold water is more costly in desert climates, unlike warm water which required no effort to keep warm) — tōn mikrōn toutōn (of these little ones — mikros, small, little: the disciples viewed from the world's perspective are small; the diminutive is not derogatory but realistic about their social standing). Ou mē apolese ton misthon autou — will certainly not lose his reward (ou mē + aorist subjunctive: the strongest possible negation of futurity). Even the smallest act of hospitality toward the disciples of Jesus has covenantal weight.

11.28: deute pros me pantes hoi kopionte kai pephortimenoi — come to me all who labor and are heavy laden — kopionto (laboring to exhaustion) + pephortimenoi (perfect passive, permanently burdened) describes the condition; deute pros me (come to me) is Wisdom's invitation. anapausis — rest, refreshment — not idleness but the rest of the Sabbath: cessation from the burden of self-justifying religious effort. chrestos (v.30) — good, pleasant, kind — the same word used for good wine that has aged well (Luke 5:39) and for the kindness of God (Rom 2:4); the yoke of Christ has the quality of what has been made right.
11.1: kai egeneto hote etelesen ho Iēsous — and it happened when Jesus had finished (etelesen aorist of teleō: to complete, bring to an end) — this formula closes each of Matthew's five major discourse blocks (7:28; 11:1; 13:53; 19:1; 26:1). Metebē ekeithen tou didaskein kai kēryssein en tais polesin autōn — he departed from there to teach and proclaim in their cities: the discourse on mission (ch10) leads directly to missionary practice.
11.2: ho de Iōannēs akousas en tō desmōtēriō ta erga tou Christou — John, hearing in prison the works of the Messiah (ta erga tou Christou: the deeds of the Christ — Matthew uses Christou here without the article, as a title) — desmōtērion (prison, place of binding): John is imprisoned by Herod Antipas (14:3), setting up the contrast between his confinement and the liberation Jesus is enacting through the miracle-list of v.5.
11.3: su ei ho erchomenos ē heteron prosdokōmen — are you the coming one or should we expect another (ho erchomenos, the coming one — a technical title for the eschatological deliverer, rooted in Ps 118:26; Mal 3:1) — the question is not doubt but identification: John wants to know whether Jesus's ministry matches the coming one he proclaimed (3:11). Prosdokōmen present subjunctive: are we to keep waiting/expecting.
11.4: poreuentes apangeilate Iōannē ha akouete kai blepete — go and report to John what you hear and see — the format echoes the prophetic pattern of Isa 29:18-19; 35:5-6; 61:1, where the messianic deliverer is identified by what he does rather than what he claims. The answer is exegetically dense: it presupposes John knows the prophetic texts and will recognize the list as the messianic inventory.
11.5: typhloi anablepousin kai chōloi peripatousin — the blind receive sight and the lame walk — the six-item list (blind, lame, lepers, deaf, dead, poor) maps precisely onto Isa 35:5-6 (blind/deaf/lame) + Isa 61:1 (poor have good news proclaimed). Nekroi egeirontai (dead are raised): inserted between the deaf and the poor, surpassing both Isaiah sources. Ptōchoi euangelizontai — the poor have good news proclaimed (euangelizō passive: the same verb as Isa 61:1 LXX; the proclamation is itself the messianic act).
11.6: makarios estin hos ean mē skandalisthē en emoi — blessed is whoever does not stumble on account of me (skandalisthē aorist passive subjunctive of skandalizō, the passive: "not be caused to stumble") — the Beatitude form applied not to a virtue but to a response to Jesus himself: the potential stumbling-block is his identity. The statement implicitly acknowledges that some do find him a stumbling-block — the response to his deeds divides (cf. Isa 8:14; 1 Pet 2:8).
11.7: ti exēlthate eis tēn erēmon theasasthai — what did you go out into the wilderness to see (theasasthai aorist infinitive of theaomai, to gaze at, observe as a spectacle — a stronger, more deliberate looking than blepō) — kalamon hypo anemou saleuomenon (a reed being shaken by the wind): the question-and-dismissal format of vv.7-9 is rhetorical, eliminating two wrong answers before arriving at the right one. A wind-blown reed is a figure of fickleness; the crowd did not go out for something trivially unstable.
11.8: anthrōpon en malakois hēmphiesmenon — a man clothed in soft/delicate garments (malakos, soft — used of clothing to mean fine, luxurious; used of persons to mean morally soft) — idou hoi ta malaka phorountes en tois oikois tōn basileōn eisin (those wearing soft things are in the houses of kings): John's clothing (camel hair, leather belt — 3:4) is the antithesis of malaka. The contrast locates John in the prophetic tradition, not the royal court.
11.9: alla ti exēlthate idein, prophētēn — but what did you go out to see, a prophet? — nai legō hymin kai perissoteron prophētou (yes I tell you and more than a prophet: perissoteron comparative of perissos, exceeding): the double affirmation acknowledges John as prophet and then immediately surpasses the category. What makes John more than a prophet is revealed in v.10 — he is the messenger long expected.
11.10: houtos estin peri hou gegraptai — this is the one about whom it is written (gegraptai perfect passive: it stands written, the perfect marking enduring scriptural authority) — the citation is a composite of Exod 23:20 LXX (idou apostellō ton angelon mou) and Mal 3:1 (pros kataskeuasai tēn hodon sou emprosthen sou). Matthew shifts Malachi's "before me" to "before you," applying the divine messenger-sending directly to Jesus. The combination identifies John as both the Exodus wilderness-guide and the Malachi forerunner.
11.11: en gennētois gynaikōn — among those born of women (gennētos gynaikōn, a Semitic idiom for every human being, emphasizing creaturely humanity; cf. Job 14:1) — meizōn Iōannou tou baptistou oudeis egēgertai (no one greater has arisen — egēgertai perfect passive: has been raised up, by divine agency). Ho de mikroteros en tē basileia tōn ouranōn meizōn autou estin — the lesser in the kingdom of the heavens is greater than he: mikroteros comparative; the kingdom establishes a different greatness-scale than birth-among-women.
11.12: hē basileia tōn ouranōn biazetai — the kingdom of the heavens is subjected to violence / forces its way / presses forward (biazetai: middle or passive of biazō, disputed; middle = "is advancing forcefully"; passive = "is being violated/seized") — kai biastai harpazousin autēn (and violent people are seizing it — harpazō, to seize, snatch): the verse is among Matthew's most debated. The range runs from "the kingdom forcefully advances and the forceful lay hold of it" (positive reading) to "the kingdom suffers violence and violent men assault it" (negative, applied to Herod/opposition to John/Jesus).
11.13: pantes gar hoi prophētai kai ho nomos heōs Iōannou eprophēteusan — for all the Prophets and the Law prophesied until John — the unusual order (Prophets before Law) may be deliberate emphasis; the standard phrase was "the Law and the Prophets" (5:17; 7:12). Heōs Iōannou (until John): John marks the terminus of the prophetic age. This supports v.10 — John is not just a prophet but the endpoint of prophetic anticipation, beyond which the thing anticipated has arrived.
11.14: kai ei thelete dexasthai — and if you are willing to receive it (thelete present: if you are presently willing — a conditional on receptivity) — autos estin Ēlias ho mellōn erchesthai (he himself is Elijah who is about to come). The identification is conditional: only for those willing to receive it. John does not share Elijah's manner but fills the Elijah function: a voice calling Israel to repentance before the day of the LORD (Mal 4:5).
11.15: ho echōn ōta akouetō — the one having ears, let them hear — a formulaic call to attention (akouetō present imperative: begin/continue hearing), used in Matthew after weighty or paradoxical sayings (13:9,43). Its placement after the Elijah identification implies that this identification is hidden to casual listening but available to those who press into the implications of the prophetic texts.
11.16: tini de homoiōsō tēn genean tautēn — to what shall I compare this generation (homoiōsō aorist subjunctive: the deliberative use) — hē genea autē (this generation): the term carries a moral/spiritual load in Matthew; it is the generation characterized by resistance to the kingdom. The comparison deploys a children's game parable: the generation refuses every mode of engagement — neither the celebratory invitation nor the penitential one.
11.17: ēulēsamen hymin kai ouk ōrchēsasthe — we played the pipe for you and you did not dance — ēulēsamen aorist of auleō (to play the flute, a wedding/celebration instrument); ōrchēsasthe aorist of orcheomai (to dance): the wedding scenario. Ethrēnēsamen kai ouk ekopsasthe (we sang a dirge and you did not mourn — thrēneō, to sing a funeral lament; koptomai, to beat one's breast in mourning): the funeral scenario. Both modes are refused. The parable is about the generation's refusal to engage with either John or Jesus on any terms.
11.18: ēlthen gar Iōannēs mēte esthiōn mēte pinōn — for John came neither eating nor drinking — the double mēte...mēte marks absolute abstinence: John's ascetic practice (3:4; 9:14) was extreme even by the standards of fasting disciples. Kai legousin daimonion echei (and they say: he has a demon): the accusation against John takes the form of a demonization of his abstinence — his refusal of normal social-food is explained as demonic possession rather than prophetic calling.
11.19: ēlthen ho huios tou anthrōpou esthiōn kai pinōn — the Son of Man came eating and drinking — the contrast with John (v.18) is total: Jesus participates in normal meals and social eating. Idou anthrōpos phagos kai oinopotēs (Look, a glutton and a wine-drinker — phagos, glutton; oinopotēs, wine-drinker/drunkard): the slanders are the inverse of John's charge. Edikaioōthē hē sophia apo tōn ergōn autēs — wisdom was justified/vindicated by her deeds (edikaioōthē aorist passive: stands justified; Luke has "by her children"): the deeds of Jesus and John together are wisdom's self-vindication.
11.20: tote ērxato oneidizein tas poleis — then he began to denounce the cities (oneidizō, to reproach, upbraid — a strong word for public condemnation; used of mocking Jesus in 27:44) — en hais egenonto hai pleistai dynameis autou (in which most of his miracles had happened — dynameis, powers, mighty works): the denunciation is grounded in the specific history of miraculous witness. Greater exposure produces greater accountability; the woes follow the logic of vv.21-24.
11.21: ouai soi, Chorazin, ouai soi, Bēthsaida — woe to you, Chorazin; woe to you, Bethsaida — ouai (woe) in prophetic discourse signals divine grief-and-judgment (cf. Isa 5:8-22). Chorazin and Bethsaida are not mentioned elsewhere as miracle-sites in Matthew, suggesting a wider tradition beyond what the Gospels record. Tyrō kai Sidōni (Tyre and Sidon): notorious Gentile cities condemned by the prophets (Isa 23; Ezek 26-28). That they would have repented in sackcloth and ashes (sakkō kai spodō, formal mourning posture) makes the Galilean failure more acute.
11.22: plēn legō hymin Tyrō kai Sidōni anektoteron estai en hēmera kriseōs ē hymin — but I tell you, for Tyre and Sidon it will be more tolerable on the day of judgment than for you — plēn (but, however: a strong adversative); anektoteron (more bearable/tolerable: comparative, establishing a graduated scale of judgment). The judgment on Galilee is not the absence of judgment but its heightened form: having received more, more is required (cf. Luke 12:48).
11.23: kai sy, Kapharnaoun, mē heōs ouranou hypsōthēsē — and you, Capernaum, will you be lifted to the heavens? (hypsōthēsē future passive of hypsoō) — heōs haidou katabēsē (you will descend to Hades — katabēsē future): the contrast echoes Isa 14:13-15 (the king of Babylon's boast of heavenly ascent followed by descent to Sheol). The allusion frames Capernaum's pride in its messianic privileges as Babel-arrogance. Hoti ei en Sodomois egenēthēsan hai dynameis — for if the miracles done in you had been done in Sodom, it would have remained to this day.
11.24: plēn legō hymin hoti gē Sodomōn anektoteron estai en hēmera kriseōs ē soi — but I tell you that the land of Sodom will be more tolerable on judgment day than for you — Sodom, the paradigmatic city of destruction in Genesis 19, becomes the reference point by which Capernaum's guilt is measured. The logic mirrors vv.21-22: Gentile cities famous for wickedness face lighter judgment than Israelite cities that received the full witness of messianic miracles and rejected them.
11.25: exomologoumai soi pater kyrie tou ouranou kai tēs gēs — I confess/praise you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth (exomologeomai: to acknowledge publicly, confess, or give praise; the word carries both confession and doxology) — hoti apekrypsas tauta apo sophōn kai synetōn kai apekalypsas auta nēpiois (because you hid these things from the wise and understanding and revealed them to little children — nēpioi: infants/little ones, not the spiritually mature but the socially insignificant): the prayer is Jesus's theological response to the cities' rejection — the pattern of divine concealment-and-revelation explains what has just happened.
11.26: nai ho patēr hoti houtōs eudokia egeneto emprosthen sou — yes, Father, because such was your good pleasure before you (eudokia, good pleasure, gracious purpose — from eudokeō, to be well-pleased; the same root as the voice at the baptism 3:17) — the verse repeats and affirms v.25 as a doxology. Emprosthen sou (before you, in your sight): the divine decision to conceal and reveal is not arbitrary but the expression of the Father's gracious pleasure.
11.27: panta moi paredothē hypo tou patros mou — all things have been delivered to me by my Father (paredothē aorist passive of paradidōmi: to hand over, entrust — the same verb for handing over prisoners but here for positive transfer of authority) — kai oudeis epiginōskei ton huion ei mē ho patēr (and no one fully knows the Son except the Father — epiginōskō: intensive knowing, relational recognition). Oude ton patera tis epiginōskei ei mē ho huios kai hō ean boulētai ho huios apokalypsai — nor does anyone know the Father except the Son and whoever the Son chooses to reveal him to. This verse, often called the "Johannine thunderbolt," is the synoptic high-Christological statement: mutual, exclusive, intensive knowledge between Father and Son.
11.29: arate ton zygon mou eph hymas kai mathete ap emou — take my yoke upon you and learn from me — arate aorist imperative of airō; zygon (yoke) is both the literal farming yoke and the rabbinic metaphor for Torah-obedience: "taking the yoke of the kingdom." Mathete ap emou (learn from me — manthanō aorist imperative: the root of mathētēs/disciple): Jesus invites discipleship to himself as teacher. Hoti praus eimi kai tapeinos tē kardia — for I am gentle and humble in heart: the only place in the Gospels where Jesus describes his own character in these specific terms. Praus (meek/gentle — the third Beatitude 5:5) and tapeinos tē kardia (humble in heart, not merely external humility).
11.30: ho gar zygos mou chrēstos — for my yoke is kind/good (chrēstos: kind, gracious, pleasant, good in quality — the word used in 1 Pet 2:3 of God's goodness, quoting Ps 34:8; not merely "easy" in the sense of requiring little effort but "gracious" in character) — kai to phortion mou elaphron estin (and my burden is light — elaphron, light in weight; phortion, burden, load): the contrast with the Pharisees' "heavy burdens" of 23:4 is implicit. The chrēstos/elaphron pair defines the quality of Jesus's yoke, not merely its quantity of demands.

12.31: blasphemia tou pneumatos — blasphemy against the Spirit — uniquely among Matthew's sin-categories, this sin is ouk aphethesetai (will not be forgiven) in this age or the coming age. The context defines the blasphemy: attributing the works of the Holy Spirit (exorcism performed by the Spirit of God, v.28) to Beelzeboul (the devil). The sin is the persistent, seeing refusal to recognize the Spirit's presence in Christ's ministry — a willful inversion of light and darkness that places oneself permanently outside the reach of the Spirit who grants repentance.
12.35: ho agathos anthropos ek tou agathou thesaurou ekballei agatha — the good man out of his good treasure brings forth good things — thesauros (treasury, storehouse) as the inner reservoir from which speech flows; ekballo (to throw out, bring out) emphasizes that speech is not accidental but driven out of what is stored within. The heart-treasury precedes and determines the word: character produces speech, not vice versa.
12.1: en ekeinō tō kairō — in that season/time (kairos: a specific moment or season, not general time — chronos; this marks the Sabbath controversy as occurring within the theological moment of ch11's revelation/rejection discourse) — epeinasan (they were hungry — peinaō aorist: experienced hunger, the physical state that motivates the plucking): plucking grain by hand as a traveler was permitted under Deut 23:25; the controversy is about the day, not the act.
12.2: hoi Pharisaioi idontes eipon autō — the Pharisees seeing said to him — idou hoi mathētai sou poiousin ho ouk exestin poiein en sabbatō (look, your disciples are doing what is not lawful to do on the Sabbath): the Pharisees address Jesus, not the disciples, establishing that the teacher bears responsibility for the disciples' practice. Ouk exestin (it is not lawful): plucking was classified by some rabbinic schools as harvesting (qatzir), one of the 39 prohibited labors.
12.3: ouk anegnōte ti epoiēsen Dauid — have you not read what David did (anagnōte aorist of anaginōskō: to read the Scriptures — Jesus challenges their knowledge of texts they claim to honor) — hote epeinasen autos kai hoi met autou (when he was hungry, he and those with him): hunger is the connecting term between the disciples' situation and David's. The argument is a fortiori: if David's need justified setting aside the restriction on the showbread, the disciples' need in the company of one greater than David (v.6) justifies their action.
12.4: tous artous tēs prothēseōs — the bread of the presence/the showbread (prothesis, setting forth, display — the twelve loaves arranged on a special table in the sanctuary, Exod 25:30; Lev 24:5-9) — ho ouk exōn ēn autō phagein (which it was not lawful for him to eat — the same ouk exōn construction as the Pharisees' charge in v.2): the argument uses their own legal category. Ei mē tois hiereusin monois (except for the priests alone): the OT reference is 1 Sam 21:1-6 (where Ahimelech the priest gives David the bread).
12.5: hoi hiereis en tō hierō to sabbaton bebēlousin kai anaitioi eisin — the priests in the temple desecrate the Sabbath and are innocent (bebēlousin present of bebēloō, to make common, profane, desecrate — the root of the Fourth Commandment prohibition; the priests' temple-work on Sabbath technically "makes it common" in legal terms) — anaitioi eisin (they are without guilt/innocent): priests performing sacrifices and temple duties on the Sabbath are not guilty because the temple service overrides the Sabbath restriction. The argument: if the temple overrides Sabbath, and something greater than the temple is here (v.6), it overrides the Sabbath too.
12.6: legō de hymin hoti tou hierou meizon estin hōde — but I tell you that something greater than the temple is here (meizon neuter comparative: not "a greater one" — meizōn — but "something greater," making the reference to Jesus's presence indirect; Matthew uses the same neuter construction in v.41-42 for "greater than Jonah/Solomon") — the temple was the locus of the divine presence in Israel; to claim that something greater is present is to claim divine presence in a superior form.
12.7: ei de egnōkeite ti estin eleos thelō kai ou thysian — if you had known what this means: I desire mercy and not sacrifice (egnōkeite pluperfect of ginōskō: if you had come to know — a condition of fact implying they had not) — the citation of Hos 6:6 is used also in 9:13. Eleos (mercy, loving-kindness — the LXX rendering of Hebrew chesed, covenant-loyalty). Ou katediksate tous anaitious — you would not have condemned the innocent: the Pharisees' charge is not just legally mistaken but a violation of the very Scripture they uphold.
12.8: kyrios gar estin tou sabbatou ho huios tou anthrōpou — for the Son of Man is Lord of the Sabbath (kyrios tou sabbatou: the genitive of possession/authority — Lord over the Sabbath as an institution) — the Sabbath was given by God at creation (Gen 2:2-3) and at Sinai (Exod 20:8-11). To be its Lord is to claim the authority of its Giver. The statement closes the Sabbath debate not with a legal ruling but with a Christological claim.
12.9: kai metabas ekeithen ēlthen eis tēn synagōgēn autōn — and departing from there he came into their synagogue — autōn (their synagogue): the possessive pronoun marks increasing distance between Jesus and the Pharisees' institutions; "their" recurs in v.14. The synagogue setting for the withered-hand healing places the second Sabbath controversy in the formal liturgical-judicial space of the Pharisees' own domain, intensifying the confrontation.
12.10: kai idou anthrōpos cheira echōn xēran — and behold a man having a withered hand (xēra, withered, dried up: the hand has lost function and vitality) — kai epērōtēsan auton legontes (and they asked him, saying — epērōtaō aorist: a formal inquiry): the Pharisees frame the question as a legal test: ei exestin tois sabbasin therapeusai (whether it is lawful to heal on the Sabbath). Hina katēgorēsōsin autou (in order to bring charges against him — katēgoreō: the courtroom verb for formal accusation).
12.11: tis estai ex hymōn anthrōpos hos hexei probaton hen — which person among you who has a single sheep (probaton hen, one sheep — the singular emphasizes personal attachment) — kai ean empesē touto tois sabbasin eis bothynon (and if it falls into a pit on the Sabbath): rabbinic debates about rescuing animals on the Sabbath are attested in the Dead Sea Scrolls (CD 11:13-14 forbids it; later Pharisaic tradition may have permitted it). Jesus's question assumes the crowd would rescue the sheep, leveraging their instinctive compassion against rigid legal application.
12.12: posō oun diapherei anthrōpos probatou — how much more valuable is a person than a sheep (posō diapherei: by how much does it differ — a qal wa-chomer/a fortiori argument: if sheep rescue is permissible, human healing is more so) — hōste exestin tois sabbasin kalōs poiein (so then it is lawful to do good on the Sabbath — kalōs poiein: to do good, do well): the positive formulation transcends the narrow "healing" question into a general principle of beneficence on the Sabbath.
12.13: ekteison sou tēn cheira — stretch out your hand (ekteison aorist imperative of ekteinō, to stretch out) — kai exeteinen (and he stretched it out — the same verb; the action Jesus commands is itself part of the restoration): no spoken exorcism formula, just a command directed at the human body. Kai apokatestathē hygiēs hōs hē allē — and it was completely restored, as sound as the other (apokatestathē aorist passive of apokathistēmi: to restore to an original condition; the verb carries eschatological weight — the same root as apokatastasis, the restoration of all things).
12.14: exelthontes de hoi Pharisaioi symboulion elabon kat autou — the Pharisees going out took counsel against him (symboulion elabon: took counsel, made a plan — the first explicit mention of a plot to kill Jesus in Matthew) — hopōs auton apoleswōsin (how they might destroy him — apollymi aorist subjunctive: the same word used for perishing/destruction, now directed at Jesus): the Sabbath healing that reveals Jesus's identity produces a death plot in the same scene.
12.15: ho de Iēsous gnous anechōrēsen ekeithen — Jesus knowing withdrew from there (gnous aorist participle of ginōskō: knowing — perceptive knowledge of the plot; anechōrēsen aorist of anachorew: to withdraw, retreat — used of deliberate strategic withdrawal in Matthew) — kai ēkolouthēsan autō ochloi polloi (and large crowds followed him): the withdrawal from the Pharisees does not diminish his following. Kai etherapeuse autous pantas (and he healed them all — pantas, all, without selection).
12.16: kai epetimēsen autois hina mē phaneron auton poiēsōsin — and he rebuked them so that they would not make him known (epetimēsen aorist of epitimaō, a strong rebuke — the same verb used for rebuking storms and demons; hina mē phaneron auton poiēsōsin: purpose clause, that they should not make him publicly visible) — the messianic secrecy command following mass healing sets up the Isaiah citation: Jesus's ministry matches the Servant's pattern of non-self-promotion.
12.17: hina plērōthē to rēthen dia Ēsaiou tou prophētou legontos — in order that what was spoken through Isaiah the prophet might be fulfilled (plērōthē aorist passive subjunctive of plēroō: Matthew's fulfillment formula, used 11 times) — the citation formula introduces the longest OT citation in Matthew, covering Isa 42:1-4. Matthew's version differs from both the Hebrew MT and the LXX, suggesting either an independent translation or a Christian recension emphasizing specific elements of the Servant portrait.
12.18: idou ho pais mou hon ēretisa — behold my servant whom I have chosen (pais: servant/child — the LXX word for the Isaian servant; ēretisa, aorist of hairetizō: to choose, elect — not the standard LXX term) — ho agapētos mou eis hon eudokēsen hē psychē mou (my beloved in whom my soul delights — agapētos: the same word as the baptismal/transfiguration voice; eudokēsen aorist of eudokeō: to be well-pleased): the language directly echoes the baptism (3:17) and transfiguration (17:5). Thēsō to pneuma mou ep auton — I will put my Spirit upon him (Isa 42:1).
12.19: ouk erisei oude kraugasei — he will not quarrel nor cry aloud (erizō: to dispute, contend; kraugazō: to shout, cry out loudly) — oude akousei tis en tais plateiais tēn phōnēn autou (nor will anyone hear his voice in the streets): Matthew's version of Isa 42:2. The Servant's ministry is defined by absence of public self-assertion: no street-preaching for display, no competitive religious argument. This explains the secrecy command of v.16: Jesus follows the Servant's pattern of quiet, non-self-promoting action.
12.20: kalamon syntetrimmenon ou kateaxei — a bruised reed he will not break (syntetrimmenon perfect passive participle of syntribō, to crush thoroughly: the reed is already crushed, not merely bent) — kai linon typhomenon ou sbesei (and a smoldering wick he will not extinguish — typhomenon present participle: actively smoldering; sbesei future): the Servant's work is not to eliminate the barely-surviving but to carry them through. Heōs an ekbalē eis nikos tēn krisin — until he brings justice through to victory (nikos, victory — stronger than the LXX's alētheia).
12.21: kai tō onomati autou ethnē elpiousin — and in his name the nations will hope (ethnē, nations, Gentiles — not just Israel; the Servant's reach is explicitly universal) — Matthew's version follows neither MT nor LXX exactly: MT has "islands will wait for his law"; LXX and Matthew have "in his name nations will hope." The Gentile scope of the Servant's mission is important: at the point where the Pharisees plot to kill Jesus, the citation looks beyond Israel to the nations who will hope in his name.
12.22: tote prosēnechthē autō daimonizomenos typhlos kai kōphos — then there was brought to him a demoniac who was blind and mute (prosēnechthē aorist passive: was brought — by others; typhlos kai kōphos: blind and mute, a double condition requiring a double healing) — kai etherapeuse auton hōste ton kōphon lalein kai blepein (and he healed him so that the mute man spoke and saw — hōste + infinitive: result clause): this miracle combines two of the messianic signs from 11:5, intensifying the Beelzeboul controversy that follows.
12.23: kai existanto pantes hoi ochloi — and all the crowds were astonished (existanto imperfect of existēmi, to stand outside oneself, to be amazed — the response is ongoing, not a single moment) — mēti houtos estin ho huios Dauid (could this perhaps be the Son of David? — mēti introduces a cautious question expecting an uncertain answer: "Could this possibly be the Son of David?"): an open question, not a firm conclusion. The crowds wonder; the Pharisees (v.24) answer with an accusation.
12.24: hoi de Pharisaioi akousantes eipon — but the Pharisees hearing said — houtos ouk ekballei ta daimonia ei mē en tō Beelzeboul archonti tōn daimoniōn (this man drives out demons only by Beelzeboul the ruler of demons): the accusation (first raised in 9:34) is escalated. Beelzeboul: the name-form in Matthew and Mark; probably from Baal-Zebul, "lord of the exalted house/dwelling." The logic: exorcism requires power over demons, which can come only from the demonic chain of command — unless the exorcist has a power that supersedes it.
12.25: eidōs de tas enthymēseis autōn eipen autois — knowing their deliberations he said to them (enthymēseis: inner deliberations, used in 9:4 for the scribes' inner reasoning about blasphemy) — pasa basileia meristheisa kath heautēs erēmoutai (every kingdom divided against itself is laid waste — erēmoutai present passive: is being deserted, becoming a wasteland): the three-part reductio (kingdom, city, household) applies a universal principle: internal division leads to collapse. If Jesus drives out demons by Satan, Satan is attacking his own forces.
12.26: kai ei ho Satanas ton Satanan ekballei — and if Satan drives out Satan (ekballei present: the accusation applied directly as a hypothesis) — eph heauton emeristhē (he is divided against himself — emeristhē aorist passive): the logical impossibility stated as a completed action hypothetically. Pōs oun stathēsetai hē basileia autou (how then will his kingdom stand? — stathēsetai future passive of histēmi): the question is rhetorical; implied answer: it cannot.
12.27: kai ei egō en Beelzeboul ekballō ta daimonia — and if I drive out demons by Beelzeboul — hoi huioi hymōn en tini ekballousin (by whom do your sons drive them out? — hoi huioi hymōn: your sons, your people — Jewish exorcists within the Pharisees' own circles): the argument turns the accusation back. If the criterion is that exorcistic power implies demonic alliance, then Jewish exorcists are equally implicated. Dia touto autoi kritai esontai hymōn — therefore they will be your judges: the Jewish exorcists will testify against the Pharisees' inconsistency.
12.28: ei de en pneumati theou egō ekballō ta daimonia — but if it is by the Spirit of God that I drive out demons (pneumati theou: the Spirit of God — Matthew's version; Luke 11:20 has "finger of God," which may be the original Q form, echoing Exod 8:19) — ara ephthasen eph hymas hē basileia tou theou (then the kingdom of God has come upon you — ephthasen aorist of phthanō: to arrive before expected, to arrive already): the kingdom has come upon them without them recognizing it. Spirit-empowered exorcism is the presence of the eschatological kingdom, not a demonstration but the thing itself.
12.29: ē pōs dynatai tis eiselthein eis tēn oikian tou ischyrou — or how can anyone enter the house of the strong man (ischyros, strong/powerful — the same word used in 3:11 for John's description of Jesus as "mightier than I") — kai ta skeuē autou harpasai ean mē prōton dēsē ton ischyron (and carry off his possessions unless he first binds the strong man — dēsē aorist subjunctive of deō: to bind): the parable defines Jesus's exorcistic ministry as the "binding" of Satan, making possible the rescue of those under his dominion. Kai tote tēn oikian autou diarpasei — and then he can plunder his house.
12.30: ho mē ōn met emou kat emou estin — the one not with me is against me (mē ōn met emou present participle: the one who is not being with me, continuously not aligned) — kai ho mē synagōn met emou skorpizei (and the one not gathering with me is scattering — skorpizei present: is in the act of scattering): the gathering/scattering image recalls the shepherd (9:36; Ezek 34). No neutral position exists relative to Jesus's mission — not-gathering is actively scattering. This frames the Beelzeboul controversy: the Pharisees' accusation is not neutral observation but active opposition.
12.32: kai hos ean eipē logon kata tou huiou tou anthrōpou — and whoever speaks a word against the Son of Man (logos kata: a word directed against — the legal register) — aphethēsetai autō (it will be forgiven him — future passive divine): speech against Jesus-in-his-humiliation is forgivable. Hos d an eipē kata tou pneumatos tou hagiou ouk aphethēsetai autō oute en toutō tō aiōni oute en tō mellonti — whoever speaks against the Holy Spirit will not be forgiven, neither in this age nor in the age to come (toutō tō aiōni...tō mellonti: the two-age schema of Jewish eschatology; the Rabbinic olam ha-zeh and olam ha-ba).
12.33: ē poiēsate to dendron kalon kai ton karpon autou kalon — either make the tree good and its fruit good (poiēsate aorist imperative of poieō: make, treat as — the command is logically ironic since one cannot simply make a tree good by willing it) — ē poiēsate to dendron sapron kai ton karpon autou sapron (or make the tree bad and its fruit bad — sapros: rotten, decayed): you must choose your category consistently. The Pharisees cannot attribute good fruit (healing) to a bad tree (demonic source). Ek gar tou karpou to dendron ginōsketai — for by the fruit the tree is known: the tree-fruit principle inverted from 7:16-20.
12.34: gennēmata echidnōn — brood of vipers (gennēmata echidnōn: offspring of vipers — the same address as John's to the Pharisees and Sadducees in 3:7): the term combines origin and character. Pōs dynasthe agatha lalein ponēroi ontes (how are you able to say good things being evil? — ponēroi ontes present participle: constitutively evil): the Pharisees cannot produce good assessment because they are bad trees. Ek gar tou perisseumatos tēs kardias to stoma lalei — for the mouth speaks from the overflow of the heart (perisseuma: overflow, abundance — what pours over from a full container).
12.36: pan rēma argon ho ean lalēsōsin hoi anthrōpoi — every idle/ineffective word that people speak (rēma argon: idle word — argos literally means not-working, inactive, idle; it can mean useless or irresponsible; in context, words spoken against the Spirit-empowered ministry of Jesus "do not work" because they misidentify what is happening) — apodōsousin peri autou logon en hēmera kriseōs (they will give an account about it on the day of judgment — apodōsousin logon: to render account, a judicial phrase).
12.37: ek gar tōn logōn sou dikaiōthēsē — for by your words you will be justified/acquitted (dikaiōthēsē future passive of dikaioō: to be declared righteous, acquitted — the forensic term used here in a strict judicial sense: your words form the basis of the verdict) — kai ek tōn logōn sou katadikasthēsē (and by your words you will be condemned — katadikasthēsē future passive of katadikō): the forensic parallelism is exact. The speech-judgment principle addresses the fruits of the heart (v.34-35) that reveal what one truly is.
12.38: tote apekrīthēsan autō tines tōn grammateōn kai Pharisaiōn — then some of the scribes and Pharisees answered him — didaskale thelomen apo sou sēmeion idein (teacher, we want to see a sign from you — sēmeion: a sign, a wonder-as-proof): the request for a sign follows the Beelzeboul controversy. Having explained away the miracles as demonic, they now request a different, unmistakable sign. Thelomen (we want): the verb of volition signals what drives the request — not openness to evidence but a demand for evidence on their own terms.
12.39: genea ponēra kai moichalis sēmeion epizētei — a wicked and adulterous generation seeks a sign (ponēra kai moichalis: wicked and adulterous — moichalis is the prophetic term for covenant-unfaithfulness, Israel as an adulterous wife to YHWH; Hos 1-2; Jer 3:8; Ezek 16) — kai sēmeion ou dothēsetai autē ei mē to sēmeion Iōna tou prophētou (and a sign will not be given it except the sign of Jonah the prophet — dothēsetai future passive divine): the single exception is the Jonah-sign, which will not be recognized even when it comes.
12.40: hōsper gar ēn Iōnas en tē koilia tou kētous treis hēmeras kai treis nyktas — for as Jonah was in the belly of the sea-monster three days and three nights (kētos: a great sea-creature; treis hēmeras kai treis nyktas: citing Jonah 1:17 LXX) — houtōs estai ho huios tou anthrōpou en tē kardia tēs gēs treis hēmeras kai treis nyktas (so will the Son of Man be in the heart of the earth — en tē kardia tēs gēs: the heart/center of the earth, echoing Ps 46:2; = burial): the Jonah typology applies not to Jonah's preaching but to his entombment and emergence. The only sign given is death and resurrection.
12.41: andres Nineuitai anastēsontai en tē krisei meta tēs geneas tautēs kai katakrinousin autēn — the men of Nineveh will stand up at the judgment with this generation and condemn it (anastēsontai future of anistēmi: will rise up — the resurrection verb used for the judgment-rising of witnesses) — hoti metenoēsan eis to kērygma Iōna (for they repented at the preaching of Jonah — metenoēsan aorist: Nineveh's repentance was complete and definitive). Kai idou pleion Iōna hōde — and behold, something greater than Jonah is here: the neuter pleion (greater thing; cf. v.6,42) makes the comparison structural, not merely personal.
12.42: basilissa notou egerthēsetai en tē krisei meta tēs geneas tautēs kai katakrinei autēn — the Queen of the South will rise at the judgment with this generation and condemn it (basilissa notou: the Queen of the South, the Queen of Sheba from 1 Kgs 10:1-13) — hoti ēlthen ek tōn peratōn tēs gēs akousai tēn sophian Solomōntos (for she came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon — peratōn tēs gēs: the ends of the earth): a Gentile woman traveled the maximum distance to hear Israel's greatest king. Kai idou pleion Solomōntos hōde — and behold, something greater than Solomon is here.
12.43: hotan de to akatharton pneuma exelthē apo tou anthrōpou — when the unclean spirit goes out of a person (to akatharton pneuma: the unclean spirit — akatharton is the ritual purity word applied to the spiritual realm) — dierchetai di anydron topōn zētoun anapausin kai ouch heuriskei (it passes through waterless places seeking rest and does not find it — anydros: waterless, arid; the wilderness/desert as the haunt of evil spirits is a Second Temple Jewish belief; anapausin: rest, the same word as 11:28-29): the returning-spirit parable addresses the generation's spiritual condition.
12.44: tote legei eis ton oikon mou epistrepsō — then it says: I will return to my house (epistrepsō future: will return; the spirit claims the person as its "house") — kai elthon heuriskei scholazonta sesarōmenon kai kekosmēmenon (and coming it finds it standing empty, swept, and decorated — scholazonta present participle of scholazō: to be idle, empty, unoccupied; sesarōmenon perfect passive: having been swept; kekosmēmenon perfect passive: having been put in order/decorated): the danger is not the initial exorcism but the failure to fill the swept house. Empty order is not security.
12.45: tote poreuetai kai paralambanei meth heautou hepta hetera pneumata ponērotera heautou — then it goes and takes with it seven other spirits more wicked than itself (hepta: seven, the number of completeness; ponērotera comparative: more evil) — kai eiselthonta katoikei ekei (and entering they dwell there — katoikei present: settled, permanent dwelling): the final state is quantitatively and qualitatively worse. Kai ginetai ta eschata tou anthrōpou ekeinou cheirona tōn prōtōn — and the last things of that person become worse than the first: ta eschata (last things, end condition) is eschatological vocabulary applied to the individual. Houtōs estai kai tē genea tautē tē ponēra — so also it will be with this wicked generation.
12.46: eti de autou lalountos tois ochlois idou hē mētēr kai hoi adelphoi autou heistēkeisan exō — while he was still speaking to the crowds, behold his mother and brothers stood outside (heistēkeisan pluperfect of histēmi: had been standing — they were already there before this moment) — zētountes autō lalēsai (seeking to speak to him): the arrival of Jesus's biological family during a discourse on spiritual family creates the contrast that vv.48-50 resolve. The placement intercalates the physical family's interruption to allow the definition of the true family.
12.47: eipen de tis autō — someone said to him — idou hē mētēr sou kai hoi adelphoi sou exō hestēkasin zētountes soi lalēsai (behold, your mother and your brothers are standing outside, seeking to speak to you): the messenger's message uses the natural kinship vocabulary (hē mētēr sou kai hoi adelphoi sou: your mother and your brothers) that Jesus is about to redefine. The verse is omitted in some manuscripts (Codex Sinaiticus) but has strong textual support and is parallel to Mark 3:32.
12.48: ho de apokritheis eipen tō legonti autō — but he answering said to the one speaking to him — tis estin hē mētēr mou kai tines eisin hoi adelphoi mou (who is my mother and who are my brothers?): the rhetorical question is not a dismissal of Mary but a redefinition of the category "family." Jesus does not deny the biological family; instead he asks a question that opens the categories of mētēr (mother) and adelphos (brother) beyond biological definition to a new criterion.
12.49: kai ekteinas tēn cheira autou epi tous mathētas autou eipen — and stretching out his hand toward his disciples he said (ekteinas tēn cheira autou epi: stretching out his hand toward — the same gesture-verb ekteinō used for healing the withered hand in v.13; here it is a gesture of identification) — idou hē mētēr mou kai hoi adelphoi mou (behold my mother and my brothers): the gesture makes the answer visible before the verbal declaration. The disciples present, not the family outside, constitute Jesus's nearest family.
12.50: hostis gar an poiēsē to thelēma tou patros mou tou en ouranois — for whoever does the will of my Father in heaven (poiēsē aorist subjunctive of poieō: the general condition; to thelēma tou patros mou: the will of my Father, not the law abstractly but the Father's specific will) — autos mou adelphos kai adelphē kai mētēr estin (that person is my brother and sister and mother): the family-triad (brother, sister, mother) is now defined by the doing-of-will, not by birth. Father-will is the membership criterion for Jesus's family; the present continuous sense is implicit in the conditional construction.

13.11: hoti hymin dedotai gnona ta mysteria tes basileias ton ouranon — to you it has been given to know the mysteries of the kingdom of the heavens — mysterion (mystery, secret) in Jewish apocalyptic usage denoted the divine plan hidden in the age but now disclosed to the elect; Daniel uses the term for what only God and his messenger can reveal (Dan 2:47). The disciples receive the gift of understanding (synesis) that the parable-audience does not receive — not by their merit but by divine grace (dedotai, divine passive).
13.52: grammateus matheteutheis te basileia ton ouranon — a scribe discipled to the kingdom of the heavens — the concluding self-description of the kingdom-teacher; Matthew presents Jesus as producing a new kind of scribe who draws from both old and new. kainos kai palaios — new and old — the order is deliberate (new first): the new covenant does not discard the old treasury but brings it forward into its fulfillment; the scribe who understands the kingdom reads the old Torah through the new light Christ provides.
13.1: en tē hēmera ekeinē — on that day (the phrase links ch13 directly to ch12's rejection controversies; the parables are Jesus's response to the opposition just described) — exelthōn ho Iēsous apo tēs oikias ekathēto para tēn thalassan (Jesus going out of the house was sitting by the sea — ekathēto imperfect: was sitting, a settled posture for teaching). The movement from the house (private teaching) to the sea (public teaching) frames the parabolic discourse as a response to the situation of mixed reception described in ch12.
13.2: kai synēchthēsan pros auton ochloi polloi — and large crowds gathered to him (synēchthēsan aorist passive of synagō: were gathered, assembled — a passive of circumstance: they gathered spontaneously) — hōste auton eis ploion embanta kathēsthai (so that he got into a boat and was sitting — embanta aorist participle: having stepped in; kathēsthai present infinitive: to be sitting): the boat provides both distance from the crowd and acoustics over water. The seated posture is the formal Jewish teaching position.
13.3: kai elalēsen autois polla en parabolais — and he spoke to them many things in parables (parabolais: a parable — from paraballō, to throw alongside, to compare; in Jewish usage a mashal, a saying or story that uses one domain to illuminate another) — legōn idou exēlthen ho speirōn tou speirein (saying: behold, the sower went out to sow — the Greek is literally "the one sowing went out to the sowing of him": his purpose is built into the description). The parable opens without explaining its referent; the audience must work toward the meaning.
13.4: en tō speirein auton — while he was sowing (en tō speirein auton: an articular infinitive with subject — the standard Matthean idiom for simultaneous action) — ha men epesen para tēn hodon (some fell along the road): the four soil types are presented in ascending order of receptivity. Para tēn hodon (alongside/along the path): the hard-packed path running through a field; seed falling there lies on the surface. Elthen ta peteina kai katephagen auta (the birds came and ate them up — kataphagō aorist: devoured completely).
13.5: alla de epesen epi ta petrōdē — other fell on the rocky places (petrōdē: stony ground, rocky soil — petra + -ōdēs: full of rock, rocky) — hopou ouk eichen gēn pollēn (where it did not have much soil): the problem is not the absence of soil but its shallowness. Eutheōs exanateillen (it sprang up immediately — eutheōs: the characteristic word of Mark's urgency, here in Matthew for the false urgency of shallow enthusiasm) — dia to mē echein bathos gēs (because of not having depth of soil: the articular infinitive with dia expressing cause).
13.6: hēliou de anateilantos ekaumatisthē — the sun rising, it was scorched (anateilantos genitive absolute: the sun having risen; ekaumatisthē aorist passive of kaumatizō: to be burned by scorching heat — the same verb in Rev 16:8-9 for eschatological burning) — kai dia to mē echein rhizan exēranthē (and because of not having root it withered: rhizan accusative of rhiza, root; the absence of root is the explanation for both the quick sprouting and the quick dying). Root and depth are correlated: shallow soil produces rootless growth.
13.7: alla de epesen epi tas akanthas — other fell among the thorns (akanthai: thorns, thorn-plants; this soil has plant-growing capacity but already contains competing growth) — kai anebēsan hai akanthai kai epnixan auta (and the thorns grew up and choked them — epnixan aorist of pnigō: to choke, throttle; the same root used in 18:28 for seizing a man by the throat): the thorns did not prevent germination but prevented the seed from producing grain. The soil is good enough to grow; the competition is what kills the yield.
13.8: alla de epesen epi tēn gēn tēn kalēn — other fell on the good soil (gēn tēn kalēn: the good/fine soil — kalos, the word for beauty-in-goodness, appropriate quality) — kai edidou karpon (and was giving fruit — edidou imperfect: was continuing to give, an ongoing producing): the imperfect marks the sustained yield. Ho men hekaton ho de hexēkonta ho de triakonta (one a hundred, one sixty, one thirty): the yields are all above the typical ancient grain yield of 5-7x. A hundredfold is exceptional; even thirtyfold is generous. The variety is not graded bad-to-good but simply differentiated abundance.
13.9: ho echōn ōta akouetō — the one having ears, let them hear — the ear formula (cf. 11:15; 13:43) marks the end of the parable without explanation, creating space for active interpretation. The parable does not announce its meaning; it invites the hearer to locate themselves in the soils. Akouetō present imperative: a continuous command to hear and keep hearing. The formula also implies that not all who "hear" the parable thereby "hear" its meaning — the distinction between hearing and understanding is the theme of the next pericope.
13.10: dia ti en parabolais laleis autois — why do you speak to them in parables (dia ti: on account of what, why — a purpose question) — the disciples' question presupposes they have already understood the parabolic discourse differently from the crowd. Autois (to them): the distinction is between "them" (the crowd) and the disciples asking the question. The question opens the theological explanation of vv.11-17: the parables are not a simpler teaching method but a mode of disclosure that reveals to some and conceals from others.
13.12: hostis gar echei dothēsetai autō kai perisseuphthēsetai — for whoever has, it will be given to him and he will have abundance (dothēsetai future passive divine: it will be given by God; perisseuphthēsetai future passive: he will have it in abundance) — hostis de ouk echei kai ho echei arthēsetai ap autou (but whoever does not have, even what he has will be taken from him — arthēsetai future passive divine): the principle is not economic but revelatory: those who receive the mystery of the kingdom receive more; those who reject it lose even the initial capacity. The future passives are divine passives throughout.
13.13: dia touto en parabolais autois lalō hoti blepontes ou blepousin — this is why I speak to them in parables: seeing they do not see — dia touto (this is why): the parables are not despite the crowd's blindness but in response to it. Blepontes ou blepousin kai akouontes ouk akouousin oude syniousin (seeing they do not see, and hearing they do not hear nor understand — syniousin present of syniēmi, to put together, comprehend): the three-fold failure (seeing/seeing, hearing/hearing, understanding). This is a description of the crowd's spiritual state, not a permanent divine decree of blindness imposed from outside.
13.14: kai anaplēroutai autois hē prophēteia Ēsaiou hē legousa — and on them is being fulfilled the prophecy of Isaiah saying (anaplēroutai present passive: is being fulfilled, completed — ana + plēroō: the ana- prefix can intensify or indicate completion) — the Isaiah 6:9-10 citation follows. This is a different fulfillment formula than Matthew's usual hina plērōthē: the present tense marks ongoing fulfillment rather than a single completed event. Isaiah's commission (6:9-10) is a description of the audience Jesus now faces.
13.15: epachynthē gar hē kardia tou laou toutou — for the heart of this people has grown dull/fat (epachynthē aorist passive of pachynō: to make thick/fat; the idiom for spiritual insensitivity — a heart grown so padded it cannot feel) — kai tois ōsin bareos ēkousan (and they hear with heavy/dull ears — bareos: heavily, with weight, with difficulty): the ears are overburdened, not absent. Kai tous ophthalmous autōn ekammusan (and they have closed their eyes — kammyō: to close the eyes deliberately, a voluntary shutting). The verse diagnoses willful, not merely circumstantial, blindness.
13.16: hymōn de makarioi hoi ophthalmoi hoti blepousin — but blessed are your eyes because they see — the Beatitude form applied to seeing and hearing, reversing the Isaiah curse. The disciples' seeing is not their achievement but a gift that marks them as blessed. Kai ta ōta hymōn hoti akouousin (and your ears because they hear): the inversion of v.14-15 is complete — where the crowd cannot connect seeing/hearing to perceiving/understanding, the disciples' organs of perception actually function.
13.17: amen gar legō hymin hoti polloi prophētai kai dikaioi epethymēsan idein ha blepete — truly I tell you that many prophets and righteous people longed to see what you see (epethymēsan aorist of epithymeō: to desire ardently — the word can carry the sense of intense longing or craving; the prophets and righteous ones desired this with an unfulfilled longing) — kai ouk eidon (and did not see it). The disciples are positioned at the hinge of redemptive history: the prophets pointed forward to what is now present; the disciples receive what the prophets only anticipated.
13.18: hymeis oun akousate tēn parabolēn tou speirontos — you therefore hear the parable of the sower — hymeis oun (you therefore): emphatic second person, contrasting the disciples' privileged hearing with the crowd's deafness just described. Akousate aorist imperative: a sharp, focused act of hearing. The explanation that follows (vv.19-23) does not simply decode the parable but applies it — each soil type is identified by how one relates to ho logos tēs basileias (the word of the kingdom).
13.19: pantos akouontos ton logon tēs basileias kai mē synientos — everyone hearing the word of the kingdom and not understanding it (synientos present participle of syniēmi: not putting together, not comprehending) — erchetai ho ponēros kai harpazei to esparmenon en tē kardia autou (the evil one comes and snatches away what was sown in his heart — ho ponēros: the evil one, masculine — a personal reference to the devil as identified in v.39; harpazei present: snatches, the same aggressive seizure-verb as 12:29 for plundering the strong man's house). The path-hearer's defining feature is non-understanding (mē synieis).
13.20: ho de epi ta petrōdē spareis — the one sown on the rocky places (spareis aorist passive participle of speirō: having been sown — the person is identified with the seed sown in a location) — houtos estin ho ton logon akouōn kai eutheōs meta charas lambanōn auton (this is the one who hears the word and immediately receives it with joy — eutheōs: immediately, instantly; meta charas: with joy): the immediate joyful reception is itself diagnostic. Speed and emotion without root signal shallow engagement rather than genuine reception.
13.21: ouk echei de rhizan en heautō — but he has no root in himself (rhizan en heautō: root within himself, not rooted in external soil only but internally without rootedness) — alla proskaireos estin (but is temporary — proskairos: for a season, time-limited): the word contains kairos (appointed time); this person is only for the appointed moment. Genomenēs de thlipseōs ē diōgmou dia ton logon eutheōs skandalizatai (when trouble or persecution comes on account of the word, immediately he stumbles/falls away — skandalizatai present passive: is caused to stumble): the same word Jesus used in 11:6 for the potential offense at himself.
13.22: ho de eis tas akanthas spareis — the one sown among the thorns — hē merimna tou aiōnos kai hē apatē tou ploutou sympnigei ton logon (the worry of the age and the deceitfulness of wealth choke the word — merimna tou aiōnos: the anxiety belonging to this present age; apatē tou ploutou: the deceit/deception of wealth — apatē, deceit, the word for active falsehood; wealth deceives: it promises security and satisfaction it cannot deliver). Sympnigei present: is choking — the present tense marks an ongoing process. Kai akarpos ginetai (and it becomes unfruitful — the thorn-hearer hears and perhaps even understands, but bears nothing).
13.23: ho de epi tēn kalēn gēn spareis — the one sown on the good soil — houtos estin ho ton logon akouōn kai synieis (this is the one who hears the word and understands it — synieis present participle of syniēmi: the understanding that is the path-hearer's absence, now present). Hos dē karpophorei kai poiei ho men hekaton ho de hexēkonta ho de triakonta (who indeed bears fruit and produces — karpophorei present: is bearing fruit; poiei present: is producing, continuously): the fruit is present-tense ongoing, and the yields (hundred, sixty, thirty) represent differentiated abundance, not a ranking of good-better-best.
13.24: allēn parabolēn parethēken autois legōn — he placed another parable before them (parethēken aorist of paratithēmi: to place beside, set before — a serving/presenting verb; parables are presented as food for the hearer) — hōmoiōthē hē basileia tōn ouranōn anthrōpō speironti kalon sperma en tō agrō autou (the kingdom of the heavens is like a man who sowed good seed in his field): the kingdom is compared to the whole situation, not merely to the man or the seed. The noun anthrōpō (a man) identifies the figure who is later revealed as the Son of Man (v.37).
13.25: en de tō katheudein tous anthrōpous ēlthen autou ho echthros kai epespeiren zizania — but while the people were sleeping his enemy came and sowed weeds among the wheat (katheudein tous anthrōpous: the people sleeping — not negligence but normal rest; echthros: enemy; epespeiren zizania: sowed tares/weeds over — zizanion, probably Lolium temulentum, darnel, a weed that closely resembles wheat in early growth stages and is toxic) — ana meson tou sitou (among the midst of the wheat): the tares are sown specifically among, not separately from, the wheat. The mixture is intentional.
13.26: hote de eblastēsen ho chortos kai karpon epoiēsen — when the grain sprouted and bore fruit (eblastēsen aorist of blastanō, to sprout, germinate) — tote ephanē kai ta zizania (then the tares also appeared — ephanē aorist passive of phainō: appeared, became visible): the tares are recognizable only when both have matured enough to distinguish. Darnel is visually indistinguishable from wheat until the grain heads form; the enemy exploits this by planting them together. Early separation would destroy wheat; only at harvest is safe separation possible.
13.27: proselthontes de hoi douloi tou oikodespotou eipon autō — approaching, the servants of the householder said to him — kyrie ouchi kalon sperma espeiras en tō sō agrō (sir/lord, did you not sow good seed in your field? — kyrie: the address of a servant to a master): the servants presuppose the master's competence; the question expresses genuine puzzlement, not accusation. Pothen oun echei zizania (how then does it have tares? — pothen: from where? whence? a question of source): they want to identify the origin of the contamination.
13.28: ho de ephē autois echthros anthrōpos touto epoiēsen — he said to them: an enemy man did this (echthros anthrōpos: a hostile person — the repetition of echthros from v.25 identifies the source without naming the devil yet; that identification comes in the interpretation of v.39) — hoi de douloi legousin autō theleis oun apelthontes syllexōmen auta (the servants say to him: do you want us therefore, going out, to gather them up — syllexōmen aorist subjunctive: to collect, pull out): the servants' offer is premature. Their instinct for immediate purity would destroy the wheat.
13.29: ho de phēsin ou — he says: no (phēsin historical present: says — the vividness of direct discourse in narrative) — mēpote syllегontes ta zizania ekrizoōsēte hama autois ton siton (lest gathering the tares you uproot the wheat along with them — mēpote: lest, for fear that; ekrizoōsēte aorist subjunctive of ekrizoō: to uproot completely, to pull up by the roots): the danger is not the tares but the collateral damage of premature separation. The entanglement of root systems makes clean separation impossible until harvest.
13.30: aphete synauxanesthai amphotera heōs tou therismou — let both grow together until the harvest (synauxanesthai present passive infinitive: to grow together, co-grow — the syn- prefix marks simultaneous growth) — kai en kairō tou therismou erō tois theristais (and at the time of the harvest I will say to the harvesters — kairō: the appointed moment): the harvesters are identified as angels in v.39. The harvest-sequence (weeds first → bundled → burned; wheat → gathered into the barn) is the reverse of typical priorities: removal of bad before collection of good, emphasizing the thoroughness of the final separation.
13.31: allēn parabolēn parethēken autois legōn — he placed another parable before them — homoia estin hē basileia tōn ouranōn kokkō sinapeōs (the kingdom of the heavens is like a grain of mustard seed — kokkos sinapeōs: a mustard grain, probably Sinapis alba or Brassica nigra): the kingdom is compared to the whole scenario, not just the seed. Hon labōn anthrōpos espeiren en tō agrō autou (which a man took and sowed in his field — labōn...espeiren: the two-verb sequence of human action, deliberate planting).
13.32: ho mikroteron men estin pantōn tōn spermatōn — which is smaller than all seeds (mikroteron comparative: smaller — the mustard seed was proverbially the smallest in Palestinian agricultural knowledge; pantōn tōn spermatōn: of all seeds, in the field/garden context; botanically not the world's smallest seed) — hotan de auxēthē meizon tōn lachanōn estin kai ginetai dendron (but when it grows it is greater than the garden plants and becomes a tree — dendron: a tree, though mustard shrubs are not true trees): the contrast between the proverbially small beginning and the bird-sheltering result is the point, not botanical precision. Ta peteina tou ouranou...kataskhēnoun — birds of the sky nesting in its branches echoes Ezek 17:22-24; 31:6; Dan 4:12 (the nations sheltering under the great kingdom-tree).
13.33: allēn parabolēn elalēsen autois — he spoke another parable to them — homoia estin hē basileia tōn ouranōn zymē (the kingdom of the heavens is like leaven/yeast — zymē: leavening agent, normally a negative symbol in Jewish thought for corrupting influence, e.g., Passover unleavened bread requirements): the use of leaven as a positive image is striking. Hēn labousa gynē (which a woman took — the woman is the agent; the three sata = three sata measures, about 13 liters each = ~39 liters of flour total: an enormous quantity, enough for a large communal meal). Enekrypsen eis aleurou sata tria (she hid in three measures of flour — enekrypsen: hid, embedded — the leaven is not mixed on the surface but hidden within). Heōs hou ezymōthē holon — until all of it was leavened: the totality, not just the portion.
13.34: tauta panta elalēsen ho Iēsous en parabolais tois ochlois — all these things Jesus spoke in parables to the crowds (en parabolais: in parables — not occasionally but as the exclusive mode for crowd teaching) — kai chōris parabolēs ouden elalei autois (and without a parable he said nothing to them): the absolute statement covers this discourse; Matthew does not claim Jesus never taught the crowds directly anywhere, but that in this parabolic discourse, parable was the only vehicle. The verse prepares the fulfillment citation of v.35.
13.35: hopōs plērōthē to rēthen dia tou prophētou legontos — in order that what was spoken through the prophet might be fulfilled — the citation is Ps 78:2 (Asaph): anoixō en parabolais to stoma mou (I will open my mouth in parables). Ereugomai kekrymena apo katabolēs kosmou (I will utter/pour out things hidden since the foundation of the world — ereugomai future of ereugomai: to belch/utter forth forcefully — a strong emission verb, used in LXX Ps 19:2 for the heavens pouring out speech). Katabolēs kosmou (foundation/laying down of the world): the hidden things predate creation.
13.36: tote apheis tous ochlous ēlthen eis tēn oikian — then leaving the crowds he came into the house — the movement from public (boat/crowds) to private (house/disciples) mirrors 13:1 in reverse. Kai prosēlthon autō hoi mathētai autou legontes diasaphēson hēmin tēn parabolēn tōn zizaniōn (and his disciples came to him saying: explain to us the parable of the weeds — diasaphēson aorist imperative of diasapheō: to make thoroughly clear, explain in detail — a stronger verb than simply "tell us the meaning"): the disciples, inside the house with Jesus, ask for the full disclosure that the crowd cannot receive.
13.37: ho speirōn to kalon sperma estin ho huios tou anthrōpou — the one sowing the good seed is the Son of Man — the explanation is an allegory: each element is decoded. Ho de agros estin ho kosmos (the field is the world — kosmos: the whole created order, not just Israel): the kingdom's scope is universal, not national. The Son of Man is identified as the sower, identifying Jesus's mission with the planting of the good-seed people in the world-field. The identification of agros = kosmos is exegetically significant: the field is not the church but the world.
13.38: to de kalon sperma houtoi eisin hoi huioi tēs basileias — the good seed are the sons of the kingdom (huioi tēs basileias: a Semitic genitive-of-characterization: those who belong to the kingdom, those shaped by it) — ta de zizania houtoi eisin hoi huioi tou ponērou (the tares are the sons of the evil one — hoi huioi tou ponērou: sons of the evil one, parallel to "sons of the kingdom"; those shaped by and belonging to the evil one): the two peoples coexist in the world, not in the church. The parable is about eschatological separation of humanity, not church discipline.
13.39: ho de echthros ho speiras auta estin ho diabolos — the enemy who sowed them is the devil (diabolos: the slanderer, accuser — the Greek translation of Hebrew satan) — ho de therismos synteia aiōnos estin (the harvest is the end of the age — synteleia aiōnos: the completion/consummation of the age): synteleia is the word for final consummation, not merely cessation; the age comes to its intended completion. Hoi de theristai angeloi eisin (the harvesters are angels): the agents of final separation are divine, not human — the disciples are not to perform this separation themselves.
13.40: hōsper oun syllegetai ta zizania kai pyri katakaieta — as therefore the tares are gathered and burned in fire (katakaieta present passive: are being burned up completely) — houtōs estai en tē synteleia tou aiōnos (so it will be at the end of the age): the harvest-scenario is the paradigm for the final judgment. The fire is not punitive inflammation added from outside but the natural destiny of material fit only for burning. Synteleia tou aiōnos is repeated from v.39, marking this as the controlling temporal reference for the entire parable's application.
13.41: apostelei ho huios tou anthrōpou tous angelous autou — the Son of Man will send his angels (apostelei future: will send with a commission) — kai syllexousin ek tēs basileias autou panta ta skandala kai tous poiountas tēn anomian (and they will gather out of his kingdom everything that causes stumbling and those practicing lawlessness — panta ta skandala: all the causes-of-stumbling, the trap-springs; tous poiountas tēn anomian: those actively practicing lawlessness — anomia: the absence of law, the refusal of God's order; cf. 7:23 where the same phrase closes the Sermon on the Mount's false-prophet warning).
13.42: kai balousin autous eis tēn kaminon tou pyros — and they will throw them into the furnace of fire (kaminon tou pyros: the furnace of fire — kaminos is the word for a kiln or smelting furnace, an intense enclosed fire; appearing also in Rev 1:15; 9:2) — ekei estai ho klauthmos kai ho brygmos tōn odontōn (there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth — klauthmos: loud weeping, lamentation; brygmos tōn odontōn: grinding/gnashing of teeth — a phrase Matthew uses six times, always in eschatological contexts): the formula describes the combination of grief (klauthmos) and rage/anguish (brygmos) in final separation.
13.43: tote hoi dikaioi eklamphousin hōs ho hēlios — then the righteous will shine like the sun (eklamphousin future of eklampō: to shine out, to blaze forth — the ek- prefix marks emergence into full radiance; the verb is only used here in the NT) — en tē basileia tou patros autōn (in the kingdom of their Father): the righteous inherit the Father's kingdom as children, not merely subjects. Ho echōn ōta akouetō — the one having ears, let them hear: the ear formula closes the parable-interpretation, marking the eschatological disclosure as requiring the same active hearing as the parables themselves.
13.44: homoia estin hē basileia tōn ouranōn thēsaurō kekrymmenō en tō agrō — the kingdom of the heavens is like treasure hidden in a field (thēsauros: treasure, a stored collection of value; kekrymmenō perfect passive participle: having been hidden, currently hidden) — hon heurōn anthrōpos ekrypsen (which a man found and hid again — he re-conceals the treasure to secure his claim before purchase): the re-hiding is not deceptive but protective. Kai apo tēs charas autou hypagei kai pōlei panta hosa echei — and from his joy he goes and sells all he has (apo tēs charas: from/out of his joy — the joy is the motivation for the total sell-off; the kingdom is recognized as worth everything by one who encounters it).
13.45: palin homoia estin hē basileia tōn ouranōn emporos zētounti kalous margaritas — again the kingdom of the heavens is like a merchant seeking fine pearls (emporos: a traveling merchant, a wholesale trader — a person who knows the pearl market; kalous margaritas: fine/beautiful pearls — kalos, fine in quality, not just visually attractive): the merchant is an expert in pearls, not a casual buyer. His expertise is what enables him to recognize the exceptional pearl when he finds it.
13.46: heurōn de hena polytimō margaritēn — having found one extremely valuable pearl (polytimō: of great price, highly valued — poly + timē: much-price, maximum value) — apelthōn pepraken panta hosa eichen kai ēgorasen auton (going away he sold all that he had and bought it): the merchant's expertise culminates in recognition and total investment. Unlike the hidden-treasure man who stumbled on the find, the merchant was actively seeking. Both parables end with total divestment for total acquisition: the kingdom worth everything is actually acquired by those who count it as everything.
13.47: palin homoia estin hē basileia tōn ouranōn sagēnē blētheisē eis tēn thalassan — again the kingdom of the heavens is like a dragnet thrown into the sea (sagēnē: a large dragnet or seine net — a net weighted at the bottom and with floats at the top, dragged through the water and sweeping up everything in its path, not selective) — kai ek pantos genous synagagousē (and gathering fish of every kind — ek pantos genous: from every kind; synagagousē aorist participle of synagō: having gathered): the net is not a trap for a specific species but a total sweep. The sorting happens after the catch, not during.
13.48: hēn hote eplērōthē anabibazein epi ton aigialon — when it was full they drew it up onto the shore (eplērōthē aorist passive: was filled, reached capacity; anabibazein: to drag up) — kai kathisantes synelaexan ta kala eis angē (and sitting down they gathered the good into containers — kathisantes aorist participle: having sat down, the formal working posture; angeia: containers, vessels): the parable parallels the weeds-and-wheat explanation. The good fish are gathered first (vessels), then the bad are cast out — the inverse order from v.30, but the same outcome of final separation. The ta kala (the good/beautiful) contrasts with ta sapra (the rotten, v.48b).
13.49: houtōs estai en tē synteleia tou aiōnos — so it will be at the end of the age — the repetition of the synteleia formula (cf. v.40) frames the net-parable as the same eschatological scenario. Exeleusontai hoi angeloi kai aphoriousin tous ponērous ek mesou tōn dikaiōn (the angels will go out and separate the wicked from the midst of the righteous — aphoriousin future of aphorizō: to mark off by boundaries, to separate distinctly): the angels separate the wicked from the midst of the righteous — the mixture is inside the good, as in the field.
13.50: kai balousin autous eis tēn kaminon tou pyros — and they will throw them into the furnace of fire — the formula repeats v.42 verbatim: ekei estai ho klauthmos kai ho brygmos tōn odontōn (there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth).
