The repetition of the eschatological formula in both parable-interpretations (weeds and net) emphasizes the unity of the teaching: the same final separation is described from two angles (the field/humanity as a whole; the sea/the full scope of the mission's catch).
13.51: synēkate tauta panta — have you understood all these things? (synēkate aorist of syniēmi: the understanding-verb used throughout ch13, v.13,14,15,19,23; to bring together, comprehend) — legousin autō nai (they say to him: yes): the disciples' affirmative answer to Jesus's direct question sets up the final parable of v.52. Their understanding (however partial) qualifies them as the "scribes instructed in the kingdom of the heavens" described next. The question and answer mark the transition from parable-hearing to parable-understanding.
13.53: kai egeneto hote etelesen ho Iēsous tas parabolēs tautas — and it happened when Jesus had finished these parables (etelesen aorist of teleō: to complete — the discourse-closing formula, cf. 7:28; 11:1) — metēren ekeithen (he departed from there): the geographical transition marks the end of the parabolic discourse and the beginning of the hometown rejection pericope. The parables that revealed the kingdom to disciples and concealed it from the unperceptive now face a concrete test: the response of Jesus's own hometown.
13.54: kai elthōn eis tēn patrida autou edidasken autous en tē synagōgē autōn — coming to his hometown he was teaching them in their synagogue (patrida: homeland, native country — hometown; autōn: their synagogue, the possessive marking the same institutional distance as 12:9) — hōste ekplēssesthai autous (so that they were astonished — ekplēssesthai imperfect passive: were being knocked out, struck with astonishment — the strongest word for amazement): the reaction is not neutral wonder but surprised displacement. Pothen toutō hē sophia autē kai hai dynameis (from where does this man get this wisdom and these powers? — pothen: whence, the question of source that reveals they have no framework for the answer).
13.55: ouch houtos estin ho tou tektonos huios — isn't this the son of the craftsman? (tektōn: craftsman, worker in materials — the word covers carpenter, builder, stonemason, woodworker; it is not narrowly "carpenter" in the modern sense but a skilled craftsperson in the construction trades; Mark 6:3 applies the same term directly to Jesus) — Mariam: Mary is named directly; Iakōbos, Iōsēph, Simōn, Ioudas: the four brothers named. The family's ordinariness is the problem: the crowd's knowledge of Jesus's earthly origins prevents them from receiving his heavenly origin.
13.56: kai hai adelphai autou ouchai pasai pros hēmas eisin — aren't all his sisters here with us (hai adelphai: the sisters are mentioned but unnamed; their number is implied by pasai, all — suggesting at least two, probably more) — pothen oun toutō tauta panta (where then does this man get all these things? — the repeated pothen question frames the hometown rejection as a failure to reconcile Jesus's known origins with his unprecedented claims and deeds): the neighbors' intimate knowledge of his family becomes a barrier rather than a credential.
13.57: kai eskandalizonto en autō — and they were being caused to stumble by him (eskandalizonto imperfect passive: were being continually tripped up — the passive marks the cause as Jesus himself; skandalizō: to set a trap, to cause to stumble, the same word used for the path-hearer in 13:21 and for the stumbling-block of 11:6) — ho de Iēsous eipen autois: Jesus's response cites a proverb: ouk estin prophētēs atimos ei mē en tē patridi kai en tē oikia autou (a prophet is not dishonored except in his hometown and in his own house — atimos: without honor, dishonored): the proverb is attested in varied forms in ancient literature but receives its most pointed application here.
13.58: kai ouk epoiēsen ekei dynameis pollas dia tēn apistian autōn — and he did not do many miracles there because of their unbelief (apistia: unbelief, lack of faith — a- privative + pistis: the absence of the faith that is the receptor for miraculous healing; cf. 9:29: "according to your faith let it be done") — Matthew says pollas (many) rather than Mark's "none"; some healing occurred but the scale was drastically limited. The verse is not a theological claim that Jesus lacked power but that the reception of miracle requires the soil of faith; in its absence, miracles remain largely unsown.

14.27: tharsein — take courage, be of good cheer — an imperative of encouragement used in crisis moments; Jesus speaks it to the storm-panicked disciples and to the paralytic (9:2) and to the hemorrhaging woman (9:22). ego eimi — I am — the LXX divine self-disclosure formula (Exod 3:14, Isa 41:4, 43:10); spoken in the darkness on the sea to terrified disciples, the formula identifies Jesus with the divine presence that crossed the Red Sea. me phobeisthe — do not fear — the standard divine reassurance formula throughout the OT (Gen 15:1, Isa 41:10, 43:1).
14.1: en ekeinō tō kairō ēkousen Hērōdēs ho tetrarchēs tēn akoēn Iēsou — at that time Herod the tetrarch heard the report about Jesus (tetrarchēs: ruler of a quarter — Herod Antipas held the tetrarchy of Galilee and Perea under Roman appointment after his father Herod the Great's death; the title is less than "king" — v.9 uses "king" loosely as a courtesy title) — tēn akoēn Iēsou (the report/hearing about Jesus — akoē: what is heard, the public report): Herod's hearing connects back to the disciples being sent out in ch10 and the spreading fame since ch9.
14.2: kai eipen tois paisin autou — and he said to his servants/attendants — houtos estin Iōannēs ho baptistēs (this is John the Baptist): Herod's identification is diagnostic of a guilty conscience — he sees the miraculous deeds of Jesus and interprets them through the lens of his act against John. Autos ēgerthē apo tōn nekrōn (he has risen from the dead — ēgerthē aorist passive of egeirō: the resurrection verb; Herod uses the standard resurrection vocabulary to describe his fear): kai dia touto hai dynameis energousin en autō (and therefore miraculous powers are at work in him — energousin: are working, are active — the word from which "energy" derives).
14.3: ho gar Hērōdēs kratēsas ton Iōannēn edēsen — for Herod had seized John and bound him (kratēsas aorist participle of krateō: to seize, take hold of forcefully; edēsen aorist of deō: to bind — the binding vocabulary of 12:29 now applied literally to John) — kai en phylakē apetheto dia Hērōdiada tēn gynaika Philippou tou adelphou autou (and placed him in prison because of Herodias, the wife of his brother Philip): Herodias had left Philip to marry Antipas; John's denunciation of this as unlawful (v.4) was politically and dynastically dangerous.
14.4: elegen gar ho Iōannēs autō — for John had been saying to him (elegen imperfect: was repeatedly saying, habitually denouncing — not a single accusation but a sustained prophetic rebuke) — ouk exestin soi echein autēn (it is not lawful for you to have her — ouk exestin: the same lawfulness-formula as the Sabbath controversies of ch12): John applies the same Torah-appeal to a political marriage that Jesus applies to Sabbath healing. The Levitical prohibitions (Lev 18:16; 20:21) forbade a man from marrying his brother's wife while the brother lived.
14.5: kai thelōn auton apokteinai ephobēthē ton ochlon — wanting to kill him he was afraid of the crowd (thelōn present participle: continuously wanting/desiring to kill him; ephobēthē aorist passive: was made afraid): Herod's murderous desire is restrained not by scruple but by political calculation. Hoti hōs prophētēn auton eichon (because they held him as a prophet — eichon imperfect: were holding, in their considered estimation): the crowd's recognition of John as a prophet is the same recognition that produces Herod's restraint — and that produces the crowd's recognition in 21:26.
14.6: genesiois de genomenos tou Hērōdou — on the birthday celebration of Herod (genesia: birthday celebration — the word can refer to the birthday itself or the festivities; birthday celebrations for rulers were known in the Hellenistic world) — ōrchēsato hē thygatēr tēs Hērōdiados en tō mesō (the daughter of Herodias danced in the midst — ōrchēsato aorist of orcheomai: to dance; dancing as entertainment for male guests was associated with lower social status; a princess dancing publicly would have been unusual and attention-seizing) — kai ēresen tō Hērōdē (and pleased Herod — areskō): the dancing produces the rash oath.
14.7: hothen met horkous hōmologēsen autē dounai ho ean aitēsētai — whereupon he promised with an oath to give her whatever she might ask (met horkous: with oaths — plural, sworn solemnly; hōmologēsen aorist of homologeō: to publicly declare, promise, confess — the oath-making is a formal public commitment, not a private aside): the oath binds Herod before witnesses. Ho ean aitēsētai (whatever she might ask — aitēsētai aorist subjunctive: a fully open promise): the terms are blanket and unconditional, which the next verse exploits.
14.8: hē de probibastheisa hypo tēs mētros autēs — but she, prompted/led forward by her mother (probibastheisa aorist passive participle of probibazō: to cause to step forward, to incite, to put up to something — the mother is the agent behind the request) — dos moi phēsin hōde epi pinaki tēn kephalēn Iōannou tou baptistou (she says: give me here on a dish the head of John the Baptist — hōde: here, immediately; epi pinaki: on a platter, on a serving dish): the gruesome specificity of the request — a severed head delivered on serving-ware at a banquet — is designed to make refusal maximally embarrassing before the guests.
14.9: kai lypētheis ho basileus — and being grieved, the king (lypētheis aorist passive participle of lypeō: being grieved, distressed — Herod is not indifferent; his distress is real) — dia tous horkous kai tous synanakeimenous (because of the oaths and those reclining with him — the dinner guests): the two competing pressures are the public oath (which would dishonor him if broken) and the opinion of the guests (before whom he must appear strong). Herod is trapped by his own rash promise and his need to maintain honor. Ekeleusen dothēnai (he commanded it to be given).
14.10: kai pempsas apekephalisen Iōannēn en tē phylakē — and sending he had John beheaded in the prison (apekephalisen aorist of apokephalzō: to behead — a compound verb; apo: away from + kephalē: head): the execution is narrated with clinical brevity. Matthew provides no scene-setting; the bare fact of John's death stands as a marker of the opposition that Jesus will face. John's fate anticipates Jesus's: both die by the political authority's hand under pressure from religious/domestic opponents.
14.11: kai ēnechthē hē kephalē autou epi pinaki kai edothē tō korasio — and his head was brought on a platter and given to the girl (ēnechthē aorist passive: was brought/carried; the passive voice removes the bearers from sight, focusing on the object): the head's journey from prison to platter to girl to mother is told in four passive or impersonal verbs. Kai ēnenken tē mētri autēs (and she brought it to her mother): the chain of agency runs backward — Herod → executioner → girl → mother; the mother who initiated the sequence receives the result.
14.12: kai proselthontes hoi mathētai autou ēran to ptōma kai ethapsan auton — and his disciples coming took the body and buried it (ptōma: corpse, fallen body — the word for a fallen/dead body, from piptō: to fall) — kai elthontes apēngilan tō Iēsou (and coming they reported to Jesus): John's disciples perform the final act of loyalty — burial — and then bring the news to Jesus. The report to Jesus closes the John narrative and opens the withdrawal/feeding pericope: Jesus receives the news of John's death and withdraws (v.13).
14.13: akousas de ho Iēsous anechōrēsen ekeithen en ploiō eis erēmon topon — hearing this, Jesus withdrew in a boat to a deserted place (akousas aorist participle: having heard; anechōrēsen: withdrew — the same deliberate withdrawal verb as 12:15; erēmon topon: a deserted/solitary place — erēmos: without inhabitants, wild) — kat idian (privately, by himself): Jesus's withdrawal in response to John's death is more than grief; it is a strategic recognition that the political danger has escalated. Kai akousantes hoi ochloi ēkolouthēsan autō pezē apo tōn poleōn (the crowds, hearing, followed him on foot from the cities): the withdrawal is followed, not achieved.
14.14: kai exelthōn eiden polun ochlon — and going out he saw a large crowd — kai esplanchnisthē ep autois (and he had compassion on them — esplanchnisthē aorist passive of splagchnizomai: the strongest compassion-verb, from splanchnon/intestines; cf. 9:36): the compassion overrides the private grief that motivated the withdrawal. Kai etherapeuse tous arrōstous autōn (and he healed their sick ones — arrōstous: literally without strength, weakened, sick): the compassion-response is healing, not merely sympathy. The scene recalls 9:36 where compassion led to the harvest-prayer; here it produces direct ministry.
14.15: opsias de genomenes prosēlthon autō hoi mathētai legontes — evening coming on, the disciples came to him saying — erēmos estin ho topos kai hē hōra ēdē parēlthen (the place is deserted and the hour has already passed — parēlthen aorist: has gone by, is past; the meal-time has elapsed): the disciples' assessment is logistically accurate. Apoluson tous ochlous hina apelthontes eis tas kōmas agorasōsin heautois brōmata (send the crowds away so that going to the villages they might buy food for themselves): the disciples' solution is dispersal; they have no framework yet for what Jesus is about to do.
14.16: ho de Iēsous eipen autois ou chreian echousin apelthein — but Jesus said to them: they do not need to go away — dote autois hymeis phagein (you give them something to eat — hymeis: emphatic, YOU give them — the pronoun is the challenge): Jesus redirects responsibility back to the disciples. The command is absurd by any calculation of their resources (v.17: five loaves and two fish); its point is to expose the disciples' inadequacy and transfer the solution to Jesus. The feeding that follows is not a demonstration of Jesus's power independent of the disciples but happens through them: he gives to them, they give to the crowd.
14.17: hoi de legousin autō ouk echomen hōde ei mē pente artous kai duo ichthyas — they say to him: we have here only five loaves and two fish — the ei mē (except/only) clause marks the minimum: five loaves, two fish is subsistence for a handful of people, not thousands. The specification of the supplies before the miracle is a standard feature of miracle accounts that establishes the baseline against which the abundance will be measured. Hōde (here, at this location) further limits even this small supply to what is immediately at hand.
14.18: ho de eipen pherete moi hōde autous — he said: bring them here to me — the command pherete moi (bring to me) is the pivot: the disciples' inadequate resources become the material for Jesus's action when placed in his hands. The transfer of the loaves to Jesus precedes the multiplication; the miracle happens not by Jesus creating food independently but by transforming what is given to him. The pattern anticipates the disciples' role in distribution: they give to him; he multiplies; they distribute.
14.19: kai keleusas tous ochlous anapesein epi tou chortou — and commanding the crowds to recline on the grass (anapesein: to recline, the posture of reclining for a meal) — labōn tous pente artous kai tous duo ichthyas anablepas eis ton ouranon eulogēsen (taking the five loaves and the two fish, looking up to heaven, he blessed — anablepas eis ton ouranon: looking up to heaven, the prayer posture; eulogēsen aorist of eulogeō: to bless, to speak blessing over — the Jewish berakah form: "Blessed are you, Lord our God, king of the universe, who brings forth bread from the earth"): kai klesas edōken tois mathētais tous artous (and breaking he gave the loaves to the disciples — klesas: having broken; the eucharistic language anticipates 26:26).
14.20: kai ephagon pantes kai echortasthēsan — and they all ate and were satisfied (echortasthēsan aorist passive of chortazō: to be filled, to be satisfied — the word used in the fifth Beatitude, 5:6, for those "hungry and thirsting" for righteousness who will be filled; the same root as the animal-feeding satisfaction the Lord provides in Ps 147:9) — kai ēran to perisseuon tōn klasmatōn dōdeka kophinous plēreis (and they gathered the surplus of the fragments, twelve baskets full — kophinos: a wicker basket, specifically a Jewish type of basket; twelve baskets correspond to the twelve disciples, or symbolically to the twelve tribes).
14.21: hoi de esthiontes ēsan andres hōsei pentakischilioi — those eating were about five thousand men (andres: men, adult males — specifically counted; hōsei: approximately) — chōris gynaikōn kai paidiōn (besides women and children): Matthew adds this qualifier, absent from Mark; the total crowd is therefore substantially more than five thousand. The counting of men (andres) follows the military census custom; the surplus population (women and children) are additional. This specification increases the miraculous scale beyond what any simple estimate would yield.
14.22: kai eutheōs ēnankasen tous mathētas embēnai eis to ploion — and immediately he compelled the disciples to get into the boat (ēnankasen aorist of enankazō: to compel, force — a strong word: not a suggestion or invitation but a compelling, as if the disciples needed to be moved with force) — kai proagein auton eis to peran (to go on ahead of him to the other side): the disciples' departure before the dismissal of the crowds is forced by Jesus — perhaps because they would have wanted to stay with him. Heōs hou apolysei tous ochlous (while he dismissed the crowds — apolysei future: will dismiss; he completes the crowd-dismissal alone).
14.23: kai apolusas tous ochlous anebē eis to oros kat idian proseuxasthai — dismissing the crowds he went up on the mountain privately to pray (anebē eis to oros: went up on/into the mountain — the mountain in Matthew is frequently a locus of revelation and prayer: 5:1; 17:1; 28:16; kat idian: privately, by himself; proseuxasthai aorist infinitive of proseuchomai: to pray) — opsias de genomenes monos ēn ekei (evening coming, he was there alone): the evening is the second evening of v.15 (first evening = meal-time; second evening = nightfall). Jesus is alone on the mountain in prayer while the disciples battle the sea.
14.24: to de ploion ēdē stadious pollous apo tēs gēs apeichen — the boat was already many stades from land (stadious: a stade = ~185 meters; "many stades" = a significant distance from shore) — basanizomenon hypo tōn kymatōn (being tormented by the waves — basanizomenon present passive participle of basanizō: to torment, put to torture, originally of testing metals with a touchstone; used for the extreme distress of being battered by waves) — ēn gar enantios ho anemos (for the wind was contrary — enantios: against, opposing, adverse): the boat makes no progress against an opposing wind while Jesus is alone on the mountain and the disciples are alone on the sea.
14.25: tetartē de phylakē tēs nyktos ēlthen pros autous peripatōn epi tēn thalassan — in the fourth watch of the night he came to them walking on the sea (tetartē phylakē: the fourth watch — by Roman military reckoning, 3-6 AM; the darkest, most exhausted hour of the night) — peripatōn epi tēn thalassan (walking on the sea — peripatōn present active participle: actively walking, in motion): the divine act of walking on water echoes Job 9:8 LXX (God "walks on the sea as on firm ground") and Ps 77:19 (God's way through the sea). The OT background makes the action a theophanic sign.
14.26: hoi de mathētai idontes auton epi tēs thalassēs peripatounta etarachthēsan — the disciples seeing him walking on the sea were troubled (etarachthēsan aorist passive of tarassō: to be shaken, disturbed, troubled — the strongest word for inner agitation) — legontes hoti phantasma estin kai apo tou phobou ekraxan (saying it is a ghost, and from fear they cried out — phantasma: a phantom, apparition — the disciples' terror interprets Jesus's approach as a ghost): the cry from fear (apo tou phobou) precedes Jesus's word of reassurance. Fear is the disciples' first response to theophanic presence before recognition.
14.28: apokritheis de autō ho Petros eipen kyrie ei sy ei — Peter answering him said: Lord, if it is you (ei sy ei: if you are the one — the conditional is not doubt but verification; Peter wants confirmation before the request) — keleuson me elthein pros se epi ta hydata (command me to come to you on the waters — keleuson aorist imperative: command; pros se: toward you, not just "across the water" but specifically to Jesus): Peter's request is not a demonstration of faith-testing but an act of faith in Jesus's capacity to enable walking. The request positions the miracle as coming from Jesus's command, not Peter's initiative.
14.29: ho de eipen elthet kai katabalōn apo tou ploiou ho Petros periepatēsen epi ta hydata — he said: come; and getting down from the boat Peter walked on the water (elthe aorist imperative: come; katabalōn aorist participle of katabainō: having stepped down; periepatēsen aorist: walked) — kai ēlthen pros ton Iēsoun (and came toward Jesus): the single verb periepatēsen is the action; the coming-toward-Jesus is the goal. Peter's walking on the water is real and brief — the miracle happens and is then interrupted by a change of focus.
14.30: blepōn de ton anemon edeiiliasen — but seeing the wind he was afraid (blepōn present participle: seeing — the problem is the ongoing looking at the wind, not a glance; edeiliaseen aorist of deiliaō: to be cowardly, to be afraid) — kai arxamenos katapontizesthai (and beginning to sink — katapontizesthai: to go down under water, to be engulfed by the sea — a compound of kata: down and pontos: the deep sea): ekraxen legōn kyrie sōson me (he cried out saying: Lord, save me! — sōson aorist imperative of sōzō: the salvation word used for healing; Peter's cry invokes both physical rescue and the full weight of sōzō).
14.31: eutheōs de ho Iēsous ekteinas tēn cheira epelabeto autou — immediately Jesus stretching out his hand took hold of him (eutheōs: immediately — the same immediacy-word that marked shallow enthusiasm in 13:5,20; here it marks divine rescue speed; ekteinas tēn cheira: stretching out his hand — the reaching-gesture used in 8:3 for healing the leper and 12:49 for identifying the disciples as family) — oligopiste (you of little faith — oligopiste: a compound coined by Matthew, appearing in 6:30; 8:26; 16:8; 17:20: oligos small/little + pistis faith). Eis ti edistasas (why did you waver/doubt — distasas aorist of distazō: to be divided, to stand in two directions, to waver).
14.32: kai anabantōn autōn eis to ploion ekopasen ho anemos — and when they got into the boat the wind ceased (anabantōn autōn genitive absolute: they having gone up into the boat; ekopasen aorist of kopazō: to grow weary, to cease, to abate): the same sequence as the storm-calming of 8:26 where the rebuke of the wind produced a great calm. Here the wind stops at the moment of boarding without an explicit rebuke. The cessation is simultaneous with, but not structurally caused by, the boarding — the storm's end marks the completion of the theophanic episode.
14.33: hoi de en tō ploiō prosekynēsan autō legontes — those in the boat worshipped him saying (prosekynēsan aorist of proskyneō: to worship, to bow in reverence — the same word used in 2:2,11 for the Magi's worship and in 28:9,17 for post-resurrection worship; distinguished from the "marveling" of the crowd in 8:27 after the storm) — alēthōs theou huios ei (truly you are the Son of God — alēthōs: truly, genuinely — an emphatic adverb of affirmation; theou huios: Son of God with the genitive): this is the first corporate confession of Jesus as Son of God in Matthew; it answers the demons' correct identification (8:29) with genuine worship.
14.34: kai diaperasantes ēlthon epi tēn gēn Gennēsaret — crossing over they came to the land of Gennesaret (diaperasantes aorist participle of diaperaō: having crossed over, completed the sea-crossing) — Gennesaret is the fertile plain on the northwest shore of the Sea of Galilee, south of Capernaum. The geographical specificity grounds the ensuing healing summary (vv.35-36) in a specific locale, where Jesus's reputation is sufficient for the population to mobilize before he has even disembarked.
14.35: kai epignontes auton hoi andres tou topou ekeinou apesteilan eis holēn tēn perikhōron ekeinēn — recognizing him, the men of that place sent into all the surrounding region (epignontes aorist participle of epiginōskō: having recognized — the intensive recognition-verb) — kai prosēnegkan autō pantas tous kakōs echontas (and they brought to him all those who were ill — pantas: all; kakōs echontas: those having it badly, those ill): the regional mobilization to bring the sick to Jesus is the practical outworking of the recognition. Faith in his healing capacity is demonstrated by logistical effort before any word is spoken.
14.36: kai parekaloun auton hina monon apsōntai tou kraspedou tou himatiou autou — and they were beseeching him to let them just touch the fringe of his garment (parekaloun imperfect: were continuously beseeching; apsōntai aorist subjunctive: to touch once; kraspedon: the fringe/tassel of the garment, the tassels required by Num 15:38-39 — the same word as in 9:20 for the hemorrhaging woman) — kai hosoi ēpsanto diesōthēsan (and as many as touched were completely saved/healed — diesōthēsan aorist passive of diasōzō: to save/heal completely, the compound intensifying the totality): the report is collective; individuals are healed as the crowds make contact with the fringe.

15.8: ho laos houtos tois cheilesin me tima, he de kardia auton porro apechei ap emou — this people honors me with the lips but their heart is far from me — Matthew cites Isa 29:13 LXX as the proof-text for the Pharisees' tradition-over-Torah priority. Kardia (heart) in the Jewish anthropology is the seat of will, understanding, and intention — not merely emotion; far-heartedness is not coldness of feeling but absence of genuine covenantal alignment. matēn de sebontai me — in vain do they worship me — maten (vainly, to no purpose) makes the worship not merely imperfect but empty, producing nothing that constitutes true worship.
15.1: tote proserchontai tō Iēsou apo Ierosolymōn Pharisaioi kai grammateis — then Pharisees and scribes come to Jesus from Jerusalem (apo Ierosolymōn: from Jerusalem — this is not a local Galilean confrontation but a delegation from the religious capital; the phrase signals official, institutional opposition): the Jerusalem origin elevates the confrontation; these are not provincial critics but representatives of the establishment. The timing ("then") connects the purity controversy to the Gentile expansion hinted at in the feeding of ch14.
15.2: dia ti hoi mathētai sou parabainousin tēn paradosin tōn presbyterōn — why do your disciples transgress the tradition of the elders (paradosis tōn presbyterōn: the tradition of the elders — paradosis, that which is handed down, a transmitted tradition; presbyterōn, elders: the oral tradition attributed to revered predecessors, not the Torah itself) — ou gar niptontai tas cheiras autōn hotan arton esthiōsin (for they do not wash their hands when they eat bread): the charge is violation of oral-traditional purity rules, not a Mosaic command. The handwashing regulation is Pharisaic extension, not Sinai legislation.
15.3: dia ti kai hymeis parabainete tēn entolēn tou theou dia tēn paradosin hymōn — why do you also transgress the commandment of God for the sake of your tradition? — Jesus's counter-accusation inverts the charge: the Pharisees accuse the disciples of breaking human tradition; Jesus accuses the Pharisees of breaking divine commandment. Dia tēn paradosin hymōn (for the sake of your tradition — dia with the accusative: on account of, motivated by): tradition is the cause; the violated commandment is the effect. The Corban example (vv.4-6) is the evidence.
15.4: ho gar theos eipen tima ton patera kai tēn mētera — for God said: honor your father and mother (Exod 20:12; Deut 5:16) — kai ho kakologōn patera ē mētera thanatō teleutatō (and whoever speaks evil of father or mother, let them die — kakologōn present participle of kakologeō: to speak evil, to curse; thanatō teleutatō: let them die by death — the intensive idiom from Exod 21:17): the two commandments are placed together as a unit — the Fifth Commandment (honor) and the death penalty for its inversion (cursing). The positive and negative frame the absolute importance God attaches to parental honor.
15.5: hymeis de legete hos an eipē tō patri ē tē mētri — but you say: whoever says to his father or mother — dōron ho ean ex emou ōphelēthēs (a gift — whatever you would have received from me is Corban/devoted to God — dōron: a gift, an offering): the Corban formula (korban in Mark 7:11, Aramaic for dedicated offering) allowed a person to declare property "devoted to God" as a vow, thereby making it legally unavailable to parents even while the person retained practical use of it. The declaration was a legal evasion of the Fifth Commandment's financial obligations.
15.6: kai ou mē timēsei ton patera autou — and he will not honor his father (ou mē + subjunctive: emphatic negation — will certainly not honor him) — kai ēkyrōsate ton logon tou theou dia tēn paradosin hymōn (and you have nullified the word of God for the sake of your tradition — ēkyrōsate aorist of akyroō: to invalidate, make void — a legal term for annulling a binding decree): the accusation is precise: the oral tradition does not supplement but cancels the divine command. Ton logon tou theou (the word of God) is the same phrase used in 13:19 for the word of the kingdom.
15.7: hypokritai kalōs eprophēteusen peri hymōn Ēsaias — hypocrites, Isaiah prophesied well about you (hypokritai: actors, those who play a role while concealing their true identity — the theatrical word now established as Jesus's standard term for the Pharisees; cf. 6:2,5,16; 23:13-29) — kalōs eprophēteusen (prophesied well/rightly): the Isaiah citation is not a forced application but an exact match; Isaiah described them before they existed, which Jesus calls prophesying "well" (accurately, fittingly).
15.9: matēn de sebontai me didaskontes didaskalias entalmata anthrōpōn — they worship me in vain, teaching as teachings commandments of men (matēn: in vain, for nothing, pointlessly — an adverb of futility; sebontai: they revere, worship — the worship is genuine in form but empty in content) — didaskalias entalmata anthrōpōn (commandments of men as teachings — entalma: a commandment, a prescribed rule; the phrase echoes Isa 29:13 LXX): the Corban ruling is not a marginal case but the symptom of a systemic substitution of human prescription for divine command.
15.10: kai proskalesamenos ton ochlon eipen autois — calling the crowd to him he said to them — akouete kai syniete (hear and understand — akouete present imperative: keep hearing; syniete present imperative: keep putting it together, keep understanding): the double imperative invites the crowd into the purity debate that the Pharisees framed as purely internal to the tradition. Jesus makes the issue public, democratizing what the scribal system had restricted to expert debate.
15.11: ou to eisercomenon eis to stoma koinoi ton anthrōpon — not what enters the mouth defiles the person (koinoi present of koinoō: to make common, to defile ritually — koinos means common in the sense of ordinary/unclean, not the sacral category) — alla to ekporeuomenon ek tou stomatos touto koinoi ton anthrōpon (but what goes out of the mouth, this defiles the person): the statement is revolutionary — it relocates the locus of defilement from external contact (food, handwashing) to internal production (speech, and by implication thought; v.18-19 makes this explicit). Mark adds "thus he declared all foods clean" (7:19).
15.12: tote proselthontes hoi mathētai legousin autō — then the disciples coming said to him — oidas hoti hoi Pharisaioi akousantes ton logon eskandalisthēsan (do you know that the Pharisees hearing the word were offended/caused to stumble — eskandalisthēsan aorist passive of skandalizō: were caused to stumble, took offense; the word carries the connotation of a fatal tripping, not mere irritation): the disciples are worried about the political fallout. Jesus's response (vv.13-14) does not address the politics but judges the Pharisees' spiritual root.
15.13: pasa phyteia hēn ouk ephyteusen ho patēr mou ho ouranios ekrizōthēsetai — every plant that my heavenly Father has not planted will be uprooted (phyteia: a planting, something planted — phyteuō: to plant; ekrizōthēsetai future passive of ekrizoō: to uproot, pull up by the roots): the imagery of planting and uprooting echoes the parable of the wheat and tares (13:24-30) and the Baptist's warning (3:10: "every tree not bearing good fruit is cut down"). The heavenly Father as the gardener determines what survives the final assessment.
15.14: aphete autous typhlos eisin hodēgoi — leave them; they are blind guides (typhlos hodēgoi: blind guides — the compound describes their double failure: they lead without sight) — typhlon de typhlos ean hodēgē (if blind leads blind — the two blindnesses in the chain) — amphoteroi eis bothynon pesountai (both will fall into a pit — bothynon: a hole in the ground, a well-pit): the Pharisees are not merely wrong but actively dangerous to their followers. The image was proverbial in antiquity; Jesus applies the proverb with precision to the situation of Israel's religious leadership.
15.15: apokritheis de ho Petros eipen autō phrason hēmin tēn parabolēn — Peter answering said to him: explain the parable to us (phrason aorist imperative of phrazō: to explain, point out, indicate clearly) — the request uses the parable-explanation vocabulary of 13:36, where the disciples asked for the weeds-parable explanation. Peter is asking for the private decoding the disciples received in ch13. The disciples are still in the transitional state between public parable-hearing and private parable-understanding.
15.16: ho de eipen akmēn kai hymeis asyneti este — he said: are you still also without understanding (akmēn: still, even now — a classical Greek adverb marking continuation of a state; asyneti: without understanding — a- privative + synetos, the adjective from syniēmi: lacking the faculty of synthesis/comprehension): the gentle rebuke connects to the understanding-vocabulary of ch13. The disciples, though privileged to receive explanations, are still not fully understanding.
15.17: ou noeite hoti pan to eisporeuomenon eis to stoma eis tēn koilian chōrei — do you not perceive that everything entering the mouth goes into the stomach (noeite present of noeō: to perceive, apprehend intellectually — the cognitive dimension of understanding; the stomach serves only as a way-station, not a defiler) — kai eis aphedrōna ekballetai (and is cast out into the latrine — aphedrōn: privy, toilet — a frank anatomical term): the digestive argument is deliberately physical; food passes through and out of the body without leaving a lasting moral trace. The moral life is not determined by what the body processes.
15.18: ta de ekporeuomena ek tou stomatos ek tēs kardias exerchetai — but what comes out of the mouth comes from the heart (exerchetai present: comes out, flows out — a continuous outward flow from the inner source; kardia: the heart as the center of will, desire, intention, and moral character in Jewish anthropology) — kakeina koinoi ton anthrōpon (and those things defile the person — kakeina: and those things; the pronoun refers to the mouth's outputs that originate in the heart): the principle reverses the Pharisaic framework — purity is an inside-out matter, not an outside-in ritual.
15.19: ek gar tēs kardias exerchontai dialogismoi ponēroi — for out of the heart come evil deliberations/thoughts (dialogismoi ponēroi: evil deliberations — dialogismos: a reasoning, calculation, deliberate thought; not passing emotions but considered choices that lead to action) — the list that follows covers the second table of the Decalogue plus extensions: phonoi (murders), moicheiai (adulteries), porneiai (sexual immoralities), klopai (thefts), pseudomarturiai (false testimonies), blasphēmiai (slanders/blasphemies): six plural nouns covering the full range of interpersonal and religious violations. The heart is the origin of all of these, not external contamination.
15.20: tauta estin ta koinounta ton anthrōpon — these are the things that defile the person — to de aniptois chersin phagein ou koinoi ton anthrōpon (but eating with unwashed hands does not defile the person): the return to the original charge (v.2) completes the argument. The purity controversy is resolved not by lowering the standard of purity but by relocating it: unwashed-hands eating is a non-issue; speaking from an evil heart is the real defilement. The verdict is direct and unambiguous — "does not defile" (ou koinoi).
15.21: kai exelthōn ekeithen ho Iēsous anechōrēsen eis ta merē Tyrou kai Sidōnos — going out from there Jesus withdrew to the regions of Tyre and Sidon (anechōrēsen: withdrew — the deliberate-withdrawal verb; ta merē Tyrou kai Sidōnos: the regions of Tyre and Sidon — the non-Jewish northwest coastal territories): the Canaanite woman encounter happens in Gentile territory — a geographical shift that frames her faith as exceptional precisely because she is outside the covenant. The cities Jesus denounced in 11:21-22 for not repenting are now the setting for a faith-encounter.
15.22: kai idou gynē Chananaios apo tōn horiōn ekeinōn exelthousa ekrazen — and behold, a Canaanite woman from those regions came out and cried (Chananaios: Canaanite — an archaic LXX ethnic label for the pre-Israelite inhabitants of the land; Mark 7:26 uses "Syro-Phoenician" as the contemporary geographic term. Matthew's use of the archaic name invokes the covenant-history of Canaan and Israel) — kyrie huie Dauid eleēson me (Lord, Son of David, have mercy on me — the Davidic-Messiah address from a Canaanite, not an Israelite; she knows the messianic title even outside the covenant community).
15.23: ho de ouk apekrithē autē logon — but he did not answer her a word (ouk apekrithē autē logon: the idiom is literally "did not answer her a word" — a complete, deliberate silence): the silence is not indifference but a test. The disciples' response — apoluson autēn hoti krazei opisthen hēmōn (send her away, for she keeps crying after us) — is ambiguous: they may want her dismissed or want Jesus to grant her request so she stops following. Either way, their motive is self-interest, not compassion.
15.24: ouk apestellēn ei mē eis ta probata ta apolōlota oikou Israēl — I was sent only to the lost sheep of the house of Israel (ouk...ei mē: not...except, only; the exclusive limitation is stated directly; ta probata ta apolōlota: the lost sheep — perfect passive participle of apollymi: the sheep in a state of lostness; echoing 10:6 where the disciples were sent to the same lost sheep) — the limitation is not permanent (28:19 gives the universal commission) but is the scope of the earthly mission to Israel. The Canaanite woman's faith will overcome this territorial limitation.
15.25: hē de elthousa prosekynei autō legousa kyrie boēthei moi — but she came and was worshipping him saying: Lord, help me (prosekynei imperfect: was continuously worshipping, kneeling — an ongoing posture of petition; boēthei present imperative of boētheō: help, assist — boē + theō: run to a cry): the woman persists despite the silence and the limitation-statement. Her address shifts from "Son of David" (messianic title) to "Lord" (kyrie, the personal address of relationship), marking an escalation of intimacy in her appeal.
15.26: ouk estin kalon labein ton arton tōn teknōn kai balein tois kynarois — it is not good to take the children's bread and throw it to the little dogs (kalon: good, appropriate, fitting; teknōn: children, the covenant people; kynarois: diminutive — little dogs, puppies, house dogs — not the derogatory kyōn of 7:6 but the domestic diminutive, softening the comparison): the image sets up the woman's response. The diminutive kynarion leaves space for the response of v.27 — household dogs eat under the table alongside the children.
15.27: hē de eipen nai kyrie kai gar ta kynarion esthiei apo tōn psichion — but she said: yes Lord, for even the little dogs eat from the crumbs (nai: yes — she accepts the comparison without resentment; kai gar: for even, precisely because of this: the particle combination accepts and extends the argument) — tōn piptontōn apo tēs trapezēs tōn kyriōn autōn (that fall from the table of their masters — trapezēs tōn kyriōn autōn: the masters' table): she takes the image and turns it: if she is a household dog, then the crumbs of the children's meal are still a share in it. Her argument is not a claim of equality but of proximity.
15.28: tote apokritheis ho Iēsous eipen autē ō gynai megalē sou hē pistis — then answering Jesus said to her: O woman, your faith is great (megalē sou hē pistis: your faith is great — megalē, the adjective of greatness; the contrast with the disciples' oligopistia is sharp: the Canaanite woman receives the superlative-positive while the disciples repeatedly receive the negative) — genēthētō soi hōs theleis (let it be done for you as you wish — genēthētō aorist passive imperative: let it become, let it happen — the same form as "your will be done" in the Lord's Prayer). Kai iathē hē thygatēr autēs apo tēs hōras ekeinēs — her daughter was healed from that hour.
15.29: kai metabas ekeithen ho Iēsous ēlthen para tēn thalassan tēs Galilaias — moving on from there Jesus came alongside the Sea of Galilee (metabas aorist participle of metabainō: having moved on from one place to another; the verb marks deliberate geographical transition) — kai anabas eis to oros ekathēto ekei (and going up on the mountain he was sitting there — ekathēto imperfect: was sitting in the teaching position): the mountainside setting for the healing summary deliberately echoes the Sermon on the Mount (5:1). Jesus sits in the position of the authoritative teacher; the healings that follow are the teacher's actions, not merely miracles.
15.30: kai prosēlthon autō ochloi polloi echontes meth heautōn chōlous typhlous — and large crowds came to him having with them lame, blind — the list continues: kyllous (crippled), kōphous (mute/deaf), and many others. Kai erripsan autous para tous podas autou (and cast them at his feet — erripsan aorist of rhiptō: to throw down, cast at the feet — the same verb used in 9:36 for the crowds being "cast down" like helpless sheep): the casting at Jesus's feet is a formal act of bringing helpless people to the healer's authority. Kai etherapeuse autous — and he healed them.
15.31: hōste tous ochlous thaumasai bleponton kōphous lalountas — so that the crowds marveled, seeing mute people speaking — the healing list includes kyllous hygieis (crippled made sound), chōlous peripatountas (lame walking), typhlous blepontas (blind seeing): the same categories as the messianic answer to John (11:5). Kai edoxasan ton theon Israēl (and they glorified the God of Israel — Israēl: the God of Israel specifically, which suggests these crowds may be Gentiles marveling at Israel's God): the praise identifies the source of the healings as the covenant God.
15.32: ho de Iēsous proskalesamenos tous mathētas autou eipen splanchnizomai epi ton ochlon — calling his disciples to him Jesus said: I have compassion for the crowd (splanchnizomai: the visceral compassion verb, used also in 9:36 and 14:14; here Jesus speaks the compassion explicitly rather than the narrator reporting it) — hēmerai treis prosmenousin moi kai ouk echousin ti phagōsin (they have remained with me three days and have nothing to eat): the three-day duration suggests a sustained teaching event, not a single encounter. Kai apolusai autous nēsteis ou thelō (and I do not want to send them away hungry — ou thelō: I am not willing; the verb of will).
15.33: kai legousin autō hoi mathētai pothen hēmin en erēmia artoi tosoutoi — the disciples say to him: where in a deserted place do we have enough bread for such a crowd — the disciples' question is remarkable given that they participated in the first feeding. Their failure to connect the two situations is not stupidity but a pattern of forgetting that Jesus will explicitly rebuke in 16:9-10. Hōste chortasai ochlon tosouton (so as to satisfy such a crowd — chortasai: to fill to satisfaction, the same verb as 14:20): the question expresses genuine inadequacy, not unbelief.
15.34: kai legei autois ho Iēsous posous artous echete — Jesus says to them: how many loaves do you have? (posous: how many — the question is identical to Mark 8:5): hoi de eipan hepta kai oligia ichthydia (they said: seven, and a few small fish — oligia ichthydia: a few small fish; the diminutive ichthydia emphasizes their smallness): the inventory is different from the first feeding (five loaves + two fish) but equally inadequate for the crowd. The specific number seven will be recalled in 16:10.
15.35: kai parangeilas tō ochlō anapesein epi tēn gēn — commanding the crowd to recline on the ground (parangeilas aorist participle of parangellō: to pass along an order, to command authoritatively): the command to sit is a direct action by Jesus, identical in structure to 14:19. The crowd's compliance before any food is visible is itself an act of trust. The reclining posture (anapesein) is the formal meal-posture, making the feeding a proper meal event.
15.36: kai labōn tous hepta artous kai tous ichthyas eucharistēsas eklasen — taking the seven loaves and the fish, giving thanks he broke them (eucharistēsas aorist participle of eucharistō: to give thanks — distinguished from 14:19's eulogēsen (blessed/invoked blessing): the first feeding uses eulogeō, the Jewish berakah form; the second uses eucharistō, the word that gives "Eucharist" its name) — kai edidou tois mathētais hoi de mathētai tois ochlois (and he was giving to the disciples, and the disciples to the crowds): the distribution chain is identical to ch14.
15.37: kai ephagon pantes kai echortasthēsan — they all ate and were satisfied (echortasthēsan: the satisfaction-verb, cf. 14:20) — kai to perisseuon tōn klasmatōn ēran hepta spyridas plēreis (and they picked up the leftover fragments, seven baskets full — spyridas: large wicker hampers, a different word from the kophinoi of 14:20 which were personal Jewish wicker baskets; spyris is a larger carrying-basket): the different basket-type is not incidental — Jesus specifically references both numbers and basket-types in 16:9-10, indicating the distinction matters for interpretation.
15.38: hoi de esthiontes ēsan tetrakischilioi andres — those eating were four thousand men (tetrakischilioi: four thousand — a round number; the distinction between the first (five thousand) and second (four thousand) feedings suggests two separate events, not two versions of one) — chōris gynaikōn kai paidiōn (besides women and children — the same qualifier as 14:21): Matthew's consistent addition of women and children to the male count indicates the total was substantially more than four thousand.
15.39: kai apolusas tous ochlous enebē eis to ploion kai ēlthen eis ta horia Magadan — dismissing the crowds he got into the boat and came to the region of Magadan (apolusas: having dismissed; Magadan: an otherwise unattested place name; Mark 8:10 has Dalmanutha; both may be variant names for the same location on the western shore of the Sea of Galilee): the geographical transition moves from the mountain-feeding to the sign-demand of ch16:1.

16.16: su ei ho Christos ho huios tou theou tou zontos — you are the Christ the Son of the living God — Peter's confession stacks three titles: Christos (the anointed one = the Messiah), huios tou theou (Son of God = the one in a unique filial relationship to the Father), and tou zontos (of the living God = the God who is distinctively, characteristically alive, contrasted with dead idols). The full Christological confession combines royal Messianism, divine sonship, and the living covenant-God.
16.18: su ei Petros kai epi taute te petra oikodomeso mou ten ekklesian — you are Peter (Petros) and on this rock (petra) I will build my church — the wordplay is clearer in Aramaic where both are kepha (Cephas). The referent of petra is debated: Peter himself as the founding confessor, Peter's confession of Christ, or Christ himself as the foundational rock (1 Cor 10:4). Matthew's context suggests Peter-as-confessor: the one who receives the revelation (v.17) is the one on whom the community is built. ekklesia — assembly, congregation — Matthew is the only Gospel to use the word; it translates Hebrew qahal (the covenant assembly of Israel).
16.1: kai proselthontes hoi Pharisaioi kai Saddoukaioi peirazontes — the Pharisees and Sadducees came, testing him (peirazontes present participle of peirazō: testing with hostile intent — the same word used in 4:1 for Satan's temptation of Jesus) — epērōtēsan auton sēmeion ek tou ouranou epideixai autois (asked him to show them a sign from heaven — ek tou ouranou: from heaven, as distinct from earthly/miraculous signs; they want a cosmic, divine-authentication sign): the Pharisees and Sadducees are normally opponents; their united front signals the gravity of the test. The same demand (with "except the sign of Jonah") appears in 12:38-40.
16.2: opsias genomenas legete eudia — evening coming, you say: fair weather (opsias: evening, the late afternoon sky before sunset; eudia: fair weather, clear sky) — pyrrazei gar ho ouranos (for the sky is red/fiery — pyrrazei present of pyrrazō: to be red like fire): the weather-reading examples work by the same logic as the sign-reading Jesus demands of them. Red sky at evening = good weather; red sky in the morning = storm. The observation is accurate meteorology for the Mediterranean.
16.3: kai prōi sēmeron cheimōn — and in the morning, today stormy (cheimōn: storm, winter weather) — pyrrazei gar stygnazon ho ouranos (for the sky is red and threatening/overcast — stygnazon: dark, gloomy, threatening): the weather-sign contrast establishes the principle. To men prosōpon tou ouranou ginōskete diakrinein (you know how to discern the face of the sky — diakrinein: to distinguish, discern precisely): ta de sēmeia tōn kairōn ou dynasthe (but the signs of the times you cannot — ta sēmeia tōn kairōn: the signs of the appointed moments, the marks of the eschatological season Jesus's ministry inaugurates).
16.4: genea ponēra kai moichalis sēmeion epizētei — a wicked and adulterous generation seeks a sign — the same verdict as 12:39. Kai sēmeion ou dothēsetai autē ei mē to sēmeion Iōna (and a sign will not be given except the sign of Jonah — the single exception: the death-and-resurrection sign): kai katalipōn autous apēlthen (and leaving them he went away): the departure is abrupt and complete. The Pharisees and Sadducees receive only the answer they already received in ch12; there is nothing more to say.
16.5: kai elthontes hoi mathētai eis to peran epelathonto artous labein — coming to the other side the disciples forgot to take bread (epelathonto aorist of epilanthanō: to forget entirely — they had completely forgotten) — the transition from the sign-demand (v.4) to the disciples' bread-forgetfulness (v.5) sets up the leaven-warning of v.6. The disciples' physical bread-anxiety will be contrasted with the spiritual "leaven" warning Jesus is about to deliver; their confusion between literal and metaphorical bread (v.7) exposes their still-incomplete understanding.
16.6: ho de Iēsous eipen autois horate kai prosechete apo tēs zymēs tōn Pharisaiōn kai Saddoukaiōn — Jesus said to them: see and beware of the leaven of the Pharisees and Sadducees (horate kai prosechete: two imperatives — see/look out and be careful; prosechete apo: guard yourselves from, beware of — a warning verb) — zymēs tōn Pharisaiōn kai Saddoukaiōn (the leaven of the Pharisees and Sadducees): v.12 decodes this as their teaching, but the disciples initially take it literally because they forgot bread.
16.7: hoi de dielogizonto en heautois legontes hoti artous ouk elabomen — they were debating among themselves saying that we took no bread (dielogizonto imperfect of dialogizomai: were deliberating, reasoning back and forth — the same internal-reasoning verb as 9:3-4 where the scribes deliberated about blasphemy) — the disciples assume Jesus is criticizing them for the bread-forgetting rather than warning against a metaphorical contamination. Their literal-mindedness after two miraculous feedings is precisely what Jesus will rebuke in v.8-10.
16.8: gnous de ho Iēsous eipen ti dialogizesthe en heautois oligopistoi — knowing this, Jesus said: why are you deliberating among yourselves, you of little faith (oligopistoi: the coined compound, plural — addressed to the whole group for the first time in this form): hoti artous ouk echete (that you have no bread?): the question is partly rhetorical — Jesus is not confused about what they are discussing but names their deliberation as the product of small faith. The bread-anxiety reveals that they have not drawn the lessons of the two feedings.
16.9: oupō noeite — do you still not understand (noeite: perceive, apprehend — the intellectual perception verb; oupō: not yet, still not): oude mnēmoneuete tous pente artous tōn pentakischiliōn kai posous kophinous elabete (nor do you remember the five loaves for the five thousand and how many baskets you gathered — kophinous: the Jewish wicker baskets of ch14, named specifically to distinguish from the second feeding's spyridas in v.10): Jesus cites the specific basket-type from each feeding, showing the two events are distinct and both are to be remembered.
16.10: oude tous hepta artous tōn tetrakischiliōn kai posous spyridas elabete — nor the seven loaves for the four thousand and how many baskets you gathered (spyridas: the large hampers of ch15 — Jesus uses the same word Matthew used in 15:37, confirming these are two distinct events, not one account retold): the juxtaposition of kophinous (ch14) and spyridas (ch15) in Jesus's own summary of both feedings demonstrates that Matthew's use of different basket-words is intentional and carries meaning: two feedings, two communities (Jewish/Gentile contexts suggested), two basket-types.
16.11: pōs ou noeite hoti ou peri artou eipon hymin — how is it you do not understand that I did not speak to you about bread — the question is a gentle frustration: after two miraculous feedings, anxiety about physical bread is irrational. The disciples have a repeated inability to move from literal to metaphorical when Jesus uses material imagery. Prosechete de apo tēs zymēs tōn Pharisaiōn kai Saddoukaiōn (but beware of the leaven of the Pharisees and Sadducees): the repetition of the warning without decoding it invites the disciples to make the metaphorical move themselves.
16.12: tote synēkan hoti ouk eipen prosechein apo tēs zymēs tōn artōn — then they understood that he did not say to beware of the leaven of bread (synēkan aorist of syniēmi: they understood at once — the breakthrough understanding that has been building through the chapter) — alla apo tēs didachēs tōn Pharisaiōn kai Saddoukaiōn (but from the teaching of the Pharisees and Sadducees): the decoding reveals that "leaven" = "teaching": pernicious, spreading influence. The sign-demand of v.1 and the return-with-no-bread of v.5 were both setups for the disciples to learn to read spiritual signs over physical ones.
16.13: elthōn de ho Iēsous eis ta merē Kaisareias tēs Philippou — coming to the region of Caesarea Philippi (Kaisareia hē Philippou: Caesarea of Philip — the Hellenistic city built by Philip the Tetrarch near Banias/Paneas at the foot of Mount Hermon; a heavily Gentile city with a sanctuary to Pan, hence the ancient name Paneas — the northern limit of Jesus's recorded travels) — ērōta tous mathētas autou legōn tina legousin hoi anthrōpoi einai ton huion tou anthrōpou (he was asking his disciples saying: who do people say the Son of Man is?): the location — far from Jerusalem, in Gentile-associated territory — heightens the confession that follows.
16.14: hoi de eipan hoi men Iōannēn ton baptistēn — they said: some say John the Baptist — the popular identifications (John the Baptist, Elijah, Jeremiah, one of the prophets) all have in common that they place Jesus within the prophetic tradition rather than exceeding it. Alloi de Ēlian (others Elijah): the Malachi-Elijah expectation (Mal 4:5). Heteroi de Ieremian ē hena tōn prophētōn (others Jeremiah or one of the prophets): Jeremiah's association with Jesus is unique to Matthew; the tradition of Jeremiah's return may relate to the legend of Jeremiah hiding the temple vessels before the exile.
16.15: legei autois hymeis de tina me legete einai — he says to them: but who do you say I am (hymeis de: emphatic you — contrasted with "people" of v.13; the question now comes directly to the disciples; tina: who, what identity?) — the shift from third-person ("who do people say") to second-person ("who do you say") is the pivot of the entire Gospel to this point. The disciples have traveled with Jesus, received private explanations, witnessed miracles, and received special teaching. Now they must answer for themselves who they believe he is.
16.17: apokritheis de ho Iēsous eipen autō makarios ei Simōn Bar Iōna — answering, Jesus said to him: blessed are you, Simon son of Jonah (Bar Iōna: Aramaic patronymic — son of Jonah/John; the Aramaic patronymic in a Greek text marks a moment of special personal address) — hoti sarx kai haima ouk apekalypsēn soi (because flesh and blood did not reveal this to you — sarx kai haima: the Semitic idiom for human capacity, human resources, the physical-creaturely realm): alla ho patēr mou ho en tois ouranois (but my Father who is in the heavens): the confession is declared a supernatural disclosure, not a reasoned conclusion reached by human intelligence.
16.19: dōsō soi tas kleidas tēs basileias tōn ouranōn — I will give you the keys of the kingdom of the heavens (kleidas: keys — the instrument of opening and closing, granting or denying access; in Jewish imagery, the key-holder has administrative authority: the key of David in Isa 22:22; Rev 3:7) — kai ho ean dēsēs epi tēs gēs estai dedemenon en tois ouranois (and whatever you bind on earth will be bound in heaven — dedemenon perfect passive: will be in a state of having been bound — the perfect passive in heaven may indicate that heaven ratifies the earthly binding; the rabbinic formula for binding/loosing was used for prohibiting/permitting halakhic rulings, not just for church discipline): kai ho ean lysēs epi tēs gēs estai lelumenon en tois ouranois — and whatever you loose on earth will be loosed in heaven.
16.20: tote diesteilato tois mathētais hina mēdeni eipōsin hoti autos estin ho Christos — then he charged the disciples that they should tell no one that he is the Messiah (diesteilato aorist of diastellomai: to give explicit orders, to charge strictly) — the messianic secret following Peter's confession is not denial of the title but management of its timing and interpretation. The disciples must not announce the Messiahship before its meaning has been defined by the passion (v.21). An uninterpreted "Messiah" announcement would be misunderstood in political terms; the passion must first reframe what "Messiah" means.
16.21: apo tote ērxato ho Iēsous deiknynai tois mathētais autou — from that time Jesus began to show his disciples (apo tote ērxato: from that time he began — one of Matthew's structural markers; the first "from that time" is 4:17 at the start of the Galilean ministry; this second marks a new phase: the passion-instruction phase) — hoti dei auton eis Ierosolyma apelthein (that it is necessary for him to go to Jerusalem — dei: it is necessary, divinely necessary — not compelled externally but internally required by the divine plan): the first passion prediction names Jerusalem, suffering by elders/chief priests/scribes, death, and resurrection on the third day.
16.22: kai proslabomenos auton ho Petros ērxato epitiman autō legōn — taking him aside Peter began to rebuke him saying (proslabomenos: taking aside — Peter separates Jesus from the others for the private rebuke; ērxato epitiman: began to rebuke — epitimaō, the rebuke-verb used for silencing demons and rebuking wind/waves) — ileos soi kyrie (God be merciful to you, Lord — ileos soi: may God be gracious/merciful to you; not a mere interjection but an invocation of divine protection): ou mē estai soi touto (this will never happen to you — the strongest possible negation).
16.23: ho de strapheis tō Petrō eipen hypage opisō mou Satana — turning to Peter he said: get behind me, Satan (strapheis: turning — a deliberate physical reorientation; hypage opisō mou: go behind me — the discipleship-command reversed: Peter who was called to follow-behind-Jesus is now told to return to behind-Jesus, away from in-front-of-Jesus where he was blocking the path) — skandalon ei emou (you are a stumbling-block to me — skandalon: a trap, an obstacle, a stumbling-block — placed in Jesus's path): hoti ou phroneis ta tou theou alla ta tōn anthrōpōn (for you are minding the things of God, but the things of men — phroneis: the present tense of phroneō, to set the mind on, to be oriented toward).
16.24: tote ho Iēsous eipen tois mathētais autou ei tis thelei opisō mou elthein — then Jesus said to his disciples: if anyone wants to come after me (opisō mou elthein: to come behind me — the standard discipleship formula) — aparnēsasthō heauton kai aratō ton stauron autou kai akoloutheitō moi (let him deny himself and take up his cross and follow me): three aorist imperatives forming the discipleship-definition: self-denial (aparnēsasthō: to deny completely, disown oneself — the same verb as Peter's denial in 26:70,72); cross-taking (aratō ton stauron autou: take up his cross — the specific instrument of execution; cf. 10:38); following (akoloutheitō present imperative: ongoing following).
16.25: hos gar ean thelē tēn psychēn autou sōsai apolesei autēn — for whoever wants to save his life will lose it (psychēn: life/soul — the word spanning physical survival, personal identity, and the whole self; sōsai: to save, preserve; apolesei future: will lose, will destroy) — hos d an apolese tēn psychēn autou heneken emou heurēsei autēn (but whoever loses his life for my sake will find it — heneken emou: for my sake, on account of me — the criterion): the paradox operates on psychē: protecting the self-life destroys it; surrendering it to Jesus finds the true life. Cf. 10:39 where the same saying appears in the mission discourse.
16.26: ti gar ōphelēthēsetai anthrōpos ean ton kosmon holon kerdēsē — for what will it profit a person if he gains the whole world (ōphelēthēsetai future passive of ōpheleō: what benefit will accrue; kerdēsē aorist subjunctive of kerdainō: to gain, to profit financially) — tēn de psychēn autou zēmiōthē (but loses his soul — zēmiōthē aorist passive of zēmioō: to suffer loss, to be penalized, to forfeit — a commercial/legal term for incurring a loss): ē ti dōsei anthrōpos antallagna tēs psychēs autou (or what will a person give in exchange for his soul — antallagna: a counter-exchange, the price paid to recover something surrendered).
16.27: mellei gar ho huios tou anthrōpou erchesthai en tē doxē tou patros autou meta tōn angelōn autou — for the Son of Man is about to come in the glory of his Father with his angels (mellei: is about to, is going to — the verb of imminent futurity; en tē doxē tou patros autou: in the glory of his Father — the eschatological coming in divine glory) — kai tote apodōsei hekastō kata tēn praxin autou (and then he will repay each one according to his practice/actions — praxin: action, practice, deed — the Judgment involves what one habitually does, not merely a single act): the ground for the cross-bearing saying (v.24-26) is the coming judgment.
16.28: amen legō hymin hoti eisin tines tōn hōde hestōtōn — truly I tell you there are some of those standing here (tōn hōde hestōtōn: those standing here — a specific reference to the physical persons present) — oitines ou mē geusōntai thanatou heōs an idōsin ton huion tou anthrōpou erchomenon en tē basileia autou (who will not taste death until they see the Son of Man coming in his kingdom — geusōntai thanatou: taste death, a Semitic idiom for experiencing death; en tē basileia autou: in his kingdom): the saying has generated sustained debate — interpretations include the Transfiguration (immediately following in 17:1-8), Pentecost, the destruction of Jerusalem, or the parousia.

17.5: nephele photeinen — a bright/luminous cloud — the cloud of divine presence (shekinah) throughout the OT (Exod 13:21, 40:34-38, 1 Kgs 8:10-11); the Transfiguration cloud is the visible form of the divine glory-presence investing itself in the scene. akouete autou — listen to him — the addition to the baptism-declaration (3:17) echoes Deut 18:15 (him you shall listen to = the prophet like Moses); the Transfiguration completes the Moses/Elijah pattern by declaring that the one who surpasses them is the one to be heard.
17.1: kai meth hēmeras hex paralambanei ho Iēsous ton Petron kai Iakōbon kai Iōannēn ton adelphon autou — after six days Jesus takes Peter and James and John his brother (meth hēmeras hex: after six days — the specific time-reference connects this to the confession of 16:16; only the three members of the inner group are taken; cf. the same three at Gethsemane in 26:37) — kai anapherei autous eis oros hypsēlon kat idian (and leads them up on a high mountain privately — anapherei: to lead/carry up, an ascent-verb used in Heb 13:15 for offering sacrifice): the mountain is the locus of divine encounter throughout Matthew.
17.2: kai metemorphōthē emprosthen autōn — and he was transfigured before them (metemorphōthē aorist passive of metamorphoō: to transform in form — not a change of outer costume but a radiance emerging from within; the passive voice implies the action is done to Jesus or occurs in him) — kai elampsen to prosōpon autou hōs ho hēlios (and his face shone like the sun — elampsen aorist of lampō: to shine, to radiate): the solar comparison echoes Moses's face after Sinai (Exod 34:29-35) but exceeds it: Moses's face reflected divine glory; Jesus's face is the source of its own radiance. Ta de himatia autou egeneto leuka hōs to phōs — his garments became white as the light.
17.3: kai idou ōphthē autois Mōysēs kai Ēlias synlalounte met autou — and behold, there appeared to them Moses and Elijah talking with him (ōphthē aorist passive of horaō: were seen, appeared — the passive of revelation; Mōysēs kai Ēlias: Moses and Elijah represent the Law and the Prophets; Luke 9:31 adds that they spoke of Jesus's "departure/exodus" — exodon): the two figures who represent the entire Hebrew Scripture appear alongside Jesus, placing him as the fulfillment and culmination of both streams. Synlalounte met autou — conversing with him: an active dialogue, not a mere appearance.
17.4: apokritheis de ho Petros eipen tō Iēsou kyrie kalon estin hēmas hōde einai — Peter answering said to Jesus: Lord, it is good for us to be here (apokritheis: answering — used even where no prior question was asked; Peter responds to the situation) — ei theleis poiēsō hōde treis skēnas (if you wish I will make here three tents/booths — skēnas: tents, booths, tabernacles; the Feast of Tabernacles/Sukkot involved building booths): Peter's impulse to build three equal shelters for Jesus, Moses, and Elijah misunderstands the hierarchy — Moses and Elijah are witnesses to Jesus, not equals. The cloud-voice corrects the equivalence.
17.6: kai akousantes hoi mathētai epesan epi prosōpon autōn kai ephobēthēsan sphodra — hearing this the disciples fell on their faces and were very afraid (epesan epi prosōpon autōn: they fell on their faces — the prostration response to theophany; cf. Ezek 1:28; Dan 8:17; Rev 1:17; ephobēthēsan sphodra: were greatly afraid — sphodra: intensely, exceedingly): the divine voice from the cloud produces prostration, not the light or the figures. The voice is the locus of maximum terror, because it directly addresses them as hearers.
17.7: kai prosēlthen ho Iēsous kai hapsamenpos autōn eipen — and Jesus coming touched them and said (hapsamenps aorist participle of haptō: to touch — the healing/comforting touch; Jesus has not departed; he is physically present and responds to their prostration with a touch): egerthēte kai mē phobeisthe (rise up and do not be afraid — egerthēte aorist passive imperative of egeirō: the resurrection verb used for ordinary rising; mē phobeisthe present imperative: do not be in a state of fear). The first word after the theophany is "rise up" — the resurrection vocabulary applied to the disciples' prostration.
17.8: eparantes de tous ophthalmous autōn oudena eidon ei mē auton Iēsoun monon — lifting up their eyes they saw no one except Jesus alone (eparantes tous ophthalmous: lifting up the eyes — the gesture of recovery from prostration, looking up after the fall; monon: alone, only): Moses and Elijah have withdrawn; the cloud has lifted. What remains after the theophany is Jesus alone. The singularity of "Jesus alone" is the interpretive key to the Transfiguration: Law and Prophets give way; Jesus stands as their fulfillment and summation.
17.9: kai katabainontōn autōn ek tou orous eneteilato autois ho Iēsous legōn — as they were coming down from the mountain Jesus commanded them saying (eneteilato aorist of entellomai: to command, give an injunction) — mēdeni eipēte to horama heōs hou ho huios tou anthrōpou ek nekrōn egerthē (tell the vision to no one until the Son of Man has been raised from the dead — to horama: the vision, a visionary experience — the word for prophetic seeing; heōs hou...egerthē: until the Son of Man is raised — the resurrection is the condition for disclosure: the Transfiguration makes sense only from the other side of the resurrection).
17.10: kai epērōtēsan auton hoi mathētai legontes — the disciples asked him saying — ti oun hoi grammateis legousin hoti Ēlian dei elthein prōton (why then do the scribes say Elijah must come first? — Ēlian dei elthein prōton: Elijah must come first; the expectation is Mal 4:5): the disciples' question shows they understood the Transfiguration's Elijah-appearance as potentially the fulfillment of the Elijah-first expectation. Jesus's answer distinguishes between Elijah-in-type (John the Baptist, who has already come) and Elijah-as-person (who may still have an end-time role).
17.11: ho de apokritheis eipen Ēlias men erchetai kai apokatastēsei panta — answering he said: Elijah indeed comes and will restore all things (apokatastēsei future of apokathistēmi: to restore to an original or proper state — the eschatological restoration word; cf. Acts 1:6; the same root as apokatastasis) — Jesus affirms the Elijah-first tradition as real; the question is whether it has been fulfilled or is still future. The present tense erchetai (comes, is coming) followed by the future apokatastēsei (will restore) suggests an ongoing or future role, not a completed one.
17.12: legō de hymin hoti Ēlias ēdē ēlthen — but I tell you that Elijah has already come (ēdē ēlthen: has already come — the aorist with the adverb of current relevance) — kai ouk epegnōsan auton alla epoiēsan en autō hosa ēthelēsan (and they did not recognize him but did to him whatever they wished — ouk epegnōsan: did not recognize — the intensive recognition-verb in the negative; John came in the Elijah-role but was unrecognized): houtōs kai ho huios tou anthrōpou mellei paschein hyp autōn (so also the Son of Man is about to suffer at their hands): John's fate previews Jesus's fate; the pattern of rejection-of-God's-messenger repeats.
17.13: tote synēkan hoi mathētai hoti peri Iōannou tou baptistou eipen autois — then the disciples understood that he spoke to them about John the Baptist (synēkan aorist of syniēmi: the understanding-breakthrough; the aorist marks the moment of comprehension): the understanding that Elijah-who-came = John-the-Baptist becomes explicit; they had the pieces from 11:14 (where Jesus said "if you are willing to accept it, he is Elijah who is to come") but the Transfiguration context makes it clear. The disciples' understanding is developing through the Gospel by a series of such breakthrough moments.
17.14: kai elthontōn pros ton ochlon prosēlthen autō anthrōpos gonyp etōn kai — coming to the crowd, a man came to him kneeling (gonyp etōn: kneeling, genuflecting — the posture of petition before authority; the same form of approach as the Canaanite woman in 15:25) — the scene shifts from the mountain of transfiguration to the ordinary crowd with its suffering and the disciples' inability to help. The contrast between the mountain-glory and the valley-failure intensifies both events: the disciples' spiritual position has not translated into healing capacity.
17.15: kyrie eleēson mou ton huion hoti selēniazetai kai kakōs paschei — Lord, have mercy on my son for he is a lunatic and suffers terribly (selēniazetai: to be "moon-struck" — from selēnē: moon; the term for a condition associated with the lunar cycle, epilepsy or seizure disorder: kakōs paschei: suffers terribly — the generic suffering-formula) — pollakis gar piptei eis to pyr kai pollakis eis to hydōr (for often he falls into fire and often into water): the symptoms describe violent, dangerous falls associated with demonic seizure; the medical description and the demonic explanation coexist in the ancient framework.
17.16: kai prosēnenka auton tois mathētais sou kai ouk ēdunēthēsan auton therapeusai — I brought him to your disciples and they were unable to heal him (ouk ēdunēthēsan aorist passive of dynamai: were not able — a total inability, not partial success): the failure is stark. The disciples were given authority over unclean spirits in 10:1 and 10:8; their failure here will be explained by Jesus in terms of faith-size rather than authority-absence. The father's report to Jesus, after the disciples' failure, is an implicit appeal that what the disciples lacked, Jesus possesses.
17.17: apokritheis de ho Iēsous eipen ō genea apistos kai diestrammenē — answering Jesus said: O unbelieving and perverted generation (apistos: without faith, faithless — the opposite of pistos/faithful; diestrammenē perfect passive participle of diastrephō: to twist thoroughly, to pervert — a generation that has been twisted out of its proper orientation) — heōs pote meth hymōn esomai heōs pote anexomai hymōn (how long will I be with you, how long will I put up with you — anexomai: to bear with, endure, put up with — a patience-exhaustion expression): the rebuke is addressed to the generation, not narrowly to the disciples, though the disciples' failure is the trigger.
17.18: kai epetimēsen autō ho Iēsous kai exēlthen ap autou to daimonion — Jesus rebuked it and the demon came out of him (epetimēsen aorist of epitimaō: to rebuke, command — the standard exorcism-rebuke verb; exēlthen aorist: went out completely): kai etherapeuthē ho pais apo tēs hōras ekeinēs (and the boy was healed from that hour — tēs hōras ekeinēs: from that hour — the same formula as 15:28 and 9:22, marking instantaneous healing): the cure is immediate; the preceding diagnosis and the coming explanation of the disciples' failure frame it as a teaching event about faith.
17.19: tote proselthontes hoi mathētai tō Iēsou kat idian eipon — then the disciples came to Jesus privately and said (kat idian: privately — the private teaching context that follows difficult public events throughout Matthew) — dia ti hēmeis ouk ēdynēthēmen ekbalein auto (why were we unable to drive it out? — ekbalein: to drive out — the exorcism verb): the question is sincere; the disciples do not doubt Jesus's authority but cannot explain their own failure. Jesus's answer addresses the root cause: faith-size, not authority-level.
17.20: ho de legei autois dia tēn oligopistian hymōn — he says to them: because of your little faith (oligopistian: little faith — the noun form of the compound oligopistos; the only use of the noun in Matthew; the accusation is general, describing the disciples' faith-level as insufficient) — amen gar legō hymin ean echēte pistin hōs kokkos sinapeōs (for truly I tell you, if you have faith like a mustard seed — kokkos sinapeōs: a grain of mustard, the proverbially smallest seed; here the comparison is not to the tree but to the grain): ereite tō orei toutō metabēthi enteuthen ekei kai metabēsetai — you will say to this mountain: move from here to there, and it will move. Kai ouden adynatēsei hymin — nothing will be impossible for you.
17.21: touto de to genos ouk ekporeuetai ei mē en proseuchē kai nēsteia — but this kind does not go out except by prayer and fasting — the verse is absent from some early manuscripts (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) but present in others and in Mark 9:29 (without "fasting"). If original to Matthew, it specifies that certain severe demonic cases require an intensified prayer-and-fasting posture beyond the general authority granted in 10:1. The note qualifies the faith-as-mustard-seed saying of v.20 without contradicting it: mustard-seed faith that operates through prayer and fasting.
17.22: systrephomenōn de autōn en tē Galilaia eipen autois ho Iēsous — as they were gathering in Galilee Jesus said to them — mellei ho huios tou anthrōpou paradidosthai eis cheiras anthrōpōn (the Son of Man is about to be delivered into the hands of men — paradidosthai present passive infinitive of paradidōmi: to be delivered, handed over — the betrayal/passion vocabulary): the second passion prediction uses paradidosthai, linking the passion directly to the Judas-betrayal that will occur in ch26. The irony of "delivered into the hands of men" is that "Son of Man" (a divine figure) is handed over to human power.
17.23: kai apoktenousin auton kai tē tritē hēmera egerthēsetai — and they will kill him, and on the third day he will be raised (egerthēsetai future passive of egeirō: will be raised — the divine passive: God raises the Son of Man who was killed by men) — kai elypēthēsan sphodra (and they were deeply grieved — elypēthēsan sphodra: were distressed exceedingly; sphodra: intensely): the disciples heard the passion prediction and were grieved without full understanding (Luke 9:45 notes they did not grasp it). The grief is genuine; the comprehension is partial; they cannot yet hold together death and resurrection as a unity.
