17.24: elthontōn de autōn eis Kapharnaoun prosēlthon hoi ta didrachma lambanontes tō Petrō — coming to Capernaum those who collect the two-drachma tax came to Peter (didrachma: two drachmas — the annual temple tax of half a shekel, based on Exod 30:13-16, collected for the Jerusalem Temple; every adult Jewish male paid this; the collectors approach Peter, not Jesus, perhaps because Peter is recognizable as the group's spokesperson): ho didaskalos hymōn ou telei ta didrachma (does not your teacher pay the two-drachma tax?): the question is not hostile but administrative.
17.25: legei nai kai elthonta eis tēn oikian proephthasen auton ho Iēsous legōn — he says: yes. And coming into the house Jesus anticipated him saying (proephthasen aorist of prophthanō: to get ahead of, to anticipate — Jesus speaks first before Peter can report the exchange) — ti soi dokei Simōn hoi basileis tēs gēs apo tinōn lambanousin telē ē kēnson (what do you think, Simon? The kings of the earth — from whom do they collect toll or tax?): the question is practical and political: apo tōn huiōn autōn ē apo tōn allotriōn (from their own sons or from strangers?).
17.26: eipantos de apo tōn allotriōn ephē autō ho Iēsous ara ge eleutheroi eisin hoi huioi — when he said: from the strangers, Jesus said to him: then the sons are free (ara ge: therefore indeed/then certainly — a strong logical inference; eleutheroi eisin hoi huioi: the sons are free, exempt — eleutheros: free person, not slave; free from the obligation): the argument: earthly kings exempt their own children from taxation; if God is the Father and the Temple is his house, then the Son of God is exempt from the Temple tax. The analogy is compressed but clear.
17.27: hina de mē skandalisōmen autous — but so that we do not cause them to stumble (skandalisōmen aorist subjunctive of skandalizō: the stumbling-cause verb; mē + subjunctive: a prohibition of purpose) — the tax is voluntarily paid not because it is owed but to avoid causing offense. Poreutheis eis thalassan bale agkistron (going to the sea, throw out a hook — agkistron: a fishhook, not a net; a single targeted catch): kai ton prōton ichthyn anabanta aron (and taking the first fish that comes up — ton prōton: the very first): kai anoixas to stoma autou heurēseis statēra — opening its mouth you will find a stater (a four-drachma coin = one shekel, covering the tax for two people).

18.18: hosa ean desete epi tes ges estai dedomena en ourano — whatever you bind on earth will have been bound in heaven — the exact same binding/loosing language as 16:19 (the authority given to Peter) is now given to the community as a whole in the context of church discipline. Deo/luo (bind/loose) were standard rabbinic terms for declaring something forbidden or permitted (halakic ruling); here applied to the community's disciplinary decisions and intercessions. The perfects (dedemenon/lelymeron) indicate that the heavenly ratification precedes and grounds the earthly decision.
18.1: en ekeinē tē hōra — in that hour — The temporal marker links directly to the immediately preceding temple-tax exchange; the disciplesâ question about greatness is provoked by observing Jesusâ deference.
18.2: proskalesámenos paidíon — having called a child — Paidíon is the diminutive of paîs, emphasizing smallness. The aorist participle proskalesámenos (having summoned) shows the child is a deliberate visual aid, not accidental.
18.3: straphête kai génēsthe hōs ta paidía — unless you turn and become as children — Straphête (aorist passive of stréphō) carries the sense of radical reorientation, cognate to epistréphō (repentance-turn). The aorist génēsthe demands an actual becoming, not merely childlike demeanor. The double condition (ean mē + subjunctive + subjunctive) makes entry to the kingdom contingent on both the turn and the becoming.
18.4: hóstis oun tapeinōsei heautón — whoever therefore humbles himself — Tapeinoō (to make low) is used here reflexively; the one who actively places himself in a position of dependence and low status. The child is not being praised for virtue but for social rank — lowest in the ancient household.
18.5: hòs eàn déxētai paidíon toioûto — whoever receives one such child — Déxētai (aorist subjunctive of déchomai) = receives hospitably, welcomes. The phrase epì tô̧ onómati mou (on the basis of my name) grounds hospitality to the least in identification with Christ himself.
18.6: hóristhos mýlos onikos — a donkey millstone — Mýlos onikos = the large upper millstone turned by a donkey (vs. the hand-mill). The image intensifies drowning: not a small stone tied around the neck but a massive grinding stone. Skandalísē (aorist subjunctive of skandalízō) = causes to stumble, causes to fall away from faith.
18.7: ouaì tô̧ kósmō̧ apò tôn skandálōn — woe to the world because of stumbling blocks — Ouaí is a lament-curse interjection. Skándalon (from skandalḗthron, the trigger-stick of a trap) = anything that causes one to trip and fall. The Greek distinguishes structural necessity (anánkē gàr eltheîn = for it is necessary for them to come) from personal guilt (ouaì dè tô̧ anthrṓpō̧).
18.8: ei dè hē cheír sou è ho poús sou skandalízei se — if your hand or foot causes you to stumble — The conditional is third-class (remote possibility; eán + present subjunctive), not endorsing literal self-mutilation but using hyperbole to insist that moral excision is preferable to total destruction. Kullón (maimed) and chōlón (lame) describe the diminished but living state that is better than hell.
18.9: mónophthalmon eiseltheîn eis tḕn zōḗn — one-eyed to enter into life — Monóphthalmos (one-eyed) is a compound hapax in Matthew; parallels 18:8. The repetition of the kólasin aiṓnion / pŷr aiṓnion / géennan toû pyrós vocabulary across 18:8-9 reinforces the eschatological stakes behind the community discourse.
18.10: hoi ángeloi autôn en ouranoîs — their angels in heaven — The phrase horáō dià pantòs tò prósōpon (they always see the face) echoes the idiom of throne-room access in Persian court protocol, here applied to the highest angels as personal guardians of the little ones. Dià pantós = continuously, not intermittently.
18.11: [v.11 absent in the best manuscripts — NA28 brackets; textual insertion from Luke 19:10]
18.12: tí hymîn dokeî — what do you think? — A Matthean rhetorical opener inviting deliberation. Apoplanaō (has gone astray) is aorist passive suggesting the sheep was led astray, not merely wandered; emphasizing vulnerability. Tà enenḗkonta ennéa = ninety-nine, left epì tà órē (on the mountains), exposed.
18.13: chairei ep' autô̧ mâllon — rejoices over it more — Mâllon (more, rather) marks a comparative that overturns natural expectation: the recovered one occasions greater joy than the stable ninety-nine. The present indicative chaírei expresses the ongoing character of this rejoicing.
18.14: oútōs ouk éstin thélēma émprosthen toû patròs hymôn — thus it is not the will before your Father — Émprosthen = before, in the presence of. The expression hína apólētai (that one of these little ones should perish) uses the aorist subjunctive in a negated purpose clause, the strongest Greek construction for divine intention: God categorically wills none lost.
18.15: hýpage kai élegxon autòn metaxù soû kaì autoû mónon — go and rebuke him between you and him alone — Elégchō = expose, rebuke, convict — a legal term (cf. LXX Job 13:3). The adverb mónon (alone) and metaxý (between) insist on private confrontation first; the goal is expressed as ekérdēsas (you have gained), treating the brother as an asset to be recovered, not an enemy to be punished.
18.16: epì stómatos dýo martyrōn — on the mouth of two witnesses — Alludes directly to Deut 19:15 (epì stómatos dýo martyrōn kaì trîon martyrōn). The community procedure adapts Mosaic juridical law for intra-church discipline; the phrase pantò rhêma stathȩ̂ (every matter may be established) carries forensic weight.
18.17: éstō soi hōsper ho ethnikòs kaì ho telṓnēs — let him be to you as the Gentile and the tax collector — Ironic: Jesus elsewhere models compassion toward exactly these groups. The instruction is not about contempt but about treating the unrepentant member as outside covenant community, requiring re-evangelization rather than fellowship discipline. Parakúō (ignores, refuses to listen) governs the escalation.
18.19: symphōnḗsōsin ek tês gês perì pantòs prágmatos — agree on earth concerning any matter — Symphōnéō (agree together, be in harmony) is the verb behind English 'symphony.' The condition hó eàn aítēsōntai (whatever they ask) is governed by the communal discernment context, not a blank promise for any private desire.
18.20: hou gár eisin dýo è treîs synagménoi eis tò emòn ónoma — where two or three are gathered into my name — Synágō eis ónoma (gather into/around a name) is the assembly formula for Jewish court decisions (Pirkei Avot 3:2 uses the same pattern for Torah study). Jesus substitutes his own name for the divine name, an implicit Christological claim.
18.21: posákis hamartḗsei eis emè ho adelphós mou — how many times will my brother sin against me — Peterâs proposed limit of seven times invokes the perfect number; he likely thinks he is being generous. Aphḗsō autô̧ (shall I forgive him) uses the future indicative, framing forgiveness as a measured policy decision.
18.22: héōs hebdomēkontákis heptá — up to seventy-seven times — The phrase alludes to Gen 4:24 (LXX: hebdomēkontákis heptá), Lamechâs boast of unlimited vengeance. Jesus inverts the vengeance-escalation formula of Lamech into an forgiveness-escalation: where Lamech claimed seventy-seven-fold retribution, disciples extend seventy-seven-fold pardon. Some MSS read hebdomēkontakis heptá = seventy times seven (490), but the Lamech allusion favors 77.
18.23: hōmoiṓthē hē basileía tôn ouranôn anthrṓpō̧ basilêi — the kingdom of heaven was likened to a king — The aorist passive hōmoiṓthē opens a parable formula. Synaîrai lógon (to settle accounts) is commercial terminology from papyrus receipts; lógon here = ledger account.
18.24: myríōn talántōn — ten thousand talents — Mýrioi = ten thousand, the highest numeral in Greek, hence 'myriad.' One talent = 6,000 denarii (a laborerâs ~20-year wage). Ten thousand talents = roughly 200,000 yearsâ wages — a deliberately impossible sum, signaling that the debt is not meant to be realistic but to represent unpayable moral indebtedness before God.
18.25: apodídōmi pánt' ósōn échō — sell all that he has — Prathênai (to be sold, aorist passive infinitive) with family included reflects ancient debt-slavery practice. The servantâs pledge apodídōmi pánt' (I will repay everything) is mathematically absurd given the sum, signaling desperation rather than credible promise.
18.26: prosekýnei autô̧ légōn — he prostrated himself before him saying — Proskyneō = full prostration (not merely bow), the posture of absolute submission before royalty. The imperfect prosekýnei suggests ongoing/prolonged prostration as he pleaded.
18.27: esplanchnísthē ho kýrios toû doúlou ekeínou — the lord of that servant was moved with compassion — Splagchnízomai recurs (cf. 9:36; 14:14; 15:32) — visceral compassion seated in the inner organs. Apélyen (released) is the verb for releasing a debt/prisoner; aphêken (forgave) adds the legal acquittal. Both verbs in aorist, decisive and complete acts.
18.28: hekatòn dēnaríōn — one hundred denarii — ~100 daysâ wages, a real but repayable sum — the sharp contrast with 10,000 talents (v.24) is structurally central to the parableâs indictment. Épnigen (was choking/strangling him) is graphic physical violence, showing the released servantâs ruthlessness.
18.29: pesṑn oun eis toùs pódas autoû parekálei — falling at his feet he begged — The vocabulary is identical to the first servantâs plea to the king (v.26), making the parallel structurally precise and the irony unmistakable.
18.30: ouk ḗthelen dè — but he was not willing — The imperfect ḗthelen (was willing/wanting) denotes ongoing refusal; a sustained decision not a momentary lapse. Apelthṑn ébalen = went and threw him into prison, the aorist pairing showing decisive action.
18.31: elypḗthēsan sphodra — they were greatly grieved — Lypaō = to grieve, pain deeply; sphodra = greatly, intensely. The other servantsâ moral anguish moves them to report (diesará¸£hṓthēsan = explained fully, spread out the whole account) to the master.
18.32: doûle ponēré — wicked servant — The address ponēré (wicked, evil) echoes the characterization of the evil one throughout Matthew. Pâsan tḕn opheliéan ekeínēn (all that debt) recaps the total; the kingâs mercy was not partial cancellation but total remission.
18.33: ouk édei kaì sè eleêsai — was it not necessary for you to have mercy — Édei (it was necessary, imperfect of deî) frames mercy toward the co-servant as a moral obligation arising from the mercy received, not merely an option. The aorist infinitive eleêsai refers to a specific act of mercy that should have occurred.
18.34: orgistheìs ho kýrios autoû paredōken autòn — having become angry the master handed him over — Orgízomai (to become angry) aorist participle; the anger results in judicial action. Paredōken (handed over) to the torturers (basanistâs) until full payment — echoing the eschatological language of judgment and eternal consequence.
18.35: outōs kaì ho patḗr mou ho ouránios poiḗsei hymîn — so also my heavenly Father will do to you — The parableâs application is direct and future: poiḗsei (will do) = divine judgment. Apò tôn kardiôn hymôn (from your hearts) specifies that surface forgiveness is insufficient; the forgiveness must be genuine and inward.

19.4: ho ktisas ap arches arsen kai thely epoiesen autous — the one who created from the beginning made them male and female — Jesus grounds the marriage argument in the arche (beginning = creation order), which has theological priority over Mosaic legislation. The creation-given (ktisas, aorist passive) is the theological bedrock from which human institutional arrangements derive; Moses's concession accommodated fallen humanity but did not abrogate the creation norm. The two-become-one-flesh (Gen 2:24) is not a Mosaic law but a creational institution that precedes law.
19.1: metêren apò tês Galilaías — departed from Galilee — Metaírō (to lift up, move away) appears only in Matthew (11:1; 13:53; 19:1) as a structural marker for Jesusâ movements at discourse-end. Péran toû Iordánou = beyond the Jordan, the territory of Perea in Transjordan.
19.2: ēkoloúthēsan autô̧ óchloi polloí — many crowds followed him — The imperfect ēkoloúthēsan suggests continuous following as Jesus traveled. Etherápeusen (healed) aorist, summarizing healing activity without detailing individual cases.
19.3: katà pâsan aitian — for any reason — This phrase marks the Pharisaic test as a Hillelite/Shammaite debate question: Hillel permitted divorce for any displeasure (kol davar), Shammai restricted it to sexual immorality. Peirázōn (testing) marks their intent as adversarial. Ei éxestin = whether it is lawful (the standard Pharisaic form for halakhic inquiry).
19.5: kataleíptei patéra kaì mētéra — shall leave father and mother — Quotes Gen 2:24 LXX (proskollēthḗsetai = shall be joined/glued to, future passive). The verb proskolláō (to glue to, cleave) indicates the new primary bond surpasses even the parent-child bond.
19.6: hṑste oukéti eisìn dýo allà sàrx mía — so that they are no longer two but one flesh — Hōste marks consequence; sàrx mía (one flesh) is not merely metaphor but an ontological description. Ho theòs synézeuxen (God yoked together) uses syzeugnýō (to yoke, harness a pair together), the technical agricultural term for yoking two animals as one working unit.
19.7: dià tí oun Mōysês enetéilato doûnai biblíon apostasiou — why then did Moses command to give a certificate of divorce? — Enetéilato (commanded) vs. Jesusâ correction: the Pharisees read Deut 24:1 as a positive command; Jesus will reframe it as a concession. Biblíon apostasiou (LXX Deut 24:1) = document of separation.
19.8: Mōysês pròs tḕn sklērokardían hymôn — Moses with a view to your hardness of heart — Sklērokardía (hard-heartedness, from sklērós = hard + kardía = heart) is a compound biblical term (LXX Deut 10:16; Jer 4:4). The preposition prós (toward, in relation to) marks the divorce permission as accommodation to a condition, not endorsement. Ap' archês dè ou gégonen (but from the beginning it was not so) — the aorist perfect negation asserts that original design never included divorce.
19.9: mḕ epì porneía — except for sexual immorality — The porneia exception clause (also 5:32) is uniquely Matthean; Mark and Luke omit it. Porneía spans illicit sexual unions (premarital, adultery, incest) in Hellenistic usage. The clauseâs precise scope has generated extensive patristic and Reformation debate; the Greek syntax attaches the exception only to the divorce, not to remarriage in all MSS.
19.10: ei oútōs estìn hē aitíà toû anthrṓpou metà tês gynaikós — if such is the case of a man with his wife — The disciplesâ response reveals they operated with the Hillelite assumption: if divorce is nearly impossible, perhaps celibacy is preferable. Symphérei (it is advantageous) is utilitarian calculation.
19.11: ou pántes chōroûsin tòn lógon toûton — not all receive this word — Chōréō (to have room for, hold, receive) is metaphorical for intellectual/spiritual capacity. The lógon toûton (this word) refers to the demanding standard of marital permanence just stated, not to celibacy specifically. Hois dédotai (to whom it has been given) = divine passive, God-given capacity.
19.12: eunouchoí — eunuchs — Three categories: (1) born eunuchs (ek koilías mētrós), (2) made eunuchs by men, (3) those who eunouchísan heautoús (made themselves eunuchs) for the kingdom. The third category is clearly metaphorical — Origenâs literal self-castration was universally rejected as misreading. Ho dynámenos chōreîn chōreítō (let the one able to receive it receive it) echoes the selective call of v.11.
19.13: prosénechthēsan autô̧ paidía — children were brought to him — Prosféreō (to bring to, offer) is the verb for presenting offerings; used here of presenting children for blessing (epithȩ̂ tas cheîras = lay hands on and pray). The disciplesâ rebuke (epetímēsan) reveals lingering hierarchy assumptions just overturned in ch18.
19.14: áphete tà paidía kaì mḕ kōlýete autà — let the children come and do not hinder them — Kōlýō (to hinder, prevent) is the technical baptism-refusal verb (Acts 8:36; 10:47); its use here suggests an early connection to the question of childrenâs participation in the covenant community. Tôn toioútōn estin hē basileía (of such ones is the kingdom) = genitive of possession or belonging, not merely comparison.
19.15: epitheis tas cheîras autoîs — having laid hands on them — Epitíthēmi cheîras is the laying-on-of-hands formula for blessing, ordination, and healing throughout the NT. Eporeúthē ekeîthen (departed from there) marks movement.
19.16: didáskale, tí agathòn poiḗsō — teacher, what good thing shall I do — The address didáskale (teacher) rather than kýrie (Lord) distinguishes this questionerâs orientation: he approaches Jesus as a moral expert, not as divine Lord. His question frames eternal life as achievable by a single act (poiḗsō, future deliberative).
19.17: tí me erōtâs perì toû agathoû — why do you ask me about the good? — Jesus redirects the question from his own person to God: heîs estin ho agathós (one is good). The question challenges the premise that goodness is a human achievement category; goodness is ontologically Godâs alone. Then he answers pragmatically: têrei tàs entolás (keep the commandments).
19.18: poías — which ones? — The manâs follow-up question is not ignorant but probing: which subset of commandments constitutes the required minimum? Jesusâ response (tó: ou phoneúseis through agapḗseis tòn plēsíon) cites the Decalogueâs second table (duties to humans) plus Lev 19:18, grounding the social ethics in love-of-neighbor as summary.
19.19: timá tòn patéra kaì tḕn mētéra — honor your father and mother — Citing Ex 20:12 / Deut 5:16 LXX. The Decalogue items are listed in non-sequential order (murder, adultery, theft, false witness, honor parents); the neighbor-love summary from Lev 19:18 is added as a final encompassing principle.
19.20: pánta taûta ephylaxa — all these things I have kept — Phylássō (to guard, keep, observe) in aorist — he claims complete past observance. Tí éti hysterô̧ (what do I still lack) reveals his sense of insufficiency despite legal compliance, a remarkable admission that drives the encounter forward.
19.21: ei théleis téleos eînai — if you wish to be perfect — Téleios (perfect, complete, mature) in Matthew points to wholeness of undivided commitment (5:48). The command pṓlēsón sou tà hypárchonta (sell your possessions) is aorist imperative — immediate, total action. Thēsauròn en ouraî̧ (treasure in heaven) is the exchange offered. The barrier exposed is not intellectual but volitional: déuro akolouthei moi (come, follow me) — he wonât.
19.22: apêlthen lypoúmenos — he went away grieved — Lypoúmenos (present passive participle of lypéō) = being grieved, in pain. The grief itself indicates the man understood the invitation; he was not indifferent. Ên gàr échōn ktḗmata pollá (for he had many possessions) — the ên échōn periphrastic imperfect stresses his possession as a continuous state, not a temporary circumstance.
19.23: dyskolōs ploúsios eiseleúsetai — with difficulty a rich man will enter — Dýskolos (difficult, hard, troublesome) is an adverb; the placement is emphatic. The future indicative eiseleúsetai states a general truth about difficulty, not impossibility.
19.24: kámēlon dià trymaliâs rhaphis — a camel through the eye of a needle — Kámēlos (camel) is the large quadruped; a few MSS read kamilos (shipâs cable/rope) but the camel reading is overwhelmingly supported. No archaeological evidence supports the 'Needle Gate' tradition (a small Jerusalem gate) — this appears post-medieval. The image is intentionally absurd: maximum impossibility. Trymaliá rhaphis = needle-hole (rhaphis = sewing needle).
19.25: tís ára dýnatai sōthênai — who then is able to be saved? — The disciplesâ shock (exeplḗssonto sphodra = were utterly amazed) shows they held the common assumption that wealth indicates divine favor (cf. Deut 28). If even the prosperous cannot be saved, who can? The question exposes the bankruptcy of merit-based soteriology.
19.26: parà anthrṓpois toûto adýnatón estin — with men this is impossible — Parà + dative (with, beside, from the perspective of). Parà dè theô̧ pánta dynatá (but with God all things are possible) — the contrast is absolute. Salvation is not a human achievement category at all; it is entirely divine gift.
19.27: hēmeîs aphḗkamen pánta — we left everything — Peterâs aorist aphḗkamen (left) echoes the exact word used for the rich manâs failure: he was unwilling to leave (aphés). Tí ára éstai hēmîn (what then will there be for us) — future deliberative question about reward.
19.28: en tȩ̂ palingenesiá — in the regeneration/renewal — Palingenesía (from pálin = again + génesis = birth/origin) appears only twice in NT: here and Tit 3:5. Here it refers to the cosmic renewal at the consummation, not individual rebirth. Hótan kathísē ho huiòs toû anthrṓpou epì thrónou dóxēs autoû — the enthroned Son of Man imagery from Dan 7. The Twelve will sit on twelve thrones judging the twelve tribes: eschatological co-regency.
19.29: pás hóstis aphêken oikías è adelphoús — everyone who has left houses or brothers — The catalog of forsaken relationships (family, property) is exhaustive. Hekatontaplasíona lḗmpsetai (will receive a hundredfold) is future indicative; zōḕn aiṓnion klēronomeî (will inherit eternal life) uses the inheritance idiom of covenant fulfillment.
19.30: polloi dè ésontai prôtoi éschatoi kaì éschatoi prôtoi — but many first will be last and last first — The future ésontai places this reversal in the eschatological order. The saying frames the parable that follows in ch20 as its illustration and serves as an inclusio: 19:30 and 20:16 bracket the parable with the same reversal-formula.

20.28: dounai ten psychen autou lytron anti pollon — to give his soul as a ransom in exchange for many — lytron (ransom price) is the price paid to release a slave or captive; the LXX uses it for the redemption-payment. Anti (in exchange for, instead of) is a substitutionary preposition: the soul of Jesus stands in the place of the many. Psyche here means life/person-as-a-whole; Jesus gives himself entirely as the exchange-price. The Isaianic Servant background (Isa 53:10-12) provides the theological content: the Servant makes his soul an offering for sin and thereby justifies many.
20.1: hōmoíōthē gàr hē basileía tôn ouranôn anthrṓpō̧ oikodespótȩ̄ — for the kingdom of heaven is like a householder — Oikodespótēs (householder, master of the house) = the estate owner who has authority over household and workers. Hóstis exêlthen hama prōí (who went out early in the morning) — hama prōí = at dawn, the first of four hiring times in the parable.
20.2: symphōnḗsas dè metà tôn ergatôn ek dēnaríou tḕn hēméran — having agreed with the workers for a denarius per day — Symphōnéō (to agree, be in accord) is the contract verb. One denarius per day was standard daily wage; the agreement is explicit and fair. This sets up the legal baseline from which the parable will depart.
20.3: állous hestôtas en tȩ̂ agorâ̧ argous — others standing in the marketplace idle — Argoús (idle, unemployed — from a- + érgon = without work) describes unemployed day laborers waiting to be hired. The agora (marketplace/forum) was the hiring plaza; this is realistic depiction of ancient labor practices.
20.4: hó eàn ȩ̂ díkaion dṓsō hymîn — whatever is just I will give you — The second group receives no fixed-sum contract, only the promise of what is díkaion (just, right). This ambiguity is deliberate; the parable turns on what díkaion means from the ownerâs perspective.
20.5: péktēn kaì enátēn — the sixth and ninth hours — Sixth hour = noon, ninth hour = 3 PM. Two more hiring rounds; still no fixed sum promised, only the ownerâs just determination.
20.6: perì dè tḕn hendekátēn — about the eleventh hour — One hour before end of working day (twelfth hour = sunset). Tí hestékate hólēn tḕn hēméran argoí (why have you stood the whole day idle?) — the ownerâs question highlights their day-long unemployment, not laziness; they answer hóti oudeìs hēmâs emisthṓsato (no one hired us).
20.7: hypágete kaì hymeîs eis tòn ampelôna — go, you also, into the vineyard — No wage specified at all for this last group. The parable withholds any contract with the eleventh-hour workers, forcing the ambiguity: what will they receive? The readerâs expectation mirrors the early workersâ reasonable assumption.
20.8: arxámenos apò tôn eschátōn héōs tôn prṓtōn — beginning from the last to the first — The reversal of payment order (arxámenos apò tôn eschátōn) is a Matthean literary device to create the conflict: the first workers see the last receive a full denarius and expect more. Epítropos (manager/steward) carries out the ownerâs instructions.
20.9: élabon anà dēnárion — they received a denarius each — Aná (distributive: each) stresses individual equal payment. The eleventh-hour workers receive a full dayâs wage for one hourâs work — the operative category is need/grace, not merit/proportion.
20.10: enómisan hóti pleîon lḗmpsontai — they supposed they would receive more — Nomízō (to suppose, consider customary) + future indicative lḗmpsontai = their expectation was reasonable by any merit-based logic. The aorist élabon kaì autoì anà dēnárion (they also received a denarius) delivers the shock.
20.11: egóggyzōn katà toû oikodespótou — grumbling against the householder — Goggýzō (to grumble, murmur) echoes LXX Israelâs murmuring in the wilderness (Ex 16:7-8; Num 14:2). The imperfect egóggyzōn suggests continuous complaining.
20.12: mían hṓran epoíēsan — they worked one hour — The early workersâ complaint highlights duration (mían hṓran vs. tò báros tês hēméras kaì tòn kaúsōna = the burden of the day and the scorching heat). Kaúsōn = burning heat, the Mediterranean afternoon heat blast; a legitimate hardship claim.
20.13: hetaîre — friend — Hetaîros (companion, associate) is a less intimate address than phílos (dear friend); it carries a slight distancing — the same term Jesus uses to Judas in 26:50. Ouk adikô̧ se (I am not wronging you) — present indicative, strong denial of injustice.
20.14: árōn tò sòn kaì hýpage — take what is yours and go — The imperative sequence árōn... hýpage (take... go) is a legal dismissal: the agreed contract is fulfilled. Toutô̧ dè tô̧ eschátō̧ doûnai hōs kaì soí (but to give this last one the same as you) — the ownerâs sovereign prerogative is the theological key.
20.15: ouk éxestín moi hò thélō poiêsai en toîs emoîs — is it not lawful for me to do what I wish with what is mine? — Éxestin (it is permitted/lawful) frames the question in terms of property rights. Hò thélō (what I wish) asserts the ownerâs sovereign freedom over his own property. Ho ophthalmos sou ponērós estin hóti egṑ agathós eimi (is your eye evil because I am good?) — the pōonēròs ophthalmos (evil eye) idiom = envy/stinginess (cf. Deut 15:9 LXX); to have an evil eye toward anotherâs good fortune.
20.16: éschatoi prôtoi kaì prôtoi éschatoi — last first and first last — The reversional formula (also 19:30) forms an inclusio around the parable. This is not a statement about who enters the kingdom but about the reordering of expectation: merit-based hierarchies are overturned by sovereign grace.
20.17: anabáinōn dè ho Iēsoûs eis Hierosólyma — going up to Jerusalem — Anabáinō (to go up) is the standard verb for ascent to Jerusalem (both geographical and cultic elevation). Kat' idían (privately, apart) indicates a separate instruction for the Twelve from the crowd, signaling the gravity of what follows.
20.18: idoù anabáinomen eis Hierosólyma — behold, we go up to Jerusalem — Third passion prediction (cf. 16:21; 17:22-23). Paradothḗsetai (will be handed over) — divine passive, future indicative. Toîs archierêusin kaì grammateûsin (to the chief priests and scribes) — the Jerusalem establishment, not Roman authorities, initiate the process.
20.19: paradṓsousin autòn toîs éthnesin — they will hand him over to the Gentiles — The specification toîs éthnesin (to the Gentiles/nations) adds new detail over the 16:21 prediction: Roman involvement now specified. The four verbs — empaîxai (mock), mastigôsai (flog), staurôsai (crucify), egertheî (raised) — provide a precise preview of the Passion sequence, with the resurrection as the final action.
20.20: prosêlthen autô̧ hē mḗtēr tôn huiôn Zebedaíou — the mother of the sons of Zebedee came to him — Matthew substitutes the mother for James and John themselves (cf. Mark 10:35). Proskynoûsa (worshiping/bowing) signals the request is presented with reverence. Aiteî ti ap' autoû (asking something from him) — the indefinite ti before specifying shows she knows the request is presumptuous.
20.21: eipè hína kathísōsin houtoi hoi dýo huioí mou — say that these two sons of mine may sit — Kathísōsin (aorist subjunctive of kathízō) in a hína + subjunctive request clause. The ek dexiôn sou kaì ex euōnýmōn (right and left) of the Messianic king recall the enthronement psalm idiom; these are positions of highest co-regency in ancient courts.
20.22: tò potḗrion hò egṑ méllō píein — the cup that I am about to drink — Potḗrion (cup) as a metaphor for divinely-appointed suffering echoes Ps 75:8; Isa 51:17; Jer 25:15-16. Méllō + infinitive = imminent future. Dýnasthe (are you able?) — present indicative question probing their capacity. Their confident dynámetha (we are able) is not rebuked but redirected.
20.23: tò mèn potḗrión mou píesthe — my cup you will indeed drink — The mén... dé contrast: yes they will share the cup (prophesying their martyrdom), but the eschatological seating is not Jesusâ to grant. Hoîs hētoimastai hypò toû patrós mou (for whom it has been prepared by my Father) — perfect passive hētoimastai: God has already determined eschatological positions; they are not up for dispensation.
20.24: ēganáktēsan perì tôn dýo adelphôn — they were indignant concerning the two brothers — Aganaktéō (to be indignant, angered) is a strong emotional verb; the tenâs anger reveals that they themselves coveted the positions. The request exposed ambition latent in the entire group.
20.25: oi árchontes tôn ethnôn katakyrieuousin autôn — the rulers of the Gentiles lord it over them — Katakyrieuō (to exercise dominion over, lord over) uses the kata- prefix for intensive, downward exercise of power. Katexousiázousin (exercise authority over) pairs with it: both verbs describe hierarchical domination as the Gentile model. Ou houtōs éstai en hymîn (it shall not be so among you) — future of prohibition.
20.26: hós eàn thélȩ̄ en hymîn mégas genésthai — whoever wishes to become great among you — Mégas (great) and prôtos (first) are the status-markers just sought. Éstai hymôn diákonos (shall be your servant) — diákonos (table-servant, deacon) is a role of active service, not honorary title. The future indicative éstai makes this a descriptive statement of the kingdom order, not merely moral advice.
20.27: hós eàn thélȩ̄ en hymîn eînai prôtos — whoever wishes to be first among you — Escalation from mégas (great, v.26) to prôtos (first) matched by escalation from diákonos (servant) to doûlos (slave). Doûlos = bond-slave, the lowest social rank; the trajectory of greatness runs exactly opposite to social expectation.
20.29: ekporeuoménōn autôn apò Hierichoû — as they were going out from Jericho — Note the Synoptic variation: Mark 10:46 and Luke 18:35 place the healing at the entrance to Jericho. Matthew has two blind men (not one as in Mark/Luke); multiplication of witnesses is a Matthean pattern (cf. two demoniacs, 8:28).
20.30: eleêsón hēmâs, kýrie, huiòs Dauid — have mercy on us, Lord, Son of David — Eleéō (to have mercy, show compassion) is the LXX verb for divine compassion (hesed). The vocative huiè Dauid (Son of David) is the Messianic title used by those outside the inner circle who perceive Jesusâ identity. Ékraxan légontes (cried out saying) — krázō suggests urgent, loud crying.
20.31: epetímēsan autoîs ho óchlos — the crowd rebuked them — Epitimáō (to rebuke) is the same verb used for silencing demons; the crowdâs attempt to silence the blind men echoes the disciplesâ rebuke of the children (19:13). Meîzon ékraxan (they cried out all the more) — comparative: the louder opposition drives louder appeal.
20.32: stàs ho Iēsoûs ephṓnēsen autoús — Jesus stopped and called them — Stás (aorist participle of histēmi = having stopped) marks deliberate halt. Ephṓnēsen (called, summoned) — Jesus hears, stops, and calls them rather than going to them, requiring the exercise of their faith in approaching.
20.33: tí thélете poiḗsō hymîn — what do you wish I should do for you? — Verbatim parallel to the question to the Zebedee sonsâ mother (v.21), creating a deliberate contrast: she asked for thrones; the blind men ask only for sight. Anoigôsin hoi ophthalmoì hēmôn (that our eyes may be opened) — the humble petition for basic restoration.
20.34: splanchnistheìs dè ho Iēsoûs — moved with compassion Jesus — Splagchnízomai (visceral compassion, from splágchna = inner organs) recurs as the characteristic response of Jesus to human need (9:36; 14:14; 15:32; 18:27). Hḗpsato tôn ommátōn autôn (touched their eyes) — háptomai + genitive; Jesus touches the specific affected organ. Anéblepsan (they saw again, recovered sight) and ēkolúthēsan autô̧ (followed him) — sight leads immediately to discipleship, the Matthean pattern of healing as call.

21.42: lithon hon apedokimasan hoi oikodomountes houtos egeneto eis kephalen gonias — the stone which the builders rejected, this one became the head of the corner — apedokimasan (rejected after examination, found to be unfit) is the technical term for the stone-mason's rejection of a stone after inspection. Kephalen gonias (head of the corner) denotes either the capstone (keystone completing an arch) or the cornerstone (the principal foundation stone that sets the line of the building). Both readings apply Christologically: the rejected Jesus becomes the load-bearing stone of the new community. The citation is Ps 118:22-23 LXX.
21.1: ēggisan eis Hierosolyma — drew near to Jerusalem — Eggizō (to draw near) marks the beginning of the Passion Week narrative. Bethphage (Bēthphagē = house of unripe figs in Aramaic) is on the eastern slope of the Mount of Olives; the phrase tote Iēsous apesteilen (then Jesus sent) opens the deliberate arrangement of the entry.
21.2: onon dedemenen kai pōlon met' autēs — a donkey tied and a colt with her — Matthew uniquely has two animals (cf. Mark/Luke: one). The dedemenen (perfect passive participle: having been tied) and lysantes (loosing, aorist) echo a prophetic staging. The ho kyrios autōn chreian echei (the Lord has need of them) phrase uses chreian echei = has need, an expression of genuine requirement.
21.3: ho kyrios autōn chreian echei — the Lord has need of them — The absolute use of ho kyrios (the Lord) without further specification echoes LXX divine-address usage; the disciples apply it directly to Jesus. Euthys de apostellei autous (and immediately he will send them) — the future apostellei implies a prearranged signal.
21.4: hina plērōthē to rhēthen dia tou prophētou — in order that what was spoken through the prophet might be fulfilled — Hina plērōthē + aorist passive subjunctive is Matthewâs standard fulfillment formula. The citation combines Isa 62:11 (LXX) and Zech 9:9, both messianic texts; Matthewâs conflation of sources is deliberate editorial work.
21.5: idou ho basileus sou erchetai soi prays — behold your king comes to you meek — From Zech 9:9 LXX. Prays (meek, gentle) recurs from 11:29 (Jesusâ self-description) and 5:5 (Beatitudes). Epibebēkōs epi onon kai epi pōlon (mounted on a donkey and on a colt) — the Hebrew parallelism of Zech 9:9 (synonymous: one animal) is taken as two animals by Matthewâs literal reading.
21.6: poiēsantes kathōs synetaxen autois ho Iēsous — having done as Jesus directed them — Syntassō (to arrange, direct, command) aorist; the disciplesâ obedience is exact and immediate. The aorist participle poiēsantes precedes the main verb, emphasizing compliance before arrival.
21.7: epethēkan ep' autōn ta himatia — they put the garments on them — Draping garments over the animals and spreading them on the road echoes the royal acclamation of Jehu in 2 Kgs 9:13 (LXX: elabon hekastos to himation autou kai ethēkan). Ekathisen epanō autōn (he sat upon them) — ambiguous plural; likely refers to the garments, not both animals.
21.8: ho de pleistos ochlos estrōsan — the very large crowd spread — Pleistos is the superlative of polys: the largest/very great crowd. Estrōsan (spread, strewed) their garments on the road; others cut kladous apo tōn dendrōn (branches from the trees) — not specifically palm branches as in John 12:13; Matthewâs kladoi = leafy branches generically.
21.9: hōsanna tō huiō Dauid — Hosanna to the Son of David — Hōsanna is the Greek transliteration of Heb. hōšîaʿ-nāʾ (save, please! / O save!) from Ps 118:25-26. By Jesusâ day it had become an acclamation of royal welcome, having lost its strict imperative force. Eulogēmenos ho erchomenos en onomati kyriou quotes Ps 118:26 LXX verbatim; ho erchomenos (the coming one) is a recognized Messianic designation (cf. 11:3).
21.10: eseisthē pasa hē polis — all the city was shaken — Seiō (to shake, quake) is used for earthquake (27:51; 28:4); its application to the cityâs reaction creates an eschatological resonance. Tis estin houtos (who is this?) — the cityâs question mirrors the sea-stilling disciplesâ question (8:27); Jerusalem does not recognize its king.
21.11: houtos estin ho prophētēs Iēsous ho apo Nazareth tēs Galilaias — this is the prophet Jesus from Nazareth of Galilee — The crowd identifies Jesus as ho prophētēs (the prophet), recalling the eschatological prophet of Deut 18:15-18. The full geographical designation (ho apo Nazareth tēs Galilaias) marks regional identity; Jerusalem crowds may intend a slight by the Galilean label.
21.12: exebalen pantas tous pōlountas kai agorazontas — he drove out all those selling and buying — Ekballō (to throw out, expel) is the exorcism verb; its use here frames the temple cleansing as analogous to casting out an unclean presence. Katestrepsen tas trapezas (overturned the tables) — katastrephō = to turn over completely, overturn.
21.13: ho oikos mou oikos proseuchēs klēthēsetai — my house shall be called a house of prayer — Quoting Isa 56:7 LXX. Klēthēsetai (shall be called) future passive = this is its established divine designation. Hymeis de auton poieite spēlaion lēstōn (but you are making it a den of robbers) — quotes Jer 7:11 LXX. Spēlaion (cave, den) + lēstōn (robbers, bandits — Josephus uses lēstēs for violent insurgents) is a jarring contrast to oikos proseuchēs.
21.14: proseēlthan autō typhloi kai chōloi — the blind and the lame came to him — The temple healing follows immediately on the cleansing; the restored temple becomes a place of healing. Typhloi (blind) and chōloi (lame) are those excluded from the temple precincts in Lev 21:18 and 2 Sam 5:8; Jesus reverses the exclusion.
21.15: ta thaumasia ha epoiēsen — the wonderful things he did — Thaumasia (wonderful, marvelous things) is the LXX word for Godâs mighty acts (Ps 71:17 LXX). The chief priestsâ and scribesâ indignation at hōsanna tō huiō Dauid from children reveals their opposition to the Davidic Messianic claim.
21.16: ek stomatos nēpiōn kai thēlazontōn katērtisō ainon — from the mouth of infants and nursing ones you have prepared praise — Quoting Ps 8:3 LXX (MT: ʿoz = strength/power; LXX: ainon = praise — Matthew follows LXX). Jesus applies this to the childrenâs cry; the infant praise vindicates the legitimacy of the acclamation the authorities reject.
21.17: exelthen exō tēs poleōs eis Bēthanian — he went out of the city to Bethany — Jesusâ nightly withdrawal to Bethany (cf. 21:18) is a structural feature of Passion Week; he does not lodge in Jerusalem. Ēulisthē ekei (spent the night there) — aulizomai = to spend the night outdoors or in a courtyard, appropriate to Passover practices.
21.18: prōï de epanagōn eis tēn polin epeinase — returning early to the city he was hungry — Prōï (early) and epanagōn (returning, aorist participle) frame the fig tree episode on the morning after the entry. Epeinase (was hungry) — the aorist describes a state; Matthewâs Jesus experiences genuine physical hunger, affirming full humanity.
21.19: ouden heuron en autē ei mē phylla monon — he found nothing in it except leaves only — Ei mē phylla monon (except leaves only) — the monon (only) is emphatic. Fig trees in Palestine develop fruit buds before full foliage; leaves without any fruit signal abnormality. Exēranthē parachrēma hē sykē (the fig tree withered immediately) — parachrēma = immediately, at once; Matthew compresses Markâs two-day sequence into one.
21.20: pōs parachrēma exēranthē hē sykē — how did the fig tree wither immediately? — The disciplesâ amazement focuses on the instantaneous effect (parachrēma). Exēranthē (aorist passive of xērainō = to dry up, wither) is the same verb used for the withering of seed sown on rocky ground (13:6).
21.21: ean echēte pistin kai mē diakrthēte — if you have faith and do not doubt — Diakrinō in the passive or middle = to doubt, be divided (in judgment); literally to separate, discriminate. Ou monon to tēs sykēs poiēsete (not only the fig tree thing will you do) — the to tēs sykēs (the fig-tree thing) is a demonstrative articular phrase referring to the specific act. Tō̧ orei toutō̧ (to this mountain) = presumably the Mount of Olives.
21.22: panta hosa an aitēsēte en tē proseuchē pisteuontes lēmpsesthe — all things whatever you ask in prayer believing you will receive — Pisteuontes (present active participle: believing) is the condition; the promise is governed by faith-in-prayer, not raw petition. Lēmpsesthe future indicative = you will receive — the confidence of promise, not mere possibility.
21.23: en poiā exousiā tauta poieis — by what authority do you do these things? — Exousia (authority, from exesti = it is permitted) was the central question in Jewish religious politics: who had authorized teaching, healing, and temple action? Kai tis soi edōken tēn exousian tautēn (and who gave you this authority?) — both the basis and source of authority are challenged.
21.24: erōtēsō hymas kagō logon hena — I will ask you also one word — Jesusâ counter-question (logon hena = one question/matter) employs the rabbinic debate technique of silencing an adversary with a counter-dilemma. Hon ean eipēte moi kagō hymin erō (which if you tell me I will also tell you) — the conditional is a formal debate commitment.
21.25: to baptisma to Iōannou pothen ēn — the baptism of John, from where was it? — Ex ouranou ē ex anthrōpōn (from heaven or from humans) — the binary forces recognition: divine authorization or human invention. Ex ouranou is the circumlocution for divine origin common in Jewish speech to avoid the divine name.
21.26: pantes gar hōs prophētēn echousin ton Iōannēn — for all hold John as a prophet — The authoritiesâ reasoning is transparent: political calculation rather than theological judgment. Phoboumenoi ton ochlon (fearing the crowd) governs their answer; this reveals that their authority rests on crowd management, not divine truth.
21.27: oude egō legō hymin en poiā exousiā tauta poiō — neither do I tell you by what authority I do these things — The refusal is not evasion but a declaration: their disqualification from receiving the answer. Ouk oidamen (we do not know) was dishonest — they had deliberated the answer; their own dishonesty disqualifies them.
21.28: anthrōpos eichen tekna dyo — a man had two children — The parable of the two sons sets up a direct application to the authorities and toll collectors. Teknon (child) rather than huios (son) emphasizes the paternal relationship. Hypage sēmeron ergazou en tō̧ ampelōni (go today, work in the vineyard) — the sēmeron (today) stresses urgency.
21.29: ho de apokritheis eipen ou thelō — he answered and said: I do not wish to — The direct refusal ou thelō (I do not wish) is then reversed: hysteron metamelētheis apēlthen (later having changed his mind he went). Metamélomai (to regret, have a change of feeling) differs from metanoeō (repentance from sin); it is emotional reversal leading to action.
21.30: ho de apokritheis eipen egō kyrie — he answered and said: I, lord — The second sonâs instant formal compliance (egō kyrie = I, sir/lord) and then failure to act exposes the hypocrisy. Ouk apēlthen (did not go) — the aorist negation is definitive; he never went.
21.31: hoi telōnai kai hai pornai proagousin hymas eis tēn basileian — the tax collectors and prostitutes go before you into the kingdom — Proagousin (lead the way, go before) is a shocking reversal: those considered most impure enter first. Telōnai (toll collectors) and pornai (prostitutes) represent religious and moral outsiders respectively — both responded to Johnâs call.
21.32: ēlthen gar Iōannēs pros hymas en hodō dikaiosynēs — for John came to you in the way of righteousness — Hodos dikaiosynēs (way/path of righteousness) is a Hebrew idiom (derek ṣedeq) for righteous conduct and teaching. Ou metemelēthēte hysteron (you did not afterwards change your mind) — the same verb metamélomai from v.29; the authorities lack even the emotional reversal the first son displayed.
21.33: allēn parabolēn akousate — hear another parable — The Wicked Tenants parable draws on Isa 5:1-7 (Song of the Vineyard); phragmon (fence/hedge), lēnon (winepress), pyrgon (tower) all appear in Isaiahâs vineyard description, making the allusion deliberate and clear to any biblically literate hearer.
21.34: apesteilen tous doulous autou pros tous geōrgous — he sent his servants to the tenants — Geōrgos (farmer, vine-dresser, tenant) = one who works the earth. Labein tous karpous autou (to receive his fruits) — the ownerâs expectation is a share of the produce, the standard lease arrangement. The servants represent the prophets.
21.35: labontes hoi geōrgoi tous doulous autou — the tenants having seized his servants — Three fates: edeiran (flogged/beat), apektenan (killed), elithobolēsan (stoned) — the escalating violence against prophets mirrors Israelâs historical rejection of prophets (cf. Neh 9:26; Heb 11:36-37).
21.36: apesteilen allous doulous pleionas tōn prōtōn — he sent other servants more than the first — Pleionas (more in number) — the second delegation is larger; the same treatment ensues. Matthewâs two waves of servants (vs. Markâs individual sequence) emphasizes the pattern of rejection.
21.37: hysteron de apesteilen pros autous ton huion autou — last of all he sent his son to them — Hysteron (finally, last of all) marks the climax. Entrapēsontai (they will respect, be shamed before) uses entrepō = to turn toward with respect; the ownerâs reasoning is generous but fatally optimistic — the dramatic irony is complete.
21.38: deute apokteinōmen auton kai schōmen tēn klēronomian autou — come let us kill him and possess his inheritance — Deute (come!) is an imperative of invitation/incitement. Klēronomia (inheritance) = the estate that would pass to the heir; killing the heir before the ownerâs death was a known legal strategy in some Roman contexts. The tenantsâ logic reveals deliberate murderous calculation.
21.39: ekballon exō tou ampelōnos apekteinan — having thrown him outside the vineyard they killed him — The sequence ekballon exō (throwing outside) before killing mirrors Jesusâ own fate: he is taken outside Jerusalem (exō tēs pylēs, Heb 13:12) before crucifixion. Matthew places this in the same order as the historical Passion.
21.40: hotan oun elthē ho kyrios tou ampelōnos — when therefore the lord of the vineyard comes — Jesus turns the parable into a question, forcing the authorities to pronounce their own judgment. Tí poiēsei toîs geōrgois ekeinois (what will he do to those tenants?) — the future deliberative implicates them as judges of their own case.
21.41: kakous kakōs apolesei autous — evildoers he will utterly destroy — The figure kakous kakōs is a Greek wordplay (kakos/kakōs: evil ones evilly) constituting a paronomasia: evil men will meet an evil end. Ekdōsetai ton ampelōna allois geōrgois (he will lease the vineyard to other tenants) — the authorities unwittingly pronounce the transfer of the covenant community to others.
21.43: arthēsetai aph' hymōn hē basileia tou theou — the kingdom of God will be taken from you — Arthēsetai (future passive of airō = to take up, remove) is a decisive eschatological statement. Kai dothēsetai ethnei poiounti tous karpous autēs (and given to a nation producing its fruits) — ethnos here likely refers not to Gentiles specifically but to the reconstituted people of God who bear kingdom fruit.
21.44: ho pesōn epi ton lithon touton synthlasthēsetai — the one falling on this stone will be broken to pieces — Synthlaomai (to shatter, crush to pieces) and likmēsei auton (it will grind him to powder) — likmáō = to winnow, scatter; LXX uses it for divine judgment-scattering. The two images (fall on stone = shattered; stone falls on you = pulverized) both end in destruction; the stone is inescapable in either direction.
21.45: egnōsan hoti peri autōn legei — they knew that he was speaking about them — Egnōsan (aorist of ginōskō) — the recognition is complete; the application of the parable to themselves is understood. The perception drives their desire to seize him rather than repentance.
21.46: kai zētountes auton kratēsai ephobēthēsan tous ochlous — seeking to seize him they feared the crowds — Kratēsai (to seize, arrest) aorist infinitive; ephobēthēsan (feared) governed by crowd support for Jesus. Hoti eis prophētēn auton eichon (because they held him as a prophet) — the same identification as 21:11; the popular perception protects Jesus from immediate arrest.

22.37: agapeseis kyrion ton theon sou en hole te kardia sou kai en hole te psyche sou kai en hole te dianoia sou — you shall love the Lord your God with all your heart and all your soul and all your mind — the Shema (Deut 6:5 LXX) adds dianoia (mind, understanding) to the MT's heart-soul-strength, creating a triad that covers the affective, volitional, and cognitive dimensions of the whole person. The totality-markers (holos = whole, in all three cases) indicate that the love commanded is total — leaving no part of the person divided or uncommitted.
22.44: eipen kyrios to kyrio mou — the LORD said to my Lord — the LXX renders Ps 110:1's YHWH ladon (YHWH to my Adonai); Jesus's argument turns on David calling his own descendant Lord (kyrios), which would be unseemly if the son were merely human. The Davidic messianic expectation assumed the son-of-David would be David's inferior; Jesus uses Ps 110:1 to demonstrate that the Messiah is simultaneously son and superior — a paradox that points to the divine sonship.
22.1: apokritheis ho Iēsous palin eipen — Jesus answering again spoke — Apokritheis (answering) even without a preceding question is a Semitism inherited from LXX (wayyaʿan); it introduces a new discourse. Palin (again) links this parable directly to the preceding sequence.
22.2: hōmoiōthē hē basileia tōn ouranōn anthrōpō̧ basilei — the kingdom of heaven was likened to a king — The Wedding Banquet parable (cf. Luke 14:15-24 — a different version). Hostis epoiēsen gamous tō̧ huiō̧ autou (who made a wedding feast for his son) — gamos (wedding, wedding feast) is the central image; the singular and plural forms both occur in vv. 2-12.
22.3: apesteilen tous doulous autou kalesai tous keklēmenous — he sent his servants to call those who had been invited — Tous keklēmenous (the having-been-called, perfect passive participle) — those pre-invited; the initial invitation is assumed. Kai ouk ēthelon elthein (and they were not willing to come) — the imperfect ēthelon = ongoing unwillingness, sustained refusal.
22.4: ta sitista mou etoimasin — my fatted animals are prepared — Sitista (fatted animals, grain-fed cattle) indicates a lavish feast. Panta hetoima (all things are ready) — the perfect hetoima (in a state of readiness) underscores that the only remaining action is the guestsâ arrival; they have no legitimate excuse.
22.5: amelēsantes de apēlthon — having been careless/indifferent they went off — Ameleō (to be unconcerned, neglect) is the key word; the refusal is not hostile but indifferent — ho men eis ton idion agron (one to his own field), ho de eis tēn emporian autou (another to his business). Economic preoccupation displaces the invitation.
22.6: hoi de loipoi kratēsantes tous doulous autou hybrizan kai apekteinan — the rest seizing his servants insulted and killed them — Hybrizō (to insult, mistreat, outrage) connotes deliberate humiliation beyond mere violence. The escalation from indifference (v.5) to murder (v.6) parallels the Wicked Tenants sequence, reinforcing the allegory of Israelâs rejection of prophets.
22.7: orgistheis de ho basileus apesteilen ta strateumata autou — the king having become angry sent his armies — Strateumata (armies, troops) — unusual for a wedding-feast context; the allegorical dimension is transparent. Eneprēsen tēn polin ekeinēn (burned that city) is widely read as a reference to the destruction of Jerusalem in AD 70 (the aorist describing a future-from-Jesusâ-perspective event).
22.8: ho men gamos hetoimos estin — the wedding feast is ready — The present estin maintains the feastâs continued readiness despite the refusals. Hoi de keklēmenoi ouk ēsan axioi (those who were called were not worthy) — axios (worthy, deserving) — the judgment is on fitness, not on Godâs desire to include them.
22.9: epi tas diexodous tōn hodōn — to the crossings of the roads — Diexodos (thoroughfare, where roads cross and split, outer edges) = the main intersections and exits of the city, where the most diverse traffic would be found. Hosous ean heurēte kalesate eis tous gamous (whoever you find call to the wedding) — the universality of the new invitation.
22.10: synēgagon pantas hous heuron ponērous te kai agathous — they gathered all they found, evil and good alike — Ponērous te kai agathous (both evil and good) — the mission to the crossroads gathers without discrimination; the mixed community prefigures the tares-among-wheat dynamic (cf. 13:24-30). Eplēsthē ho nymphōn anakeimenōn (the wedding hall was filled with those reclining) — anakeimenōn (reclining at table) is the posture of banquet guests.
22.11: eiselthen de ho basileus theasasthai tous anakeimenous — the king came in to see those reclining — Theasasthai (to look at carefully, behold) aorist infinitive of theaomai; a formal inspection. Eidon ekei anthrōpon ouk endedymenon endyma gamou (he saw there a man not clothed with a wedding garment) — endyma gamou (wedding garment) — the absence is the focus, not just improper dress.
22.12: hetaire pōs eisēlthes hōde mē echōn endyma gamou — friend, how did you enter here not having a wedding garment? — Hetaire (companion, friend — distancing form; cf. 20:13; 26:50) used three times in Matthew in contexts of gentle rebuke. Ho de ephimōthē (he was silenced) — phimoō (to muzzle, silence) aorist passive; the man has no answer, indicating his guilt is recognized even by himself.
22.13: dēsantes autou podas kai cheiras ekballete auton — bind his feet and hands and cast him out — Dēsantes (having bound) aorist participle; the binding renders the man helpless. Eis to skotos to exōteron (into the outer darkness) with ho klauthmos kai ho brygmos tōn odontōn (the weeping and gnashing of teeth) = the standard Matthean formula for eschatological judgment (8:12; 13:42,50; 24:51; 25:30).
22.14: polloi gar eisin klētoi oligoi de eklektoi — for many are called but few are chosen — Klētoi (called, invited) and eklektoi (chosen, selected — from eklegō = to choose out) are distinct: the call is universal, the election is particular. This serves as the parableâs theological summary and anticipates the judgment scene of 25:31-46.
22.15: poreutheies synebouleusanto hopōs auton pagideuōsin en logō̧ — having departed they plotted to trap him in a word — Pagideuō (to trap, ensnare — from pagis = snare) is a hunting metaphor; the Pharisees aim to catch Jesus in a statement that either alienates the crowd or invites Roman action. En logō̧ (in a word/statement) = by means of his own words.
22.16: alēthēs ei kai tēn hodon tou theou en alētheia didaskeis — you are truthful and teach the way of God in truth — The flattery (vv. 16b) is transparent but formally correct; it sets up the question as if they sincerely sought Jesusâ unbiased opinion. Ou melei soi peri oudenos (you care about no one) = you are impartial, immune to social pressure — the very quality they hope to exploit.
22.17: exestin dounai kēnson Kaisari ē ou — is it lawful to pay tribute to Caesar or not? — Kēnsos (census tax, poll tax — from Latin census) was the annual per-head tax paid to Rome, deeply resented. The dilemma: yes = betrayal of Jewish national sentiment; no = sedition against Rome. Either answer seemed to destroy Jesus.
22.18: gnous de ho Iēsous tēn ponērian autōn — Jesus knowing their wickedness — Gnous (aorist participle of ginōskō) = having known (supernatural insight). Tēn ponērian (their wickedness, evil intent) — Matthew uses the strong moral term ponēria. Ti me peiraxete hypokritai (why do you test me, hypocrites?) — hypokritēs (stage-actor, one who plays a part) is Jesusâ sharpest address for religious performance.
22.19: epideíxaté moi to nomisma tou kēnsou — show me the tribute coin — Nomisma (coin in common use, currency — from nomos = law/custom) = the standard coin; specifically a denarius bearing Caesarâs image. Jesusâ request for the coin shifts the burden to them — they produce it, implying their participation in the Roman economy.
22.20: tinos hē eikōn hautē kai hē epigraphē — whose image and inscription is this? — Eikōn (image, likeness) and epigraphē (inscription, superscription) together establish identity and ownership. The question invites them to articulate the coinâs nature before Jesus draws the theological conclusion.
22.21: apodote oun ta Kaisaros Kaisari kai ta tou theou tō̧ theō̧ — give back therefore the things of Caesar to Caesar and the things of God to God — Apodidōmi (to give back, render) rather than didōmi (to give) implies returning what is already owed — obligation, not gift. The answer is neither capitulation to Rome nor seditious refusal; it distinguishes two orders of obligation without subordinating the divine to the imperial. Ta tou theou tō̧ theō̧ — the humanity who bears Godâs image (eikōn, Gen 1:26-27) belongs to God.
22.22: thaumasantes aphēkan auton kai apēlthon — marveling they left him and went away — Thaumazō (to marvel, be amazed) without the further step of faith recurs in Matthew as a response that falls short of discipleship. The adversaries are silenced but not converted.
22.23: Saddoukaioi hoi legontes mē einai anastasin — Sadducees who say there is no resurrection — Matthewâs parenthetical gloss identifies the Sadducees by their distinctive doctrinal position: rejection of bodily resurrection (held only Torah as authoritative, not the Prophets or Writings where resurrection texts appear). Epērōtēsan auton (questioned him) — aorist, a single formal question.
22.24: Mōysēs eipen ean tis apothanē mē echōn tekna — Moses said if someone dies not having children — The Sadducees cite Deut 25:5-10 (Levirate marriage law: epigambrieuein = to marry the brotherâs widow). Anastēsei sperma tō̧ adelphō̧ autou (will raise up offspring for his brother) — a technical legal obligation to preserve the deceased brotherâs lineage.
22.25: ēsan de par' hēmin hepta adelphoi — there were among us seven brothers — The constructed scenario escalates to absurdity (seven levirate marriages) precisely to make the resurrection afterlife appear logically incoherent. Aphēken tēn gynaika autou tō̧ adelphō̧ autou (left his wife to his brother) — standard levirate fulfillment vocabulary.
22.26: homoiōs kai ho deuteros kai ho tritos heōs tōn hepta — likewise the second and the third through the seven — The rapid enumeration (homoiōs = likewise, in the same way) through all seven compresses the scenario to reveal its reductio ad absurdum intent.
22.27: hysteron de pantōn apethanen hē gynē — last of all the woman also died — The final death closes the hypothetical chain. Hysteron... pantōn (last of all) places the womanâs death as the culminating event that makes the resurrection question unavoidable.
22.28: en tȩ̄ anastásei oun tinos tōn hepta estai gynē — in the resurrection therefore whose wife of the seven will she be? — The question assumes resurrection must replicate earthly social structures (marriage and ownership) — the very assumption Jesus will demolish. Pantes gar eschon autēn (for all had her) — the final phrase is designed for maximum logical shock.
22.29: planasthe mē eidotes tas graphas mēde tēn dynamin tou theou — you are deceived not knowing the scriptures nor the power of God — Planasthe (present passive: you are being led astray, deceived) is a strong charge; the Sadduceesâ error stems from two ignorances: tas graphas (the scriptures) and tēn dynamin tou theou (the power of God). Both are required to understand resurrection.
22.30: en tȩ̄ anastásei oute gamousin oute gamizontai — in the resurrection they neither marry nor are given in marriage — Gameō (to marry, active: the manâs act) and gamizō (to give in marriage, the fatherâs act of betrothal) distinguish the two parties in Jewish marriage customs. Resurrection life transcends the social institution entirely. All' hōs angeloi en tō̧ ouranō̧ eisin (but as angels in heaven they are) — the comparison is to angelic existence, not to dissolution of personhood.
22.31: peri de tēs anastaseōs tōn nekrōn ouk anegnōte — concerning the resurrection of the dead have you not read? — Jesus now defeats the Sadducees on their own canonical ground: the Torah itself (not the Prophets). To rhēthen hymin hypo tou theou (what was spoken to you by God) — direct divine speech, not merely human author.
22.32: egō eimi ho theos Abraam kai ho theos Isaak kai ho theos Iakōb — I am the God of Abraham and the God of Isaac and the God of Jacob — Ex 3:6 LXX cited with precision. The present tense eimi (I am) is the argument: God speaks of himself as the God of Abraham in the present, not the past — therefore Abraham exists presently in relationship with God. Ouk estin ho theos nekrōn alla zōntōn (he is not God of the dead but of the living) — the logical inference: if God is Abrahamâs God presently, Abraham must be living.
22.33: kai akousantes hoi ochloi exeplēssonto epi tȩ̄ didachȩ̄ autou — the crowds hearing were astonished at his teaching — Ekplēssō (to strike out of oneâs senses, astound) perfect passive — the astonishment is complete and ongoing. Epi tȩ̄ didachȩ̄ autou (at his teaching) — it is the content and method of the argument, not merely a miracle, that produces this response.
22.34: hoi de Pharisaioi akousantes hoti ephimōsen tous Saddoukiaous — the Pharisees having heard that he had silenced the Sadducees — Phimoō (to muzzle, silence) aorist: Jesusâ defeat of the Sadducees satisfies the Pharisees doctrinally but renews their own challenge. Synēchthēsan epi to auto (were gathered together) — a formal assembly to coordinate the next question.
22.35: nomikos peirazōn auton — a lawyer testing him — Nomikos (lawyer, legal expert in Torah — not Roman law) appears only here in Matthew; Mark 12:28 describes him more sympathetically. Peirazōn (testing, present participle) marks adversarial intent, though the question itself is a legitimate and important one in rabbinic discussion.
22.36: poia entolē megalē en tō̧ nomō̧ — which commandment is great in the law? — The question about the greatest commandment (megalē entolē) was a live rabbinic debate; various sages offered summaries. The lawyer seeks either to catch Jesus in a controversial ranking or to hear his answer for debate purposes.
22.38: hautē estin hē megalē kai prōtē entolē — this is the great and first commandment — Jesus identifies Deut 6:5 (the Shema continuation) as megalē kai prōtē (great and first). En holȩ̄ tȩ̄ kardia̧ sou kai en holȩ̄ tȩ̄ psychȩ̄ sou kai en holȩ̄ tȩ̄ dianoia̧ sou — Matthewâs version adds dianoia (mind/understanding) to the Deut 6:5 triad of heart/soul/strength, following the LXX variant.
22.39: deutera de homoia autȩ̄ — and a second is like it — Homoia (like, similar) — Jesusâ addition of the second commandment (Lev 19:18: agapēseis ton plēsion sou hōs seauton) was not asked; he volunteers it as inseparably paired with the first. The neighbor-love command is not subordinate but homoia = of the same kind and order.
22.40: en tautais tais dysin entolais holos ho nomos krematai kai hoi prophētai — on these two commandments hang all the law and the prophets — Krematai (hangs, depends — from kremannymi) is a striking metaphor: the entire Torah and prophetic corpus hangs from these two love-commands as weights hang from a beam. This is the Matthean summary of scriptural ethics (cf. 7:12).
22.41: synēgmenōn de tōn Pharisaiōn — the Pharisees being gathered together — Jesus now takes the initiative; having answered all challenges he poses the decisive Christological question. Epērōtēsen autous ho Iēsous (Jesus questioned them) — the reversal: the interrogated becomes the interrogator.
22.42: tinos huios estin ho christos — whose son is the Christ? — The fundamental question of identity: whose descendant (huios) is the Messiah? Their answer tou Dauid (of David) is correct but insufficient; Jesus will show it is incomplete. The question anticipates 22:43-45.
22.43: pōs oun Dauid en pneumati kalei auton kyrion — how then does David in the Spirit call him Lord? — En pneumati (in the Spirit) = under divine inspiration, the standard understanding of prophetic authorship (cf. 2 Sam 23:2; 2 Tim 3:16). Davidâs psalm-composition is Spirit-inspired, giving his words in Ps 110 the character of divine oracle.
22.45: ei oun Dauid kalei auton kyrion — if therefore David calls him Lord — The conditional is straightforward: if David (as author) called this figure kyrios, and the Messiah is Davidâs descendant, a contradiction appears under ordinary kinship logic. Pōs huios autou estin (how is he his son?) — the question forces recognition that the Messiah transcends human genealogical categories; he is simultaneously Davidâs son and Davidâs Lord.
22.46: kai oudeis edynato apokrithēnai autō̧ logon — and no one was able to answer him a word — Apokrithēnai logon (to answer a word) = to give any reply at all. The silencing of all challengers is total and deliberate: the chief priests, Herodians, Sadducees, and Pharisees have all failed. Oude etólmēsen tis (nor did anyone dare) — tolmaō (to dare, have courage) — even the attempt at further question was abandoned.

23.23: apodekatoun to heduosmon kai to anethon kai to kyminon kai aphekate ta baryteta tou nomou — you tithe mint and dill and cumin and neglect the weightier matters of the law — baryteta (weighty things, the heavy end of a scale) is the metaphor: some laws have greater weight than others. The three weighty matters named are krisis (justice/judgment), eleos (steadfast covenant-love/mercy), and pistis (faithfulness); these are the OT prophetic priorities (Mic 6:8, Hos 6:6) that the scribal tradition had flattened by treating every regulation equally.
23.1: tote ho Iēsous elālēsen tois ochlois kai tois mathētais autou — then Jesus spoke to the crowds and to his disciples — Tote (then) marks a decisive shift from controversy dialogues to direct address. The dual audience (crowds kai disciples) is unusual; the woes that follow are public indictment, not private instruction.
23.2: epi tēs Mōseōs kathedrās ekathisan — they sit on Moses’ seat — Kathedra Mōsēos (Moses’ seat) is a physical chair in synagogues representing authoritative succession to the Mosaic teaching office. Ekathisan (aorist: have sat down, have taken their seat) acknowledges the institutional authority. The tension is between legitimate office and corrupt practice.
23.3: panta oun hosa ean eipōsin hymin poiēsate kai tēreite — all therefore whatever they say to you, do and keep — The command is qualified immediately: kata de ta erga autōn mē poieite (but do not do according to their deeds). The problem is not their teaching authority but the hypocrisy of teaching without practicing.
23.4: desmeuo usin de phortia barēa kai dysbastakta — they bind heavy and hard-to-bear burdens — Desmeūō (to bind, bundle up) + phortia (loads, burdens) + barēa kai dysbastakta (heavy and difficult to carry). Tō daktylō̧ de autōn ou thelousin kinēsai auta (but with their finger they are unwilling to move them) — the contrast between imposing and alleviating: they use one finger metaphorically to add load while refusing to lift a finger to help.
23.5: platūnousin tā phylaktēria — they widen the phylacteries — Phylaktēria (phylacteries) = the tefillin boxes worn on head and arm during prayer (from Deut 6:8; 11:18; Ex 13:9,16). Larger boxes signaled greater piety; platūnō (to make broad/wide) criticizes competitive public display. Megalunousin ta kraspeda (enlarge the tassels) = lengthen the tzitzit on the prayer shawl (Num 15:38-39).
23.6: philousin de tēn prōtōklisian en tois deipnois — they love the place of honor at feasts — Prōtōklisia (first-reclining-place, seat of honor) = the couch nearest the host at a formal banquet. Prōtokathedrias en tais synagōgais (chief seats in the synagogues) = the front benches facing the congregation. Both are public-honor markers.
23.7: tous aspasmous en tais agorais — greetings in the marketplaces — Public greetings with the title Rhabbi (my great one, my teacher — from Heb. rab = great) conferred honor before witnesses. Kaleōsthai hypo tōn anthrōpōn Rhabbi (to be called Rabbi by people) — the passive klēthenai (to be called, to be addressed) is the coveted public recognition.
23.8: hymeis de mē klēthēte Rhabbi — but you, do not be called Rabbi — The aorist passive prohibition mē klēthēte is strong. The grounds: heis gar estin hymōn ho didaskālos (for one is your teacher) and pantes de hymeis adelphoi este (and you are all brothers). The community is horizontally structured under one Teacher (didaskalos), not vertically ranked.
23.9: kai patera mē kalēsēte hymōn epi tēs gēs — and call no one your father on the earth — Patēr (father) as a religious title (equivalent to spiritual father/founder of a school); heis gar estin hymōn ho patēr ho ouranios (for one is your Father, the heavenly one). The prohibition targets honorific titling that displaces divine fatherhood, not biological naming.
23.10: mēde klēthēte kathēgētai — nor be called guides/leaders — Kathēgētēs (guide, instructor, leader — a rarer term than didaskalos; used for philosophical and religious teachers who lead others). Heis gar estiñ hymōn ho Kathēgētēs ho Christos (for one is your Guide, the Christ) — Christ as sole authoritative teacher.
23.11: ho de meizon hymōn estai hymōn diakonos — the greater among you shall be your servant — The comparative meizon (greater) picks up the status-vocabulary of the Woes and redirects it: greatness is servant-function (diakonos = table-servant). This repeats the community principle from 20:26-27.
23.12: hostis de hypsōsei heauton tapeinōthēsetai — whoever exalts himself will be humbled — The divine passives (tapeinōthēsetai = will be humbled; hypsōthēsetai = will be exalted) are eschatological reversals enacted by God. The pattern inverts all social-honor logic and is a structuring principle throughout the Sermon and beyond.
23.13: ouai de hymin grammateis kai Pharisaioi hypokritai — woe to you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites — Ouai is a lament-curse (not primarily imprecation but prophetic grief over coming judgment). Hoti kleiete tēn basileian tōn ouranōn emprosthen tōn anthrōpōn (because you shut the kingdom of heaven before people) — kleiō (to shut, lock); they neither enter nor permit others entry (tous eiserchotas).
23.14: [v.14 absent in earliest manuscripts — NA28 omits; a textual insertion parallel to Mark 12:40 / Luke 20:47]
23.15: periagete tēn thalassan kai tēn xēran poiēsai hena prosēlyton — you travel the sea and the dry land to make one proselyte — Prosēlytos (proselyte, convert — from prosēlythō = to come to) = a Gentile who fully converted to Judaism including circumcision and immersion. The zeal is acknowledged; the indictment is that the product (huion geennēs = son of Gehenna) ends worse than before: diploteron hymōn (twice as much as yourselves).
23.16: hodoi typhloi — blind guides — Hodos (guide, one who leads on the path) + typhloi (blind) = a self-defeating combination. The Woe focuses on casuistry about oath-binding: swearing by the naos (inner sanctuary) vs. the gold of the naos. Jesus’ critique targets selective oath-binding that creates loopholes in solemn commitments.
23.17: mōroi kai typhloi — fools and blind ones — Mōros (fool, dull, stupid — a rare strong reproach; in 5:22 its use against a brother was condemned) used here by Jesus himself against the scribes in their capacity as false teachers. The rhetorical question: tis gar meizon estin ho chrysos ē ho naos ho hagiasas ton chryson (which is greater, the gold or the sanctuary that sanctified the gold?) inverts the reasoning: the sanctuary confers sanctity on the gold, not vice versa.
23.18: hos d’ an omōsē en tō̄ thysiastēriō̧ ouden estin — whoever swears by the altar is nothing — The same casuistry applied to the altar: swearing by the altar is dismissed as non-binding while swearing by the gift on it is binding. Opheilei (is obligated, owes) = carries legal force. Jesus dismantles the loophole structure entirely.
23.19: typhloi — blind ones — The repetition of typhloi emphasizes the moral blindness underlying the casuistry. Ti gar meizon to dōron ē to thysiastērion to hagiazōn to dōron (for what is greater, the gift or the altar that sanctifies the gift?) — the same logic as v.17: the sanctifying agent is greater than what it sanctifies.
23.20: ho oun omōsas en tō̄ thysiastēriō̧ omnuei en autō̧ kai en pasin tois epanō autou — whoever swears by the altar swears by it and by all things on it — Jesus’ resolution collapses all the casuistic distinctions: swearing by any part of the temple complex implicates the whole and ultimately God himself. The sequence builds through vv.20-22 to the same conclusion: all oaths invoke God.
23.21: kai ho omōsas en tō̄ naō̧ omnuei en autō̧ kai en tō̄ katoikounti auton — and whoever swears by the sanctuary swears by it and by the one dwelling in it — Ho katoikōn (the one who dwells) = God, whose Shekinah presence fills the naos. Every oath by the temple is ultimately an oath by the divine Presence.
23.22: kai ho omōsas en tō̄ ouranō̧ omnuei en tō̄ thronō̧ tou theou — and whoever swears by heaven swears by the throne of God — The final step: swearing by heaven invokes the divine throne. Jesus’ argument (drawing on 5:33-37) shows all oath-distinctions collapse into direct appeal to God; therefore straightforward speech (nai/ou) is required.
23.24: hodoi typhloi hoi diyōlizein ton kōnōpa — blind guides who strain out the gnat — Diűlizō (to strain through, filter) — wine was filtered to remove insects that would make it ritually impure. Kōnōps (gnat, mosquito) = the smallest ritually impure insect; kamēlon de katapinon tes (but swallowing the camel) = the largest land animal. The gnat/camel contrast is extreme comic hyperbole; both are unclean (Lev 11), but the proportions reveal absurd priority inversion.
23.25: katharizete to exōthen tou potēriou kai tēs paropsidos — you cleanse the outside of the cup and dish — Exōthen (outside) vs. esōthen (inside) — the Woe targets ritual purity practice that leaves the interior (gemousin ex harpagēs kai akrasias = full of extortion and self-indulgence) untouched. Harpagē (plunder, extortion) and akrasia (lack of self-control, incontinence) describe the moral contents contrasted with the clean exterior.
23.26: Pharisaie typhle katharison prōton to entos tou potēriou — blind Pharisee, first cleanse the inside of the cup — The singular Pharisaie (Pharisee) shifts from plural address to singular, making the charge personal. Hina genētai kai to ektos autou katharon (so that the outside also becomes clean) — interior purification naturally produces exterior cleanness; the reverse is impossible.
23.27: paromoiazete taphois kekoniamenois — you are like whitewashed tombs — Kekoniamenos (perfect passive participle of koniáō = to whitewash with lime) — tombs were whitewashed before Passover (Nisan) so pilgrims would not inadvertently touch them and incur corpse-impurity. Exōthen men phainontai horaioi (outwardly appear beautiful/clean) — horaios (beautiful, seasonable, timely) contrasts with esōthen de gemousin osteon nekrōn (but inside full of dead men’s bones).
