23.28: houtos kai hymeis exōthen men phainesthe tois anthrōpois dikaioi — so you also outwardly appear righteous to people — Phainō (to appear, seem) vs. genuine reality: esōthen de este mestoi hypokriseōs kai anomias (but inside you are full of hypocrisy and lawlessness). Hypokrisis (stage-playing, pretense) and anomia (lawlessness, without-law-ness) are the twin interior conditions behind the righteous facade.
23.29: oikodomei te ta mnēmeia tōn prophētōn — you build the tombs of the prophets — The final Woe targets the self-deceiving claim that the current generation would not have killed the prophets as their ancestors did (legete: ei ēmetha en tais hēmerais tōn paterōn hēmōn). The tomb-building is presented as a claim to discontinuity with the murderous past.
23.30: ouk an ēmetha koinōnoi autōn — we would not have been partners with them — Koinōnōs (partner, sharer, participant) in the blood of the prophets. The second-class conditional (ei ēmetha... ouk an ēmetha) expresses a counterfactual, but Jesus’ response (v.31-32) shows their claim is self-indicting.
23.31: hoste martyreite heautois hoti huioi este tōn phoneusāntōn tous prophētas — so you witness against yourselves that you are sons of those who murdered the prophets — Martyreite heautois (you testify against yourselves) — their own admission of ancestry is the self-incriminating testimony. Huioi (sons) here = those who continue the character/pattern, not merely biological descendants.
23.32: kai hymeis plērōsate to metron tōn paterōn hymōn — and you fill up the measure of your fathers — Plērōsate (aorist imperative or prophetic aorist: fill up completely) — metron (measure) = the fixed capacity that must be reached before judgment falls (cf. Gen 15:16; 1 Thess 2:16). Jesus speaks as if the murder of himself is the act that completes the measure.
23.33: opheis genēmata echidnōn — serpents, brood of vipers — The same epithet John the Baptist applied to Pharisees (3:7; 12:34). Pōs phygēte apo tēs kriseōs tēs geennēs (how will you flee from the judgment of Gehenna?) — kriseos (genitive of krisis) + geenna (Gehenna = Valley of Hinnom, the place of eschatological fire).
23.34: prophētas kai sophous kai grammateis — prophets and wise ones and scribes — Jesus sends these emissaries (apōstellō = I send; first-person: Jesus himself sends, in divine authority). Three fates: apokteneite (kill), staurosete (crucify), mastigōsete (flog), diōxete (persecute) — the full range of violence against Christian missionaries anticipated.
23.35: haimatos dikaiou ekchynnomenou epi tēs gēs — righteous blood shed upon the earth — From Habel tou dikaiou (Abel the righteous, Gen 4:8) to Zachariou huiou Barachious (Zechariah son of Barachiah) — the canonical span from Genesis to 2 Chronicles (the last book in the Hebrew canon). Ekchynnomenon (being poured out, present passive participle) keeps the spilled blood as ongoing cry before God.
23.36: hēxei tauta panta epi tēn genean tautēn — all these things will come upon this generation — Genea autē (this generation) throughout Matthew’s Passion block refers to the contemporaries of Jesus; the fulfillment in AD 70 is the historical horizon. Hēxei (future: will come) is a judicial visitation term.
23.37: Iērousalom Iērousalom hē apokteinousa tous prophētas — Jerusalem, Jerusalem, the one killing the prophets — The doubled vocative (Iērousalom Iērousalom) is a lament formula (cf. 2 Sam 18:33; LXX repeated vocative as grief). Pōsákis ēthelēsa episynagagein ta tekna sou (how often I wished to gather your children) — episynagō (gather together) + hōs ornis episynagei tā nossia heautēs (as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings) — a maternal image of divine protection-longing.
23.38: idou aphietai hymin ho oikos hymōn eremos — behold your house is left to you desolate — Ho oikos (the house) = the temple; eremos (desolate, abandoned) echoes the Shekinah’s departure in Ezek 10-11. The divine aphietai (is left, abandoned — present passive) implies God’s withdrawal from the temple.
23.39: ou mē me idēte ap’ arti heōs an eipēte — you will by no means see me from now until you say — Ou mē + aorist subjunctive = the strongest Greek negation. Eulogēmenos ho erchomenos en onomati kyriou (blessed is the coming one in the name of the Lord) = Ps 118:26, the same acclamation from the Triumphal Entry (21:9); the future return will be greeted with the same words, but then as recognition.

24.30: kai tote phanesetai to semeion tou huiou tou anthropou en ourano — and then the sign of the Son of Man will appear in heaven — the sign (semeion) is disputed: whether the cross appearing in the sky, or the Son of Man himself as the sign, or the Shekinah-cloud. The citation of Dan 7:13 that follows (erchomenon epi ton nephelos tou ouranou) suggests the sign is the appearing of the Son of Man himself in the divine glory-cloud. kopsontai pasai hai phylai tes ges — all the tribes of the earth will mourn — citing Zech 12:10 (the mourning that follows seeing the one they pierced); grief and recognition converge at the parousia.
24.1: eporeueto apo tou hierou — was going away from the temple — Poreuomai (imperfect: was going away) signals a definitive departure from the temple. Prosēlthon hoi mathētai autou epideixai autō̧ tās oikodomās tou hierou (the disciples came to point out the buildings of the temple) — oikodomās (buildings, constructions) in the plural refers to the entire temple complex.
24.2: ou mē aphethē̄ ōde lithos epi lithon — there will by no means be left here stone upon stone — Ou mē + aorist passive subjunctive = absolute negation of any remaining structure. Hos ou kataly thēsetai (that will not be thrown down) — katalyō (to loosen down, demolish) is used both literally for building-destruction and metaphorically (26:61; 27:40). Fulfilled in AD 70 under Titus.
24.3: tis hē sēmeia tēs sēs parousias kai synteleiās tou aiōnos — what is the sign of your presence/coming and of the completion of the age? — Parousia (presence, arrival — used for royal/imperial visits) is the technical NT term for the Second Advent. Synteleias tou aiōnos (completion/end of the age) — synteleia is consummation, bringing to full end; tou aiōnos = of the age/era.
24.4: blepete mē tis hymas planēsē̄ — see to it that no one leads you astray — Planāō (to cause to wander, to mislead) is the eschatological deception verb; it recurs in vv.5, 11, 24. The primary danger is not persecution but deception — being misled about the identity of the Christ and the timing of the end.
24.5: polloi gar eleusontai epi tō̄ onomati mou legontes egō eimi ho christos — for many will come in my name saying I am the Christ — Epi tō̄ onomati mou (in/on my name) = using the authority of the name of Jesus as their credential. Egō eimi ho christos (I am the Christ) — the false messiah claim. Pollōs planēsousin (they will lead many astray) — the deception’s scope is wide.
24.6: mellēsete de akouein polemos kai akoai polemōn — you will hear of wars and rumors of wars — Akoai polemōn (reports/rumors of wars) — the hearing of distant conflicts. Horāte mē throeisthe (see that you are not alarmed) — throeō (to cry out in alarm, to be disturbed) — these are conditions not to be interpreted as the end: all’ oupo estin to telos (but the end is not yet).
24.7: egerthēsetai gar ethnos epi ethnos kai basileia epi basileian — for nation will rise against nation and kingdom against kingdom — The double epi (against) construction with repetition is a LXX war-oracle pattern (Isa 19:2; 2 Chr 15:6). Loimoi kai seismoi kata topous (plagues and earthquakes in various places) — kata topous (in various/diverse places) limits the scope: localized, not final.
24.8: panta de tauta archē ōdinōn — all these are the beginning of birth pangs — Ōdines (birth pains, labor pangs) is an OT apocalyptic metaphor for the distresses preceding the Messianic age (Isa 26:17; Jer 22:23; Mic 4:9-10; 1QH). Archē (beginning) positions these events as the onset of a process, not the culmination.
24.9: tote paradosousin hymas eis thlipsin — then they will hand you over into tribulation — Paradidōmi (to hand over, the passion vocabulary) is now applied to disciples. Thlipsis (tribulation, pressure, affliction) — the same word used for the great tribulation in v.21. Apoktenousin hymas (will kill you) — direct persecution. Misounōmenoi hypo pantōn tōn ethnōn (hated by all the nations) — universal hostility.
24.10: kai tote skandalisthēsontai polloi — and then many will fall away — Skandalizō (to cause to stumble, to cause to fall away) — the passive future: many will be caused to stumble from faith. Allēlous paradosousin (they will betray one another) — communal breakdown; persecution produces internal betrayal. Misēsousin allēlous (will hate one another).
24.11: polloi pseudoprophetai egerthesontai — many false prophets will arise — Pseudoprophētēs (false prophet) — one who claims prophetic authority falsely. Planēsousin pollous (will lead many astray) — the deception vocabulary recurs (cf. v.4,5). The false prophets operate within the community, not merely from outside.
24.12: dia to plēthyn thēnai tēn anomian psēgēsetai hē agapē tōn pollōn — because of the increase of lawlessness the love of many will grow cold — Anomīa (lawlessness) as cause; psúchō (to cool down, grow cold) as effect on agapē (love). The causal connection: sustained exposure to ethical collapse erodes love. Tōn pollōn = of the many (the majority), not universally.
24.13: ho de hypominās eis telos houtos sōthēsetai — but the one who endures to the end will be saved — Hypominō (to remain under, to endure — not passive resignation but active steadfastness under pressure). Eis telos (to the end) — whether the end of one’s life or the end of the age. Sōthēsetai (will be saved) future passive = eschatological salvation.
24.14: kērych thēsetai touto to euangelion tēs basileias en holȩ̄ tē̄ oikoumenē̄ — this gospel of the kingdom will be proclaimed in all the inhabited world — Oikoumenē (the inhabited world — the Greek term for the civilized/Roman world) is here given universal scope. Eis martyrion pasin tois ethnesin (as a testimony to all the nations) — martyrion (witness, testimony, evidence). Kai tote hēxei to telos (and then the end will come) — the gospel-to-all-nations is a precondition for the end.
24.15: to bdelygma tēs erēmōseōs — the abomination of desolation — Bdelygma (abomination — from bdelyssōmai = to be disgusted by, to abhor) is the LXX term for idolatrous desecration (Dan 9:27; 11:31; 12:11 LXX). Hestōs en topō̧ hagiō̧ (standing in a holy place) — the masculine participle hestōs with the neuter noun bdelygma may suggest a person (antichrist figure) rather than an object. Ho anaginnōskōn noeitō (let the reader understand) is a direct address to the reader of the written prophecy.
24.16: tote hoi en tē̄ Ioudaiā̧ pheugetōsan eis ta orē — then let those in Judea flee to the mountains — Pheugetōsan (present imperative 3rd plural: let them flee, keep fleeing) — urgency of continuous flight. The command applies to those in Judea specifically, not universally; the geographical specificity supports a first-century Jerusalem referent.
24.17: ho epi tou dōmatos mē katabatō — let the one on the rooftop not come down — Palestinian flat-roofed houses had exterior stairs; one could flee directly from rooftop to street without entering the house to gather possessions. Ârai ta ek tēs oikias autou (to take the things from his house) — the urgency of escape outweighs property retrieval.
24.18: kai ho en tō̄ agrō̧ mē epistrepsatō opisō — and the one in the field, let him not turn back — Epistrepho (to turn back, return) — a worker in the field should not return home for his cloak (himation). The same urgency governs both rooftop and field scenarios.
24.19: ouai de tais en gastri echousais kai tais thēlazousais — woe to those who are pregnant and to those nursing — Ouai here is genuine lamentation over physical vulnerability in flight: pregnant women and nursing mothers cannot flee quickly. The ouai is compassionate, not judicial.
24.20: proseuchesthe de hina mē genētai hē phygē hymōn cheimonos mēde sabbatō̧ — pray that your flight not happen in winter nor on a Sabbath — Winter flight is physically dangerous (flooding wadis, cold). Sabbath flight raises a Jewish law issue: travel beyond the Sabbath limit (techum Shabbat = ~2000 cubits) is prohibited; a Sabbath escape would force a choice between legal observance and survival. The prayer assumes continued Sabbath observance in the community.
24.21: estai gar tote thlipsis megalē hoia ou gegonen ap’ archēs kosmou — for then there will be great tribulation such as has not been from the beginning of the world — Thlipsis megalē (great tribulation) — the decisive eschatological crisis. The comparison formula hoia ou gegonen (such as has not been) echoes Dan 12:1 LXX. The phrase heōs tou nyn (until now) + oud’ ou mē genētai (nor will ever be) brackets the event as uniquely catastrophic.
24.22: ei mē ekolobōthēsan hai hēmerai ekeinai ouk an esōthē pasa sarx — if those days had not been cut short, no flesh would be saved — Koloboō (to cut short, to mutilate by cutting off — from kolobos = maimed) — the shortening is divine mercy. Pasa sarx (all flesh) = all living humans. Dia de tous eklektous (but for the sake of the elect) — the elect’s preservation motivates the divine shortening of tribulation.
24.23: tote ean tis hymin eipē̄ idou hōde ho christos ē hōde — then if anyone says to you: here is the Christ, or here! — The repeated warning against false location-claims for the Christ (vv.23-26) uses idou hōde... ē hōde (here... or there) to characterize false messiahs as geographically bounded. Mē pisteusēte (do not believe) — aorist subjunctive prohibition.
24.24: egerthēsontai gar pseudochristoi kai pseudoprophetai — for false christs and false prophets will arise — Pseudochristos (false christ, counterfeit anointed one) and pseudoprophētēs (false prophet) are paired. Dōsousin sēmeia megala kai terata (will give great signs and wonders) — the deception’s power is real; the signs are genuine miracles. Hōste planēsai ei dynaton kai tous eklektous (so as to lead astray, if possible, even the elect) — ei dynaton (if possible) — the elect cannot ultimately be permanently deceived, but the attempt is real.
24.25: idou proeireka hymin — behold I have told you beforehand — Prolegō (to say beforehand, to forewarn) perfect active: the warning is completed and stands on record. The pre-announcement serves as authentication when the events occur and as inoculation against surprise.
24.26: ean oun eipōsin hymin idou en tē̄ erēmō̄ estin mē exelthēte — if therefore they say: he is in the wilderness, do not go out — Two false-location scenarios: wilderness (erēmos — where messianic movements gathered, cf. Acts 21:38) and inner rooms (tameiois — secret, hidden location). Both are rejected: the true Parousia will not be localized or secret.
24.27: hōsper gar hē astrapē exerchetai apo anatolōn — for as the lightning comes from the east — Astrapē (lightning flash) + phainetai heōs dysmōn (shines to the west) — lightning is universally visible, not localized. The Parousia of the Son of Man will be similarly visible to all simultaneously, requiring no one to point it out. Houtōs estai hē parousia tou huiou tou anthrōpou — the comparison is precise.
24.28: hopou ean ȩ̄ to ptōma ekei synachthēsontai hoi aetoi — wherever the corpse is there the eagles will be gathered — Ptōma (fallen body, corpse) and aetoi (eagles or vultures; in Palestine, likely griffon vultures that are drawn to carrion from great distances). The proverb may signal that judgment follows its object as inevitably as scavengers find a carcass, or that the Parousia (like lightning v.27) is unmistakably obvious when it comes.
24.29: eutheos de meta tēn thlipsin tōn hēmerōn ekeinōn — immediately after the tribulation of those days — Eutheōs (immediately, at once) connects the cosmic signs directly to the tribulation. Ho hēlios skotisthēsetai (the sun will be darkened) — cosmic de-creation language from Isa 13:10 (Babylon oracle) and Joel 2:10,31; 3:15. The powers of the heavens being shaken (hai dynameis tōn ouranōn saleuthhsontai) echoes Isa 34:4 and Hag 2:6,21.
24.31: apostelei tous angelous autou meta sal pignos megalēs phōnēs — he will send his angels with a great trumpet sound — Salpi nx megalē (great trumpet) echoes Isa 27:13 (the great trumpet gathering the scattered of Israel) and the Shofar Gadol of Jewish eschatology. Episynaxousin tous eklektous autou (will gather together his elect) — episynagō (to gather together to oneself) from ek tōn tessarōn anemōn (from the four winds) = universal gathering from every direction.
24.32: apo de tēs sykēs mathete tēn parabolēn — from the fig tree learn the parable — Mathete (learn, aorist imperative from manthanō) the lesson that nature teaches. Hotan ēdē ho klados autēs genētai hapalos (when already its branch becomes tender) — hapalos (tender, soft) of fresh spring growth. The fig tree’s budding is a natural sign that summer (theros) is near; the eschatological signs similarly indicate nearness.
24.33: ginos kete hoti eggys estin epi thyrais — know that it is near, at the doors — Eggys (near, close) + epi thyrais (at the doors) = immediacy idiom for imminent arrival. The subject is ambiguous: the end, or the Son of Man; the ambiguity is probably intentional.
24.34: ou mē parelthu hē genea autē — this generation will by no means pass away — Ou mē + aorist subjunctive = strongest negation. Genea autē (this generation) is the key exegetical question: Jesus’ contemporaries (first-century fulfillment in AD 70 for the near horizon) or the generation that sees the end-time signs (far-horizon reading). The double referent allows both readings.
24.35: ho ouranos kai hē gē pareleusontai hoi de logoi mou ou mē parelthōsin — heaven and earth will pass away but my words will by no means pass away — The contrast between cosmic impermanence (pareleusontai = will pass away, future indicative) and the permanence of Jesus’ words (ou mē parelthōsin = will by no means pass away) is a Christological claim: Jesus’ word has greater ontological stability than creation itself.
24.36: peri de tēs hēmeras ekeinēs kai hōras oudeis oiden — but concerning that day and hour no one knows — Oudeis oiden (no one knows) — absolute ignorance across all categories: oude hoi angeloi tōn ouranōn (not even the angels of heaven) and oude ho huios (not even the Son — present in some MSS, absent in others; Matthean text-critical debate). Ei mē ho patēr monos (except the Father alone).
24.37: hōsper gar ai hēmerai tou Nōe houtōs estai hē parousia — for as the days of Noah so will be the Parousia — The Noah analogy emphasizes normalcy and surprise: trogontes kai pinontes (eating and drinking), gamoyntes kai gamizonte s (marrying and giving in marriage) — ordinary life, not eschatological alertness. Heōs hēs ēmeras eisēlthen Nōe eis tēn kibōton (until the day Noah entered the ark).
24.38: ēsan gar en tais hēmerais ekeinais priston tou kataklysmou trogontes kai pinontes — for in those days before the flood they were eating and drinking — Kataklysmos (flood, inundation — from kataklyzo = to flood over) is the standard Greek term for the Noahic flood. Priston tou kataklysm ou (before the flood) — the total unawareness despite Noah’s preaching.
24.39: kai ouk egnosan hōs ēlthen ho kataklysmos — and they did not know until the flood came — Ouk egnōsan (did not know) — deliberate ignorance or culpable inattention; the flood’s arrival (ēlthen) broke their normalcy without warning. Kai ēren hapantas (and took them all away) — airō (to lift up, take away) in the judgment sense. Houtōs estai kai hē parousia tou huiou tou anthrōpou (so will be the Parousia of the Son of Man).
24.40: tote duo esontai en tō̄ agrō̧ — then two will be in the field — The paired-person scenarios (field, mill) show that identical external situations will have opposite outcomes. Heis paralam bānetai kai heis aphietai (one is taken and one is left) — paralambanō (to take alongside, to receive) vs. aphiēmi (to leave behind, dismiss) — the direction of the verbs suggests the taken one is received/saved and the left one is abandoned to judgment (though interpretations vary).
24.41: dyo alēthousai en tō̄ mylōni — two women grinding at the mill — Alēthō (to grind grain) + mylos (mill, millstone) — domestic labor, typically women’s work. The same division: mia paralambanetai kai mia aphietai (one is taken and one is left). The separation occurs within ordinary domestic life, not during extraordinary circumstances.
24.42: grēgoreite oun hoti ouk oidate poiā hēmera ho kyrios hymōn erchetai — watch therefore because you do not know on what day your Lord comes — Grēgoreō (to be awake, be alert, watch — from egerthēnai = to arise from sleep) is the eschatological watchword. The ignorance of the day (ouk oidate) motivates continuous vigilance, not passive waiting.
24.43: ekeino de ginōskete hoti ei ē̄dei ho oikodespotēs — but know this: if the householder had known — Oikodespotēs (householder, master of the house) is used in a second-class conditional (ei + aorist indicative... an + aorist indicative) = contrary to fact: he did not know, so he was robbed. Poia phylakē̄ ho klēptēs erchetai (what watch the thief comes) — the night was divided into four watches (Roman system).
24.44: dia touto kai hymeis ginesthe heti moi hoi — therefore you also be ready — Ginesthe h etoimoi (present imperative: keep being ready) — ongoing state of preparedness, not a one-time act. Hō̄ ou dokei ōra (at an hour you do not expect) — the unexpected hour is the precise one that comes; expectation does not determine timing.
24.45: tis ara estin ho pistos doulos kai phronimos — who then is the faithful and wise servant? — The parable of the faithful and wicked servants interprets eschatological watchfulness through the figure of a household steward. Pistos kai phronimos (faithful and wise) — the pairing of reliability and practical wisdom. Epi tēs oiketeias autou (over his household) — oiketeia (household, domestic staff) — the steward’s responsibility is the whole household’s provision.
24.46: makarios ho doulos ekeinos hon elthōn ho kyrios autou heurēsei houtōs poiounta — blessed is that servant whom his lord when he comes will find so doing — Makārios (blessed — the Beatitude word) applied to the faithful servant found at work on the master’s return. Poiounta (present participle: doing, actively engaged) — not waiting passively but actively serving.
24.47: epi pasin tois hyparchousin autou katastesei auton — over all his possessions he will appoint him — Kath istēmi (to appoint, install, set in place) — the faithful steward’s reward is expanded responsibility, not retirement. Epi pasin tois hyparchousin (over all his possessions) = complete estate management.
24.48: ean de eipē̄ ho kakos doulos ekeinos — but if that wicked servant says — The third-class conditional (ean + aorist subjunctive) presents the wicked servant’s reasoning as possible, not certain. Chronizei mou ho kyrios (my master is delayed) — chronizō (to take time, delay, be a long time) — the delay itself becomes the rationalization for misconduct.
24.49: arxētai de typtein tous syndoulous autou — he begins to beat his fellow servants — Syndoūlos (fellow servant, co-slave) — the wicked servant exploits those under him while the master is absent. Esthī̄ de kai pinē̄ meta tōn methyontōn (and eats and drinks with the drunk ones) — self-indulgence and violent domination are paired as the twin abuses of delegated authority.
24.50: hēxei ho kyrios tou doulou ekeinou en hēmera h̄ ou prosdokā̄ — the master of that servant will come on a day he does not expect — Hēxei (will come, future indicative of hēkō = to have arrived, to be present) — the arrival is certain; only the timing is unknown. The doubled ou prosdokā̄ (does not expect) + en hōrā̄ ou ginōskei (at an hour he does not know) emphasizes complete unpreparedness.
24.51: dichotomēsei auton kai to meros autou meta tōn hypokritōn thēsei — he will cut him in two and put his portion with the hypocrites — Dichotomēsei (to cut in two, to bisect — from dicha = in two + temnō = to cut) is a brutal image of complete destruction/dismemberment used for execution. Meta tōn hypokritōn (with the hypocrites) — the final category for the unfaithful steward. Ho klauthmos kai ho brygmos tōn odontōn recurs as the judgment formula.

25.40: eph hoson epoiesate heni touton ton adelphon mou ton elachiston emoi epoiesate — inasmuch as you did it to one of the least of these my brothers, you did it to me — the identification of Jesus with the least (elachistos, the lowest-ranked, the most inconsequential) is the theological core of the passage. The phrasing eph hoson (to the degree that, inasmuch as) makes the identification proportional and complete: every act of mercy to the suffering is simultaneously an act toward Jesus himself. This is not metaphor but an ontological claim about Christ's solidarity with human suffering.
25.1: tote homoiōthēsetai hē basileia tōn ouranōn deka parthenois — then the kingdom of heaven will be likened to ten virgins — Tote (then) ties the parable to the preceding eschatological discourse. Parthenois (virgins, maidens) — unmarried young women attending the bridal party. Aitines laboũsai tas lampadas heautōn exēlthon eis hypantēsin tou nymphiou (who having taken their lamps went out to meet the bridegroom) — hypantēsin (meeting, encounter — legal/ceremonial term for going out to receive an arriving dignitary).
25.2: pente de ex autōn ēsan mōrai kai pente phronimoi — five of them were foolish and five were wise — Mōrai (foolish, dull — cf. 5:22; 7:26; 23:17) and phronimoi (wise, prudent, practically intelligent — cf. 7:24; 10:16; 24:45). The split is exactly equal; the parable is not about a minority of fools but equal distribution, making the outcome sobering.
25.3: hai gar mōrai labousai tas lampadas autōn ouk elabon meth' heautōn elaion — for the foolish having taken their lamps did not take oil with them — Elaion (olive oil) for the lamps. The foolishness is not having lamps but failing to bring adequate oil for an unpredictably long wait. The present participle labousai (having taken) is followed by the aorist negation ouk elabon — the taking of lamps and the not-taking of extra oil are simultaneous oversights.
25.4: hai de phronimoi elabon elaion en tois aggeioīs — but the wise took oil in their vessels — Aggeia (vessels, containers — from aggos = container) = the extra flasks carried alongside the lamps. Meta tōn lampadōn heautōn (with their lamps) — the wise carry both the lamp and the reserve supply.
25.5: chronizōntos de tou nymphiou enystaxan pãsai kai ekoimēthēsan — the bridegroom delaying, they all slumbered and slept — Chronizō (to be delayed, to take time — cf. 24:48) — the bridegroomâs delay is deliberate in the story; in the allegory it represents the unexpectedly long interval before the Parousia. Enystaxan (aorist of nystazō = to nod off, doze) and ekoimēthēsan (fell asleep) — both verbs; all ten sleep, wise and foolish alike.
25.6: mesēs de nyktos kraugē gegonen idou ho nymphios exerchetai — in the middle of the night a cry has come: behold the bridegroom is coming — Mesēs de nyktos (in the middle of the night) = midnight, the most unexpected hour. Kraugē (a shout, cry — from kraugazō) — sudden announcement. Exerchesthe eis apantēsin (go out to the meeting) — the formal escort-reception.
25.7: ēgeiran pasai hai parthenoi ekeinai kai ekosmēsan tas lampadas heautōn — all those virgins arose and trimmed their lamps — Egeírō (to raise, arouse, awaken) — all ten respond equally to the cry. Kosmēō (to put in order, trim, adorn) the lamps — either trimming the wick or refilling the oil reservoir. The crisis reveals the hidden difference.
25.8: hai de mōrai tais phronimois eipon — the foolish said to the wise — Dote hēmin ek tou elaiou hymōn (give us some of your oil) — dote (give, aorist imperative) + ek tou elaiou (from the oil, partitive genitive). Hoti hai lampades hēmōn sbennyntai (because our lamps are going out) — sbennymi (present passive: are being extinguished, going out) — the present tense shows the crisis is immediate.
25.9: mēpote ou mē arkesē̄ hēmin kai hymin — lest there might not be enough for us and for you — Mēpote (lest, perhaps — a cautionary conjunction). Ou mē arkesē̄ (might by no means be sufficient) — the wise are not selfish; they recognize the oil is not transferable — preparedness cannot be borrowed at the last moment. Poreuesthe mallon pros tous pōlountas (go rather to the sellers) — mallon (rather, instead) redirects them to the only available source.
25.10: aperchoménōn de autōn agorásai ēlthen ho nymphios — while they were going to buy, the bridegroom came — The genitive absolute aperchoménōn... agorásai (while they were going to buy) frames their absence as the decisive moment. Ai hetoimoi eisēlthon met' autou eis tous gamous (the prepared ones went in with him to the wedding feast) — hetoimoi (the ready ones, from hetoimos). Ekleisthē hē thyra (the door was shut) — the aorist passive is definitive: the door has been shut and remains shut.
25.11: hysteron de erchontai kai hai loipai parthenoi legousai — later the rest of the virgins also come saying — Hysteron (later, afterwards) — they arrive after the closing. Kyrie kyrie anoixon hēmin (Lord, Lord, open for us) — the double kyrie echo 7:21-22 (not everyone who says Lord, Lord). The plea is urgent but belated.
25.12: ho de apokritheis eipen amēn legō hymin ouk oida hymas — answering he said: truly I say to you, I do not know you — Ouk oida hymas (I do not know you) is the language of covenant recognition; to be known by God is to be in covenant relationship (cf. 7:23 oudepote egnōn hymas). The amēn (truly) solemnizes the exclusion.
25.13: grēgoreite oun hoti ouk oidate tēn hēmeran oude tēn hōran — watch therefore because you do not know the day nor the hour — Grēgoreō (to be awake, alert — cf. 24:42) summarizes the parableâs lesson. The specific ignorance is of hēmera (day) and hōra (hour) — neither the date nor the time. The parableâs answer to this ignorance is preparedness (hetoimos), not calculation.
25.14: hōsper gar anthrōpos apodēmōn ekalesen tous idious doulous — for it is just as a man going on a journey called his own servants — Apodēmeō (to be away from home, travel abroad) — the departure initiates the stewardship period. Tous idious doulous (his own servants) = his personal household slaves. Paredōken autois ta hyparchonta autou (entrusted to them his possessions) — paradidōmi (to hand over, entrust) in the stewardship sense.
25.15: kai hō men edōken pente talanta — and to one he gave five talents — Talanton (talent) = the largest monetary unit; 1 talent = 6000 denarii (~20 yearsâ wages). The distribution is kata tēn idian dynamin (according to his own ability/capacity) — each servant receives what he can handle, not equal amounts. The differentiated trust is the premise of the parable.
25.16: poreutheis ho ta pente talanta labōn ērgasato en autois — having gone the one who received five talents worked with them — Ērgasato (aorist of ergazomai = to work, to trade) — the five-talent servant immediately (poreutheis = having gone = right away) put the capital to work. Ērgasato en autois (worked with them) — active deployment of the entrusted resources.
25.17: hōsautōs kai ho ta dyo — likewise also the one with two — Hōsautōs (in the same way, likewise) — the two-talent servant behaves identically in proportion. The parallelism emphasizes that the principle applies regardless of the quantity entrusted.
25.18: ho de to hen labōn apelthōn ōryxen gēn kai ekrysen to argyrion tou kyriou autou — but the one who received the one, having gone, dug in the ground and hid his master's silver — Ōryxen gēn (dug earth) — burying valuables was a recognized ancient security practice; but in this context it represents refusal to risk, refusal to trade. To argyrion tou kyriou autou (the silver of his master) — he acknowledges it belongs to the master but treats preservation as sufficient.
25.19: meta de chronon polun erchetai ho kyrios tōn doulōn ekeinōn — after a long time the master of those servants comes — Meta chronon polun (after much time) — the delay is explicit; this grounds the expectation of a long inter-advent period in the parableâs structure. Kai synaïrei logon met' autōn (and settles accounts with them) — synaïrō logon (to settle accounts, to reckon up a ledger — papyrus commercial term).
25.20: proseuthēn ho ta pente talanta labōn prosēnegken alla pente talanta — the one who had received five talents came forward bringing another five — Proseutheis (coming forward, presenting himself) — the presentation is formal. Alla pente talanta (another five talents) = 100% return. Kyrie pente talanta moi paredōkas idou alla pente talanta ekerdēsa (Lord, you entrusted me five talents; behold I have gained another five) — kerdainō (to gain, to earn profit) aorist.
25.21: eu doule agathe kai piste — well done, good and faithful servant — Eu (well! — the adverb of approbation) + doule agathe kai piste (good and faithful servant) — agathos (morally good) and pistos (trustworthy, faithful). Epi oliga ēs pistos epi pollōn se katastēsō (over few things you were faithful, over many things I will appoint you) — the principle of proportional promotion. Eiselthe eis tēn charan tou kyriou sou (enter into the joy of your master) — chara (joy) as the content of reward.
25.22: proseuthēn de kai ho ta dyo talanta — and also the one with two talents came forward — The two-talent servant presents with identical percentage return: alla dyo talanta ekerdēsa (I have gained another two talents). The response he receives is verbatim the same as the five-talent servant (v.23 = v.21), confirming that faithfulness is measured proportionally, not by absolute amount.
25.23: eu doule agathe kai piste epi oliga ēs pistos — well done, good and faithful servant, over few things you were faithful — The exact repetition of the masterâs commendation affirms that the two-talent servant is equally rewarded. Epi pollōn se katastēsō (over many I will appoint you) — both servants receive identical promotion, regardless of the five vs. two starting point.
25.24: proseuthēn de kai ho to hen talanton eilēphōs eipen — the one who had received one talent also came forward and said — Eilēphōs (perfect active participle of lambanō: having received and holding) — the perfect tense emphasizes he still has the original talent in his possession. His speech reveals his reasoning: egnōn se hoti sklēros ei anthrōpos (I knew you as a hard man).
25.25: kai phobētheis apelthōn ekrysa to talanton sou en tē̄ gē̄ — and having been afraid I went and hid your talent in the ground — Phobētheis (having been afraid, aorist passive participle) — fear is the stated motive for inaction. Ekrysa (I hid, aorist) — the hiding is presented as a cautious protective measure. Ide echeis to son (here you have what is yours) — the servant considers returning the original principal as sufficient; he has not lost anything.
25.26: ponēre doule kai oknēre — wicked and lazy servant — Ponēros (wicked, evil) and oknēros (shrinking, lazy, hesitant — from oknos = reluctance, shrinking) are the masterâs characterization. The charge is not theft but failure to deploy. Ēdeis hoti therizo hopou ouk espeira kai synagō hothen ou dieskorp isa — the master accepts the servantâs characterization of him and asks: given that assessment, why did you not at minimum deposit the money?
25.27: edei se oun balein ta arguria mou tois trapezitais — you ought therefore to have deposited my silver with the bankers — Trapezitai (money-changers, bankers — from trapeza = table/counter) = those who lend at interest. Kai elthōn egō ekomisamen an to emon syn tokō̧ (and coming I would have received my own with interest) — tokos (interest, usury — from tiktō = to beget, birth) = money âbegottenâ from money. Even passive interest-bearing was preferable to burial.
25.28: arate oun ap' autou to talanton kai dote tō̄ echonti ta deka talanta — take therefore from him the talent and give to the one who has ten talents — Arate (take away, aorist imperative of airō) — the removal is judicial. The transfer to the one with ten is not about maximizing returns but about the kingdom principle: entrusted resources that produce nothing are reassigned to those who will deploy them.
25.29: tō̄ gar echonti panti dothēsetai kai perisseuēthēsetai — for to the one who has, all will be given and he will have abundance — The principle tō echonti... tō de mē echonti (to the one who has... from the one who does not have) is a paradox of kingdom economics: faithful stewardship generates further entrusting; failure to use generates loss. Arthēsetai ap' autou (will be taken from him) — the divine passive.
25.30: kai ton achreon doulon ekballete eis to skotos to exōteron — cast the useless servant into the outer darkness — Achreos (useless, unprofitable — from a- + chreia = without utility/use) — the one-talent servant is declared achreos: not wicked in an active sense but failing his purpose. Eis to skotos to exōteron with the weeping-and-gnashing formula (cf. 8:12; 22:13; 24:51) — the standard Matthean judgment formula.
25.31: hotan de elthē̄ ho huios tou anthrōpou en tē̄ doxē̄ autou — when the Son of Man comes in his glory — The Sheep and Goats pericope opens the final judgment scene of Matthew. En tē̄ doxē̄ autou (in his glory) + kai pantes hoi aggeloi met' autou (and all the angels with him) — the full eschatological court. Kathisei epi thronou doxēs autou (he will sit on the throne of his glory) — echoes 19:28 (palingenesia).
25.32: kai synachthēsontai emprosthen autou panta ta ethnē — and all the nations will be gathered before him — Panta ta ethnē (all the nations) — the universal scope of this judgment. Aphorisei autous ap' allēlōn (he will separate them from one another) — aphorizō (to separate, to set apart) is the technical term for sorting (cf. 13:49). Hōsper ho poimēn aphorizei ta probata apo tōn eriphōn (as a shepherd separates the sheep from the goats) — the contrast is between probata (sheep) and eriphoi (goats — young male goats).
25.33: kai stēsei ta men probata ek dexiōn autou ta de eriphia ex euōnymōn — and he will place the sheep on his right and the goats on the left — Ek dexiōn (on the right) = the place of honor and blessing; ex euōnymōn (on the left — literally euōnymos = well-named, a euphemism for the inauspicious left). The spatial separation is symbolic of final determination.
25.34: deute hoi eulogēmenoi tou patros mou — come, the blessed of my Father — Deute (come!, plural imperative) — an invitation. Hoi eulogēmenoi (the blessed ones — perfect passive participle of eulogeō) = those who have received divine blessing. Klēronomēsate tēn hētoimasmenēn hymin basileian apo katabolēs kosmou (inherit the kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world) — apo katabolēs kosmou (from the foundation/casting-down of the world) — the kingdomâs preparation is pre-temporal.
25.35: epeinasa gar kai edōkate moi phagein — for I was hungry and you gave me food — The six categories of merciful action: hunger, thirst, stranger, naked, sick, prisoner. Phagein (to eat, aorist infinitive) — provision of food. Xenos ēmēn kai synēgagete me (I was a stranger and you gathered me in) — synagō (to gather, welcome in) used for hospitality to foreign travelers.
25.36: gymnos kai periebalete me — naked and you clothed me — Gymnos (naked, stripped down to the undergarment, inadequately clothed) — not necessarily fully nude but without adequate outer covering. Ēsthenēsa kai epeskepsasthe me (I was sick and you visited me) — episkeptomai (to look upon, to visit, to attend to — the same root as episkopē). En phylakē̄ kai ēlthete pros me (in prison and you came to me).
25.37: tote apokrithēsontai autō̄ hoi dikaioi legontes — then the righteous will answer him saying — Hoi dikaioi (the righteous ones) — their identity at the judgment. The four-fold pote se eidomen questions (vv. 37-39) express genuine surprise: when did we see you hungry... thirsty... a stranger... naked? The ignorance is authentic; their service was not performed to earn reward.
25.38: pote de se eidomen xenon kai sunēgagomen — when did we see you a stranger and welcomed you? — Xenos (stranger, foreigner, guest) — the repeated pote (when?) questions in vv.37-39 show the righteous did not consciously serve the king; they served the needy without knowing the eschatological significance.
25.39: pote de se eidomen asthenounta ē en phylakē̄ kai ēlthomen pros se — when did we see you sick or in prison and came to you? — The final two service categories complete the six. The pattern of service in vv.35-36, 38-39 is exhaustive of basic human need: physical sustenance, clothing, social welcome, medical care, prison-visiting.
25.41: poreuesthe ap' emou hoi katērarmenoi eis to pyr to aiōnion — depart from me, the cursed ones, into the eternal fire — Poreuesthe (go!, present imperative) — a dismissal, contrasting with the invitation deute (come!) to the righteous. Hoi katērarmenoi (the cursed ones — perfect passive participle of kataraomai) vs. hoi eulogēmenoi (the blessed ones, v.34). To pyr to aiōnion to hētoimasmenon tō̄ diabolō̄ kai tois angelois autou — the fire was prepared for the devil and his angels, not originally for humans; human exclusion from the kingdom is a secondary, tragic outcome.
25.42: epeinasa gar kai ouk edōkate moi phagein — for I was hungry and you did not give me food — The six acts of omission exactly mirror the six acts of commission in vv.35-36. The judgment rests on failure to act (ouk edōkate... ouk epotisate... ou synēgagete... ou periebalете... ou epeskepsasthe... ou ēlthete) — repeated aorist negations of each service category.
25.43: xenos ēmēn kai ou synēgagete me — I was a stranger and you did not welcome me — Ou synēgagete (you did not gather in, you did not welcome) — the refusal of hospitality to the stranger. The parallel structure with vv.35-36 makes the symmetry explicit: every act of mercy has its corresponding act of negligence.
25.44: tote apokrithēsontai kai autoi legontes — then they also will answer saying — Kai autoi (they also) — the goats also express surprise, mirroring the sheepâs genuine bewilderment. Kyrie pote se eidomen peinōnta ē dipōsnta ē xenon ē gymnom ē asthenounta ē en phylakē̄ — the full catalog of neglected needs is recited as a single question.
25.45: tote apokrithēsetai autois legōn amēn legō hymin — then he will answer them saying: truly I say to you — The amēn solemn introduction precedes the definitive explanation: eph' hoson ouk epoiēsate heni toutōn tōn elachistōn oude emoi epoiēsate (to the extent you did not do it to one of the least of these, neither did you do it to me). The negative identification (ouk epoiēsate) is as absolute as the positive (epoiēsate, v.40).
25.46: kai apeleusontai houtoi eis kolasin aiōnion — and these will go away into eternal punishment — Kolasis aiōnios (eternal punishment/correction — kolasis from kolazō = to curtail, to punish) vs. zōē̄n aiōnion (eternal life). Both carry the same adjective aiōnios (of the age to come, eternal/age-long) — the duration and quality of both outcomes are parallel. The same adjective applies to both, ruling out an asymmetric reading of one as temporary.

26.28: touto gar estin to haima mou tes diathēkēs to peri pollon ekchynnomenon eis aphesin hamartion — for this is my blood of the covenant which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins — the phrase draws together four OT streams: (1) covenant-blood of Sinai (haima tes diathēkēs, Exod 24:8); (2) Servant for many (peri pollon, Isa 53:12); (3) Jeremianic new covenant (Jer 31:31-34); (4) Zechariah's blood-of-the-covenant (bedam beritecha, Zech 9:11). The cup-word is a compressed Christological commentary on the whole OT sacrificial and covenantal tradition.
26.1: kai egeneto hote etelesen ho Iēsous pantas tous logous toutous — and it came to pass when Jesus finished all these words — Etelesen (aorist of teleō = to complete, finish) — the fifth and final discourse-conclusion formula in Matthew (cf. 7:28; 11:1; 13:53; 19:1). Pantas tous logous toutous (all these words) is emphatic — the entire eschatological discourse is now complete. What follows is the Passion narrative.
26.2: meta dyo hēmeras to pascha ginetai — after two days the Passover comes — Ginetai (present tense: is coming, is happening) gives immediacy to the announcement. Kai ho huios tou anthrōpou paradidotai eis to staurōthēnai (and the Son of Man is betrayed to be crucified) — paradidotai (present passive: is being handed over) — the process is already underway. Staurōthēnai (aorist passive infinitive of stauroō = to crucify) — the manner of death is specified explicitly.
26.3: tote synēchthēsan hoi archiereis kai hoi presbyteroi tou laou — then the chief priests and elders of the people were gathered — Synēchthēsan (aorist passive: were assembled) — a formal gathering of the Sanhedrin leadership. Eis tēn aulēn tou archiereōs (in the courtyard of the high priest) named Kaiaphas — the mention of the specific high priest grounds this in historical particularity.
26.4: kai synebouleusanto hina ton Iēsoun dolō kratēsōsin kai apokteinōsin — and they plotted to seize Jesus by stealth and kill him — Synebouleuō (to take counsel together, deliberate) — formal deliberation. Dolō (by trickery, by stealth — from dolos = bait, snare) — the method distinguishes their plot from open confrontation. Kratēsōsin (aorist subjunctive of krateō = to seize, arrest) and apokteinōsin (kill) — two successive aorist subjunctives in purpose.
26.5: mē en tē̄ heortē̄ hina mē thorybos genētai en tō̄ laō̧ — not during the feast lest there be a riot among the people — Thorybos (uproar, riot, tumult) — the political calculation is explicit: Passover crowds in Jerusalem (estimated up to 100,000+) made public arrest dangerous. The divine irony: their precaution to avoid the feast results in the arrest happening at Passover precisely.
26.6: tou de Iēsou genomēnou en Bēthania en oikia Simōnos tou leprou — while Jesus was in Bethany in the house of Simon the leper — Genomēnou (genitive absolute: while he was) — the temporal and spatial setting. Simon ho lepros (Simon the leper) — presumably healed; otherwise his house would be off-limits to guests and neighbors. The name is otherwise unattested in the passion story.
26.7: prosēlthen autō̄ gynē echousa alabastron myrou baretimon — a woman came to him having an alabaster flask of very precious ointment — Alabastron (alabaster flask, unguentarium) = a sealed flask of polished alabaster, typically containing expensive perfume. Myron barytimou (precious ointment — barys = heavy/weighty + timē = price/value; of great price). Katecheen epi tēs kephalēs autou (poured it on his head) — anointing the head, the prophetic/royal anointing gesture.
26.8: idontes de hoi mathētai ēganaktēsan legontes — the disciples seeing this were indignant saying — Aganakteō (to be indignant, grieved, angry) — the same verb used for the Twelveâs response to the Zebedee request (20:24). Eis ti hē apōleia autē (for what purpose this waste?) — apōleia (waste, destruction, loss — the word for Judas: huios tēs apōleias in John 17:12) is striking applied to the ointment.
26.9: edynato gar touto prathēnai pollou kai dothēnai ptōchois — for this could have been sold for much and given to the poor — Prathēnai pollou (sold for much — genitive of price) — the practical calculation is not wrong in itself; the disciplesâ error is valuing the ointment economically while missing its eschatological significance. Mark 14:5 specifies more than 300 denarii.
26.10: gnous de ho Iēsous eipen autois ti kopous parechete tē̄ gynaiki — Jesus knowing said: why do you cause trouble for the woman? — Gnous (having known — divine insight into their grumbling). Kopous parechete (you cause trouble/burdens) — kopos (toil, trouble, annoyance). Ergon gar kalon ērgasato eis eme (for a beautiful work she has done toward me) — kalon (beautiful, noble, excellent) rather than merely agathos (good).
26.11: tous ptōchous gar pantote echete meth' heautōn — for the poor you always have with you — Echoes Deut 15:11 (ou gar mē ekleipē̄ chreia apo tēs gēs). The statement is not dismissive of the poor but establishes a temporal distinction: the poor are always present for service; Jesusâ physical presence is not. Eme de ou pantote echete (but me you do not always have) — the temporal constraint governs the womanâs actionâs appropriateness.
26.12: balousa gar autē to myron touto epi tou sōmatos mou — for she, pouring this ointment on my body — Pros to entaphiasai me epoiēsen (she did it for my burial preparation) — entaphiazō (to prepare for burial, to entomb) — Jesus interprets the anointing as anticipatory burial unction. The woman acts prophetically without knowing it.
26.13: amēn legō hymin hopou ean kērychthē̄ to euangelion touto — truly I say to you wherever this gospel is proclaimed — Hopou ean... en holō̄ tō̄ kosmō̄ (wherever... in all the world) — universal scope. Kai ho epoiēsen autē lalēthēsetai eis mnēmosynon autēs (what she did will be spoken as a memorial of her) — mnēmosynon (memorial, remembrance — cf. LXX Lev 2:2 for the memorial portion of the grain offering; here applied to a person).
26.14: tote poreutheis heis tōn dōdeka ho legomenos Ioudas Iskariōtēs — then one of the Twelve, the one called Judas Iscariot — Tote (then) — the anointingâs juxtaposition with Judasâ betrayal is deliberate. Ho legomenos Ioudas Iskariōtēs (the one called Judas Iscariot) — Iskariōtēs likely = ish-Kerioth (man of Kerioth, a town in Judah) — identifying him by origin.
26.15: ti thelete moi dounai kagō hymin paradōsō auton — what are you willing to give me and I will hand him over to you? — Ti thelete moi dounai (what do you wish to give me?) — Judas initiates the price negotiation. Hoi de estēsan autō̄ triakonta argyria (they weighed out to him thirty silver pieces) — histēmi (to weigh, to set, to pay) — triakonta argyria = thirty silver pieces, echoing Zech 11:12 (the price of a slave, Ex 21:32), the prophetic valuation of the good shepherd.
26.16: kai apo tote ezētei eukairian hina auton paradō̄ — and from then he sought an opportune time to hand him over — Apo tote (from then) — a structural marker (cf. 4:17; 16:21). Eukairian (opportune time, the right moment — from eukairia = good timing, eu + kairos). Paradō̄ (aorist subjunctive of paradidōmi = to hand over, betray) — the passion verb.
26.17: tē̄ de prōtē̄ tōn azymōn prosēlthon hoi mathētai — on the first day of Unleavened Bread the disciples came — Azymōn (unleavened bread — genitive plural of azymos: without yeast) — the seven-day Passover/Unleavened Bread festival. Pou theleis hetoimasōmen soi phagein to pascha (where do you wish we prepare for you to eat the Passover?) — the technical Passover preparations required an approved room and a slaughtered lamb.
26.18: hypagete eis tēn polin pros ton deina — go into the city to a certain person — Ton deina (a certain one, so-and-so) — deliberately anonymous; Matthew may be protecting the householderâs identity. Ho kairos mou eggys estin (my time is near) — Kairos here = appointed time, the decisive moment of fulfillment (cf. Gal 4:4). Pros se poiō to pascha (at your house I will keep the Passover) — future indicative of determined intention.
26.19: kai epoiēsan hoi mathētai hōs synetaxen autois ho Iēsous — the disciples did as Jesus directed them — Syntassō (to arrange, direct, command) — the same verb for the disciplesâ obedience at the Triumphal Entry (21:6). The aorist hetoimasan to pascha (prepared the Passover) summarizes without detail.
26.20: opsias de genomenes anekeito meta tōn dōdeka — when evening came he reclined with the Twelve — Opsia (evening) — the Passover meal was eaten after sunset. Anekeito (reclined, imperfect of anakeimai) — the reclining posture of the symposium/banquet; Passover was eaten reclining as a sign of freedom (not the slaveâs standing posture). Meta tōn dōdeka (with the Twelve) — the full inner circle.
26.21: kai esthiontōn autōn eipen amēn legō hymin — while they were eating he said: truly I say to you — The genitive absolute esthiontōn autōn (while they were eating) situates the announcement mid-meal. The amēn solemn introduction signals the gravity of what follows: heis ex hymōn paradōsei me (one of you will hand me over).
26.22: kai lypumenoi sphodra ērxanto legein autō̄ heis hekastos — deeply grieved they began to say to him, one by one — Lypeomai sphodra (to be deeply grieved, intensely pained) — sphodra (intensely, greatly). Heis hekastos (each one individually) — each disciple separately asks mēti egō eimi kyrie (surely it is not I, Lord?) — the mēti (particle expecting a negative answer) reveals each believes himself incapable of betrayal.
26.23: ho embapsas met' emou tēn cheira en tō̄ trybliō̧ houtos me paradōsei — the one who dipped his hand with me in the dish, this one will betray me — Embapto (to dip, immerse) in tryblio (a common dish, bowl) — the shared dipping of the haggadah sop. The identification is not narrowed to one person; many at the table would dip together. The act specifies intimate table fellowship as the context of betrayal — echoing Ps 41:9.
26.24: ho men huios tou anthrōpou hypagei kathōs gegraptai peri autou — the Son of Man goes as it has been written concerning him — Hypagō (to go away) as a euphemism for going to death; kathōs gegraptai (as it has been written) — the divine necessity of scripture fulfillment. Ouai de tō̄ anthrōpō̧ ekeinō̧ di' hou ho huios tou anthrōpou paradidotai — the woe (ouai) on the betrayer is juxtaposed with the divine dei (necessity): scriptureâs fulfillment does not absolve the agent.
26.25: apokritheis de Ioudas ho paradidous auton eipen mēti egō eimi Rhabbi — Judas who was betraying him answered: surely it is not I, Rabbi? — Judas uses Rhabbi (Rabbi) while the other disciples used Kyrie (Lord) — the title distinction may reflect Judasâs relationship to Jesus as teacher but not as Lord. Legei autō̄ sy eipas (he says to him: you have said it) — sy eipas (you said it) is an affirmative formula confirming the questionâs truth without a direct yes.
26.26: esthiontōn de autōn labōn ho Iēsous arton — while they were eating Jesus took bread — The institution narrative (vv.26-29) occurs during the meal. Eulogēsas eklāsen (having blessed he broke) — the berakah blessing and breaking of bread. Kai edōken tois mathētais kai eipen labete phagete touto estin to sōma mou (took, gave to the disciples and said: take, eat; this is my body) — sōma (body) without qualifier; the identification is direct.
26.27: kai labōn potērion kai eucharistēsas edōken autois legōn — and taking a cup and giving thanks he gave to them saying — Eucharistēsas (having given thanks — from eucharisteō, the source of the word Eucharist) vs. the earlier eulogēsas (blessed) over the bread; the two forms of blessing-prayer are functionally synonymous in Jewish practice. Piete ex autou pantes (drink from it, all of you) — the pantes (all) is inclusive and imperative.
26.29: ou mē piō ap' arti ek toutou tou genēmatos tēs ampelou — I will by no means drink from now from this produce of the vine — Ou mē + aorist subjunctive = the strongest negation: absolutely not. Ap' arti (from now, from this moment) — the eschatological vow of abstinence: Jesus will not drink wine again until the kingdom comes. Heōs tēs hēmeras ekeinēs hotan auto pinō meth' hymōn kainon en tē̄ basileiā̧ tou patros mou — the future Messianic banquet (kainon = new, fresh) is the anticipated reunion.
26.30: kai hymnēsantes exēlthon eis to oros tōn elaiōn — and having sung a hymn they went out to the Mount of Olives — Hymneō (to sing a hymn) — at Passover the Hallel psalms were sung: Pss 113-114 before the meal, 115-118 after. The Hallelujah psalms concluded the Passover observance. Eis to oros tōn elaiōn (to the Mount of Olives) — east of Jerusalem across the Kidron Valley.
26.31: pantes hymeis skandalisthēsesthe en emoi en tē̄ nykti tautē̄ — all of you will fall away because of me this night — Skandalizō (to cause to stumble, to fall away) — en emoi (because of me, on account of me) = Jesus himself will be the occasion of their stumbling. Gegraptai gar (for it is written) — Zech 13:7: pataxō ton poimena kai diaskorpisthēsontai ta probata (strike the shepherd and the sheep will be scattered).
26.32: meta de to egerthēnai me proaxō hymas eis tēn Galilaian — but after my being raised I will go before you to Galilee — To egerthēnai me (my being raised — aorist passive infinitive) — the resurrection is assumed even in the prediction of scattering. Proaxō (I will go before, lead the way) — the risen Jesus will precede the disciples to Galilee, the place of mission origin. This anticipates the Great Commission scene (28:16-20).
26.33: apokritheis de ho Petros eipen autō̄ ei kai pantes skandalisthēsontai en soi — Peter answered: even if all fall away because of you — Peterâs ei kai pantes (even if all) — he dissociates himself from the group prediction. Egō oudepote skandalisthēsomai (I will never fall away) — oudepote (never, not at any time) is the strongest possible self-assertion.
26.34: ephē autō̄ ho Iēsous amēn legō soi hoti en tautē̄ tē̄ nykti — Jesus said: truly I say to you, in this very night — The amēn introduction precedes a counter-prophecy. Prin alektora phōnēsai tris aparnēsē̄ me (before a cock crows you will deny me three times) — alektōr (rooster) — the third watch of the night (midnight to 3 AM) was called the alektorophonia (cock-crow watch). Aparneomai (to deny, disown, utterly refuse association with).
26.35: kan dei me syn soi apothanein ou mē se aparnēsomai — even if I must die with you I will by no means deny you — Kan (even if — contracted from kai ean). Dei (it is necessary) + apothanein (to die, aorist infinitive). Ou mē aparnēsomai (I will by no means deny) — the double negative with aorist subjunctive: Peterâs vow matches the force of Jesusâ prediction. Homoiōs de kai pantes hoi mathētai eipan (similarly all the disciples also said) — collective overconfidence.
26.36: tote erchetai met' autōn ho Iēsous eis chōrion legomenon Gethsēmani — then Jesus comes with them to a place called Gethsemane — Gethsēmani (from Aramaic gat-šĕmānê = oil press) — an olive garden/press on the western slope of the Mount of Olives. Kathisate autou heōs hou apelthōn ekei proseuxōmai (sit here while I go there and pray) — Jesus separates the eight from the three.
26.37: kai paralabōn ton Petron kai tous dyo huious Zebedaiou — and taking Peter and the two sons of Zebedee — The inner circle of three (Peter, James, John) who witnessed the Transfiguration (17:1) are now brought to witness the agony. Ērxato lypeisthai kai adēmonein (he began to be grieved and distressed) — adēmoneō (to be distressed, to be in anguish — a rare verb suggesting disorientation and anxiety) paired with lypeō (to grieve).
26.38: perilypos estin hē psychē mou heōs thanatou — my soul is deeply grieved to the point of death — Perilypos (overwhelmed with grief, surrounded by sorrow — peri = around + lypē = grief) — the intensified compound. Heōs thanatou (to death, as far as death) — the grief is mortal in its intensity, echoing Ps 42:6,11 LXX. Meinate hōde kai grēgoreite met' emou (stay here and watch with me) — the call for companionship in suffering.
26.39: kai proelthōn mikron epesen epi prosōpon autou proseuchomenos — going a little further he fell on his face praying — Epesen epi prosōpon autou (fell upon his face) — full prostration, the most intense posture of supplication. Pater ei dynaton estin parelthātō ap' emou to potērion touto (Father, if it is possible let this cup pass from me) — ei dynaton (if possible) — genuine submission to the Fatherâs capacity to provide another way. Plēn ouch hōs egō thelō all' hōs sy (yet not as I will but as you) — the subordination of the Sonâs desire to the Fatherâs.
26.40: kai erchetai pros tous mathētas kai heuriskei autous katheudōntas — he comes to the disciples and finds them sleeping — Heuriskei (present tense: finds) gives the discovery immediacy. Kai legei tō̄ Petrō̧ houtōs ouk ischysate mian hōran grēgorēsai met' emou (and says to Peter: so you could not watch one hour with me?) — the address singles out Peter, who had just professed willingness to die with Jesus.
26.41: grēgoreite kai proseuchesthe hina mē eiselthēte eis peirasmon — watch and pray lest you enter into temptation/trial — Eiselthēte eis peirasmon (enter into temptation/trial) — peirasmos (test, trial, temptation) echoes the Lordâs Prayer petition (6:13). To men pneuma prothumon hē de sarx asthenēs (the spirit is willing/ready but the flesh is weak) — prothumon (eager, ready, forward-moving — from pro + thymos = passion rushing forward) contrasted with asthenēs (without strength, feeble).
26.42: palin ek deuterou apelthōn prosēuxato — going away again a second time he prayed — Ek deuterou (a second time) marks the repetition explicitly. Pater mou ei ou dynatai touto parelthein ean mē auto piō (my Father, if this cannot pass unless I drink it) — the second prayer intensifies the submission: the condition shifts from ei dynaton (if possible) to ei ou dynatai... ean mē auto piō (if it cannot pass unless I drink it) — progressive acceptance of the cup.
26.43: kai elthōn palin heurenautous katheudōntas — coming again he found them sleeping — Ēsan gar autōn hoi ophthalmoi bebarēmenoi (for their eyes were heavy, weighed down) — bebarēmenoi (perfect passive participle of bareō = to weigh down, burden) — the perfect describes a settled state of heaviness. The disciples cannot maintain even this minimal companionship.
26.44: kai apheis autous palin apelthōn prosēuxato ek tritou — leaving them again he went away and prayed a third time — Ek tritou (a third time) — three prayers mirror the three denials Peter will make; the structural parallelism is notable. Ton auton logon eipōn palin (saying the same word again) — verbatim repetition of prayer language indicating sustained, consistent submission.
26.45: tote erchetai pros tous mathētas kai legei autois katheudete loipon — then he comes to the disciples and says: sleep on now — Katheudete loipon (sleep on now, and take your rest) — the tone is ambiguous: either permission/irony (the moment of arrest is upon them) or a genuine brief rest before the arrest. Idou ēggiken hē hōra (behold the hour has drawn near) — ēggiken perfect: has drawn near and is now here.
26.46: egeiresthe agōmen — rise, let us go — Egeirō (to raise, arouse) aorist imperative + agōmen (let us go) present subjunctive — a decisive call to action. Idou ēggiken ho paradidous me (behold the one handing me over has drawn near) — the same perfect ēggiken: Judasâ arrival coincides with the announcement of the hourâs coming.
26.47: kai eti autou lalountos idou Ioudas — and while he was still speaking, behold Judas — Eti autou lalountos (genitive absolute: while he was still speaking) — the immediacy is dramatic; the prediction and its fulfillment are simultaneous. Heis tōn dōdeka ēlthen (one of the Twelve came) — the repeated designation heis tōn dōdeka (one of the Twelve) sharpens the betrayalâs horror: intimacy makes the act worse.
26.48: ho de paradidous auton edōken autois sēmeion — the one betraying him had given them a sign — Ho paradidous (the one handing over/betraying — present active participle, almost a technical title for Judas). Sēmeion (a sign, signal) — a pre-arranged identification signal. Hon an philēsō autos estin (whoever I kiss, he is the one) — philēsō (aorist subjunctive of phileō = to love, kiss as a greeting) — a kiss (philēma) was the standard greeting between teacher and student.
26.49: kai eutheōs proselthōn tō̄ Iēsou eipen chaire Rhabbi — and immediately approaching Jesus he said: greetings, Rabbi — Chaire (hail, rejoice, greetings — the standard Greek greeting). Rhabbi again (cf. 26:25) — not kyrie. Kai katephilēsen auton (and kissed him warmly) — kataphileō (intensive form with kata-: to kiss affectionately, to kiss repeatedly) — the intensified compound makes the betrayalâs tenderness more grotesque.
26.50: ho de Iēsous eipen autō̄ hetaire eph' ho parei — Jesus said to him: friend, for what purpose are you here? — Hetaire (companion — the distancing form; third use in Matthew: 20:13; 22:12) — gentle but not intimate. Eph' ho parei (for what purpose you have come) — traditionally taken as a question but may be a statement: do what you have come for. Tote proselthontes epebalon tas cheiras epi ton Iēsoun kai ekratēsan auton (then approaching they laid hands on Jesus and seized him).
26.51: kai idou heis tōn meta Iēsou ekteinas tēn cheira apespasen tēn machairan autou — behold one of those with Jesus drew his sword — Apespasen (drew, pulled out — aorist of aposoaō) the sword. Kai pataxas ton doulon tou archiereōs apheilen autou to ōtion (and striking the servant of the high priest cut off his ear) — ōtion (diminutive: the ear; ous = ear; John 18:10 names him Malchus and the striker as Peter).
26.52: tote legei autō̄ ho Iēsous apostrepson tēn machairan sou eis ton topon autēs — then Jesus says: put your sword back into its place — Apostrepō (to turn back, return) imperative — a direct command. Pantes gar hoi labontes machairan en machairā̄ apolountai (for all those who take the sword by the sword will perish) — a proverbial statement: en machairā̄ (by the sword, instrumental dative) — violence self-propagates until it consumes its agents.
26.53: ē dokeis hoti ou dynamai parakalesai ton patera mou — do you think I cannot call on my Father? — Parakalesai (to call alongside, appeal to, summon) — Jesus could appeal to the Father for rescue. Kai parastēsei moi arti pleio ē dōdeka legiones angelōn (and he would at once provide me more than twelve legions of angels) — legiōn (Roman legion = ~6,000 soldiers) — twelve legions = ~72,000 angels. The capacity for divine rescue exists; the choice not to use it is itself the obedience.
26.54: pōs oun plērōthōsin hai graphai hoti houtōs dei genesthai — how then would the scriptures be fulfilled that it must happen this way? — Plērōthōsin (be fulfilled, aorist passive subjunctive) — the fulfillment of scripture is the governing constraint on Jesusâ actions. Dei genesthai (it must happen, it is necessary to happen) — the divine dei of eschatological necessity.
26.55: hōs epi lēstēn exēlthate meta machairōn kai xylōn syllabein me — as against a bandit you came out with swords and clubs to arrest me — Lēstēs (bandit, robber, insurgent — the same word as Barabbas and the crucified men) — the armed arrest befits a violent criminal, not a teacher. Kath' hēmeran en tō̄ hierō̄ ekathezomēn didaskōn (daily I was sitting in the temple teaching) — ekathezomēn imperfect: continuous daily public presence; no secrecy required arrest in the dark.
26.56: touto de holon gegonen hina plērōthōsin hai graphai tōn prophētōn — all this has happened so that the writings of the prophets might be fulfilled — Touto de holon (but all this) — the entire arrest sequence is enclosed within divine purpose. Tote hoi mathētai pantes aphentes auton ephygon (then all the disciples leaving him fled) — aphentes (having left behind) fulfills the scattering prediction of v.31 and Zech 13:7.
26.57: hoi de kratēsantes ton Iēsoun apēgagon pros Kaiaphan — those who had seized Jesus led him away to Caiaphas — Apēgagon (led away, aorist of apagō = to lead off) — the judicial transfer. Hopou hoi grammateis kai hoi presbyteroi synēchthēsan (where the scribes and elders were assembled) — the Sanhedrin in extraordinary night session.
26.58: ho de Petros ēkolouthei autō̄ makrothen — Peter was following him from a distance — Makrothen (from a distance, afar) — the adverb signals Peterâs ambivalence: he follows but maintains protective distance. Heōs tēs aulēs tou archiereōs (as far as the courtyard of the high priest). Kai eiselthōn esō ekathēto meta tōn hypēretōn (and entering inside he sat with the officers) — hypēretai (officers, attendants, servants) = the temple police/Sanhedrin staff.
26.59: hoi de archiereis kai to synedrion holon ezētoun pseudomartyrian kata tou Iēsou — the chief priests and the whole council were seeking false testimony against Jesus — Pseudomartyria (false testimony — pseudēs + martyria) — Matthew characterizes the testimony as false from the outset. Hopōs auton thanatōsōsin (in order that they might put him to death) — thanatōsōsin (aorist subjunctive of thanatoō = to put to death, to cause to die) — capital intent governs the proceedings.
26.60: kai ouch heuron pollōn proselthontōn pseudomartyrōn — and did not find it, though many false witnesses came forward — The negative ouch heuron (did not find) implies that the testimony was insufficient or contradictory (cf. Mark 14:56: hai martyriai ouk ēsan isai = the testimonies were not consistent). Two witnesses were required by Deut 17:6; 19:15 for capital charges.
26.61: houtos ephē dynamai katalysai ton naon tou theou — this one said: I am able to tear down the temple of God — The false witnesses misquote Jesusâ statement (cf. John 2:19 where Jesus said lysate = you destroy); they substitute dynamai katalysai (I am able to tear down — first person boast) for Jesusâ second-person conditional. Kai dia triōn hēmerōn oikodomēsai (and in three days to rebuild it).
26.62: kai anastas ho archiereus eipen autō̄ ouden apokrinou — the high priest stood up and said: do you answer nothing? — Anastas (having stood up, rising) signals judicial importance. Ti houtoi sou katamartyrousi (what these testify against you?) — the high priest presses for a response that would either condemn or acquit.
26.63: ho de Iēsous esiōpa — Jesus was silent — Siopaō (imperfect: was remaining silent, maintained silence) — the fulfillment of Isa 53:7 (hōs probaton epi sphagēn: as a sheep before its shearers is silent). The silence is deliberate and meaningful, not inability. The high priest then invokes the divine oath: exorkizō se kata tou theou tou zōntos (I adjure you by the living God) — a formal oath-charge compelling testimony.
26.64: plēn legō hymin ap' arti opsesthe ton huion tou anthrōpou — but I tell you, from now you will see the Son of Man — Sy eipas (you have said it) first affirms the title, then adds plēn (nevertheless, but I tell you) with a further disclosure. Kathēmenon ek dexiōn tēs dynameōs (sitting at the right hand of Power) — hē dynamis (Power = divine name by circumlocution). Kai erchomenon epi tōn nephelōn tou ouranou quotes Dan 7:13 — the coming with clouds of heaven.
26.65: tote ho archiereus dierrēxen ta himatia autou — then the high priest tore his garments — Diarrēgnymi (to tear apart, rip through) the garments — the prescribed gesture upon hearing blasphemy (bēasphēmia) in rabbinic law (m. Sanh. 7:5). Blasphēmēsen (he has blasphemed) — the accusation; the high priestâs own judgment (ti eti chreian echomen martyrōn = what further need have we of witnesses?) uses the first-person plural, implicating the whole council.
26.66: ti hymin dokei — what do you think? — The judicial vote-request. Hoi de apokrithentes eipan enochos thanatou estin (answering they said: he is liable to death) — Enochos (liable to, guilty of — genitive of charge) + thanatou (death) — a capital verdict rendered by the full council.
26.67: tote eneptysan eis to prosōpon autou — then they spat in his face — Emptyo eis prosōpon (to spit into the face) — the deepest contempt gesture in ancient culture (Num 12:14; Job 30:10). Ekolaphisan auton (they struck him with their fists) — kolaphizō (to strike with the knuckles). Hoi de errapisan (others slapped him) — rhapizō (to slap with an open hand).
26.68: prophēteuson hēmin christe tis estin ho paisas se — prophesy to us, Christ, who is the one who struck you? — Prophēteuson (prophesy! — aorist imperative) + christe (Christ! — the messianic title used as mockery) — the taunt forces a demonstration of the very messianic gift he claimed. The guards who struck him blindfolded him first (Mark 14:65; Luke 22:64), making identification impossible by natural means.
26.69: ho de Petros ekathēto exō en tē̄ aulē̄ — Peter was sitting outside in the courtyard — The narrative shifts back to Peter (cf. v.58). Kai prosēlthen autō̄ mia paidiskē (and a servant girl came to him) — paidiskē (a young female slave, a servant girl) — the contrast between the accused Jesus inside and the accuserâs servant outside is structurally deliberate.
26.70: ho de ērnēsato emprosthen pantōn — he denied before all — Arneomai (to deny, to disavow) — the verb for denial that Jesus predicted. Ouk oida ti legeis (I do not know what you are saying) — the first denial is categorical ignorance: oida (to know) negated.
26.71: exelthonta de auton eis ton pylōna — going out to the gateway — Peter moves to the exit area; another servant girl (allē paidiskē) makes a more direct claim: kai houtos ēn meta Iēsou tou Nazōraiou (this one was with Jesus the Nazarene) — meta (with) = association/discipleship.
26.72: kai palin ērnēsato meta horkou — again he denied with an oath — Meta horkou (with an oath) — the escalation from simple denial (v.70) to an oath-reinforced denial. Hoti ouk oida ton anthrōpon (that I do not know the man) — ton anthrōpon (the man) — Peter distances himself with the indefinite reference rather than naming Jesus.
26.73: kai hē lalia sou dēlon se poiei — your speech makes you evident — Lalia (speech, way of speaking, dialect) — the Galilean accent or dialect was distinctive to Jerusalem ears. Dēlon se poiei (makes you manifest, obvious) — the very speech that denies association betrays it. Kai gar Galilaios ei (for indeed you are a Galilean).
26.74: tote ērxato anathematizein kai omnynai hoti ouk oida ton anthrōpon — then he began to call down curses and to swear: I do not know the man — Anathematizō (to call down a curse, to invoke a curse on oneself) — invoking divine judgment on oneself if the statement is false. Omnymi (to swear an oath) — the third denial combines a self-curse and an oath, the most intense verbal repudiation possible. Kai eutheōs alektōr ephōnēsen (and immediately a cock crowed) — the fulfillment of 26:34.
26.75: kai emnēsthē ho Petros tou rhēmatos Iēsou — Peter remembered the word of Jesus — Emnēsthē (aorist passive of mimnēskomai = to remember) — the memory is involuntary, brought on by the cockâs crow. Tou rhēmatos (the word, the statement) — specific recall of the prediction. Kai exelthōn exō eklausen pikrōs (and going outside he wept bitterly) — pikrōs (bitterly, from pikros = sharp, piercing) — the adverb intensifies the weeping into anguished grief.

27.46: eli eli lema sabachthani — My God, my God, why have you forsaken me — the Aramaic form (Matthew uses transliterated Aramaic, Mark has Aramaic too; both differ slightly from each other and from the LXX) reproduces the opening of Ps 22:1. The question hina ti (why, for what purpose) is not a denial of God's sovereignty but a genuine cry from within the experience of abandonment. Jesus prays the whole Psalm, which ends in vindication (Ps 22:24-31); the cry of dereliction is the beginning of the Passion Psalm, not its whole message. The darkness and the cry together communicate the full weight of bearing divine wrath on behalf of others.
27.1: prōias de genomenes symbo ulion elabon pantes hoi archiereis kai hoi presbyteroi — when morning came all the chief priests and elders took counsel — Prōias de genomenes (morning having come, genitive absolute) — after the night trial a formal daytime session is required for a legally valid Sanhedrin capital verdict. Symbolion elabon (took counsel, formed a plan) — symbolion (council, deliberation) — the formal morning ratification of the nocturnal vote.
27.2: kai dēsantes auton apēgagon kai paredōkan Pilatō̄ tō̄ hēgemoni — and binding him they led him away and handed him over to Pilate the governor — Dēsantes (having bound, aorist participle) — binding a prisoner for transport. Apēgagon (led away) and paredōkan (handed over) — the two passion verbs in sequence. Hēgemōn (governor, one who leads) = the Roman prefectâs title; Pilate served as prefect of Judea AD 26-36.
27.3: tote idōn Ioudas ho paradous auton hoti katekrithē metamelētheis — then Judas who had betrayed him seeing that he was condemned felt remorse — Metamelomai (to feel regret, to have a change of feeling — not the same as metanoeō: repentance leading to restoration). The aorist passive katekrithē (was condemned) — Judas did not anticipate this outcome. Estrēpsen ta triakonta argyria (brought back the thirty silver pieces) — strephō (to turn, return) the coins.
27.4: hēmarton paradous haima athō̄on — I sinned betraying innocent blood — Hēmarton (I sinned, aorist) — a confession of sin. Haima athō̄on (innocent blood — athō̄os = without penalty, guiltless) is a legal and theological term; shedding innocent blood incurred divine judgment (Deut 19:10; 1 Kgs 2:31). Ti pros hēmas sy opsei (what is that to us? See to it yourself) — the leadersâ dismissal is callous legal language.
27.5: kai rhipsas ta argyria eis ton naon anechōrēsen — throwing the silver into the sanctuary he departed — Rhipsas (having thrown, cast) — a violent gesture of rejection. Eis ton naon (into the sanctuary/inner temple) — only priests could enter; Judas throws the coins from outside into the sacred space. Apelthōn apēgxato (departed and hanged himself) — apagchomai (to strangle oneself, hang oneself) — cf. Acts 1:18âs different account of the death.
27.6: hoi de archiereis labontes ta argyria eipan — the chief priests taking the silver said — Ouk exestin balein auta eis ton korbōnan (it is not lawful to throw them into the treasury) — korbōnas (the temple treasury — from Hebrew qorban = offering dedicated to God). The irony: they have no scruple about paying for innocent blood but scruple about returning the money to Godâs treasury. Epei timē haimatos estin (since it is the price of blood).
27.7: symbo ulion de labontes ēgorasan ex autōn ton agron tou kerameos — taking counsel they bought with them the potterâs field — Agros tou kerameos (the potterâs field) — a clay-rich plot used for pottery manufacture, purchased for taphēn tois xenois (burial of strangers/foreigners). The leadersâ decision ironically fulfills prophecy through their own initiative.
27.8: dio eklēthē ho agros ekeinos Agros Haimatos heōs tēs sēmeron — therefore that field was called Field of Blood until today — Agros Haimatos = Hakeldama (Aramaic, Acts 1:19). The heōs tēs sēmeron (until today) formula marks an etiology — explaining a place-name by its origin story, a common OT narrative device.
27.9: tote eplērōthē to rhēthen dia Ieremiou tou prophētou — then was fulfilled what was spoken through Jeremiah the prophet — The attribution to Jeremiah is puzzling; the citation conflates Zech 11:12-13 with Jer 18-19 (the potterâs field/Hinnom Valley imagery). Some scholars suggest Ieremiou is a scribal error for Zachariou; others note Jeremiahâs purchase of a field (Jer 32) as the deeper background. Matthewâs LXX-influenced citation is a composite prophecy.
27.10: kai edōkan auta eis ton agron tou kerameos kathōs synetaxen moi kyrios — and gave them for the potterâs field as the Lord directed me — Kathōs synetaxen moi kyrios (as the Lord directed/commanded me) — citing the prophetic word as divine directive.
